TL;DR: This paper proposes a perceptual-quality-aware AMC framework for mmWave/sub-THz systems, replacing traditional CQI with a SSIM-based Perceptual Quality Indicator (PQI) and using a DnCNN denoiser at the receiver, achieving up to 2× spectral efficiency gains on a 5G mmWave testbed while maintaining perceptual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-throughput applications (e.g., UHD video, XR) prioritize perceptual quality over bit-level accuracy. In mmWave/sub-THz systems, severe channel impairments (path loss, phase noise) degrade spectral efficiency, motivating a link adaptation framework that optimizes for human-perceived quality rather than strict bitwise reconstruction.

Method: The authors extend conventional AMC by replacing the CQI with a PQI derived from SSIM. The receiver uses a DnCNN denoiser to enhance post-decoding image quality before feedback estimation. The AMC loop adapts to maximize spectral efficiency under a perceptual-fidelity constraint.

Result: Experiments on a 5G-compliant mmWave testbed show up to a twofold gain in spectral efficiency while maintaining perceptual fidelity compared to conventional bit-error-rate-based AMC.

Conclusion: Perception-optimized link adaptation significantly improves spectral efficiency for perceptual-quality-driven applications in challenging mmWave/sub-THz channels, demonstrating the potential of human-perception-aware wireless system design.

Abstract: For high-throughput applications such as ultra-high-definition video streaming and immersive extended-reality, perceptual quality rather than bit-level accuracy defines the primary performance criterion and provides a more informative and spectrally efficient objective than strict bitwise reconstruction. This is particularly relevant in millimeter-wave (mmWave) and sub-Terahertz (sub-THz) systems, where path loss, short channel coherence times and phase noise introduce severe fluctuations that degrade link spectral efficiency. We propose an extension to conventional Adaptive Modulation and Coding (AMC) framework that incorporates perceptual quality awareness into link adaptation. In this framework, the decision metric is a Perceptual Quality Indicator (PQI) derived from the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM). The receiver employs a Denoising Convolutional Neural Network (DnCNN) denoiser to enhance post-decoding image quality before feedback estimation. The resulting perceptual metric replaces the standard Channel Quality Indicator (CQI) in the AMC loop, enabling adaptation to maximize spectral efficiency while satisfying a perceptual-fidelity constraint. Experiments on a 5G-compliant mmWave testbed demonstrate up to a twofold gain in spectral efficiency while maintaining perceptual fidelity, underscoring the potential of perception-optimized link adaptation.

</details>


### [138] [Cooperative Uplink Channel Estimation in User-Centric Cell-free Massive MIMO Communication Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31510)
*Pourya Behmandpoor,Marc Moonen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a cooperative MMSE-based uplink channel estimation approach for cell-free mMIMO networks, where APs share linearly compressed signals with cluster peers. This one-shot, non-iterative method achieves centralized-level performance with lower communication overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CE methods originate from cellular mMIMO and do not account for CFmMIMO's unique properties, such as shorter AP-UE distances leading to Rician channels with prominent LoS components, which motivates AP cooperation for improved performance.

Method: A cooperative MMSE-based uplink CE approach where APs share linearly compressed signals (fused signals) with other APs in the same cluster. Optimal fusion filters and estimators are derived non-iteratively in one shot using required correlation matrices.

Result: The proposed approach achieves performance equivalent to centralized CE (which shares uncompressed raw signals) but with lower communication overhead. Numerical experiments show superior CE accuracy and convergence rate.

Conclusion: The cooperative MMSE-based CE approach is optimal and efficient for CFmMIMO systems, leveraging one-shot fusion to balance performance and communication overhead effectively.

Abstract: Cell-free massive multi-input-multi-output (CFmMIMO) communication networks aim to provide uniform quality of service by distributing access points (APs) across a coverage area. In user-centric variants, each user equipment (UE) can choose a cluster of APs with the best channel conditions (e.g., the closest APs) for accessing service. This approach eliminates the notion of cells with dedicated regions and APs, as found in cellular mMIMO communication networks. Estimating uplink channels between UEs and APs is a crucial step in CFmMIMO communication networks; however, existing channel estimation (CE) approaches typically originate from mMIMO systems without considering the unique properties of CFmMIMO communication networks. For instance, shorter AP-UE distances in CFmMIMO systems result in Rician channel models with prominent line of sight (LoS) components between APs and UEs, motivating cooperation between APs for improved performance. In this paper, we propose a cooperative minimum-mean-squared-error (MMSE)-based uplink CE approach where APs share their linearly compressed signals as fused signals with other APs in the same cluster. The proposed approach is optimal, i.e., its performance is equivalent to that of the centralized CE approach, where APs share their uncompressed raw signals. Notably, this optimality is achieved in one shot; that is, given the required correlation matrices, the optimal fusion filters and estimators are derived non-iteratively. Consequently, the proposed approach guarantees lower communication overhead for cooperative CE compared to the centralized approach. Numerical experiments corroborate the superior performance of the proposed cooperative CE approaches in terms of CE accuracy and convergence rate.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [139] [Structured interactions improve distributed coordination beyond model scaling in a real-world multi-robot system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30383)
*Junping Wang,Zhizhong Zhang,Yongqiang Tang,Geng Zheng,Jiaming Zhang,Shiji Song,Yanmei Li,Yushan Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper compares communication topology redesign vs. scaling model size in multi-robot systems, finding that switching from fully connected to modular hierarchical interactions yields 47-point performance gains vs. only 9 points from doubling neural network hidden size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether restructuring communication among robots yields larger gains than increasing onboard model size under matched hardware budgets, addressing a system-level design question in real-world multi-robot coordination.

Method: Used a transport-and-mapping task with 10 physical robots (5 runs per condition, 60 runs total), comparing fully connected vs. modular hierarchical topologies and varying neural network hidden sizes. Nested mixed-effects models and SMAC replications were used for validation.

Result: Topology redesign improved performance by 47 points vs. 9 points from doubling hidden size. Model fit was substantially better for topology. Performance saturation beyond 1024 hidden units was observed in simulation-calibrated extrapolation.

Conclusion: Interaction structure can play a dominant role over model scaling within the tested multi-robot system, though broader quantitative generalization remains to be established.

Abstract: Scaling individual robot capabilities is common but costly. Here we investigate a system-level design question in real-world multi-robot coordination: given matched hardware budgets, does restructuring communication among robots yield larger gains than increasing onboard model size? Using a representative transport-and-mapping task with 10 physical robots (5 runs per condition, 60 runs total), we find that switching from fully connected to modular hierarchical interactions improves normalised performance by 47 points (0--100), whereas doubling neural network hidden size yields at most 9 points. Nested mixed-effects model comparisons show a substantially larger improvement in model fit for topology than for scale. The pattern is confirmed in independent SMAC replications; heterogeneous benchmark reanalyses provide secondary supporting consistency checks rather than primary evidence. Performance saturation beyond 1024 hidden units is observed in simulation-calibrated extrapolation, not directly on hardware. These results indicate that interaction structure can play a dominant role within the tested system and task setting, while broader quantitative generalisation remains to be established.

</details>


### [140] [Learning-Based Navigation for Indoor Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30468)
*Tri-Tin Nguyen,Tien-Dat Nguyen,Gia-Uy Le,Vinh Nguyen,Vinh-Hao Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid navigation framework for indoor mobile robots combining a supervised neural global planner (trained from A* expert trajectories) with a learning-based DWA local planner refined via PPO, achieving safe and efficient goal-directed navigation in simulated and real environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of traditional navigation methods (e.g., A* + DWA) by integrating learning-based techniques for both global route planning and local motion control, improving feasibility and adaptability in indoor obstacle-rich environments.

Method: (1) Supervised neural global planner trained on cost-aware A* expert trajectories. (2) Learning-Based DWA local planner: first behavior cloning, then refined via PPO with feasibility-aware masking. (3) Evaluated in simulated and real-world indoor settings.

Result: The method generates feasible global routes and reliable local motion commands, enabling safe goal-directed navigation with obstacles. Demonstrated effectiveness in both simulation and real-world indoor environments.

Conclusion: Integrating learning-based global planning with RL-refined local control is effective for indoor mobile robot navigation; source code is publicly available.

Abstract: This paper presents a learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots. The proposed method combines a supervised neural global planner, trained from cost-aware A* expert trajectories, with the proposed Learning-Based DWA local planner, which is formulated as discrete candidate selection over the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) action lattice. For local planning, the policy is first trained by behavior cloning and then refined by Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under feasibility-aware masking. The framework is implemented and evaluated in both simulated and real-world indoor environments. Experimental results show that the proposed method generates feasible global routes and reliable local motion commands for safe goal-directed navigation in the presence of obstacles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating learning-based global planning with reinforcement-learning-refined local control for indoor mobile robot navigation. The source code will be released at https://ntdathp.github.io/rl_robot_web/.

</details>


### [141] [ELAN4D: Embodiment-Centric 4D Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Models via Plug-and-Play Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30484)
*Zeyuan He,Bowen Yang,Zhirui Fang,Keru Zhou,Lei Jiang,Jingjing Qian,Fan Mo,Junchi Yan,Philip Torr,Xiu Li,Li Jiang,Jialin Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ELAN4D enhances VLA robotic manipulation policies by adding future robot keypoint tracks as 4D spatio-temporal supervision, improving robustness under out-of-distribution perturbations without changing the inference interface.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA policies operate reactively by directly regressing actions from current observations without explicitly modeling future dynamics, limiting their generalization under out-of-distribution perturbations.

Method: ELAN4D uses forward kinematics from proprioceptive states to derive 3D displacement tracks of robot keypoints, injecting a plug-and-play auxiliary branch with a lightweight track decoder into the action expert while preserving the pretrained vision-language backbone through gradient isolation. The track decoder is discarded during inference.

Result: Consistent improvements over strong VLA baselines on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0, and real-world tasks, with substantial gains under camera, background, and layout shifts.

Conclusion: Embodiment-centric 4D supervision is effective for building more robust and generalizable manipulation policies.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise for robotic manipulation, yet most existing policies operate reactively by directly regressing actions from current observations, without explicitly modeling future dynamics. This limits their ability to generalize under out-of-distribution perturbations. To address this issue, we propose ELAN4D, an embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training framework that enhances VLA policies with future robot keypoint tracks as predictive spatio-temporal supervision. Using only forward kinematics from proprioceptive states, we derive 3D displacement tracks of robot keypoints, such as joints and the end-effector, with negligible preprocess cost. These tracks provide metric and compact supervision without requiring external trackers or reconstruction. A plug-and-play auxiliary branch with a lightweight track decoder injects this 4D signal into the action expert while preserving the pretrained vision-language backbone through gradient isolation. The track decoder is discarded during inference, leaving the base policy interface unchanged. Extensive experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0 and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that ELAN4D consistently improves over strong VLA baselines, achieving the best overall performance and substantial gains under out-of-distribution perturbations, including camera, background, and layout shifts. These results highlight the effectiveness of embodiment-centric 4D supervision for building more robust and generalizable manipulation policies.

</details>


### [142] [CoMo3R-SLAM: Collaborative Monocular Dense SLAM with Learned 3D Reconstruction Priors for Outdoor Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30488)
*Zhihao Cao,Qi Shao,Shuhao Zhai,Feng Tian,Anh Nguyen,Hesheng Wang,Baoru Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoMo3R-SLAM is the first collaborative monocular dense RGB SLAM system for outdoor multi-robot mapping, using learned feed-forward 3D reconstruction priors to achieve real-time tracking, dense fusion, and globally consistent metric maps without depth sensors, matching RGB-D performance at 8 FPS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing collaborative dense SLAM systems depend on depth sensors (heavy payload, power, cost), while monocular approaches suffer from scale ambiguity and unreliable inter-agent data association in outdoor scenes with low overlap and repetitive structures.

Method: Each agent runs a prior-guided front-end for real-time tracking and local dense fusion; a coordinator performs dense pointmap matching for cross-agent verification, closed-form Sim(3) gauge synchronization, and GPU-accelerated global bundle adjustment with segment-level depth optimization.

Result: On Tanks and Temples and Waymo, achieves best ATE on 3 of 4 Tanks and Temples scenes and competitive Waymo accuracy, matching/exceeding state-of-the-art RGB-D methods while running online at 8 FPS.

Conclusion: CoMo3R-SLAM demonstrates that robust collaborative monocular dense mapping without depth sensors is feasible, achieving RGB-D level accuracy with lighter hardware requirements.

Abstract: Collaborative dense SLAM is essential for multi-robot teams to achieve scalable and consistent 3D perception across large-scale outdoor environments. Existing systems typically depend on depth sensors, incurring significant payload, power, and calibration costs. Monocular RGB cameras are a lightweight alternative, but collaborative monocular dense SLAM remains difficult due to scale ambiguity, unreliable inter-agent data association, especially in outdoor scenes where low overlap and repetitive structures make traditional feature matching unreliable, motivating robust geometric information. We propose CoMo3R-SLAM, the first collaborative monocular dense RGB SLAM system that leverages robust learned feed-forward 3D reconstruction priors for outdoor multi-agent mapping. Each agent runs a prior-guided front-end for real-time tracking and local dense fusion, while a coordinator performs dense pointmap matching for cross-agent verification, closed-form Sim(3) gauge synchronization, and GPU-accelerated global bundle adjustment with segment-level depth optimization. Requiring neither depth sensors nor parametric intrinsics, our system produces robust cross-agent constraints and globally consistent metric maps from monocular RGB alone. On Tanks and Temples and Waymo sequences, CoMo3R-SLAM achieves the best ATE on three of four Tanks and Temples scenes and competitive Waymo accuracy, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art RGB-D methods while running online at 8 FPS.

</details>


### [143] [Physics-informed Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning under Hybrid Contact Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30503)
*Vittorio Giammarino,Anastasios Manganaris,Ahmed H. Qureshi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper identifies that existing physics-informed goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (Pi-GCRL) methods degrade in contact-rich manipulation tasks due to hybrid dynamics, mode-dependent controllability, and nonsmooth value landscapes. The authors propose contact-aware and hierarchical formulations that selectively apply physics-informed inductive biases to address these challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Pi-GCRL methods work well in navigation and object-free goal-reaching but fail in contact-rich manipulation because contact interactions introduce hybrid dynamics, mode-dependent controllability, and nonsmooth value landscapes that degrade naive applications of physics-informed biases.

Method: The authors analyze the failure modes of existing Pi-GCRL on contact-rich tasks, then introduce contact-aware and hierarchical formulations that selectively apply physics-informed inductive biases across different parts of the manipulation problem.

Result: Existing Pi-GCRL methods degrade when naively applied to contact-rich manipulation due to structural properties of contact dynamics; the proposed contact-aware and hierarchical formulations provide a principled improvement.

Conclusion: This work takes a principled step toward extending Pi-GCRL to contact-rich manipulation by introducing formulations that selectively apply physics-informed biases to handle hybrid, contact-dependent dynamics.

Abstract: Learning to reach arbitrary goals from sparse feedback requires agents to infer a rich notion of reachability across state--goal pairs. Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) tackles this challenge by learning policies that generalize across goals, but this generalization becomes increasingly difficult as the underlying dynamics become high-dimensional, hybrid, or contact-dependent. To address this issue, physics-informed GCRL (Pi-GCRL) introduces optimal-control-inspired inductive biases into goal-conditioned value learning. While Pi-GCRL methods have proven effective in navigation and object-free goal-reaching domains, their reliability in contact-rich tasks remains unclear, where contact interactions induce hybrid dynamics, mode-dependent controllability, and nonsmooth value landscapes. In this work, we show that these structural properties can cause existing Pi-GCRL methods to degrade when applied naively to contact-rich manipulation. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce contact-aware and hierarchical formulations that apply physics-informed inductive biases selectively across the manipulation problem. Our results provide a principled step toward extending Pi-GCRL to contact-rich manipulation.

</details>


### [144] [VLM-GLoc: Vision-Language Model Enhanced Monte Carlo Localization for Robust Semantic Global Localization in Cluttered Quasi-Static Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30506)
*Shivendra Agrawal,Bradley Hayes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLM-GLoc uses Vision-Language Models for hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization to achieve robust global localization in geometrically ambiguous indoor environments, outperforming traditional methods with 70-74% success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional localization methods fail in geometrically aliased environments (grocery stores, offices) due to repetitive layouts and long-tail semantic distributions. Existing approaches rely on distinct geometric features or domain-specific pipelines that struggle with ambiguity and visual clutter.

Method: VLM-GLoc integrates open-vocabulary VLMs as a semantic observation front-end for hierarchical Monte Carlo Localization, featuring: (1) discriminative text feature extraction, (2) implicit quality filtering of blurry/dynamic objects, (3) permanence reasoning for data augmentation, and an inverse semantic proposal mechanism for text-to-map particle seeding.

Result: Achieved 70% global localization success in a 3,500 sq. ft. grocery store (cellphone platform) and 74% in a 3,700 sq. ft. lab space (quadruped platform), substantially outperforming geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

Conclusion: VLMs provide a unified, effective semantic front-end for robot localization in challenging quasi-static indoor environments with geometric aliasing, enabling better robustness without requiring domain-specific training.

Abstract: Global localization in geometrically aliased, quasi-static environments such as grocery stores, offices, schools, and hospitals poses a significant challenge for mobile robots. Grocery stores with parallel aisles and a long tailed distribution of products, as well as offices and labs with repetitive furniture such as chairs, desks, monitors, and doors, exemplify common indoor environments that present geometric and even semantic ambiguity. Traditional approaches rely either on distinct geometric features or on domain-specific vision pipelines that struggle with long-tail semantic distributions and transient visual clutter. We present VLM-GLoc, a method for hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) that leverages open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a unified semantic observation front-end. We hypothesize a three-fold benefit from VLMs: (1) extracting highly discriminative rich text features, (2) implicit quality filtering of blurry or dynamic objects, and (3) permanence reasoning for targeted data augmentation. We introduce an inverse semantic proposal mechanism that seeds particles via text-to-map retrieval. Evaluated across two real-world environments with different characteristics and two different platforms: a 3,500 sq. ft. grocery store with a cellphone and a 3,700 sq. ft. lab space with a quadruped, VLM-GLoc achieves 70% and 74% global localization success respectively, substantially outperforming traditional geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

</details>


### [145] [ARISTO Hand: Sensing-Driven Distal Hyperextension for Fine-Grained Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30508)
*Aaron Kim,Dong Ho Kang,Mark Helwig,Mingyo Seo,Kazuto Yokoyama,Tetsuya Narita,Luis Sentis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The ARISTO Hand is a tendon-driven robotic hand with active distal hyperextension and hybrid fingertip sensing (rigid nail-mounted force-torque sensor + soft capacitive tactile array), improving pull-out force by 2.76x for thin objects (1-20 mm) while maintaining grasp capability, validated through SD card manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manipulating thin objects is challenging due to the need for precise contact geometry and reliable force perception. Anthropomorphic robotic hands often lack the mechanical and sensing capabilities for such fine interactions.

Method: The ARISTO Hand integrates active distal hyperextension (beyond standard flexion limits) with a hybrid fingertip-sensing architecture combining a rigid nail-mounted force-torque sensor and a soft capacitive tactile array.

Result: Active hyperextension increased pull-out force by 2.76x for object thicknesses of 1-20 mm while preserving nominal grasp capability. The rigid nail-mounted sensor provides reliable force measurements during edge contacts where proprioceptive estimation degrades.

Conclusion: The proposed fingertip architecture with active hyperextension and hybrid sensing enables reliable thin object manipulation, validated through quantitative force characterization and a multi-stage SD card extraction and insertion task.

Abstract: Manipulating thin objects requires precise contact geometry and reliable force perception, yet many anthropomorphic robotic hands lack the mechanical and sensing capabilities needed for such interactions. We present the ARISTO Hand, a tendon-driven robotic hand that integrates active distal hyperextension with a hybrid fingertip-sensing architecture that combines a rigid, nail-mounted force-torque sensor and a soft capacitive tactile array. Active hyperextension enables controlled fingertip engagement beyond the kinematic limits of standard flexion, increasing pull-out force by 2.76x for object thicknesses of 1-20 mm while preserving the nominal grasp capability. The rigid nail-mounted sensor provides reliable force measurements during edge contacts, where the sensitivity of proprioceptive force estimation degrades as the contact geometry approaches kinematic singularities. We validate the proposed architecture through quantitative force characterization and a multi-stage SD card extraction and insertion task. Video and supplementary materials are available at: https://aristohand.github.io

</details>


### [146] [Any-ttach: Quick End-effector Swapping Enables Manipulation Dexterity with Simplicity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30569)
*Weizhe Ni,Jinzhou Li,Haoyu Li,Cody Andres Alessio-Bunnell,Wenjing Pan,Xianyi Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Any-ttach is a tool-centric manipulation framework that achieves dexterity through rapid end-effector swapping rather than complex multi-fingered hands, featuring an automatic swapping mechanism, demonstration collection system, and task planning for diverse tool-use skills.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human hand complexity may largely exist to enable tool use and making, suggesting that robotic dexterity can be achieved through tool swapping rather than building increasingly complex high-DoF hands.

Method: The system combines: (1) a low-cost automatic swapping mechanism for open-close robot interfaces, (2) a handheld demonstration collection device, and (3) a task planning framework composing learned, parameterized, and planned tool-use skills.

Result: Any-ttach improves tool-swapping reliability, increases demonstration efficiency, reduces tool-pose variability, and successfully executes six tool-use subskills in two long-horizon tasks (making a sandwich and preparing a cucumber) through end-effector switching and execution monitoring.

Conclusion: Robots can expand manipulation capability not only through more complex end-effectors but also through rapidly exchangeable tools and end-effector modules.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation dexterity is often pursued by building increasingly complex high-DoF multifingered hands. While many robotic hands are designed to replicate human morphology, the functional role of human hands suggests a different perspective: much of their complexity may exist to enable tool use and tool making. This observation motivates Any-ttach, a tool-centric manipulation framework that treats quick end-effector swapping as a mechanism for dexterity with simplicity. Any-ttach combines a low-cost automatic swapping mechanism for an open-close robot interface, a handheld device for collecting human demonstrations, and a task planning framework that composes learned, parameterized, and planned tool-use skills. The system supports diverse tools and end-effector modules, including daily tools, articulated tools such as scissors, Fin Ray fingers, and a low-cost anthropomorphic hand, through the same shared interface. Our experiments show that Any-ttach improves tool-swapping reliability, increases demonstration efficiency, reduces tool-pose variability, and supports diverse tool-use skills. In two long-horizon tasks, making a sandwich and preparing a cucumber, Any-ttach executes six tool-use subskills through end-effector switching and execution monitoring. These results suggest that robots can expand manipulation capability not only through more complex end-effectors, but also through rapidly exchangeable tools and end-effector modules. More details and videos are available at https://any-ttach.github.io/.

</details>


### [147] [Caspar: CUDA Accelerator for Symbolic Programming with Adaptive Reordering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30583)
*Emil Martens,Aaron Miller,Matias Varnum,Annette Stahl*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Caspar is a library that makes GPU optimization accessible in robotics by auto-generating CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions. It delivers 5-20x speedup over state-of-the-art bundle adjusters with less memory usage and similar accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between expressive symbolic Python programming and high-performance GPU runtimes in C++, making GPU power more accessible for robotics optimization problems.

Method: Caspar automatically generates optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions (built on SymForce). Users define symbolic residual functions, and Caspar uses symbolic differentiation to generate GPU kernels and interfaces for nonlinear optimization.

Result: On the Bundle Adjustment in the Large (BAL) dataset, Caspar is 5-20x faster than the best alternative bundle adjuster, requires less memory, and achieves similar accuracy.

Conclusion: The symbolic GPU programming approach is highly effective for robotics optimization, as demonstrated by significant speed and memory improvements over existing methods.

Abstract: We present Caspar, a library that makes the power of modern GPUs more accessible in robotics and provides a state-of-the-art nonlinear GPU solver that can be applied to a wide range of different optimization problems. Caspar bridges the gap between expressive symbolic programming in Python and high-performance GPU runtimes in C++ by automatically generating optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions. Building on the SymForce library, users can easily define and combine symbolic expressions, including Lie group operations, to generate custom CUDA kernels. To use Caspar as a solver, users need only define the symbolic residual functions; Caspar then uses symbolic differentiation to generate the necessary GPU kernels and interfaces to perform nonlinear optimization.
  In this paper, we present the core components of Caspar and showcase its performance by performing bundle adjustment on the Bundle Adjustment in the Large (BAL) dataset. We benchmark Caspar against other state-of-the-art bundle adjusters and show that it is 5 to 20 times faster than the best alternative, requires less memory, and achieves similar accuracy. This illustrates the benefit of our symbolic GPU programming approach. Caspar is released as part of SymForce and is freely available at https://github.com/symforce-org/symforce

</details>


### [148] [ZAPS-DA: Zero-Phase Action Policy Smoothing with Decoupled Actor for Continuous Control in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30612)
*Faiq Shamass*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ZAPS-DA reduces action jitter in off-policy RL policies by training a separate decoupled actor via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered replay buffer targets, achieving smooth deployment without filters, phase lag, or hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous control policies trained with off-policy RL produce high-frequency action jitter that prevents direct physical deployment; existing solutions (post-hoc filtering or smoothness penalties) introduce phase lag or conflate reward regression with aggressive smoothing.

Method: ZAPS-DA pairs an unmodified main actor (trained by base RL loss) with a decoupled actor trained via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets from the replay buffer. The deployed policy is the decoupled actor (feed-forward, no filter, no action-history input) — termed "causal distillation of a non-causal filter." Uses a magnitude-matched MSE loss for zero-hyperparameter portability.

Result: In MetaDrive: 14-21x steering jitter reduction, 3-5x throttle jitter reduction (p<10^-4), matching task-completion (p=0.28 success, p=0.31 crash) at 6.3% reward cost. In Webots: Pareto improvement — reward parity (p=0.121), 8-45x steering jitter reduction, failure rate from 2.0% to 0.7%.

Conclusion: ZAPS-DA effectively eliminates action jitter at deployment with negligible phase lag and no post-processing, validated across two driving simulators with strong statistical significance.

Abstract: Continuous control policies trained with off-policy reinforcement learning frequently exhibit high-frequency action jitter, rendering direct deployment on physical actuators impractical. Post-hoc filtering attenuates jitter but introduces phase lag; embedding smoothness penalties in the actor's loss couples them with the RL gradient and conflates reward regression with over-aggressive smoothing. We present ZAPS-DA, a framework that reduces action jitter at deployment with negligible phase lag and no post-processing. ZAPS-DA pairs an unmodified main actor (trained by the base RL loss) with a separate decoupled actor trained via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets stored in the replay buffer. The deployed policy is the decoupled actor: a feed-forward map from the current observation to a smooth action, with no inference-time filter and no action-history input -- a mechanism we term causal distillation of a non-causal filter. A magnitude-matched MSE loss provides zero-hyperparameter portability across optimizer classes. Validated with Soft Actor-Critic and a Savitzky--Golay filter in two driving simulators using paired n=150 evaluation protocols: on MetaDrive, ZAPS-DA reduces steering jitter by 14--21x and throttle jitter by 3--5x (all $p < 10^{-4}$, Bonferroni-corrected) while matching task-completion (p=0.28 success, p=0.31 crash) at a 6.3% reward cost; on a custom Webots adaptive cruise control environment, the same SG configuration produces a Pareto improvement -- reward parity (p=0.121), 8--45x steering jitter reduction, and total task-failure rate reduced from 2.0% to 0.7%.

</details>


### [149] [Exploiting Chordal Sparsity for Globally Optimal Estimation with Factor Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30617)
*Avinash Subramanian,Connor Holmes,Timothy D. Barfoot,Frank Dellaert,Frederike Dümbgen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a new method to automatically construct convex SDP relaxations for factor graphs in GTSAM, and exploits the Bayes tree to decompose the SDP for faster computation, achieving more robust global estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard local solvers in factor-graph frameworks like GTSAM can converge to poor local minima, posing safety risks. Meanwhile, convex relaxation techniques (SDPs) can globally solve or certify problems but are hard to formulate and computationally expensive.

Method: 1) Automatically constructs convex SDP relaxations for any factor graph with common factor/variable types within GTSAM. 2) Exploits the Bayes tree structure native to GTSAM to decompose the SDP problem, achieving speedup for chordally sparse problems.

Result: Demonstrates favorable scaling of the structure-exploiting global estimator compared to standard local solvers in two case studies: 3D pose-graph SLAM (ring graph) and 2D localization (chain graph).

Conclusion: The proposed approach bridges the gap between efficient local solvers and globally optimal convex relaxations, making global estimation more practical for robotics state estimation.

Abstract: Robust and efficient state estimation is crucial for perception, navigation, and control in robotics. State estimation problems are conveniently modeled using the factor-graph framework as enabled by modern software packages such as GTSAM or g2o. However, the standard solvers included in such frameworks are local and may converge to poor local minima, posing significant safety concerns. Conversely, techniques based on convex relaxations have been shown to provide a means of globally solving or certifying many state estimation problems. However, these relaxations 1) often require substantial effort to formulate, and 2) may incur significantly higher cost compared to efficient local solvers, as they require solving a large semidefinite program (SDP). In this work, we address both shortcomings by 1) creating a new procedure within the GTSAM framework for automatically constructing convex SDP relaxations for any factor graphs with common factor and variable types, and by 2) exploiting the Bayes tree constructions native to GTSAM to decompose the SDP problem, leading to significant speedup in solver time for chordally sparse problems. We demonstrate the favorable scaling of this structure-exploiting global estimator compared to standard local solvers for two case studies: A 3D pose-graph SLAM problem with a ring factor graph and a 2D localization problem with a chain factor graph. The software framework is available at https://github.com/borglab/gtsam.

</details>


### [150] [Bidirectional Incremental Generalized Hybrid A*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30647)
*Sidharth Talia,Oren Salzman,Siddhartha Srinivasa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bi-IGHA* is a bidirectional extension of Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* that mitigates the problem of frozen vertices hiding solution paths in anytime kinodynamic planning, offering substantial reduction in search expansions while preserving theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient anytime kinodynamic planning for systems with complex dynamics in unstructured environments is challenging because precomputing motion primitives is infeasible, and existing methods like Hybrid A* and IGHA* suffer from coupling issues or hidden solutions due to frozen vertices.

Method: Extends IGHA* into a bidirectional search (Bi-IGHA*) to mitigate the problem of frozen vertices hiding solution-supporting vertices, while preserving IGHA*'s guarantees on monotonic cost improvement and termination.

Result: Bi-IGHA* substantially reduces expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems and achieves equivalent closed-loop performance for high-speed off-road autonomy with significantly fewer expansions.

Conclusion: Bidirectional search fundamentally mitigates the frozen-vertex-induced solution hiding problem in anytime hierarchical kinodynamic planning, improving efficiency without sacrificing theoretical guarantees or practical performance.

Abstract: We focus on the problem of efficient anytime kinodynamic planning for systems with complex dynamics in unstructured environments that make precomputing motion primitives infeasible. Directly applying A* to such problems is computationally infeasible due to the curse of dimensionality. Methods such as Hybrid A* addressed this burden by discretizing the state space, but in turn creating a coupling between tree discovery and the discretization resolution. The Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* (IGHA*) performs search over a hierarchy of resolutions in an anytime fashion to break this coupling, by freezing vertices to use in later search iterations rather than pruning them. However, the frozen vertices can hide solution-supporting vertices from the search at a particular iteration. While classical bidirectional search is motivated by the reduction of search depth, extending IGHA* into the bidirectional setting (termed Bi-IGHA*) obtains additional benefit by fundamentally mitigating the behaviour induced by frozen vertices hiding solutions. We show that Bi-IGHA* preserves IGHA*'s guarantees on monotonic cost improvement and termination. We empirically show that Bi-IGHA* substantially reduces expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems, and achieves equivalent closed-loop performance with kinodynamic planning for high-speed off-road autonomy while requiring significantly fewer expansions. Website: https://personalrobotics.github.io/IGHAStar/biighastar.html

</details>


### [151] [Primitive Subspaces Mediate Few-Shot Transfer in VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30695)
*Anya Singh,Cabrel Happi,Jai Relan,Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Primitive-aware training of VLA policies enables 3× more sample-efficient few-shot transfer to new tasks by learning composable sub-skills, requiring only 3 demonstrations to reach 78% of fine-tuned performance, and primitive representations are causally necessary for this transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying VLA policies in industrial settings requires low-cost teaching of new tasks, but current VLAs need expensive fine-tuning for each new task, which is impractical.

Method: Trained OpenVLA and π_{0.5} on REASSEMBLE dataset with primitive-segmented episodes and language prompts, evaluated few-shot transfer with m∈{0,1,3,5,10} demonstrations on held-out tasks, and ablated primitive-decodable hidden states to test causality.

Result: Primitive-trained models achieved 78% of fine-tuned performance with only 3 demonstrations (vs. flat-trained requiring 10), demonstrating a 3× sample efficiency gap; ablation of primitive representations degraded transfer by 32 points while random ablation had no effect.

Conclusion: Primitive-aware training produces transferable sub-skill libraries enabling sample-efficient few-shot task transfer, and primitive representations are causally necessary for this capability.

Abstract: Deploying vision-language-action (VLA) policies in industrial environments requires the ability to teach new tasks at low cost, a property current VLAs lack, since each new task requires fine-tuning. We investigate whether primitive-aware training produces a transferable artifact: a learned library of sub-skills that can be composed at inference time, conditioned on a small number of demonstrations, to perform tasks the policy was never trained on. We train two VLA architectures with different inductive biases, OpenVLA and $π_{0.5}$, on the REASSEMBLE contact-rich assembly dataset under matched LoRA fine-tuning recipes and locked hyperparameters, varying training between flat trajectories and primitive-segmented episodes with primitive-specific language prompts. We hold out 6 object-task combinations from training and evaluate few-shot transfer: models receive $m \in \{0, 1, 3, 5, 10\}$ demonstrations of a held-out task and attempt execution without weight updates. We replicate across three training seeds and validate on a second dataset (LIBERO-Long). Primitive-trained models reach 78% of fine-tuned upper-bound performance with only m=3 demonstrations, while flat-trained models require m=10 demonstrations to reach the same level -- a $3\times$ sample efficiency gap that replicates across seeds, architectures, and datasets. To establish causation, we ablate the primitive-decodable subspace of hidden states and show few-shot transfer degrades by 32 percentage points while ablating a random subspace of equal dimensionality has no effect, indicating primitive representations are causally necessary rather than incidentally correlated with transfer. We identify and correct a methodological pitfall in evaluating chunked policies: family-wise inflation of single-step action-range gates produces order-of-magnitude higher false-failure rates against ground-truth human demonstrations.

</details>


### [152] [Geometry-Aware Control Barrier Functions for Collision Avoidance via Bernstein Polynomial Approximations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30696)
*Siwon Jo,Yanze Zhang,Yupeng Yang,Wenhao Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a geometry-aware Control Barrier Function using Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs) for safe robot navigation, offering a unified representation of obstacles and robots that reduces conservatism and constraint counts compared to traditional shape surrogates, with validation in single and multi-robot simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CBF-based safety methods rely on shape surrogates (spheres, super-ellipsoids) that are either too conservative for irregular geometries or require many local primitives, leading to high constraint counts and degraded real-time performance.

Method: Introduces a novel CBF based on Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs), which provides a unified differentiable representation of both obstacles and robots, enabling closed-loop control constraint enforcement.

Result: Validated via simulations showing efficiency and safety guarantees in single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance across different environments.

Conclusion: The BP-SDF-based CBF offers a geometry-aware, unified, and differentiable approach that balances safety and efficiency for robots with irregular geometries.

Abstract: Safe navigation often relies on well-defined conditions based on the shape of robots and obstacles, and can be challenging when they have irregular geometries. While Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) offer an efficient mechanism to enforce safe set forward invariance, common shape surrogates (e.g., spheres or super-ellipsoids) either are overly conservative in unstructured scenes or require many local primitives, which inflates constraint counts and degrades real-time performance. In this paper, we introduce a novel geometry-aware Control Barrier Function (CBF) based on Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs). It provides a unified way to represent the obstacles and robots, so as to represent the barrier function with a unified minimum distance. Benefiting from the differentiability of the Bernstein polynomials, one can easily enforce the control constraints in a closed loop. We validate the method's efficiency and performance to guarantee safety in single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance via simulations under different environments.

</details>


### [153] [GSAM: A Generalizable and Safe Robotic Framework for Articulated Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30740)
*Beichen Shao,Mengying Xie,Heng Su,Wanyi Zhang,Mingyan Li,Yan Ding,Fausto Giunchiglia,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GSAM is a robotic framework for articulated object manipulation that uses a vision-based perceiver, a fine-tuned VLM with commonsense reasoning, and an LLM-driven constraint-based planner to improve generalization and prevent destructive collisions, achieving 36% higher success rate over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for articulated object manipulation overlook object diversity and complex end-effector-handle interactions, causing limited generalization and destructive collisions.

Method: GSAM consists of (1) a vision-based perceiver for kinematic parameters, (2) a fine-tuned VLM refiner using chain-of-thought commonsense reasoning, (3) an interaction constraint function generator integrating articulation, pose, and obstacle knowledge, (4) LLM-based trajectory/posture planning, and (5) a kinematic-aware manipulation planner for reachability verification.

Result: On 50 hinge tasks across 5 object categories and 50 random configurations, GSAM reduces standard deviation by 3.1% and improves manipulation success rate by 36.0% over the best baseline.

Conclusion: GSAM demonstrates superior object generalization and interaction safety for articulated object manipulation in practical scenarios.

Abstract: Articulated object manipulation is a unique challenge for service robots. Existing methods employ end-to-end policy learning, visionmotion planning, and large-language/visual-language model (LLM/VLM), but often overlook the diversity of articulated objects and the complexity of interactions between end-effector and handle, leading to limited generalization and destructive collisions. To address this, we propose GSAM, a generalizable and safe robotic framework for articulated object manipulation. Specifically, a vision-based perceiver generates the kinematic parameters. Considering that pre-trained markers in perceiver yield raw estimations that may deviate from commonsense, we present a f ine-tuned VLM-based refiner, using chain-of-thought (COT) commonsense reasoning to refine perception. To prevent destructive collisions, we design an interaction constraint function generator, integrating articulated object, interaction pose, and obstacle avoidance knowledge into a base. LLM then functionalize these constraints and apply them to trajectory and posture planning. A kinematic-aware manipulation planner verifies reachability for trajectory and posture. Experiments on 50 hinge tasks across 5 object categories and 50 randomly initialized end-effectorhandle configurations show that GSAM reduces standard deviation by 3.1% and improves manipulation success rate by 36.0% compared to the best baseline, respectively demonstrating the superior object generalization and interaction safety of GSAM in practical scenarios.

</details>


### [154] [SSR: Scaling Surefooted and Symmetric Humanoid Traversal to the Open World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30770)
*Ruiqi Yu,Yiwen Wang,Yuan Hao,Jun WU,Qiuguo Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SSR is an end-to-end vision-based framework for humanoid robots that enables safe, stable, and natural traversal across diverse real-world terrains by jointly learning foothold guidance, bilateral coordination, and human-like motion priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending humanoid traversal to open, real-world environments is critical for practical deployment but remains challenging due to the need for vision-guided foot placement on heterogeneous terrain under dynamic motion with coordinated whole-body behaviors.

Method: SSR introduces three key components: (1) imagined foothold guidance that models swing-foot contacts to guide stable foot placement, (2) equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation for efficient bilateral coordination under visual input, and (3) terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to encourage human-like behaviors across different scenes.

Result: SSR achieves safe, stable, and high-quality locomotion on diverse real-world terrains including stairs, wide gaps, high platforms, and long-horizon traversal in open outdoor environments.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end framework effectively addresses the key challenges of vision-based humanoid traversal in complex, unstructured environments, enabling practical deployment in the open world.

Abstract: Extending humanoid traversal to the open world is key to practical deployment in human environments, but remains challenging. The robot must use vision to ensure safe and reliable foot placement on heterogeneous terrain under highly dynamic motion, while producing coordinated, natural whole-body behaviors. We propose SSR, an efficient end-to-end framework for egocentric vision-based humanoid traversal that jointly learns these capabilities. SSR introduces imagined foothold guidance, which learns to model forthcoming swing-foot contacts and evaluates their support to guide pre-touchdown swings toward stable regions, reducing edge slips. It further employs equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation to efficiently induce bilateral coordination under high-dimensional visual observations, and uses terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to encourage human-like behavior across scenes. Extensive experiments show that SSR achieves safe, stable, and high-quality locomotion on diverse real-world terrains, including stairs with varied structures and extreme challenges such as wide gaps and high platforms, while enabling reliable long-horizon traversal in open outdoor environments.

</details>


### [155] [Object-Informed Model Predictive Path Integral Control for Non-Prehensile Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30778)
*Nikola Raicevic,Bharath Raam Radhakrishnan,Chenbin Yu,Ki Myung Brian Lee,Nikolay Atanasov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical MPPI control method that uses a simplified object-level plan to guide robot-level planning for non-prehensile manipulation, achieving 40% higher success and 26% faster control frequency in simulation, and 20% higher success in real experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon planning for non-prehensile robot manipulation (e.g., pushing) is difficult due to underactuated and discontinuous interactions, making it hard for standard methods to achieve efficient, non-myopic reasoning.

Method: A hierarchical MPPI framework: first solve a simplified object-only planning problem (assuming direct actuation), then use the planned object trajectory as a reference to guide the joint robot-object MPPI planning.

Result: Object-informed MPPI improves task success by 40% with 26% faster control frequency in simulation, and by 20% in real experiments, with comparable computation to regular MPPI.

Conclusion: Hierarchical planning using object-level guidance significantly enhances long-horizon pushing task performance without additional computational overhead.

Abstract: Long-horizon planning for non-prehensile robot manipulation is challenging due to underactuated and discontinuous interactions. We propose a hierarchical formulation of model predictive path integral (MPPI) control that guides robot-level planning with a separately computed object-level plan to achieve efficient long-horizon prediction. We first solve a simplified object-only problem, assuming the object can be actuated directly, and use the planned object trajectory as a reference in solving the joint robot-object planning problem. We evaluate our method in both simulation and hardware using a 6-DoF xArm6 manipulator to perform object pushing tasks in which the target object must reach a goal while avoiding static obstacles, necessitating non-myopic reasoning. Our object-informed MPPI increases task success by 40\% with a 26\% faster control frequency in simulation, and by 20\% in real experiments with similar computation as regular MPPI.

</details>


### [156] [Two Degree-of-Freedom Vibratory Transport in a Grasp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30780)
*C. L. Yako,Shenli Yuan,Kenneth Salisbury*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates 2-DoF in-hand manipulation using asymmetric vibrations from controlled stick-slip waveforms, validating theoretical models of part motion and achieving translation/rotation of grasped parts with a parallel jaw gripper.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable precise in-hand manipulation of grasped parts using asymmetric vibrations without complex robotic hand mechanisms.

Method: Closed-loop position control generates periodic stick-slip waveforms; theoretical modeling of sticking/slipping acceleration parameters; validated with encoder-measured experimental setup; 2-DoF vibratory surface designed and paired in a parallel jaw gripper.

Result: Theoretical trends for sticking/slipping acceleration vs. average part velocity validated; 2-DoF bidirectional translation and in-plane rotation demonstrated for various parts; waveform trends confirmed for rotation.

Conclusion: Asymmetric vibration control is an effective method for 2-DoF in-hand manipulation, and can be extended to rotational motion with the same waveform principles.

Abstract: In this paper, we use asymmetric vibrations to demonstrate two degree-of-freedom (DoF) in-hand manipulation of grasped parts. The asymmetric vibrations are achieved through closed-loop position control of a moving surface, which applies a periodic stick-slip waveform to the part to be manipulated. We show analytically how two vibratory waveform parameters, the sticking acceleration and the slipping acceleration, affect average part velocity when moving against gravity. The theoretical trends are then validated using an experimental setup where the squeeze force is controlled and part motion is recorded by a high-resolution encoder. We also develop a 2-DoF vibratory surface capable of translation in one direction and rotation about the surface normal. Using two of these 2-DoF surfaces in a parallel jaw gripper configuration, we bidirectionally translate and rotate a variety of grasped parts, as well as demonstrate that the same waveform trends for translation also persist for in-plane rotation.

</details>


### [157] [Feat2Go: Visual Feature-Grounded Value Estimation for Embodied Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30795)
*Junyang Shu,Zhiwei Lin,Bingqing Wei,Yongtao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Feat2Go is a fine-grained value estimation framework that improves Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model performance in reinforcement learning by deriving continuous progress targets from a pretrained visual world model and using them to reshape terminal rewards, achieving significant success rate improvements on ManiSkill3 (17.5% to 82.9%) and RoboTwin 2.0 (88.8%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning for VLA models suffers from sparse supervision and difficulty in designing informative reward signals for long-horizon manipulation tasks, motivating a need for automated, fine-grained reward shaping without manual engineering.

Method: Feat2Go first derives a continuous progress target by measuring patch-level similarity to subgoal states from a pretrained visual world model, partitions episodes into semantic stages via trend-based clustering, then trains an embodied value model to predict this structural progress and uses it to reshape terminal rewards during policy optimization (compatible with PPO, GRPO).

Result: On ManiSkill3, OpenVLAOFT improved from 17.5% to 82.9% average out-of-distribution success while retaining 96.9% in-distribution performance. On RoboTwin 2.0, achieved 88.8% average success rate in domain-randomized settings, outperforming prior RL methods.

Conclusion: Feat2Go consistently improves VLA model performance across both single-arm and bimanual manipulation settings without manual reward engineering, demonstrating a practical approach for fine-grained reward shaping in embodied RL.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is a promising approach for improving the capabilities of vision-language-action (VLA) models while avoiding the heavy data requirements of imitation learning. However, its effectiveness for VLA models is often constrained by sparse supervision and the difficulty of designing informative reward signals for long-horizon manipulation. In this work, we present Feat2Go, a fine-grained value estimation framework for embodied reinforcement learning. Specifically, Feat2Go first derives a continuous progress target from a pretrained visual world model by measuring patch-level similarity to subgoal states and partitioning episodes into semantic stages with trend-based clustering. We then train an embodied value model to predict this structural progress from the current observation and task instruction, and use the predicted value to reshape terminal rewards during policy optimization. The proposed framework is compatible with existing VLA policy reinforcement learning pipelines, including PPO and GRPO, and does not rely on manual reward engineering. Extensive experiments on ManiSkill3 and RoboTwin 2.0 demonstrate that Feat2Go consistently improves the performance of existing VLA models under both single-arm and bimanual manipulation settings. More specifically, on ManiSkill3, Feat2Go improves OpenVLAOFT from 17.5% to 82.9% average out-of-distribution success while retaining 96.9% in-distribution performance. On RoboTwin 2.0, Feat2Go achieves an average success rate of 88.8% in domain-randomized task settings, outperforming prior reinforcement learning methods.

</details>


### [158] [Hide-and-Seek in Trajectories: Discovering Failure Signals for VLA Runtime Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30834)
*Seongheon Park,Wendi Li,Changdae Oh,Samuel Yeh,Zsolt Kira,Michael Hagenow,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hide-and-Seek is a framework that detects robot execution failures in VLA models using coarse trajectory-level supervision (no step-level labels) and contrastive learning, achieving SOTA results across multiple benchmarks and policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models for robotics are vulnerable to execution failures, and existing detection methods are either costly (action resampling/external models) or lose localized failure signals by uniformly propagating trajectory-level labels.

Method: Formulates failure detection as coarsely supervised learning, combining inter-trajectory and intra-trajectory contrastive objectives to localize failure-indicative actions from trajectory-level labels only.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art multi-task failure detection on LIBERO, VLABench, and real-world platforms across OpenVLA, π₀, and π₀.₅ policies, with good accuracy-timeliness trade-off under conformal prediction and generalization to seen/unseen tasks.

Conclusion: Hide-and-Seek effectively detects VLA execution failures without costly annotations, offering a practical and generalizable solution for robust embodied system deployment.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to follow natural language instructions and generalize across diverse tasks, but they remain vulnerable to execution failures that compromise reliability in real-world deployment. Detecting such failures during execution is therefore critical for the robust deployment of embodied systems. Existing failure detection methods either rely on expensive action resampling or external models, while alternatives propagate trajectory-level labels uniformly across every timestep, obscuring localized failure signals. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Hide-and-Seek}, a framework that formulates VLA failure detection as a coarsely supervised learning problem. By combining inter-trajectory and intra-trajectory contrastive objectives, Hide-and-Seek localizes failure-indicative actions and induces temporally structured failure signals from trajectory-level supervision alone, without any step-level annotation. We evaluate Hide-and-Seek on LIBERO, VLABench, and a real-world robotic platform across three representative VLA policies: OpenVLA, $π_0$, and $π_{0.5}$.Our method achieves state-of-the-art multi-task failure detection performance with a practical accuracy--timeliness trade-off under conformal prediction, and generalizes well to both seen and unseen tasks.

</details>


### [159] [High-Load-Density Electro-Permanent Magnetic Foot with Controllable Adhesion for Quadruped Wall-Climbing Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30849)
*An Li,Bo Tao,I-Ming Chen,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A quadruped robot uses a high-load-density electro-permanent magnetic foot with CHN-EPM units and force feedback, achieving over 1000N adhesion (200:1 load-to-weight ratio) and stable locomotion on various ferromagnetic surfaces including ceilings and walls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable climbing locomotion of quadruped robots on ferromagnetic surfaces by addressing adhesion controllability, air-gap sensitivity, and uncertain contact conditions.

Method: CHN-EPM (circular Halbach-net electro-permanent magnet) with 3D magnetic circuit, flux-concentration effect, two-stage pulse current control, and flexible pressure sensor force feedback.

Result: Maximum adhesion force >1000 N, load-to-weight ratio >200:1, successful ceiling/vertical-wall adhesion, and stable locomotion on painted, perforated, and curved ferromagnetic surfaces with Unitree GO2.

Conclusion: The CHN-EPM foot with force feedback enables robust, high-load adhesion switching and climbing locomotion on diverse ferromagnetic surfaces.

Abstract: To enable reliable climbing locomotion of quadruped robots on ferromagnetic surfaces, this paper presents a high-load-density electro-permanent magnetic foot with controllable adhesion, featuring force-feedback circular Halbach-net electro-permanent magnet (CHN-EPM) adhesion units and a magnetization control system. Due to its three-dimensional magnetic circuit structure and flux-concentration effect, the CHN-EPM enables a distributed parallel magnetic flux path with enhanced flux utilization, resulting in reduced sensitivity to air-gap variations and allowing effective adhesion to be maintained even under partial contact conditions. The proposed CHN-EPM generates a maximum adhesion force exceeding 1000 N with a load-to-weight ratio over 200:1. A magnetization driver and a two-stage pulse current control strategy are developed to regulate the excitation current amplitude and duration, enabling accurate and reliable magnetization. By incorporating a flexible pressure sensor for contact force feedback, the system can effectively monitor attachment and detachment states, ensuring robust adhesion switching under uncertain contact conditions. The proposed system is integrated into a commercial quadruped robot (Unitree GO2), demonstrating high-load adhesion on ceiling and vertical-wall surfaces and stable locomotion on painted, perforated, and curved ferromagnetic surfaces.

</details>


### [160] [Wall-OSS-0.5 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30877)
*Ryan Yu,Pushi Zhang,Starrick Liu,Brae Liu,Miracle Kang,Shalfun Li,Lights Shi,Ellie Ma,Ping Yang,Chris Pan,Jerry Chen,Dongxiu Liu,Rain Sun,Miles Guo,Byron Zhang,Hugo Zhou,Zach Xu,Vincent Chen,Harrison Huang,James Wang,Dance Kuzi,Andy Zhai,Hang Su,Roy Gan,Lucy Liang,Hao Wang,Qian Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Wall-OSS-0.5 is a 4B open-source VLA model that demonstrates VLA pretraining itself yields executable zero-shot robot behavior (not just better initialization), achieving non-trivial real-robot performance before fine-tuning and stronger results after fine-tuning across 20+ embodiments and 17 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To answer whether VLA pretraining itself produces executable robot behavior or merely provides better initialization for downstream fine-tuning.

Method: A 4B VLA (3B VLM backbone + action components) pretrained across 20+ embodiments on 1M+ robot trajectories with a gradient-bridged co-training recipe using three objectives: discrete action prediction (VLM gradients), multimodal prediction (grounded VL understanding), and continuous flow matching (deployment action interface).

Result: Zero-shot real-robot behavior achieving high task progress on 17 tasks including held-out deformable manipulation; after fine-tuning, 60.5% average task progress on 15 real-robot tasks, outperforming π_0.5 by 17.5%.

Conclusion: VLA pretraining is repositioned from an initialization strategy to a directly testable, already useful source of robot capability, with preserved vision-language competence and strengthened embodied grounding.

Abstract: Large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) pretraining is increasingly adopted as the foundation for robot policies, yet the evidence for pretrained VLAs is almost invariably reported after task-specific fine-tuning.This leaves a foundational question unanswered: does VLA pretraining itself yield executable robot behavior, or does it merely furnish a better initialization for downstream policy learning? We present Wall-OSS-0.5, an open-source 4B VLA built upon a 3B VLM backbone augmented with action-generation components, designed so that pretrained robotic capability is directly measurable on physical hardware.The model is pretrained across more than 20 embodiments, processing over one million robot trajectories per epoch alongside a grounded multimodal corpus. We adopt a gradient-bridged co-training recipe in which three objectives play distinct and complementary roles: discrete action prediction routes strong VLM-native gradients into the backbone, multimodal prediction preserves grounded vision-language understanding, and continuous flow matching serves as the deployment-time action interface. Before task-specific fine-tuning, the pretrained checkpoint achieves non-trivial zero-shot real-robot behavior, completing several tasks, including a held-out deformable manipulation task, at high task progress on a 17-task suite. After fine-tuning, the same checkpoint serves as a stronger adaptation prior, reaching 60.5% average task progress on 15 real-robot tasks and outperforming π_0.5 by 17.5%. Multimodal evaluations further confirm that action training does not erode grounded vision-language competence: the model preserves broad vision-language ability while strengthening embodied grounding. Together, these results reposition VLA pretraining from an initialization strategy to a directly testable, already useful source of robot capability.

</details>


### [161] [Trajectory Planning for Non-Communicating Mobile Robots using Inverse Optimal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30906)
*Nina Majer,Yannick Epple,Xin Ye,Stefan Schwab,Sören Hohmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a combined trajectory planning and prediction algorithm using inverse optimal control to estimate unknown goal states of non-communicating mobile robots, achieving 9.8% faster median goal-reaching times compared to constant-acceleration methods in 2-8 robot scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient interaction of non-communicating mobile robots in collision avoidance scenarios without explicit communication.

Method: Uses inverse optimal control to estimate unknown goal states from observed past trajectories, then each robot takes others' perspectives for self-prediction and solves a joint prediction problem using estimated goals.

Result: In simulations with 2-8 robots, the median duration until all vehicles reach their goals is 9.8% faster compared to constant acceleration based estimated goal states, and the solver never fails to find a solution.

Conclusion: The proposed approach is more efficient and more robust (never fails to solve) than the constant-acceleration baseline for multi-robot collision avoidance without communication.

Abstract: To enable an efficient interaction of non-communicating mobile robots in collision avoidance scenarios, we present a novel combined trajectory planning and prediction algorithm. Inverse optimal control is used to estimate unknown goal states of all robots based on observed past trajectories. Each robot also takes the perspective of other robots in considering self-prediction and solves a joint prediction problem using the estimated goal states. The resulting predictions are then considered for planning. Simulation results of scenarios with 2-8 robots show that the median of the durations until all vehicles reach their goals is 9.8 % faster compared to planning with constant acceleration based estimated goal states. Moreover, the proposed approach never leads to the solver being unable to find a solution to the planning or prediction problem.

</details>


### [162] [Enhancing Human-Likeness in Reinforcement Learning Agents via Hierarchical Macro Action Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30928)
*Usman Nizamani,M. Shaheer Luqman,Fawad Javed Fateh,Ali Shah Ali,Murad Popattia,M. Zeeshan Zia,Quoc-Huy Tran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces HiMAQ, a hierarchical macro action quantization framework that encodes human demonstrations into macro actions at two levels (subaction and action clusters) to make RL agents behave more human-like while maintaining strong performance, outperforming non-hierarchical baselines across multiple RL algorithms on D4RL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL agents are reward-driven and exhibit behaviors that differ from humans, limiting interpretability and reliability. The goal is to create human-like agents that align with human behaviors while maximizing rewards.

Method: HiMAQ (Hierarchical Macro Action Quantization) uses two successive levels of vector quantization: a lower level maps input actions to fine-grained subaction clusters, and a higher level aggregates subaction clusters into action clusters, encoding human demonstrations into macro actions.

Result: HiMAQ outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline (MAQ) on D4RL benchmarks, achieving better human-likeness scores with comparable or better success rates. Improvements generalize across IQL, SAC, and RLPD algorithms.

Conclusion: Hierarchical macro action quantization is an effective approach for making RL agents more human-like without sacrificing performance, with broad applicability across different RL methods.

Abstract: Human-like agents are a long-standing goal of artificial intelligence. Despite strong performance, most reinforcement learning (RL) agents remain reward-driven and often exhibit behaviors that differ from humans, limiting interpretability and reliability. In this work, we introduce a novel human-like RL framework that predicts action sequences closely aligned with human behaviors while maximizing rewards. Specifically, we encode human demonstrations into macro actions using a hierarchical macro action quantization approach (termed HiMAQ) consisting of two successive levels of vector quantization. The lower quantization level maps input actions to fine-grained subaction clusters, while the higher quantization level aggregates these subaction clusters into action clusters. Extensive evaluations on the D4RL benchmarks show that our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline (MAQ), achieving better human-likeness scores while maintaining comparable or better success rates than previous RL agents. The improvements generalize across integrations with various RL algorithms, namely IQL, SAC, and RLPD.

</details>


### [163] [RDGen: Demonstration Generation for High-Quality Robot Learning via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30957)
*Zijian Zhu,Menglin Zou,Zhuang Li,Yaojie Tu,Xinhai Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RDGen is a sim-to-real RL framework that generates high-quality robot demonstrations to train VLA models, outperforming human teleoperation in smoothness and downstream task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models lack high-quality robot trajectory data because human teleoperation is labor-intensive, costly, and difficult to scale.

Method: RDGen uses a VLM-based task parser, Grounding DINO object localizer, and sim-to-real RL policy as a trajectory generator. Successful rollouts are harvested as clean demonstrations for downstream VLA training, with simulation providing scalable additional data.

Result: On a pick-and-place task, the transferred RL policy achieves high task success rate. RDGen produces smoother trajectories and yields superior downstream VLA performance compared to human teleoperation.

Conclusion: RL-generated demonstrations can serve as more reliable and consistent supervisory signals for robot policy learning than human teleoperation data.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for general-purpose robot control. However, their performance remains fundamentally constrained by the availability of high-quality robot trajectory data. In current robot learning practice, such data are primarily collected through human teleoperation, which is labor-intensive, costly, and difficult to scale. In this paper, we propose RDGen, a sim-to-real reinforcement learning framework for generating high-quality robot demonstrations. Rather than employing reinforcement learning solely as the final control policy, RDGen leverages trained RL policies as a structured trajectory generator. The system consists of a VLM-based task parser that identifies task-relevant objects, a Grounding DINO-based object localizer, and an RL policy transferred from simulation to the real robot. Successful rollouts are then harvested as clean, high-quality demonstrations for downstream VLA training, while the simulation stage further provides a scalable source of additional trajectories at little marginal cost. Experiments on a pick-and-place task demonstrate that the transferred RL policy achieves a high task success rate. Compared with human teleoperation, RDGen produces significantly smoother trajectories and yields superior downstream VLA performance. These results indicate that RL-generated demonstrations can serve as more reliable and consistent supervisory signals for robot policy learning.

</details>


### [164] [A study on a Real-Time VR-Based Teleoperation Framework for Manipulator in Dynamic Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30989)
*InGyu Choi,GeonYeong Go,SunWoo Ahn,HyoJae Kang,Min-Sung Kang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a VR teleoperation framework that integrates GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization for real-time, collision-aware manipulation in environments with static and moving obstacles, validated on a 7-DoF manipulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VR teleoperation systems are primarily designed as data-collection tools for imitation learning and fail to address dynamic obstacles, collision risks, and operator mistakes required for real-world deployment in hazardous environments.

Method: The framework integrates GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization within a VR interface to generate feasible, collision-free joint commands at each control cycle under robot constraints.

Result: Experiments with a 7-DoF manipulator demonstrate stable online behavior and collision-aware motion generation across obstacle-free, static-obstacle, and moving-obstacle scenarios.

Conclusion: The approach generates motion consistent with operator commands while producing safe detours when obstacles interfere, enabling safe teleoperation in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Robot teleoperation enables safe, non-contact task execution in hazardous environments where direct human access is difficult, and its application has expanded with recent VR technologies. Many VR teleoperation studies, however, have primarily served as data-collection tools for robot imitation learning, so they often do not explicitly address dynamic obstacles, workspace changes, or collision risks during operation.
