For real deployment aimed at operator safety, teleoperation must react to dynamic situations with low latency and remain robust to mistakes made by inexperienced operators. This paper presents a VR teleoperation framework that supports real-time manipulation while handling collisions with both static and moving obstacles. The framework integrates GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization within a VR interface to generate feasible joint commands at each control cycle under robot constraints. Experiments with a 7-DoF manipulator demonstrate stable online behavior and collision-aware motion generation across three scenarios: obstacle-free, static-obstacle, and moving-obstacle environments. The results indicate that the proposed approach generates motion consistent with the operator's command while producing safe detours when obstacles interfere with the commanded path.

</details>


### [165] [Can Aerial VLA Models Cooperate? Evaluating Closed-Loop Air-Ground Coordination with CARLA-Air](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31066)
*Tianle Zeng,Yanci Wen,Xueang Yu,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces CARLA-Air, a unified simulation environment for air-ground robot cooperation, and tests current aerial VLA models on cooperative tasks, finding they struggle to transfer single-agent skills to stable multi-agent coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether aerial vision-language-action (VLA) models, which show promise in single-UAV tasks, can transfer to air-ground cooperation scenarios where UAVs and UGVs must act jointly in a shared physical world.

Method: The authors build CARLA-Air, a single-process simulation environment unifying CARLA and AirSim in Unreal Engine for physically consistent UAV-UGV interaction. They evaluate aerial VLA and planning baselines on two tasks: moving-platform landing and occlusion-recovery escort.

Result: Current aerial VLA models can track ground partners but fail to convert this into stable cooperative behavior. State prompting offers limited help; naive bidirectional interaction can amplify errors for most baselines.

Conclusion: Zero-shot cooperative air-ground VLA requires three missing components: explicit partner-state grounding, low-latency action coordination, and team-level objective alignment.

Abstract: Recent aerial vision-language-action (VLA) models show promising single-UAV capabilities, such as tracking moving objects and navigating to language-specified landmarks. However, it remains unclear whether these capabilities can transfer to air-ground cooperation, where a UAV and a UGV must act jointly in a shared, closed-loop physical world.
  We study this question with CARLA-Air, a single-process air-ground evaluation environment that unifies CARLA and AirSim inside one Unreal Engine runtime. By sharing the same world state, physics tick, and sensing pipeline, CARLA-Air enables physically consistent UAV--UGV interaction and precise measurement of simulation-timestamp alignment and effective coordination latency.
  Using CARLA-Air, we evaluate representative aerial VLA and planning baselines on two complementary diagnostic tasks: moving-platform landing and occlusion-recovery escort. The results show that current aerial VLA models can often track or follow a ground partner, but struggle to convert this single-agent competence into stable cooperative behavior. State prompting provides limited benefit, and naive bidirectional interaction fails to consistently improve performance and can amplify errors for most baselines. These findings suggest that, under the tested text-based cue interfaces, zero-shot cooperative air-ground VLA requires three components beyond the current paradigm: explicit partner-state grounding, low-latency action coordination, and team-level objective alignment. Our code is available at https://github.com/louiszengCN/CarlaAir.

</details>


### [166] [Seeing Fast and Slow: Bimodal 3D Scene Graphs for Open-set Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31067)
*Marcel Bartholomeus Prasetyo,Shrutika Vishal Thengane,A Manicka Praveen,Yi Loo,Malika Meghjani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BiMoSG is a bimodal 3D scene graph generation approach that switches between a fast (coarse) mode and a slow (fine, open-vocabulary) mode for efficient open-set task execution, outperforming existing methods in speed and enabling real-time deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-set task execution requires flexible scene representations that can switch between coarse and fine granularity based on context, as starting coarse and refining only when task-relevant regions are encountered is more efficient.

Method: BiMoSG uses a bimodal approach: a "fast" mode generates coarse 3D scene graphs by default, and a "slow" mode generates finer open-vocabulary 3D scene graphs for task-relevant objects when needed.

Result: BiMoSG is significantly faster than open-source state-of-the-art 3D scene graph generation methods, enabling real-time integration with task execution.

Conclusion: The bimodal switching strategy allows efficient scene graph generation that can be deployed in real-time for open-set task execution.

Abstract: Open-set task execution can significantly benefit from seamlessly switching between coarse and fine scene representations depending on the context and the evolving information as the robot explores the environment. For example, it is often sufficient to start with a coarse scene representation initially and only employ a finer, more granular scene representation when the robot encounters regions which are likely to contain the task relevant objects. Hence, in this work, we propose BiMoSG, a bimodal 3D scene graph generation approach for open-set tasks. BiMoSG employs a "fast" mode by default to efficiently generate a coarse 3D scene graph and can switch to a "slow" mode for generating a finer open vocabulary 3D scene graph of task relevant objects. We demonstrate that our proposed 3D scene graph generation approach is significantly faster than the open-source state-of-the-art approaches. This allows us to integrate the scene graph generation process with task execution for real-time deployment.

</details>


### [167] [Building Generalization Into Behavior Generation Via Adaptive Compositions of Regularities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31110)
*Aravind Battaje,Malte Bernhard,Vito Mengers,Oliver Brock*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper shows that generalization in robotics can emerge from adaptively composing predictable regularities (structured priors) into situation-appropriate behaviors, demonstrated via the AICON framework where a differentiable network uses sensory feedback and gradient descent to dynamically select which regularities to follow based on their informativeness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization in robotics requires prior knowledge about world structure, but this structure varies across situations. The authors aim to understand how robots can generalize by adaptively composing regularities.

Method: The authors analyze the AICON (Active InterCONnect) framework, which represents regularities as interacting processes in a differentiable network. Sensory feedback enables composition, and gradient descent generates behavior. They test on a simple simulated problem with all relevant regularities identifiable.

Result: The model generates context-appropriate behavior under novel conditions in all but one case where regularities are provably insufficient. Ablations show the network automatically modulates regularity influence based on informativeness.

Conclusion: Adaptive composition of regularities serves as a powerful inductive bias for building generalization into robotic behavior generation.

Abstract: Generalization in robotics requires prior knowledge about how the world is structured, yet this structure changes from one situation to the next. This paper investigates the proposition that generalization arises from adaptively composing regularities -- predictable relationships within the robot-environment system -- into situation-appropriate structures for behavior generation. We examine this proposition by analyzing the mechanism in AICON (Active InterCONnect), a framework representing regularities as interacting processes in a differentiable network, where sensory feedback realizes composition and gradient descent generates behavior. To isolate adaptive composition as the key mechanism, we study a simple simulated problem in which all relevant regularities can be identified. We expose the resulting model to a wide range of novel conditions not considered during design, and we find that it generates context-appropriate behavior in all but one case, where encoded regularities are provably insufficient. Ablations reveal that the network automatically modulates which regularities influence behavior based on their informativeness. These results suggest that adaptive composition of regularities constitutes a powerful inductive bias for building generalization into behavior generation.

</details>


### [168] [Don't Fool Me Twice: Adapting to Adversity in the Wild with Experience-Driven Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31119)
*Navin Sriram Ravie,Andrew Jong,Krrish Jain,John Liu,Omar Alama,Bijo Sebastian,Sebastian Scherer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes "Don't Fool Me Twice," a continual learning framework for mobile robots to learn online from disturbances by using VLMs, kernel regression, and semantic voxel modeling to predict, characterize, and recover from embodiment-dependent anomalies in unstructured environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dangers in unstructured environments are often embodiment-specific and hard to predefine. Existing VLM-based danger prediction fails to capture interaction-dependent and embodiment-specific adversities.

Method: A continual learning pipeline: (1) observe disturbances and describe effects on robot; (2) augment description with visual context and query VLM for possible causes; (3) characterize local disturbance via kernel regression for few-shot anomaly modeling; (4) estimate epistemic uncertainty via semantic voxel-centric modeling for downstream recovery.

Result: Validated through four hypotheses in both simulation and hardware experiments across different robot embodiments and adversity modes.

Conclusion: Learning online from disturbances and attributing them to semantic causes enables better prediction and planning, allowing robots to adapt to unforeseen embodiment-specific adversities.

Abstract: In robotics, dangers and adversity modes are often embodiment-specific and relative to each agent. A frontier of autonomous mobile robotics is to enable agents to operate effectively in the wild in unseen unstructured environments. A significant challenge in unseen unstructured environments is that it may not be possible to predict all the dangers to the specific robot. Although recent work has used large foundation vision-language models (VLMs) to preemptively predict an exhaustive list of common-sense dangers, it remains difficult to capture possible interaction and embodiment-dependent adversities. We propose a continual learning framework for a mobile embodied agent to learn online from disturbances and attribute anomalous behaviours to causes through semantics, enabling better prediction and planning of the world in the future. Our framework, "Don't Fool Me Twice", first observes disturbances and describes their effects on the robot; this description is augmented with visual context to query a VLM to predict possible causes; the local disturbance is characterized using kernel regression, which allows for efficient, few-shot modeling of transient anomalies. We leverage semantic voxel-centric modeling to estimate epistemic uncertainty, enabling richer downstream recovery by treating interaction-driven disturbances as learnable spatial behaviors. We present four hypotheses and validate them in simulation and on hardware across embodiments and adversity modes.

</details>


### [169] [TARIC: Memory-Augmented Traversability-Aware Outdoor VLN under Interrupted Semantic Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31121)
*Tianle Zeng,Hanjing Ye,Jianwei Peng,Jingwen Yu,Hanxuan Chen,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified outdoor VLN framework that maintains traversability-consistent executable guidance during semantic-cue interruptions by grounding semantic bearings into feasible headings via near-field traversability profiles and using a world-aligned 3D cue memory with uncertainty-aware readout, achieving significant gains in both simulation and real-world settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outdoor VLN agents frequently encounter semantic-cue interruptions (sparse/occluded/out-of-view cues), entering cue-free phases that cause backtracking, oscillatory headings, or aimless exploration. Memory-based methods fail under traversability-driven detours, as remembered cue directions become infeasible and prolonged cue-free phases degrade guidance. Traversability is identified as a stability condition for maintaining goal-directed guidance, not just local safety.

Method: The method extracts semantic bearings from visibility-gated goal/exploration cues, grounds them into executable headings using real-time near-field traversability profiles, and lifts intermittent 2D evidence into a world-aligned 3D cue memory with uncertainty-aware readout to ensure stable, reachable guidance during detours.

Result: Evaluated on quadrupedal and wheeled platforms over 600-1000m routes: improves simulation success rate by >10 percentage points over strongest baseline; achieves 40% real-world success rate vs 17.5% for strongest baseline, with substantially higher robustness during prolonged cue-free intervals.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively survives semantic-cue interruptions by maintaining traversability-consistent executable guidance, significantly outperforming baselines in both simulation and real-world outdoor VLN tasks, especially during prolonged cue-free phases.

Abstract: Outdoor vision-language navigation (VLN) in long-range, open-world environments is frequently disrupted by semantic-cue interruptions, where informative goal cues become sparse, occluded, or leave the field of view. Once such cues disappear, agents enter a cue-free phase and often degrade into backtracking, oscillatory headings, or aimless exploration. While memory-based methods attempt to bridge these gaps, they often fail under traversability-driven detours: the remembered cue direction may be infeasible, forcing detours that prolong cue-free phases and gradually render robot-centric cues stale and implicit histories blurred. This makes traversability a stability condition for maintaining goal-directed guidance, rather than merely a local safety concern.
  We propose a unified outdoor VLN framework that survives semantic-cue interruptions by maintaining traversability-consistent executable guidance throughout prolonged cue-free phases. Specifically, our method extracts semantic bearings from visibility-gated goal or exploration cues and grounds them into executable headings using a real-time near-field traversability profile, providing goal-consistent feasible guidance beyond reject-only safety filtering. To prevent guidance degradation during detours, we lift intermittent 2D evidence into a world-aligned 3D cue memory with an uncertainty-aware readout mechanism, ensuring guidance remains continuously reachable and stable as the robot moves.
  We evaluate the framework on quadrupedal and wheeled platforms over 600--1000 m routes. Our method improves simulation success rate by over 10 percentage points over the strongest baseline and achieves a real-world success rate of 40%, compared to 17.5% for the strongest baseline, with substantially higher robustness during prolonged cue-free intervals.

</details>


### [170] [Simulation of collision avoidance behavior in crowd movement by data-driven approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31210)
*Xuanwen Liang,Eric Wai Ming Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CPGAN (Collision-Penalized GAN) is a data-driven crowd simulation model that reduces collision rates by incorporating a lateral-acceleration-based collision loss function and Voronoi-based motion feature extraction, achieving collision rates comparable to controlled experiments in bidirectional flows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven crowd simulation models achieve good trajectory prediction under Euclidean metrics but suffer from excessively high collision rates, especially in bidirectional and multidirectional flows.

Method: Proposes a GAN-based model (CPGAN) with a novel lateral-acceleration-based collision loss function and Voronoi-based motion feature extraction, integrating pedestrian collision mechanisms directly into the loss function.

Result: The lateral-acceleration-based collision loss significantly reduces opposite-direction pedestrian collision rates to levels comparable with controlled experiments. CPGAN effectively reproduces lane formation and N-t curves in bidirectional flow scenarios.

Conclusion: Integrating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms (collision penalty) into loss functions is a promising direction for improving data-driven crowd simulation, reducing collisions while maintaining realistic flow patterns.

Abstract: Crowd movement simulation is essential for pedestrian safety management and facility layout optimization. Data-driven models enhance trajectory prediction accuracy under Euclidean metrics, yet they suffer from excessively high collision rates, especially in bidirectional and multidirectional flows. In this paper, we establish a novel data-driven crowd simulation model that incorporates the pedestrian collision mechanism into the loss function to reduce collisions. A new lateral-acceleration-based collision loss function and a Voronoi-based motion feature extraction approach are proposed. The model is based on a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) architecture and is termed CPGAN (Collision-Penalized GAN). We evaluate CPGAN in bidirectional flow scenarios, which involve frequent collision avoidance behaviors. Results show that the proposed lateral-acceleration-based collision loss significantly reduces opposite-direction pedestrian collision rates to levels comparable with controlled experiments. CPGAN effectively simulates bidirectional flow, reproducing lane formation and N-t curves. The research outcomes can provide inspiration for integrating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms into loss functions in data-driven crowd simulation.

</details>


### [171] [HARP-VLA: Human-Robot Aligned Representation Learning for Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31234)
*Xiang Zhu,Puzhen Yuan,Yichen Liu,Jianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HARP is a framework that aligns human and robot visual representations using paired and unpaired video data, enabling more effective VLA policy pretraining from human videos, achieving strong results on CALVIN ABC→D (4.481 avg length) and 7.1% real-world success rate improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models struggle with cross-embodiment discrepancies (different visuals and actions) between human and robot data. Latent action models reduce the action gap but still suffer from misaligned visual representations, causing domain-dependent latent actions that hinder effective co-training with human videos.

Method: HARP uses limited paired human-robot demonstrations as cross-embodiment bridges and abundant unpaired data as scalable dynamics supervision. It trains a robot-adapted visual encoder and latent action model with manipulation-centric auxiliary cues and a source-relative pair-discriminative alignment loss to adapt robot representations toward human semantics while preserving discrimination. The aligned encoder and latent action model provide unified vision-action representations for VLA policy learning.

Result: Achieves 4.481 average length on CALVIN ABC→D and 7.1% real-world success rate improvement over the strongest baseline.

Conclusion: Aligning human and robot visual representations significantly improves cross-embodiment VLA policy learning, with HARP demonstrating consistent gains in both simulation and real-world manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Learning generalizable vision-language-action (VLA) models from large-scale human videos is promising but challenging due to cross-embodiment discrepancies in both visual observations and executable actions. While latent action models reduce the action execution gap by learning action abstractions, they still rely on visual features. Thus, misaligned human and robot visual representations can lead to inconsistencies in policy inputs and induce domain-dependent latent actions, hindering effective co-training with human videos. To address this, we propose HARP, a human-robot aligned representation learning framework for more effective VLA pretraining from human videos. Specifically, HARP uses limited paired human-robot demonstrations as cross-embodiment bridges and abundant unpaired human and robot videos as a scalable dynamics supervision data source. It trains a robot-adapted visual encoder and a latent action model with manipulation-centric auxiliary cues and a source-relative pair-discriminative alignment loss, which adapts robot representations toward human semantics while preserving pair-level discrimination. The learned aligned vision encoder and latent action model provide a unified vision and action representation for VLA-style policy learning, where human and robot videos provide vision-language-to-latent-action supervision and a lightweight robot action head grounds latent actions into executable commands. Experiments on feature visualization, simulation, and realworld manipulation show improved human-robot alignment and downstream policy performance, achieving 4.481 average length on CALVIN ABC$\rightarrow$D and a 7.1\% realworld success rate gain over the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [172] [Before Parc Fermé: RL-Time Pruning for Efficient Embodied LLMs in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31256)
*Luca Benfenati,Ali Azimi,Matteo Risso,Fabio Carapellese,Daniele Jahier Pagliari,Alessio Burrello*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes BPF, a pruning strategy performed during reinforcement learning (RL) to compress embodied LLM controllers for robotic pipelines. BPF has two variants (BPF-RL and BPF-SFT/RL) and is evaluated on an autonomous-driving LLM controller (RobotxR1), showing better task-performance vs. memory/throughput trade-offs compared to post-training pruning, with up to 27% decode throughput improvement on edge hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied LLMs used as reasoning modules in robotic control suffer from high memory and latency, making real-time deployment difficult. Pruning can help, but the optimal timing of pruning (pre/post-training vs. during training) is unclear for controllers undergoing multiple training phases.

Method: BPF proposes two variants: (1) BPF-RL — iterative pruning during RL at predefined intervals, and (2) BPF-SFT/RL — pruning during SFT first, then further compression during RL with the same iterative strategy. The method is evaluated using LLM-Pruner on the RobotxR1 autonomous-driving pipeline.

Result: BPF achieves the best task-performance vs. memory/throughput trade-off among compared strategies. BPF-SFT/RL achieves 1.69× better size-performance trade-off vs. smaller dense models. Compact models improve decode throughput by up to 27% on Jetson AGX Orin.

Conclusion: Pruning during RL (BPF) is more effective than post-training pruning or using smaller dense models for embodied LLM controllers, as it leverages task-specific supervision and closed-loop feedback during compression.

Abstract: Embodied Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as reasoning modules in robotic control pipelines to improve human-robot interaction, but their memory and generation latency make real-time deployment difficult. Pruning can reduce these costs, but for controllers that undergo multiple pre- and post-training phases, the crucial question is not only how much to prune, but when pruning should occur.
  In this work, we propose Before Parc Fermé (BPF), a pruning strategy performed during RL that compresses embodied LLM controllers while they are still being optimized for closed-loop behavior. This allows pruning decisions to account for the task-specific supervision and closed-loop feedback that shape the final controller. We propose two variants: BPF-RL, which performs iterative pruning during RL by removing part of the model at predefined training intervals, and BPF-SFT/RL, which first prunes part of the model structure during SFT and then further compresses it during RL using the same iterative strategy as BPF-RL until the target pruning ratio is reached. We evaluate BPF on RobotxR1, an LLM-based autonomous-driving control pipeline, using an established LLM pruning framework (LLM-Pruner), and compare it against post-training pruning, post-training pruning with RL recovery, SFT-stage pruning, and smaller dense models from the same family.
  Our results show that BPF provides the best task-performance vs. memory and throughput trade-off among the considered pruning strategies. When compressing the larger RobotxR1 models, BPF-SFT/RL achieves a $1.69\times$ better size-end-to-end performance trade-off than directly selecting a smaller dense model from the same family, measured as removed parameters per lost percentage point of control adaptability. On the Jetson AGX Orin mounted on the target robotic platform, the compact models improve decode throughput by up to $27\%$.

</details>


### [173] [DeMaVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Foundation Model for Generalizable Deformable Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31286)
*Taiyi Su,Jian Zhu,Tianjian Wang,Youzhang He,Zitai Huang,Jianjun Zhang,Chong Ma,Hanyang Wang,Tianjiao Zhang,Munan Yin,Weihao Ding,Yi Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeMaVLA is a VLA foundation model for generalizable deformable-object folding, using a VLM backbone with flow-matching action generation, layer-pruned action expert, and human-in-the-loop DAgger training to achieve strong performance across diverse objects and household environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA systems train separate policies for different object categories, and naively mixing multi-task training suffers from task interference. Real-world deformable-object folding (e.g., clothing) requires a generalizable policy across varying categories, geometries, materials, and scenes.

Method: DeMaVLA uses a VLM backbone with a flow-matching action expert; the action expert prunes every other transformer layer while preserving layer-wise alignment with the VLM backbone for efficiency. It is pre-trained on ~5,000 hours of real-world dual-arm demonstrations, then post-trained on mixed folding data via a human-in-the-loop DAgger pipeline with corrective trajectories from real-robot failures.

Result: DeMaVLA achieves competitive performance on RoboTwin and strong real-world results on a household folding benchmark, demonstrating the value of scalable real-world data, efficient action generation, and corrective learning.

Conclusion: Scalable real-world data collection, efficient architecture design (layer pruning), and corrective learning via DAgger are effective strategies for building general-purpose VLA policies for deformable-object manipulation, moving beyond category-specific folding policies.

Abstract: Real-world household robots require Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation models that can acquire reusable manipulation skills across diverse objects, task conditions, and household environments. Deformable-object folding is a representative challenge, requiring robots to handle clothing items from random initial states across varying categories, geometries, materials, and scenes. However, existing VLA systems commonly train separate policies for different object categories, while naively mixed multi-task training often suffers from task interference and degraded performance. To move beyond category-specific folding policies, we introduce DeMaVLA, a VLA foundation model for generalizable Deformable Manipulation. DeMaVLA adopts a VLM backbone with an action expert and formulates continuous action generation using flow matching. To improve efficiency, the action expert is constructed by pruning every other transformer layer while preserving layer-wise alignment with the VLM backbone, reducing training and inference cost. DeMaVLA is first pre-trained on approximately 5,000 hours of selected real-world dual-arm demonstrations to acquire general manipulation priors. It is then post-trained on mixed folding data that aggregates self-collected demonstrations and corrective trajectories from real-robot failures across multiple folding tasks through a human-in-the-loop Data Aggregation~(DAgger) pipeline. Experiments show that DeMaVLA achieves competitive performance on RoboTwin and strong real-world results on our household folding benchmark. These results highlight the value of scalable real-world data, efficient action generation, and corrective learning for general-purpose VLA policies in deformable-object manipulation.

</details>


### [174] [AR Forcing: Towards Long-Horizon Robot Navigation World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31314)
*Yifei Yang,Zehua Fan,Huan Li,Aoqi Wang,Lida Huang,Haibao Yu,Haiyan Liu,Xuanyao Mao,Jason Bao,Liang Xu,Bingchuan Sun,Yan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AR Forcing introduces an autoregressive training strategy for diffusion-based robot navigation world models that matches training and inference distributions, improving long-horizon prediction consistency and trajectory accuracy across multiple navigation datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based robot navigation world models suffer from distribution shift between parallel supervised training and autoregressive inference, causing performance instability over long-horizon predictions.

Method: Integrates standard diffusion loss into autoregressive training loop: at each step, the model uses its own predictions to update context and optimize single-step noise prediction, exposing the model to inference state distribution during training without requiring additional discriminators or distribution-matching losses.

Result: On RECON, SCAND, HuRoN, and TartanDrive datasets, AR Forcing improved generated image consistency during long-horizon navigation and predicted trajectory accuracy, enhancing robustness in both known and unknown environments compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: AR Forcing effectively solves the training-inference distribution mismatch problem in diffusion world models for robot navigation, offering an easy-to-integrate solution that retains the original diffusion framework and sampler.

Abstract: The diffusion based robot navigation world models are typically trained using parallel supervision, while autoregressive inference is employed during path planning. This results in a distribution shift between training and inference, which destabilizes the performance over long-horizon prediction. We propose AR Forcing, an autoregressive training strategy, which integrates the standard diffusion loss into the autoregressive training loop. At each step, the model uses its own predictions to update the context and optimize the single step noise prediction objective, thereby explicitly exposing the model to the inference state distribution during training. Our method does not require additional discriminators or distribution-matching losses, retains the original diffusion framework and sampler, and is easy to integrate. Experiments on multi-domain navigation datasets (RECON, SCAND, HuRoN, TartanDrive) show that compared with strong baselines, AR Forcing improved the consistency of generated images during long-horizon navigation and the accuracy of predicted trajectories, enhancing robustness of the model in complex known and unknown environments. We will release the code soon.

</details>


### [175] [Surface Constraint Policy for Learning Surface-Constrained and Dynamically Feasible Robot Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31321)
*Shuai Ke,Jiexin Zhang,Huan Zhao,Zhiao Wei,Yikun Guo,Jie Pan,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Surface Constraint Policy (SCP) that combines diffusion-based imitation learning with explicit surface geometry modeling and dynamic movement primitives to generate robot actions satisfying free-form surface constraints from human demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based imitation learning methods lack explicit surface geometry constraint modeling and suffer from dynamic feasibility issues, leading to stochastic actions that fail to achieve reliable surface alignment and stable contact in tasks involving complex free-form surface constraints.

Method: (1) Encode surface geometry constraints using a 2D weighted Gaussian kernel function from demonstrations. (2) Use diffusion-based policy to infer task-level action intentions from multimodal sensory inputs. (3) Transform intentions into surface-constrained Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) via similarity-based action mapping.

Result: Validated on multiple surface manipulation tasks, achieving superior task success rates and contact stability under surface constraints compared to existing techniques.

Conclusion: The SCP effectively generates structured surface geometric intent and dynamically admissible actions, overcoming limitations of prior methods for free-form surface constrained manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion-based imitation learning methods have driven rapid progress in robot dexterous manipulation tasks. However, they have limitations when applied to tasks that involve complex free-form surface constraints because of their lack of explicit surface geometry constraint modeling and the dynamic feasibility issue, resulting in stochastic action generation that fails to achieve reliable surface alignment and maintain stable contact. To address these limitations, we propose a novel surface constraint policy (SCP) for generating robot actions that satisfy free-form surface constraints on the basis of human demonstrations and real-time visual observations. First, the surface geometry constraint is encoded using a two-dimensional weighted Gaussian kernel function that is derived from demonstrations. Building on the encoded surface geometry constraints, the diffusion-based policy is used to infer task-level action intentions from multimodal sensory inputs, including visual observations and robot state feedback. These intentions are further transformed into surface-constrained dynamic movement primitives (DMPs) through a similarity-based action mapping method, thereby enabling smooth and compliant motion execution. The SCP achieves generation of structured surface geometric intent and dynamically admissible actions. The proposed method is validated on multiple surface manipulation tasks and compared with existing techniques. The experimental results demonstrate superior task success rates and contact stability under surface constraints.

</details>


### [176] [Learning Terrain-Aware Whole-Body Control for Perceptive Legged Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31343)
*Sikai Guo,Yudong Zhong,Guoyang Zhao,Botao Dang,Zhihai Bi,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TA-WBC is a terrain-aware whole-body control framework for legged manipulators using RL-based unified policy, hybrid exteroception encoder, end-effector sampling based on foot contact plane, and dual-policy distillation to achieve robust loco-manipulation across complex terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing whole-body controllers for legged manipulators primarily rely on proprioception and lack exteroception for terrain topology perception, limiting adaptability to varying environments and complex terrains.

Method: 1) Hybrid exteroception encoder extracts terrain features for proactive posture and foothold adaptation. 2) End-effector sampling method based on foot contact plane to decouple manipulation from base fluctuations. 3) Dual-policy distillation module integrating whole-body motion with terrain adaptability without catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments show larger reachable space, less tracking error, and reduced unexpected stumbles compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: TA-WBC demonstrates robust terrain-aware whole-body control for legged manipulators, highlighting promising capabilities for loco-manipulation across complex terrains.

Abstract: Legged manipulators integrate exceptional terrain adaptability along with mobile manipulation capabilities, which make them highly promising for deployment in human-centric environments. By coordinating the control of both legs and arms, a whole-body controller can significantly expand the operational workspace of legged manipulators. However, many existing whole-body controllers primarily depend on proprioception and do not incorporate the critical exteroception required for effective terrain topology perception. This limitation can hinder their ability to adapt to varying environmental conditions and navigate complex terrains effectively. In this paper, we introduce TA-WBC, a terrain-aware whole-body control framework for legged manipulators, which features a novel RL-based unified policy tailored to whole-body loco-manipulation tasks in various terrains. Specifically, we employ a hybrid exteroception encoder to extract terrain features, providing an essential basis for the robot to proactively adapt posture and footholds. Furthermore, to facilitate stable cross-terrain loco-manipulation, we propose a novel end-effector sampling method based on the foot contact plane, decoupling manipulation target from base fluctuations. Moreover, a dual-policy distillation module is introduced to integrate expansive whole-body motion with terrain adaptability without catastrophic forgetting. The simulation and real-world experiments validate the robustness of our proposed controller, which leads to a larger reachable space, less tracking error, and reduced unexpected stumbles. This unified policy highlights the promising capabilities of legged manipulators in performing loco-manipulation tasks across complex terrains.

</details>


### [177] [Haptic Sorter: A Unified Planning Framework for Online Shape Estimation and Real-Time Pose Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31352)
*Zhuoyi Lu,Lin Yang,Sri Harsha Turlapati,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified framework integrating haptic perception, modeling, and manipulation planning for robotics, using Bayesian Optimization for shape inference, adaptive manipulation potential, and ODE-based real-time pose inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics manipulation often assumes known object shape and pose, but precise geometric info is unavailable in practice due to sensor uncertainties and occlusion.

Method: (i) Bayesian Optimization for haptic exploration with superellipse shape approximation; (ii) Adaptive manipulation potential encoding object geometry; (iii) Online ODE for real-time pose inference from model predictions and tactile feedback.

Result: Validated on a 2D robotic sorting task with varied geometries in both simulation and real-world multi-arm setup, showing robustness and generalizability.

Conclusion: The proposed model-based geometric framework effectively integrates haptic perception and manipulation planning without requiring prior knowledge of object shape and pose.

Abstract: Robotics manipulation usually assumes that the shape and pose of the object are known to the robot prior to motion planning. However, precise geometric information is not always available in practice, and pose inference suffers from sensor uncertainties and view occlusion. In this work, we propose a unified model-based geometric framework integrating robotic haptic perception, modeling, and manipulation planning. Our novelties involve: \textit{i)} Introducing Bayesian Optimization (BO) to guide the haptic exploration for object shape inference, where superellipses are used to approximate geometric boundary; \textit{ii)} Adaptive formulation of manipulation potential encoding object geometry for quasi-static robot-object interaction; \textit{iii)} Proposing an online Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) for real-time pose inference based on model prediction and tactile feedback. We deploy our system on a 2D robotic sorting task, and vary object geometries to validate the robustness and generalizability of our framework in both simulation and a real-world multi-arm setup.

</details>


### [178] [LiftNav: Path Planning via Semantic Lifting in TSDF-Guided Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31376)
*Hannah Schieber,Dominik Frischmann,Victor Schaack,Angela P. Schoellig,Daniel Roth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiftNav is a hybrid navigation framework combining TSDF-based geometric mapping with Gaussian Splatting for semantic understanding, using YOLO detection, 3D lifting, and B-spline optimization to achieve 100% feasible, shorter trajectories in unknown indoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches lack either semantic understanding (TSDF) or precise geometric safety (Gaussian Splatting). The authors aim to bridge this gap for autonomous navigation in unknown indoor spaces.

Method: LiftNav builds on GSFusion's TSDF+GS dual map with a real-time pipeline of YOLO-based object detection, TSDF-based 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization, plus a hinge-loss collision penalty.

Result: Evaluated on the Replica dataset in simulation, LiftNav achieves 100% feasibility rate and shorter trajectories compared to a state-of-the-art radiance field baseline.

Conclusion: The hybrid TSDF+GS approach with hinge-loss penalty enables both safe and semantically aware navigation without requiring dense 3D embeddings.

Abstract: Autonomous robots in unknown indoor environments require both reliable collision avoidance and object-level understanding. Classical representations such as TSDF support safe planning but lack semantics, while photorealistic methods like Gaussian Splatting (GS) provide rich appearance yet suffer from soft geometry, limiting precise obstacle avoidance. We present LiftNav, a hybrid navigation framework built on GSFusion's TSDF+GS dual map, augmented with a real-time pipeline of YOLO-based detection, TSDF-based 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization. This design enables flexible semantic navigation without dense 3D embeddings. We further introduce a hinge-loss-based collision penalty that improves trajectory smoothness and safety. We evaluate our approach in a simulation using the Replica dataset. Compared against a state-of-the-art radiance field baseline we show a 100% feasibility rate and shorter trajectories.

</details>


### [179] [Adaptive Artificial Time-Delay Control with Barrier Lyapunov Constraints for Euler-Lagrange Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31405)
*Saksham Gupta,Rishabh Dev Yadav,Sarthak Mishra,Amitabh Sharma,Sourish Ganguly,Wei Pan,Spandan Roy,Simone Baldi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel adaptive control framework combining time-delay estimation with barrier Lyapunov functions to simultaneously compensate for state-dependent uncertainties and enforce time-varying constraints in Euler-Lagrange systems, validated on a 5-DOF robotic manipulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing control designs fail to simultaneously address state-dependent uncertainty compensation and time-varying state constraints in Euler-Lagrange systems (e.g., robotics), leaving a gap in safe, adaptive control under dynamic uncertainties.

Method: Combines time-delay estimation (TDE) with a barrier Lyapunov function (BLF); formulates a state-dependent upper bound for TDE approximation error and uses an adaptive law for online parameter estimation; enforces time-varying position and velocity bounds via BLF.

Result: Validated on a 5-degree-of-freedom robotic manipulator; demonstrates strict adherence to safety-critical constraints under dynamic uncertainties, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive control framework is provably stable and effective for real-time constraint-aware control in Euler-Lagrange systems without prior model knowledge.

Abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of simultaneously compensating for state-dependent uncertainties and enforcing time-varying state constraints in Euler-Lagrange systems, a common requirement in robotics that remains underserved by existing control designs. A novel adaptive control framework is developed that combines an artificial time-delay-based uncertainty estimation strategy, also known as time-delay estimation, with a barrier Lyapunov function to enforce constraint-aware control design. Specifically, a state-dependent upper bound on the time-delay estimation approximation error is analytically formulated, and an adaptive law is constructed to estimate its parameters online, enabling real-time state-dependent uncertainty compensation without relying on prior model knowledge. To ensure constraint compliance, the barrier Lyapunov function-based controller enforces time-varying bounds on both position and velocity. The resulting architecture is provably stable via Lyapunov analysis. Experimental results on a five-degree-of-freedom robotic manipulator validate the framework's capability, compared with the state of the art, in maintaining strict adherence to safety-critical constraints under dynamic uncertainties.

</details>


### [180] [Shaft-integrated Force Sensing with Transformer-based Dynamics Compensation for Telesurgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31434)
*Shuyuan Yang,Grant Boone,Timo Markert,Sebastian Matich,Andreas Theissler,Martin Atzmueller,Zonghe Chua*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an accessible method for integrating a six-axis force sensor into the distal end of a cable-driven surgical instrument, using a transformer neural network to estimate end-effector forces while preserving original mechanical functionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Force sensing in RAMIS has potential for performance assessment, tactile localization, and surgical autonomy, but accessible approaches for integrating force sensing into existing tools are lacking.

Method: A six-axis commercial force sensor is integrated into the distal end of a cable-driven surgical instrument. A transformer neural network fuses sensor measurements with robot state information to estimate end-effector forces, compensating for internal cable forces from actuation.

Result: Normalized errors below 6%, better generalization to unseen conditions than proximal data-driven approaches. However, high cable forces caused sensor saturation and reduced axial force observability.

Conclusion: The balance of integrability and performance supports applications in haptic feedback, skill assessment, and force-informed autonomy in RAMIS.

Abstract: Robot-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery (RAMIS) enhances surgeon dexterity, with newer platforms leveraging haptic feedback to further improve performance. Such force information has broader potential to inform performance assessment, tactile localization, and surgical autonomy. This motivates the need for accessible approaches to integrating force sensing into RAMIS tools. This work presents a method for integrating a six-axis commercial force sensor into the distal end of a standard cable-driven surgical instrument, enabling end-effector force measurement while preserving the original mechanical functionality of the device. The proposed design emphasizes reproducibility and accessibility for research applications, requiring no specialized manufacturing tools. A transformer neural network integrates force sensor measurements with robot state information to aid estimation of applied forces at the end-effector, compensating for internal cable forces arising from actuation. Our proposed approach achieved normalized errors below 6%, and generalized to unseen conditions better than purely proximal data-driven sensing approaches. High internal cable forces caused sensor saturation and reduced axial force observability, which can degrade performance along the tool's major axis and under higher load conditions. Given current levels of performance, the balance of system integrability and performance enables applications and research into timely topics of haptic feedback, skill assessment, and force-informed autonomy in RAMIS. Videos and code are available at https://enhanced-telerobotics.github.io/shaft force sensing.

</details>


### [181] [Actuator-Aware Inverse Kinematics with Joint-Limit Admissibility for Torque-Controlled Redundant Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31436)
*Mohammad Dastranj,Mahdi Hejrati,Jouni Mattila*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an actuator-aware inverse kinematics method for torque-controlled redundant robots that accounts for downstream torque controller behavior, formulating the problem as a convex quadratic program with control barrier function constraints, joint-limit handling, and controller-compatibility objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard inverse kinematics outputs purely kinematic velocity commands without considering how the downstream torque-level controller actually realizes them, leading to suboptimal or infeasible motion execution even when tasks appear kinematically correct.

Method: Formulates a convex quadratic programming problem with: (1) control barrier function constraints for joint-limit admissibility, (2) penalized slack variables for task equations, (3) a controller-compatibility objective balancing command consistency and torque-capacity weighting.

Result: Experiments on a 7-DOF upper-limb exoskeleton show lower limit-pushing commands, bounded admissible required velocities, and improved realized task behavior compared to standard IK baselines and constrained QP baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed actuator-aware IK serves as an effective intermediate layer between trajectory planning and torque-level control without modifying the downstream controller.

Abstract: This paper proposes actuator-aware inverse kinematics for torque-controlled redundant robots under joint-limit constraints. In the considered architecture, the inverse-kinematic output is not merely a purely kinematic joint-velocity command; it is the required joint velocity supplied to a downstream torque-level controller. Therefore, a small commanded task residual may not necessarily improve realized motion. The proposed method formulates a convex quadratic programming problem whose decision variable is the joint-level required velocity. Control barrier function style bounds impose reference-level joint-limit admissibility, while the task equation is handled through a penalized slack variable. Redundancy is resolved using a controller-compatibility objective that accounts for previous-command consistency and actuator torque-capacity weighting. The method is independent of the particular torque-level controller and can serve as an intermediate IK layer between an endpoint trajectory and a redundant robot controller. Experiments on a virtual-decomposition-controlled seven-degree-of-freedom upper-limb exoskeleton compare the method with standard inverse-kinematic baselines and a constrained task-preserving quadratic programming baseline. The results indicate lower limit-pushing commands, bounded admissible required velocities, and improved realized task behavior in the tested trajectory, without modifying the downstream controller.

</details>


### [182] [On-Device Robotic Planning: Eliminating Inference Redundancy for Efficient Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31460)
*Joonhee Lee,Hyunseung Shin,Hyunmi Kim,Pei Zhang,Jeonggil Ko*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: REIS is a robotic decision-making framework that reduces reasoning latency by exploiting temporal redundancy in consecutive observations, using scene gating and KV-steered affordance routing to minimize unnecessary reasoning while maintaining task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reasoning-based robotic policies using LLMs/VLMs suffer from high inference latency that limits real-time deployment, creating a need for acceleration without sacrificing semantic planning capabilities.

Method: REIS combines three components: (1) lightweight scene gating to detect temporal redundancy, (2) KV-steered affordance routing to skip unnecessary reasoning, and (3) deliberative reasoning when needed, inspired by human cognition.

Result: REIS significantly suppresses reasoning overhead while maintaining competitive task performance on ALFRED and real-world robotic tasks.

Conclusion: Temporal redundancy in robotic reasoning can be exploited to reduce inference latency, making LLM/VLM-based robotic policies more practical for real-time deployment.

Abstract: Reasoning-based robotic policies using large language and vision-language models achieve strong semantic planning capabilities but mostly suffer from a high inference latency that limits practical real-time deployment. In this work, we observe that robotic reasoning workloads contain substantial temporal redundancy, where consecutive observations frequently produce identical actions and subgoals. Based on this insight, we present REIS, a human cognition inspired robotic decision-making framework that minimizes unnecessary reasoning while preserving semantic adaptability. REIS combines lightweight scene gating, KV-steered affordance routing, and deliberative reasoning to accelerate robotic control under embodied constraints. Experiments on ALFRED, and real-world robotic tasks demonstrate that REIS significantly suppresses reasoning overhead while maintaining competitive task performance.

</details>


### [183] [IDOL: Inverse-Dynamics-Guided Future Prediction for End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31476)
*Chenghao Zhang,Timin Li,Dongmei Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IDOL is an inverse-dynamics-guided future prediction framework for end-to-end autonomous driving that bridges BEV world model predictions and trajectory optimization, achieving SOTA on NAVSIM benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world-model-based methods predict future scene evolution but fail to tightly couple these predictions with executable motion generations—future reasoning remains descriptive rather than actionable for planning.

Method: IDOL predicts multiple future latent states via a BEV world model, then applies an inverse dynamics model to adjacent latent futures to decode transition-aware trajectory features and motion deltas. These signals optimize the planned trajectory, with a lightweight closed-loop refinement module for long-horizon consistency.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 benchmarks among comparable methods.

Conclusion: Introducing inverse dynamics into latent future reasoning tightens the coupling between world modeling and planning, turning passive scene anticipation into actionable planning guidance.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving has emerged as a compelling paradigm for learning planning directly from sensor observations, while recent world-model-based approaches further enrich this paradigm by enabling explicit reasoning about how the scene may evolve in the future. Yet future prediction alone does not guarantee better planning unless the predicted evolution can be converted into planning-relevant trajectory updates. Many current methods still forecast future scene states without explicitly decoding the motion implications hidden in state transitions. As a result, future reasoning often remains descriptively useful but only weakly coupled to executable motion generation. To address this limitation, we propose \mathbf{IDOL}, an inverse-dynamics-guided future prediction framework for world-model-based end-to-end planning in latent BEV space, where inverse dynamics serves as the key bridge between future prediction and trajectory optimization. IDOL first predicts multiple future latent scene states with a BEV world model, then applies an inverse dynamics model to adjacent latent futures to decode transition-aware trajectory features and recover planning-relevant motion deltas that explain how the latent world evolves over time. These inverse-dynamics-derived signals are used to optimize the planned trajectory, turning future forecasting from passive scene anticipation into actionable planning guidance. A lightweight closed-loop refinement module further improves long-horizon consistency by reusing the optimized trajectory for another round of future-aware reasoning. By introducing inverse dynamics into latent future reasoning, IDOL tightens the coupling between world modeling and planning. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 benchmarks show that IDOL achieves state-of-the-art performance among comparable methods.

</details>


### [184] [Batched Differentiable Rigid Body Dynamics in PyTorch for GPU-Accelerated Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31481)
*Yue Wang,Yanran Xu,Wenbo Wu,Chuanhang Qiu,Zhaoxing Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BARD is a GPU-optimized, batched PyTorch implementation of Featherstone's rigid-body dynamics that achieves up to 64x speedup over CPU-bound Pinocchio for robot dynamics computation, validated on real training pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CPU-bound libraries like Pinocchio create throughput bottlenecks in GPU-based RL training pipelines as robot control scales to large-scale reinforcement learning with in-loop dynamics computation.

Method: Three key optimizations: (1) tiered lazy-evaluation cache to avoid redundant tree traversals, (2) matmul-free joint transforms via pre-computed Rodrigues constants, and (3) level-parallel propagation reducing sequential ops to tree-depth batched steps.

Result: Up to 64x higher throughput for FK and 63x for Jacobians at batch size 4096 on H200; 8.5x faster than Pinocchio and 2.0x faster than ADAM in Isaac Lab AMP training for an 11-DOF spined quadruped with 4096 parallel environments.

Conclusion: BARD demonstrates that batched GPU dynamics computation can significantly accelerate modern robot learning pipelines while maintaining numerical accuracy and differentiability.

Abstract: As robot control shifts toward large-scale reinforcement learning with in-loop dynamics computation, the community's reliance on CPU-bound libraries such as Pinocchio creates a throughput bottleneck in GPU-based training pipelines. We present BARD (Batched Articulated Rigid-body Dynamics), a self-contained PyTorch implementation of Featherstone's rigid-body dynamics algorithms, optimized for batched GPU evaluation and automatic differentiation. Three design choices make this efficient: a tiered lazy-evaluation cache that avoids redundant tree traversals, matmul-free joint transforms via pre-computed Rodrigues constants, and level-parallel propagation that reduces sequential operations to tree-depth batched steps. On five robot models (7-23 DOFs), BARD matches Pinocchio numerically while reaching up to 64x higher throughput for Forward Kinematics and 63x for Jacobians at batch size 4096 on an NVIDIA H200. We validate differentiability through gradient-based system identification on a 7-DOF manipulator, recovering link masses to 1.24% mean error under 5% torque noise, and integrate BARD into an Isaac Lab AMP training pipeline for an 11-DOF spined quadruped with 4096 parallel environments, where it is 8.5x faster than Pinocchio and 2.0x faster than ADAM for in-loop dynamics. BARD is open-sourced at: https://github.com/YueWang996/bard-pytorch-dynamics.

</details>


### [185] [Learning Controlled Separation of Small Objects Between Two Fingers with a Tactile Skin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31486)
*Ulf Kasolowsky,Berthold Bäuml*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces and solves controlled separation of small objects (6mm pellets) using two fingers of a robotic hand, trained via reinforcement learning with tactile feedback, achieving sim-to-real transfer on the DLR-Hand II.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robotic hands to precisely control and separate small objects purely through tactile sensing (without vision), which is challenging due to the objects being smaller than finger width and requires fine manipulation skills.

Method: Reinforcement learning with sparse reward in simulation, using spatially-resolved tactile skin on fingertips. A 4x4 taxel sensor was compared against ideal high-resolution sensors and joint-only sensors. An estimator predicts ground truth contact positions.

Result: High-resolution tactile sensors achieve near-perfect separation; 4x4 taxel sensors improve performance by up to 20% over using only joint sensors. Successful sim-to-real transfer demonstrated on DLR-Hand II.

Conclusion: Spatially-resolved tactile feedback significantly improves controlled separation of small objects, and the learned policy transfers effectively from simulation to a real robotic hand.

Abstract: We introduce and solve the novel task of controlled separation of small objects with two fingers of a multi-purpose robotic hand: after grasping into a box of small objects, the task is to drop as many of them until a desired number remains between the fingers. The objects are small compared to the width of the fingers but also in absolute terms. In our case little pellets with a diameter of only 6mm are handled. We show that the task can be performed purely tactile (no vision) using a spatially-resolved tactile skin on a fingertip. The separation policy is trained in simulation via reinforcement learning using a straightforward sparse reward, which basically checks if the desired number of objects is reached. In simulation experiments, we provide an exhaustive analysis of the benefits of using spatially-resolved tactile feedback: while an ideal (high-resolution) tactile sensor allows solving the task almost perfectly, a sensor with lower spatial resolution (here 4x4 taxels) still leads to an improvement of up to 20% compared to using only the fingers' joint sensors. For this analysis, we further train an estimator alongside the policy that predicts the ground truth contact positions. Finally, we demonstrate the successful sim-to-real transfer for the DLR-Hand II equipped with a tactile skin.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [186] [QASM-Eval: A Dataset to Train and Evaluate LLMs on OpenQASM-3 Beyond Quantum Circuits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30358)
*Zhenxiao Fu,Lei Jiang,Fan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QASM-Eval is the first comprehensive dataset for training and evaluating LLMs on OpenQASM-3 programs, specifically targeting hardware-facing features like classical logic, timing scheduling, pulse control, and quantum error correction workflows. It includes 100 expert-verified test tasks and 4,000 training tasks. Evaluation shows current LLMs struggle with OpenQASM-3 coding, but fine-tuning on QASM-Eval yields significant improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OpenQASM-3 introduced hardware-facing capabilities (mid-circuit measurement, timing control, pulse-level access) needed for NISQ-era quantum computing, but there is no existing dataset designed to train and evaluate LLMs on these advanced hardware-oriented features of OpenQASM-3.

Method: QASM-Eval comprises an expert-verified test set (100 tasks) and a training set (4,000 tasks) systematically covering classical logic, timing scheduling, pulse control, and complex real-world workflows. An extended verifier checks syntax, quantum states, and program timeline for automatic validation.

Result: State-of-the-art LLMs struggle heavily on OpenQASM-3 coding tasks, but targeted fine-tuning on QASM-Eval yields significant performance gains.

Conclusion: QASM-Eval serves as a crucial benchmark and training foundation to accelerate the development of reliable LLM assistants for hardware-facing quantum programming in the NISQ era.

Abstract: Quantum computing remains in the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era, where the performance is highly constrained to noise. Addressing the limitation often requires hardware-facing capabilities beyond gate-sequence circuit specification, including mid-circuit measurement and classical feedback for quantum error correction (QEC), precise timing control for dynamical decoupling (DD), and pulse-level waveform access for calibration. OpenQASM-3 was introduced to expose exactly these capabilities, providing a hardware-level programming interface. However, despite the rapid progress of large language models in code generation, there is still no dataset specifically designed to train and evaluate LLMs on OpenQASM-3 programs that involve its advanced hardware-oriented features. To address this gap, we introduce QASM-Eval, the first comprehensive dataset designed to train and evaluate LLMs on OpenQASM-3. Rather than focusing on quantum algorithm design or reasoning, QASM-Eval explicitly targets the language's hardware-facing features. QASM-Eval comprises an expert-verified test set of 100 tasks and a training set of 4,000 tasks, systematically covering classical logic, timing scheduling, pulse control, and complex real-world workflows. To automatically validate generated programs, we check syntax, quantum states and program timeline using an extended verifier. Our evaluation reveals that while state-of-the-art LLMs struggle heavily in OpenQASM-3 coding tasks, targeted fine-tuning on QASM-Eval yields significant gains. QASM-Eval provides a crucial benchmark and training foundation to accelerate the development of reliable LLM assistants for hardware-facing quantum programming in NISQ era. Data and code: https://github.com/fuzhenxiao/QASM-Eval

</details>


### [187] [Gait2Hip-60: A Unified Deep Learning Benchmark for Predicting Hip Muscle Forces and Joint Moments from Multi-Cadence Gait Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30374)
*Jiaqi Zhang,Ji Hou,Qing Sun,Xianzhi Gao,Bo Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study developed a deep learning framework to predict hip muscle forces and joint moments from gait kinematics, comparing LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba models. Transformer achieved the best performance on healthy subjects and retained moderate accuracy in zero-shot testing on ONFH patients, demonstrating feasibility but highlighting the need for better generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Musculoskeletal simulation for estimating hip dynamics is informative but time-consuming and impractical for clinical settings, motivating the need for a faster, more accessible prediction method.

Method: Gait data from 60 healthy adults under three cadence conditions were collected. Ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles served as inputs; OpenSim-derived hip muscle forces and moments served as targets. Three deep learning models (LSTM, Transformer, Mamba) were trained and evaluated under a unified protocol and then zero-shot tested on 9 ONFH patients.

Result: Transformer achieved the best performance in healthy subjects (hip muscle force: RMSE=1.33 N/kg, R²=0.819; hip moment: RMSE=0.11 Nm/kg, R²=0.862). In zero-shot external validation on ONFH patients, Transformer maintained moderate performance (R²=0.537 for force, R²=0.569 for moment).

Conclusion: Estimating hip dynamics from gait kinematics is feasible. Transformer is a strong baseline model, but broader pathological validation and improved generalization are needed before clinical deployment.

Abstract: Estimating hip muscle forces and joint moments during gait typically relies on musculoskeletal simulation, which is informative but time-consuming and difficult to apply in clinical settings. This study developed a deep learning framework to predict these hip dynamics parameters directly from lower-limb gait kinematics and compared three representative sequence models under a unified protocol. Gait data were collected from 60 healthy adults under three metronome-guided cadence conditions. Ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles were used as inputs, and OpenSim-derived hip muscle forces and hip joint moments were used as reference outputs. Three deep learning models of LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba were trained and evaluated using the same subject-level split, preprocessing pipeline, and metrics. The best model was then directly tested on an external cohort of 9 patients with osteonecrosis of the femoral head (ONFH) without retraining. In the healthy-subject benchmark, Transformer achieved the best subject-level mean performance for both hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.33 N/kg, MAE = 0.57 N/kg, R2 = 0.819) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.11 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.07 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.862), with similar advantages across walking cadences. In zero-shot external validation, Transformer retained moderate predictive ability in ONFH for hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.51 N/kg, MAE = 0.70 N/kg, R2 = 0.537) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.17 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.12 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.569). These findings support the feasibility of estimating hip dynamics from gait kinematics, identify Transformer as a strong baseline, and highlight the need for broader pathological validation and improved generalization before clinical application.

</details>


### [188] [Unicorn: Scaling High-Dimensional Time Series Forecasting via Universal Correlation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30376)
*Haochen Yuan,Yichen Song,Yunbo Wang,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unicorn introduces a latent prototype codebook that decouples correlation modeling from channel identities, enabling scalable multi-dataset pretraining for high-dimensional time series by learning reusable, identity-agnostic interaction patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the trade-off between channel-independent models (scale well but ignore inter-channel dependencies) and channel-dependent models (expressive but dimension-bounded, struggling to generalize across heterogeneous datasets).

Method: A latent prototype codebook that projects heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, learning identity-agnostic and reusable interaction patterns that transfer across domains with different dimensionalities and semantics.

Result: Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, especially in few-shot transfer scenarios.

Conclusion: Unicorn offers a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models by enabling effective multi-dataset pretraining across heterogeneous high-dimensional time series data.

Abstract: Modern time series architectures face a fundamental trade-off: channel-independent models scale well with increasing data volume but ignore critical inter-channel dependencies, while channel-dependent models are expressive but remain ``dimension-bounded'', struggling to generalize across heterogeneous datasets.To bridge this gap, we introduce Unicorn (Universal Correlation Network), a framework for scalable, multi-dataset pretraining on high-dimensional time series. At the core of Unicorn is a latent prototype codebook that decouples correlation modeling from specific channel identities. By projecting heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, UniCorN learns identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns that transfer across domains with diverse dimensionalities and semantics. Extensive experiments show that Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, particularly in few-shot transfer scenarios, offering a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models.

</details>


### [189] [Destruction is a General Strategy to Learn Generation; Diffusion's Strength is to Take it Seriously; Exploration is the Future](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30553)
*Pierre-André Noël*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper discusses diffusion models as information-withholding techniques, arguing their destroy-then-generate approach offers flexibility advantages (especially in data-scarce settings), addresses challenges in porting reinforcement learning to diffusion contexts, and introduces a novel probabilistic graphical model to support the tutorial exposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The author aims to reframe diffusion models within a unified family of information-withholding techniques and argue for their flexibility and potential advantages in data-scarce settings, while also identifying exploration challenges when bridging reinforcement learning and diffusion models.

Method: The paper takes a conceptual/theoretical approach: (1) situating diffusion models within a broader family of information-withholding ML techniques, (2) analyzing the destroy-then-generate paradigm, (3) examining issues in porting RL techniques to diffusion, and (4) introducing a novel probabilistic graphical model for exposition.

Result: The paper does not present empirical results but offers conceptual insights: diffusion's destroy-based withholding is more flexible than hand-crafted approaches; porting RL to diffusion raises subtle exploration challenges; a novel probabilistic graphical model is introduced for tutorial purposes.

Conclusion: The paper concludes with open questions and suggested directions for future work, notably exploring diffusion-native approaches to exploration problems rather than directly porting RL techniques.

Abstract: I present diffusion models as part of a family of machine learning techniques that withhold information from a model's input and train it to guess the withheld information. I argue that diffusion's destroying approach to withholding is more flexible than typical hand-crafted information withholding techniques, providing a rich training playground that could be advantageous in some settings, notably data-scarce ones. I then address subtle issues that may arise when porting reinforcement learning techniques to the diffusion context, and wonder how such exploration problems could be addressed in more diffusion-native ways. I do not have definitive answers, but I do point my fingers in directions I deem interesting. A tutorial follows this thesis, expanding on the destroy-then-generate perspective. A novel kind of probabilistic graphical models is introduced to facilitate the tutorial's exposition.

</details>


### [190] [When LLMs Learn to Be Consistently Wrong: A Multi-Model Study of Linear Representations of Synthetic Deception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30381)
*Vahideh Zolfaghari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies deceptive alignment in transformer models by fine-tuning honest and deceptive variants of five models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) using LoRA. Linear probes detect synthetic dishonesty with near-perfect AUC (≥0.99) from early layers in four architectures, generalize across domains (TruthfulQA→MMLU), and reveal two representational regimes across models: collapse vs. preservation. The dishonesty direction consolidates in deeper layers, with optimal calibration achievable early.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study the representational basis of learned deception in LLMs as a controlled testbed for the broader challenge of deceptive alignment, using synthetic dishonesty induced via direct optimization on incorrect answers.

Method: Fine-tuned honest/deceptive variants of five transformer models using LoRA on the same question distribution, then trained linear and MLP probes on mean-pooled hidden states. Evaluated generalization on TruthfulQA→MMLU, and performed mechanistic analysis using Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, and calibration (ECE).

Result: Linear probes detect synthetic dishonesty with AUC ≥0.99 in layers 1-3 for four architectures (Pythia peaked at 0.705). Logistic regression matches or outperforms MLP probes. Probes generalize with near-zero loss across domains. Two regimes identified: representational collapse (Pythia/Llama/Qwen) vs. high-dimensional preservation (Gemma-2). Dishonesty direction consolidates in deeper layers with optimal calibration (ECE <0.01) in early layers.

Conclusion: Robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with implications for activation-based monitoring and AI safety.

Abstract: Deceptive alignment, in which models maintain accurate internal representations while deliberately producing false outputs, remains a central challenge in AI safety. While strategic deception is the primary long-term concern, synthetic dishonesty - induced via direct optimization on incorrect answers - provides a controlled testbed for studying the representational basis of learned deception. We introduce a multi-model paradigm in which honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) are fine-tuned using LoRA on the same question distribution. Linear probes trained on mean-pooled hidden states detect synthetic dishonesty with near-perfect AUC (greater than or equal to 0.99) as early as layers 1-3 in four architectures, while Pythia-1.4B reaches a peak of 0.705. Logistic regression probes consistently match or outperform MLP probes, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalize with near-zero loss (Delta AUC approx. 0) to held-out MMLU subjects. Late-layer representations show strong robustness to Gaussian noise, with Gemma-2 models exhibiting exceptional stability. Mechanistic analysis of Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, and calibration (ECE) reveals two regimes: representational collapse in Pythia/Llama/Qwen versus high-dimensional preservation in Gemma-2. Across all models, the dishonesty direction consolidates progressively in deeper layers, with optimal calibration (ECE less than 0.01 except Pythia) achievable in layers 1-4. These results demonstrate that robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with implications for activation-based monitoring.

</details>


### [191] [The Fast Mixing Mechanism for Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30600)
*Omri Lev,Moshe Shenfeld,Vishwak Srinivasan,Katrina Ligett,Ashia C. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a fast-transform-based DP sketching mechanism that improves runtime for DP linear regression while achieving state-of-the-art privacy guarantees matching Gaussian sketches up to a constant factor, yielding the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares.


<details>
