  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DP sketching methods (e.g., Gaussian sketching) achieve strong utility but do not improve runtime over classical DP approaches. The goal is to develop a DP sketching mechanism that combines the computational efficiency of structured fast transforms with strong privacy guarantees.

Method: The authors propose a new DP sketching mechanism based on fast transforms (e.g., Hadamard matrix), prove state-of-the-art privacy guarantees for it, and combine it with sketch-based DP linear regression methods to create a fast DP ordinary least squares algorithm.

Result: The mechanism matches the runtime of classical fast sketching methods. In favorable regimes, its privacy guarantees match those of the Gaussian sketch up to a constant factor. The combined algorithm yields the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares with strong utility.

Conclusion: Fast-transform-based DP sketching can bridge the gap between computational efficiency and privacy, enabling practical DP linear regression at scale without sacrificing utility.

Abstract: Randomized sketching is a central tool for compressing large-scale optimization problems while preserving accuracy. In particular, sketches that are based on structured matrices, such as the Hadamard matrix, can be applied efficiently and often yield solutions that approximate those of the original problem at much lower computational cost. In differential privacy (DP), Gaussian sketching has been used to solve DP linear regression, beginning with \citet{sheffet2017differentially, sheffet2019old} and later refined by \citet{lev2025gaussianmix, lev2026near}. However, although these methods achieve strong utility guarantees, they usually do not improve runtime over classical DP approaches. In this work, we introduce a new DP sketching mechanism based on fast transforms, which, in certain cases, matches the runtime of classical fast sketching methods. We prove state-of-the-art privacy guarantees for this mechanism and show that, in favorable regimes, they match those of the Gaussian sketch up to a constant factor. As an application, we combine this mechanism with recent sketch-based methods for DP linear regression to obtain a new algorithm with strong utility and improved runtime. We establish privacy and accuracy guarantees for this algorithm, yielding, to the best of our knowledge, the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares.

</details>


### [192] [LLMs Without Deep Neural Networks: New Architecture, Benefits and Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30385)
*Vincent Granville*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The author presents an alternative to deep neural networks for LLMs, independently discovered but similar to RBF networks, that finds the global optimum in closed form, eliminating the need for iterative training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To validate a new DNN alternative for LLMs that offers higher explainability and accuracy while eliminating the tedious training step.

Method: A model based on RBF network machinery that finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form in a single iteration, requiring no DNN training.

Result: Claims increased explainability, higher accuracy, and elimination of iterative training compared to standard DNNs.

Conclusion: The proposed model offers a more efficient alternative to standard DNNs for LLMs by finding closed-form global optima without training.

Abstract: The purpose of this article is to provide validation to my deep neural network alternative in the context of LLMs. Very recently, there has been a significant interest by Chinese researchers in a model called RBF network, as a substitute to standard DNNs, with increased explainability and higher accuracy. It turns out that my new model, discovered independently, is based on the exact same machinery. But with a major twist: it does not need DNN as it finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form, in one iteration, thus eliminating the tedious training step. Here I provide a high-level overview of my technology, with case study and comparison to similar methods.

</details>


### [193] [Functional MRI Time Series Generation via Wavelet-Based Image Transform and Spectral Flow Matching for Brain Disorder Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30387)
*Hwa Hui Tew,Junn Yong Loo,Fang Yu Leong,Julia K. Lau,Ding Fan,Hernando Ombao,Raphaël C. -W. Phan,Chee Pin Tan,Chee-Ming Ting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSFM proposes a dual-frequency flow matching framework using DWT and DCT transforms to generate realistic fMRI BOLD signals that preserve non-stationarity and spatiotemporal dynamics, improving downstream brain network classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: fMRI acquisition is resource-intensive, limiting high-fidelity data availability. Existing generative models struggle to replicate the non-stationarity, spatiotemporal dynamics, and physiological variations of raw BOLD signals.

Method: DSFM converts BOLD signals into wavelet decomposition maps (DWT) for multi-scale variations and projects into DCT space for localized energy compaction, then trains a spectral flow matching model to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representations, reconstructed via inverse transforms.

Result: The approach improves downstream fMRI-based brain network classification, demonstrating the efficacy of generated physiologically plausible BOLD signals.

Conclusion: DSFM provides a principled dual-transform approach that imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics, offering a promising solution for fMRI data generation.

Abstract: Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) provides non-invasive access to dynamic brain activity by measuring blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signals over time. However, the resource-intensive nature of fMRI acquisition limits the availability of high-fidelity samples required for data-driven brain analysis models. While modern generative models can synthesize fMRI data, they often remain challenging in replicating their inherent non-stationarity, intricate spatiotemporal dynamics, and physiological variations of raw BOLD signals. To address these challenges, we propose Dual-Spectral Flow Matching (DSFM), a novel fMRI generative framework that cascades dual frequency representation of BOLD signals with spectral flow matching. Specifically, our framework first converts BOLD signals into a wavelet decomposition map via a discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to capture globalized transient and multi-scale variations, and projects into the discrete cosine transform (DCT) space across brain regions and time to exploit localized energy compaction of low-frequency dominant BOLD coefficients. Subsequently, a spectral flow matching model is trained to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representation. The generated samples are reconstructed through inverse DCT and inverse DWT operations to recover physiologically plausible time-domain BOLD signals. This dual-transform approach imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics. Ultimately, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through improved downstream fMRI-based brain network classification. The code is available at https://github.com/htew0001/DSFM.git .

</details>


### [194] [A Novel Evaluation Metric for Unsupervised Learning in AIS-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection: MADQI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30388)
*Ismet Gocer,Zakirul Bhuiyan,Raza Hasan,Shakeel Ahmad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes MADQI, an unsupervised quality metric for evaluating anomaly detection in maritime AIS data, achieving 80.37% effectiveness by combining four metrics (ARC, PPS, SDS, ECE).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised learning algorithms (e.g., Isolation Forest) are widely used for maritime anomaly detection but lack systematic and meaningful evaluation measures due to the absence of labelled data.

Method: The framework uses Haversine distance calculations to analyze AIS data and introduces MADQI—a composite index integrating four metrics: Anomaly Rate Consistency (ARC), Physical Plausibility Score (PPS), Score Distribution Separation (SDS), and Extreme Case Evidence (ECE), combined via multi-chunk evaluation and adaptive scaling.

Result: The framework achieved a MADQI score of 80.37% on AIS datasets. ECE and ARC scored 0.907 and 1.000 respectively, showing strong performance in detecting extreme anomalies and maintaining consistency.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a reliable, meaningful, and label-free evaluation approach for unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data.

Abstract: This paper introduces a new systematic framework for detecting anomalies in maritime Automatic Identification System (AIS) datasets. These anomalies include abnormal vessel behaviours related to speed, position jumps, time gaps, and turn angles. Although unsupervised learning algorithms such as Isolation Forest are widely used for detecting anomalous vessel movements, they often lack systematic and meaningful evaluation measures. To address this limitation, we propose a novel quality metric called Maritime Anomaly Detection Quality Index (MADQI). The prosed MADQI is a composite index designed to evaluate the anomaly detection performance of machine learning models without requiring labelled data. The proposed framework uses Haversine distance calculations to analyse AIS datasets and identify anomalies based on their spatial and behavioural characteristics. The proposed MADQI evaluation framework integrates four interconnected metrics: Anomaly Rate Consistency (ARC), Physical Plausibility Score (PPS), Score Distribution Separation (SDS), and Extreme Case Evidence (ECE). These metrics are combined through automatic normalisation using multi-chunk evaluation and adaptive scaling techniques. Experimental results on the AIS dataset show that the proposed framework achieved a MADQI score of 80.37%, demonstrating its effectiveness for unsupervised anomaly detection. In particular, the algorithm performed strongly in identifying abnormal vessel behaviour. Among the individual MADQI components, ECE and ARC achieved scores of 0.907 and 1.000, respectively, indicating excellent capability in detecting extreme anomalies and maintaining anomaly rate consistency. Overall, these results are encouraging and demonstrate that the proposed framework provides a reliable and meaningful approach for evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data.

</details>


### [195] [NumLeak: Public Numeric Benchmarks as Latent Labels in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30393)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NumLeak reveals that frontier LLMs memorize public numeric benchmarks (e.g., Fama-French returns, unemployment, CPI) with high fidelity, and that closed-API probes underestimate this memorization. A white-box analysis confirms the effect, and a simple system prompt defense nearly eliminates a suffix attack without harming general performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations on public numeric benchmarks may be contaminated by pretraining memorization, making it unclear whether models exhibit out-of-sample skill or simply recall memorized data. The paper aims to quantify this memorization channel and assess its impact on benchmark validity.

Method: NumLeak combines black-box API probes on production models with white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. It measures recall fidelity on economic and climate statistics (e.g., Fama-French factors, unemployment, CPI, NOAA temperature) and compares performance on in-distribution vs. holdout data. Logprob ranking and dose-response analyses are used to detect memorization.

Result: Frontier LLMs recall Fama-French market excess return with r=0.97-0.99 and sibling factors within 0.15-25bps. Holdout data shows parse rate collapse to 21-57% but near-perfect r on answered months. Logprob ranking detects memorization missed by open-ended generation. A Sonnet regression collapses from r=0.74 to r=0.02 after residualizing out model recall. A one-line system-prompt defense blocks 99.8% of attacks with near-zero utility cost.

Conclusion: Public numeric benchmarks are heavily contaminated by memorization in frontier LLMs, and closed-API black-box probes underestimate this. A simple defense can effectively mitigate such memorization-based attacks without harming model utility.

Abstract: Public numeric benchmarks appear in pretraining, so an evaluation that conditions on a date may be measuring memorized recall rather than out-of-sample skill. We introduce NumLeak, a measurement framework that combines API-boundary probes on production models with a white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. Top-tier frontier LLMs recall the Fama-French market excess return at 3-seed pooled Pearson r=0.97-0.99 while staying within 0.15 within-25bps on the five sibling factors; comparable fidelity appears on U.S. unemployment, CPI inflation, and NOAA temperature. On a recent-release holdout, parse rate collapses to 21-57% but r stays at approximately 0.99 on months answered, the refuse-or-recall asymmetry a memorized channel predicts. The white-box experiment reproduces the dose-response, and logprob ranking detects memorization that open-ended generation misses, implying closed-API black-box probes understate the channel. A Sonnet "date to market-sentiment" regression that correlates with true Mkt-RF at r=0.74 collapses to r=0.02 once the model's own recall is residualized out. A one-line system-prompt defense blocks 99.8% of a non-adaptive single-turn suffix attack set at near-zero utility cost on conceptual and historical-narrative queries

</details>


### [196] [LongDS-Bench: On the Failure of Long-Horizon Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30434)
*Kewei Xu,Xiaoben Lu,Shuofei Qiao,Zihan Ding,Haoming Xu,Lei Liang,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongDS is a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis with 68 tasks from Kaggle, spanning 2,225 turns. Best models achieve only 48.45% accuracy, with performance dropping ~47 points from early to late turns, revealing that maintaining correct analytical state is the key bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate isolated or short interactive data analysis tasks, failing to test agents' ability to track evolving analytical context over long horizons in real-world iterative workflows.

Method: Constructed 68 tasks from real-world Kaggle notebooks spanning 2,225 turns across six domains, designed around state-evolution patterns (counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition) with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns.

Result: Best model reaches 48.45% accuracy; performance drops nearly 47 points from early to late turns; long-horizon errors account for 52%-69% of failures; additional agent steps do not necessarily improve performance.

Conclusion: The key bottleneck in long-horizon data analysis is maintaining correct analytical state rather than increasing interaction budget; LongDS is released to support research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis.

Abstract: Real-world data analysis is inherently iterative, yet existing benchmarks mostly evaluate isolated or short interactive tasks, leaving agents' ability to track evolving analytical context over long horizons untested. We introduce LongDS, a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis where agents must maintain, update, restore, and compose evolving analytical states. LongDS comprises 68 tasks constructed from real-world Kaggle notebooks, spanning 2,225 turns across six domains including Geoscience, Business, and Education. Tasks are designed around state-evolution patterns (e.g., counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition), with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models, we find that the best model reaches only 48.45% average accuracy, performance drops nearly 47 points from early to late turns, and long-horizon errors account for 52%--69% of failures. Further analysis shows that additional agent steps do not necessarily improve performance, suggesting that the key bottleneck is maintaining a correct analytical state rather than increasing interaction budget. We release LongDS to support research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/DataMind.

</details>


### [197] [Calibrated Preference Learning: The Case of Label Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30447)
*Santo M. A. R. Thies,Viktor Bengs,Timo Kaufmann,Sebastian J. Vollmer,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes calibration for probabilistic label ranking, proposes a hierarchy of calibration notions (full, sub-ranking, top-k), proves their relationships, and shows empirical evidence that popular models are poorly calibrated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration has been studied for classification and regression but not formally addressed for probabilistic label ranking, where the goal is to predict a distribution over orderings of a label set.

Method: The paper formalizes calibration for label ranking with a hierarchy covering full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k rankings, and proves theoretical relationships among them.

Result: Full-rank calibration implies sub-ranking and top-k calibration but not vice versa; sub-ranking and top-k calibration are incomparable. Popular label ranking models are often poorly calibrated, with substantial differences between metrics.

Conclusion: Calibration correlates strongly but not perfectly with benchmark accuracy in RLHF reward models, motivating future work on understanding and correcting miscalibration.

Abstract: Calibration, the alignment of predicted probabilities with true outcome frequencies, is essential for reliable decision-making. While extensively studied for classification and regression, calibration has not been formally addressed for probabilistic label ranking, where the goal is to predict a distribution over orderings of a label set. Naively treating rankings as classes ignores their structure and fails to capture important modalities such as pairwise and top-k predictions. We formalize calibration for label ranking and develop a hierarchy of notions covering full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k rankings. We prove that full-rank calibration implies the others but not conversely, and sub-ranking and top-k calibration are incomparable. Empirically, we find popular label ranking models are often poorly calibrated, with substantial differences between sub-ranking and top-k metrics. Applying our framework to RLHF reward models, we find that calibration correlates strongly but not perfectly with benchmark accuracy, suggesting it captures a meaningful quality dimension beyond top-1 accuracy. These findings motivate future work on understanding the downstream effects of miscalibration and developing methods to correct it.

</details>


### [198] [Bounded Behavioral Indistinguishability for Black-Box LLM Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30448)
*Munawar Hasan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces bounded behavioral indistinguishability as a more rigorous evaluation framework for black-box LLM distillation, showing that while LoRA distillation improves semantic similarity, it does not achieve true behavioral indistinguishability—adversarial evaluation reveals remaining artifacts in style, robustness, and domain-specific areas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard output-matching evaluation (semantic similarity) is insufficient to determine whether a student LLM is behaviorally indistinguishable from its teacher. The authors argue that distillation evaluation needs to go beyond task-consistency to capture subtle behavioral differences.

Method: Formalizes (ε,q,t,𝔸)-behavioral indistinguishability over prompt distributions, uses a 5,000-prompt behavioral probe suite on Qwen and Llama families, compares base students vs LoRA-distilled students via adversarial discriminators, pairwise category analysis, and teacher-identification experiments.

Result: LoRA raises semantic similarity but adversarial evaluation reveals nonzero distinguishing advantage; Qwen advantage drops from 0.158 to 0.081 after distillation; artifacts concentrate in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts; disagreement-guided acquisition does not consistently outperform stratified random sampling.

Conclusion: Semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient for black-box LLM distillation evaluation; bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation is required.

Abstract: Black-box LLM distillation is usually evaluated as an output-matching problem: a student is considered successful when its responses are semantically similar to, or task-consistent with, those of a teacher. However, output similarity does not imply that the student is behaviorally indistinguishable from the model it imitates. We introduce bounded behavioral indistinguishability, formalized as $(ε,q,t,\mathbb{A})$-behavioral indistinguishability over an explicit prompt distribution, where $ε$ bounds distinguishing advantage, $q$ bounds oracle queries, $t$ bounds computation, and $\mathbb{A}$ denotes the adversary class.
  We instantiate this notion on Qwen and Llama teacher-student pairs using a controlled $5,000$-prompt behavioral probe suite. For each family, we compare the teacher with both the base student and the LoRA-distilled student, measuring whether distillation reduces distinguishability rather than merely improving similarity. LoRA raises semantic similarity from $0.788$ to $0.862$ for Qwen and from $0.814$ to $0.874$ for Llama. Yet adversarial evaluation reveals remaining behavioral differences: learned discriminators retain nonzero advantage, and pairwise category analysis shows artifacts concentrated in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts. A pairwise teacher-identification adversary confirms this trend. With a different-family Llama judge and A/B-swap consistency filtering, Qwen distinguishing advantage drops from $0.158$ for the base student to $0.081$ after LoRA distillation. Query-budget experiments show that disagreement-guided acquisition does not consistently outperform stratified random sampling, indicating that coverage and diversity remain strong baselines. Our results show that semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient: black-box LLM distillation requires bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation.

</details>


### [199] [VeriGate: Verifier-Gated Step-Level Supervision for GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30451)
*Aakriti Agrawal,Minghui Liu,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VeriGate extends GRPO by using process reward supervision only when verifier rewards are degenerate, with future-cumulated token-level advantages, improving reasoning accuracy by ~12-20% while reducing zero-gradient failures and reward hacking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO suffers from sparse supervision: when all sampled trajectories get the same verifier reward, group-relative advantage collapses to zero (learning stalls), and outcome-only rewards provide no step-level credit assignment.

Method: VeriGate: (1) uses verifier rewards when they discriminate trajectories, process supervision only when degenerate; (2) converts PRM step scores into future-cumulated rewards for continuation-aware credit; (3) transforms these into group-normalized token-level advantages.

Result: Training on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models, evaluated on six reasoning benchmarks, VeriGate improves average accuracy by ~20% (1.5B) and ~12% (7B), reduces zero-gradient failures and reward hacking.

Conclusion: VeriGate effectively addresses GRPO's sparse supervision and limited credit assignment by selectively gating process supervision, achieving substantially better reasoning performance while remaining less susceptible to reward hacking.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is an effective recipe for training reasoning models with verifier-based outcome rewards, but its supervision is sparse: when all sampled trajectories for a prompt receive the same verifier reward, the group-relative advantage collapses to zero and learning stalls. Outcome-only rewards also provide no step-level credit assignment, limiting exploration and making it harder to learn robust reasoning. We present VeriGate (Verifier-Gated Step-Level GRPO), a verifier-gated extension of GRPO that addresses these limitations with three design choices. First, VeriGate keeps the verifier in charge whenever verifier rewards induce a meaningful preference among sampled trajectories, and uses process supervision only when verifier rewards are degenerate. Second, instead of collapsing Process Reward Model (PRM) step scores into a single trajectory reward, VeriGate converts them into future-cumulated rewards to assign continuation-aware credit. Third, VeriGate transforms these rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages, restoring informative gradients and fine-grained credit assignment while remaining less susceptible to reward hacking than methods that optimize aggregated PRM scores. Empirically, training on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models and evaluating on six reasoning benchmarks, VeriGate improves average accuracy by about 20% and 12% for 1.5B and 7B models respectively, substantially reduces zero-gradient failures, decreases reward-hacking behavior, and improves reasoning quality relative to outcome-only GRPO and PRM-as-outcome baselines.

</details>


### [200] [A Unified Framework for Gradient Aggregation in Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30452)
*Zeou Hu,Kelvin Ho,Yaoliang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a unifying framework for gradient aggregation in multi-objective optimization, establishing convergence rates to Pareto stationarity through a sufficient alignment condition, and introduces capped MGDA with robustness demonstrated in adversarial federated learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-objective optimization methods are proposed with different motivations, analyzed case by case, and lack a unified theoretical understanding of how gradient aggregation works.

Method: The authors develop a unifying framework centered on a sufficient alignment condition, proving that non-conflicting directions within the convex hull of gradients ensure convergence. They also present a primal optimization perspective that encompasses existing algorithms and enables new variants.

Result: Establish optimal convergence rates to Pareto stationarity; show feasibility via projection onto dual cone; introduce capped MGDA (from CVaR-based formulation) with demonstrated robustness in adversarial federated learning.

Conclusion: The proposed framework unifies existing MOO gradient aggregation methods, clarifies theoretical relationships, provides convergence guarantees, and enables design of new robust algorithms like capped MGDA.

Abstract: Many machine learning problems involve multiple inherent trade-offs that are best addressed by gradient-based multi-objective optimization (MOO) algorithms. Existing methods are often proposed with various motivations, analyzed case by case, and differ algorithmically in how the component gradients are aggregated at each step. In this work, we develop a unifying framework for gradient aggregation in MOO, establishing (optimal) rates of convergence to Pareto stationarity, the standard measure of performance in MOO. Central to our analysis is a sufficient alignment condition, from which we derive a theorem showing that non-conflicting directions, when chosen within the convex hull of gradients, form a fundamental sufficient condition for convergence. We further show that feasibility can be ensured through projection onto the dual cone, broadening the scope of methods that admit convergence guarantees. In parallel, we present a primal optimization perspective of gradient aggregation that encompasses established algorithms, clarifies their theoretical relationships, and enables the design of new variants. As an illustration, we introduce capped MGDA, derived from a CVaR-based formulation, and demonstrate its robustness in adversarial federated learning. Finally, we validate our theory through experiments on synthetic problems and practical benchmarks.

</details>


### [201] [DisjunctiveNet: Neural Symbolic Learning via Differentiable Convexified Optimization Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30456)
*Shraman Pal,Can Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified end-to-end neural network framework that enforces hard, input-dependent mixed integer linear constraints using hierarchical convex relaxations and differentiable optimization layers, achieving perfect rule satisfaction on sparse real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many scientific tasks suffer from sparse data, limiting purely data-driven methods, while rich domain knowledge (logical/linear rules) exists but is hard to enforce exactly within neural networks—existing methods use soft penalties, specialized architectures, or non-differentiable post-processing.

Method: Represents rules as disjunctive constraints, applies hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations, embeds tractable linear constraints as differentiable optimization layers within neural networks for end-to-end learning with hard rule satisfaction.

Result: Demonstrated on real-world datasets, achieving perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges neuro-symbolic reasoning with end-to-end learning, enabling hard enforcement of mixed-integer logical/linear constraints without sacrificing differentiability or accuracy.

Abstract: Many learning tasks in science and engineering are characterized by sparse datasets, which limits the effectiveness of purely data-driven approaches. At the same time, these problems are often accompanied by rich domain knowledge derived from physical laws, operational requirements, and expert heuristics. Such knowledge is frequently expressed as rules involving logical propositions and linear inequalities. Existing neuro-symbolic methods typically enforce these rules approximately through soft penalties, assume input-independent rules when designing specialized architectures, or rely on non-differentiable post-processing at inference time to achieve hard constraint satisfaction. While recent advances in differentiable optimization layers enable end-to-end feasibility enforcement within neural networks, extending these approaches to logical or mixed-integer rules remains challenging due to inherent nonconvexity. In this work, we propose a unified end-to-end framework for enforcing hard, input-dependent mixed integer linear constraints within neural networks. Our approach represents rules as disjunctive constraints and applies hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations. These relaxations yield tractable linear constraints that can be embedded as differentiable optimization layers while enabling exact rule satisfaction. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on real-world datasets, achieving perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [202] [Scalable Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning via State Augmentation and Consensus for Separable Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30461)
*Santiago Amaya-Corredor,Miguel Calvo-Fullana,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a distributed MARL approach combining state-augmented policy learning with consensus on Lagrange multipliers to solve constrained problems with separable dynamics and global resource constraints. Each agent learns an augmented policy conditioned on local state and a dual variable, and agents reach agreement on this dual variable through local communication. The method scales linearly with agent count, handles thousands of agents, and proves bounded consensus error and constraint violation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Independent learning fails to produce feasible solutions in constrained MARL because agents cannot determine appropriate individual contributions toward collective constraint satisfaction. Existing CTDE approaches have complexity that grows at least quadratically with agent count, limiting scalability.

Method: Each agent learns a policy conditioned on local state and a dual variable. During execution, agents perform lightweight neighbor-to-neighbor consensus over Lagrange multipliers to agree on constraint feedback. The method combines state-augmented policy learning with distributed consensus, proving bounded consensus error and constraint violation under mild connectivity assumptions.

Result: Scales linearly with agent count, handling thousands of agents while CTDE baselines are limited to dozens. Consensus coordination is essential for feasibility — without it, agents satisfy grid capacity constraints only by indefinitely postponing demand. With consensus, agents converge to a shared dual variable and satisfy both grid constraints and demand fulfillment.

Conclusion: The proposed consensus-based approach enables scalable constrained MARL by combining local communication with augmented policy learning, providing theoretical guarantees on constraint satisfaction and practical scalability to thousands of agents.

Abstract: We present a distributed approach for constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) that combines state-augmented policy learning with distributed consensus over dual variables. Our method targets systems where agents have separable dynamics but must coordinate to satisfy global resource constraints, a setting in which, as we demonstrate empirically, independent learning fails to produce feasible solutions because agents cannot determine appropriate individual contributions toward collective constraint satisfaction. The key technical contribution is showing that lightweight neighbor-to-neighbor consensus over Lagrange multipliers suffices for globally coordinated constraint enforcement while preserving the scalability of independent training. Each agent learns a single augmented policy offline, conditioned on both its local state and a dual variable encoding constraint feedback. During execution, agents reach agreement on this dual variable through local communication alone. We prove that under mild connectivity assumptions, the consensus error among agents' multipliers is bounded, and show that this translates to a bounded constraint violation that decreases with graph connectivity and the number of consensus rounds. Unlike centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) approaches, whose complexity grows at least quadratically with agent count, our method scales linearly in both training and execution. Experiments on smart grid demand response demonstrate that consensus coordination is \emph{essential for feasibility}: without it, agents satisfy grid capacity constraints only by indefinitely postponing demand, a degenerate non-solution. With consensus, agents converge to a shared dual variable and satisfy both grid constraints and demand fulfillment, scaling to thousands of agents while CTDE baselines are limited to dozens.

</details>


### [203] [idSCD: Identifying Training Datasets through Semantic Correlation Descriptors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30462)
*Andrada Gobeaja,Ionut Hodoroaga,Elena Burceanu,Marius Leordeanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a white-box semantic fingerprinting approach to detect whether a dataset was used in training a model by analyzing the spurious semantic correlations it induces. The proposed method uses Semantic Correlation Descriptors (SCDs) to capture dataset-specific traces, and achieves superior membership inference performance compared to existing black-box and white-box baselines across NLI, emotion classification, and medical text classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond existing membership inference methods that rely on behavioral or distributional evidence (e.g., confidence scores, losses, margins), and to exploit the insight that datasets leave unique traces in a model's learned semantic correlation structure through spurious correlations.

Method: The authors introduce Semantic Correlation Descriptors (SCDs) to capture the semantic correlation structure learned by a model. They propose an SCD-based membership score that tests whether a target dataset is part of a model's training mixture by comparing the model's SCD with the target dataset's standalone SCD, without requiring leave-one-dataset-out models.

Result: In a controlled leave-one-dataset-out diagnostic, SCDs perfectly separate matching from non-matching dataset pairs. The SCD-based classifier achieves the highest performance and lowest std across three settings (NLI, emotion classification, medical text classification), outperforming RMIA, Attack-P, LiRA, and SIF baselines, with the largest relative gain exceeding 60% in ROC-AUC.

Conclusion: Dataset membership can be reliably traced through internal semantic correlations, offering a novel and effective approach to dataset-level membership inference, particularly when dataset groups expose distinct semantic particularities.

Abstract: Can a dataset be recognized from the spurious correlations it induces during training? We argue that datasets leave dataset-specific traces in a model's learned semantic correlation structure: incidental regularities that are predictive within a dataset, but not causal for the underlying task, can be internalized during training. We use this insight to study dataset-level membership inference, moving beyond existing methods that rely on behavioral or distributional evidence such as confidence scores, losses, margins, generated samples, or query responses. We introduce a white-box semantic fingerprinting approach based on semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs), which capture the semantic correlation structure learned by a model and make it comparable across dataset mixtures. In a controlled leave-one-dataset-out diagnostic, SCDs recover dataset-specific changes and perfectly separate matching from non-matching dataset pairs. We then propose a practical SCD-based membership score that tests whether a target dataset is part of a model's training mixture using only the model's SCD and the target dataset's standalone SCD, without requiring leave-one-dataset-out models. Across three diverse experimental settings, with dataset groups for natural language inference, emotion classification, and medical text classification, we test both the advantages and limitations of SCD-based membership inference with different degrees of semantic separation and keyword support between dataset splits. On average, the classifier based on this score achieves the highest performance and the lowest std, outperforming black-box baselines RMIA, Attack-P, and LiRA, as well as the white-box SIF baseline. These results show that dataset membership can be traced through internal semantic correlations, with the largest relative gain exceeding 60% in ROC-AUC when dataset groups expose distinct semantic particularities.

</details>


### [204] [Can Subgraph Explanations Be Weaponized to Steal Graph Neural Networks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30470)
*Ojas Nimase,Jiate Li,Yue Zhao,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new model extraction attack called XSTEAL that exploits explainability interfaces in GMLaaS platforms to steal graph classification models under strict black-box constraints, using explanation-guided Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To demonstrate that explainability interfaces in GMLaaS platforms, while designed for regulatory transparency, create exploitable security vulnerabilities for model extraction attacks in graph classification tasks.

Method: (1) Uses model explanation outputs to guide Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation toward decision boundaries with Hoeffding concentration guarantees; (2) Exploits explanation subgraphs to efficiently narrow boundary search space, all under strict black-box conditions (only discrete class labels and binary explanation masks).

Result: The proposed method outperforms comparable baselines on benchmark graph datasets across multiple domains, confirming the exploitability of explainability interfaces.

Conclusion: Explainability interfaces create exploitable attack surfaces in GMLaaS, informing both defensive mechanisms and policy frameworks for explainable AI mandates.

Abstract: Graph Machine Learning as a Service (GMLaaS) platforms increasingly implement explainability interfaces to meet regulatory transparency requirements. However, this transparency creates exploitable vulnerabilities for model extraction attacks. We present the first model extraction attack specifically designed for graph classification under strict black-box constraints where the attacker observes only discrete class labels and binary explanation masks (no probability scores, gradients, or confidence values). Our method (1) uses model explanation outputs to guide Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation toward decision boundaries, with Hoeffding concentration guarantees on estimation accuracy and (2) exploits explanation subgraphs to efficiently narrow the boundary search space. Extensive experiments on benchmark graph datasets across multiple domains demonstrate our method's superiority over comparable baselines. These findings demonstrate that such explainability interfaces create exploitable attack surfaces, informing both defensive mechanisms and policy frameworks for explainable AI mandates. The implementation code is provided in https://github.com/LabRAI/XSTEAL/.

</details>


### [205] [Universal Multiclass Transductive Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30479)
*Steve Hanneke,Hongao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper characterizes universal transductive online classification with possibly unbounded label spaces, showing only two optimal rates for learnable classes (bounded or logarithmic) and introducing LCLL trees to characterize learnability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize the learnability of universal transductive online classification with possibly unbounded label spaces, where instances are known in advance but labels are revealed sequentially.

Method: Introduces a new combinatorial structure called Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree, along with the indifference property, to characterize learnability.

Result: Shows only two possible optimal rates for learnable classes: bounded or increasing logarithmically. Extends results to agnostic case and stochastic process settings.

Conclusion: The LCLL tree structure fully characterizes learnability in transductive online classification, with tight rate characterizations.

Abstract: We consider the problem of universal transductive online classification with a possibly unbounded label space. This setting considers online learning, with the sequence of instances (without labels) known to the learner in advance. We say a concept class $\mathcal{H}$ is learnable if there is a learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$, such that for every realizable sequence, the number of mistakes made by $\mathcal{A}$ grows at most sublinearly with the number of predictions. We characterize the learnability of this setting and show that there are only two possible optimal rates for the learnable classes: either bounded or increasing logarithmically. We introduce a new combinatorial structure, called ``Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree'', which, along with the indifference property, characterizes the learnability. We also extend the learnability result to the agnostic case and the case where only the stochastic process that generates the instance sequence is known.

</details>


### [206] [Discovering a Zeta Map Algorithm on Dyck Paths via Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30482)
*Xiaoyu Huang,Blake Jackson,Kyu-Hwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Using mechanistic interpretability on a small transformer trained on the zeta map for Dyck paths, the authors extract an explicit algorithm (the scaffolding map) that provably reproduces the bijection, demonstrating AI-assisted mathematical discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how mechanistic interpretability can translate learned model behavior into human-verifiable mathematical constructions, beyond standard prediction tasks.

Method: Train a small one-layer, one-head encoder-decoder transformer on the zeta map; analyze via decoder cross-attention analysis, linear probing, and causal intervention; translate discovered signals into a combinatorial algorithm.

Result: Discovery of the "scaffolding map," an explicit peak-centered traversal algorithm that matches the zeta map (modulo a reversal convention).

Conclusion: Mechanistic interpretability can produce precise, human-verifiable combinatorial algorithms from trained models, offering a controlled paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly used in mathematical discovery, but in mathematics the desired output is often not a prediction itself, but an explicit construction that can be checked independently. We study this setting through the zeta map on Dyck paths, a classical bijection in the combinatorics of the q,t-Catalan numbers. We train a deliberately small one-layer, one-head encoder-decoder transformer on this map and analyze its learned computation using mechanistic interpretability tools, including decoder cross-attention analysis, linear probing, and causal intervention. The analysis reveals a level-based mechanism: encoder representations make path levels linearly accessible, while the decoder selects and traverses input positions in a structured way. Translating these signals into combinatorics leads to the scaffolding map, an explicit peak-centered traversal algorithm for Dyck paths. We prove that this algorithm agrees with the zeta map, modulo a reversal convention in the labeling. This gives a controlled example of AI-assisted mathematical discovery in which mechanistic interpretability turns model behavior into a precise, human-verifiable combinatorial algorithm.

</details>


### [207] [Graph-Conditioned Mixture of Graph Neural Network Experts for Traffic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30486)
*Amirhossein Ghaffari,Saeid Sheikhi,Ekaterina Gilman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GC-MoE assigns each node in spatio-temporal forecasting tasks a personalized combination of frozen pretrained experts using a lightweight, graph-conditioned router, achieving strong performance with minimal trainable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different road segments (nodes) exhibit diverse traffic dynamics (functional class, structure, behavior), yet existing methods apply a single backbone uniformly. Node-wise expert specialization could better capture these variations.

Method: GC-MoE uses a graph-conditioned mixture of experts: frozen pretrained spatio-temporal GNN experts are combined via an input-aware, spatially contextualized router (trained end-to-end, ~17K params). Optionally includes a bounded output refinement layer and node-adaptive ST-LoRA adapters (for ablation).

Result: On PEMS04, PEMS07, METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, GC-MoE improves MAE over a zero-parameter ensemble baseline with competitive RMSE/MAPE, training only ~17K parameters on top of 1.5M frozen expert weights.

Conclusion: Personalized expert routing conditioned on both graph topology and recent traffic input is effective and parameter-efficient for spatio-temporal forecasting.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal forecasting on sensor graphs is commonly tackled with a single backbone architecture applied uniformly across all nodes, although graph regions can exhibit different dynamics. Road segments differ in functional class, structure, and traffic behavior, suggesting that node-wise expert specialization can be useful. We propose GC-MoE, a graph-conditioned mixture of experts framework that assigns each node a personalized combination of frozen forecasting experts based on graph topology and the recent traffic input window. GC-MoE combines frozen pretrained spatio-temporal GNN experts with an input-aware, spatially contextualized router while training only a lightweight routing module. We also study a bounded graph-conditioned output refinement layer as an optional extension and include node-adaptive ST-LoRA adapters only as an ablation diagnostic. Across four standard benchmarks (PEMS04, PEMS07, METR-LA, and PEMS-BAY), GC-MoE improves MAE over a zero-parameter ensemble baseline, with competitive RMSE and MAPE, while training only ~17K parameters on top of 1.5M frozen expert weights. The implementation is available at https://github.com/Ahghaffari/gc_moe.

</details>


### [208] [MAAT: Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30514)
*Suryash Yagnik,Shubham Gaur,Saksham Thakur,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha,Amitava Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Existing machine unlearning benchmarks severely underrepresent Why-type causal knowledge (<0.06%–1.3%), masking failures of unlearning methods on causal reasoning. 5WBENCH provides balanced 5W evaluation, revealing no existing method succeeds on Why-type questions. MAAT (Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning) is the first to achieve both high forgetting and retention on causal knowledge via a three-phase LoRA-based framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unlearning benchmarks are structurally skewed—Why-type causal/relational questions are drastically underrepresented (0.06%–1.3%), making failures on causal knowledge invisible in aggregate scores. Balanced evaluation is needed to detect this blind spot.

Method: 5WBENCH: a balanced 5,000-sample benchmark (1,000 per Who/What/When/Where/Why). MAAT: three-phase framework on LoRA adapter weights—(1) gradient-projected ascent for forgetting, (2) SVD rank-dimension pruning, (3) task vector negation + hybrid KL-hidden-state retain repair.

Result: No existing baseline simultaneously achieves high forgetting and retention on Why-type questions. Why-type difficulty stems from multi-hop reasoning chains (44% vs. ≤2%) and gradient dilution over 40.1-token answer spans. MAAT is the first to reach a new Pareto frontier point for both metrics.

Conclusion: 5WBENCH reveals systematic gaps in evaluating causal unlearning. MAAT demonstrates that targeted LoRA-based unlearning can address the Why-type challenge, marking the first method to balance forget-retain on causal knowledge.

Abstract: Machine unlearning evaluation is structurally skewed: Why-type questions, which probe causal and relational knowledge, comprise less than 0.06% of CounterFact, 0.6% of ZSRE, and less than 1.3% of TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP-Cyber. This near-zero representation means that methods that fail on causal knowledge can score highly in aggregate, and this failure is undetectable without balanced evaluation. We present 5WBENCH, a balanced 5,000-sample benchmark with 1,000 examples per 5W category (Who, What, When, Where, Why), making causal unlearning failures quantifiable for the first time. Using 5WBENCH, we show that no existing baseline simultaneously achieves high forgetting and high retention on Why-type questions: aggressive forgetting degrades retained knowledge, while conservative methods fail to forget causal facts. Why-type difficulty stems from multi-hop reasoning chains (44% of Why entries vs. less than or equal to 2% for others) and gradient dilution over 40.1-token answer spans. We present MAAT (Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning), a three-phase framework operating on LoRA adapter weights, combining gradient-projected ascent, SVD rank-dimension pruning, task vector negation, and hybrid KL-hidden-state retain repair. MAAT is the first method to simultaneously achieve high forgetting and high retention on Why-type causal knowledge, reaching a new operating point on the forget-retain Pareto frontier. We make our code publicly available.

</details>


### [209] [Revisiting Padded Transformer Expressivity: Which Architectural Choices Matter and Which Don't](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30523)
*Anej Svete,William Merrill,Ryan Cotterell,Ashish Sabharwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes exact circuit complexity equivalences for padded transformers, showing they are surprisingly robust to attention type, width, and uniformity—with numeric precision and depth as the main factors—mapping constant-precision to L-uniform AC⁰, growing-precision to L-uniform TC⁰, and looped variants to FO-uniform circuit classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing results linking transformers to boolean circuits lack exact characterizations and are sensitive to modeling choices, leaving open how robustly these equivalences hold under changes to attention type, model width, and uniformity.

Method: The authors analyze padded transformers (with filler symbols appended) under varying conditions of attention type (softmax vs. average hard attention), model width, numeric precision, depth, and uniformity. They prove equivalence mappings between padded transformer variants and circuit complexity classes.

Result: Polynomially padded L-uniform constant-precision transformers ≡ L-uniform AC⁰; growing-precision ones ≡ L-uniform TC⁰ (width independent). Looped constant-precision transformers achieve FO-uniform ACᵈ, growing-precision ones achieve FO-uniform TCᵈ. Width/precision beyond logarithmic does not increase expressivity. Results hold for both softmax and average hard attention.

Conclusion: Padded transformers are robust across attention type, width, and uniformity changes; numeric precision and model depth are the primary expressivity determinants, with clear circuit class equivalences established.

Abstract: Recent work describes what transformers can and cannot compute through connections to boolean circuits, but existing results lack exact characterizations and are sensitive to modeling choices. Padded transformers -- to whose input filler symbols such as ``...'' are appended -- emerge as a useful gadget for establishing equivalences to circuit classes by providing polynomial space for adaptive parallel computation. However, only a limited set of padded transformer idealizations has been studied, leaving open how robustly these equivalences hold under changes to attention type, model width, and uniformity. We find that, under practical assumptions, padded transformers are surprisingly robust to all of these, and identify numeric precision and model depth as the main factors affecting expressivity. Concretely, we prove that polynomially padded $\text{L-uniform}$ constant-precision transformers are equivalent to $\text{L-uniform AC}^0$, while growing-precision ones achieve $\text{L-uniform TC}^0$ regardless of width. Furthermore, looping enables sequential processing analogous to circuits: $\log^d N$-looped constant-precision transformers reach $\text{FO-uniform AC}^d$, and growing-precision ones reach $\text{FO-uniform TC}^d$. Interestingly, growing width or precision beyond logarithmic does not increase expressivity, and all our results hold for both softmax and average hard attention transformers.

</details>


### [210] [Representation Collapse in Sequential Post-Training of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30524)
*Yichen Liu,Mingyu Chen,Hao Wang,Xiaoran Xu,Chenxi Lin,Rui Zhang,Yutong Zhou,Yuxin Yang,Jiarui Wu,Wei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how sequential post-training stages (SFT, RLHF, safety tuning, etc.) compress LLM representations into low-rank, anisotropic, and homogeneous spaces, and how this compression reduces plasticity, generalization, and calibration. It proposes lightweight interventions (e.g., replay, feature refresh, diversity regularization) to preserve learnability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs undergo multiple sequential post-training stages (not just single instruction tuning), but the geometric effects of this pipeline progression on internal representations are poorly understood. The paper aims to determine whether representation concentration (low-rank, anisotropic, homogeneous features) emerges and whether it harms future adaptability.

Method: The authors define a measurement suite for hidden states, logits, token trajectories, and LoRA updates. They apply it to analyze SFT, preference optimization, safety/refusal tuning, math/code specialization, and chain-of-thought tuning under controlled stage orderings. They also evaluate interventions like mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, and LoRA update decorrelation.

Result: Excessive representation concentration predicts reduced plasticity during later adaptation, weaker out-of-domain generalization, and poorer calibration. Lightweight interventions can preserve future learnability without sacrificing post-training behavioral gains.

Conclusion: Representation compression in sequential post-training is a measurable and consequential phenomenon. Interventions that maintain diversity in feature spaces can mitigate these negative effects and preserve the model's ability to learn in subsequent stages.

Abstract: Large language models are now adapted through chains of post-training stages rather than through a single instruction-tuning pass. This paper studies whether such sequential post-training gradually compresses internal representations into low-rank, anisotropic, and homogeneous feature spaces. We define a measurement suite for hidden states, logits, token trajectories, and LoRA updates, and we use it to analyze supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, safety/refusal tuning, math and code specialization, and long chain-of-thought tuning under controlled stage orderings. The central hypothesis is that excessive representation concentration is not merely a geometric curiosity: it predicts reduced plasticity during later adaptation, weaker out-of-domain generalization, and poorer calibration. We further evaluate lightweight interventions, including mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, and LoRA update decorrelation, as ways to preserve future learnability without giving up the behavioral gains of post-training.

</details>


### [211] [Measuring, Localizing, and Ablating Alignment Signatures in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30526)
*Aniket Anand,Janvijay Singh,Zhewei Sun,Dilek Hakkani-Tür,Nick Feamster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that post-training causes language models to adopt a detectable AI-like style, and introduces PASTA, a method that ablates internal alignment signatures to reduce AI detectability while maintaining coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether post-training introduces or amplifies AI-like stylistic regularities in language models and whether these regularities have a localized internal signature.

Method: PASTA (Post-training Alignment Signature Targeted Ablation) estimates a post-training alignment signature from aligned-base residual contrasts and ablates the corresponding direction during decoding.

Result: Across 11 aligned models and 6 AI detectors, PASTA lowers detection rates for most aligned models; the effect transfers well across detectors and is not reproduced by random directions.

Conclusion: AI-like stylistic effects of post-training can be measured, localized, and causally tested through activation ablation.

Abstract: Aligned language models often exhibit a recognizable AI-like style, yet its connection to post-training and internal representations remains poorly understood. In this work, we study whether post-training introduces or amplifies AI-like stylistic regularities and whether these regularities have a localized internal signature. To this end, we compare human text, base-model generations, and aligned-model generations under matched human-source prefixes. Aligned generations show lower human-corpus affinity and higher AI-detection rates than base generations, suggesting that post-training shifts generated text away from human-corpus style and toward detector-visible AI-like text. We then introduce PASTA (Post-training Alignment Signature Targeted Ablation), a training-free method that estimates a post-training alignment signature from aligned-base residual contrasts and ablates the corresponding direction during decoding. Across 11 aligned models and 6 AI detectors, PASTA lowers the detection rate for most aligned models; this effect transfers well across detectors and is not reproduced by random directions. Qualitative analysis suggests that PASTA generations remain relevant and coherent while exhibiting greater stylistic variation. Together, these results show that AI-like stylistic effects of post-training can be measured, localized, and causally tested through activation ablation.

</details>


### [212] [The Long-Term Effects of Data Selection in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30537)
*Yuxin Yang,Aoxiong Zeng,Xiangquan Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a long-horizon perspective on LLM data selection for multi-stage fine-tuning, showing that myopic selection strategies that optimize immediate performance can cause rank reversal (slowing future learning, increasing forgetting). It proposes a Long-Horizon Aware Selection (LHAS) objective with coverage, transfer, and anti-concentration terms to mitigate these issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data selection methods optimize for immediate utility (loss, diversity, quality), ignoring how choices affect future adaptation, forgetting, and robustness across multiple fine-tuning stages.

Method: The authors compare representative selection families (random, loss-based, gradient-based, diversity-based, quality-based, utility-diversity) under a unified multi-stage protocol, formalize myopic selection with rank reversal analysis, and propose the LHAS objective.

Result: Short-term selectors exhibit rank reversal: they improve current-stage performance but hinder subsequent learning and increase forgetting, confirming the myopic selection phenomenon.

Conclusion: Data selection should be evaluated as a training intervention that shapes the learning trajectory over multiple stages, not just as a local data-efficiency mechanism.

Abstract: Data selection is increasingly used to reduce the cost of large language model (LLM) fine-tuning, with recent methods prioritizing samples by current utility, diversity, quality, or influence. This paper studies a different question: when fine-tuning occurs over multiple stages, can selection strategies that look optimal now make the model less adaptable later? We introduce a long-horizon view of LLM data selection in which a selector is evaluated not only by immediate task performance, but also by future adaptation speed, forgetting, capability imbalance, and out-of-distribution robustness. We compare representative random, loss-based, gradient-based, diversity-based, quality-based, and utility-diversity selection families under a unified multi-stage protocol. Through controlled experiments designed to instantiate this protocol, we show how short-term selectors can exhibit rank reversal: they improve the current stage while slowing subsequent learning and increasing forgetting. We formalize this behavior as \emph{myopic selection}, provide a simple local analysis of why it can occur, and propose a diagnostic Long-Horizon Aware Selection (LHAS) objective that augments immediate utility with coverage, future-proxy transfer, and anti-concentration terms. The study argues that data selection should be evaluated as a training intervention that shapes the model's learning trajectory, rather than only as a local data-efficiency mechanism.

</details>


### [213] [DisasterLex: An Expert Concept-to-Schema Knowledge Graph for Geospatial Reasoning in Disaster Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30538)
*Yiming Xiao,Ankit Basu,Kai Yin,Sahil Vartak,Christian Swords,Ali Mostafavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DisasterLex is a knowledge-graph-mediated framework that improves text-to-SQL for disaster management by inserting an Expert Knowledge Graph between user queries and databases, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by 1.4x to 2.75x on a 75-query test set spanning 36 geospatial tables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disasters are increasingly costly and effective response depends on querying structured tabular data. Current text-to-SQL methods transfer poorly to the disaster domain because queries span heterogeneous geospatial schemas and require reasoning over causal relations.

Method: DisasterLex is a knowledge-graph-mediated framework with an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) of curated concepts and typed causal edges. It runs four stages: (1) identifying query entities, (2) routing to operational domain, (3) planning over causal edges, and (4) grounding the SQL, restricting the schema passed to the model at each step.

Result: On seven base models (proprietary and open-weight), DisasterLex beats four state-of-the-art baselines (LightRAG, HippoRAG 2, ReFoRCE, CHESS) by 1.4x to 2.75x, with absolute scores of 1.65 to 3.56 (out of 5.0). Error analysis shows baseline failures cluster in routing and multi-table SQL composition.

Conclusion: DisasterLex effectively addresses the domain gap in text-to-SQL for disaster management through explicit knowledge graph mediation and staged schema restriction, with code, data, and EKG artifact publicly available.

Abstract: Disasters are inevitable and increasingly costly, and effective response depends on querying structured tabular data: precise, information-dense records of hazard, exposure, vulnerability, and lifeline infrastructure that underpin disaster management. Current text-to-SQL methods enable natural-language access to such tables but transfer poorly to the disaster domain, where queries span heterogeneous geospatial schemas and require reasoning over causal relations. We introduce DisasterLex, a knowledge-graph-mediated framework that inserts an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) of curated concepts and typed causal edges between the user query and the database, bridged to schema by concept-to-table links. The orchestration runs four stages (identifying query entities, routing to the operational domain, planning over causal edges, and grounding the SQL), restricting the schema passed to the model at each step. We instantiate it on a disaster-analytics database (36 geospatial tables, 150 columns) with an EKG of 107 concepts, 117 causal edges, and 52 concept-to-schema links, evaluated on a 75-query test set. On all seven base models spanning proprietary and open-weight families, DisasterLex beats four state-of-the-art baselines (LightRAG, HippoRAG 2, ReFoRCE, CHESS) by 1.4x to 2.75x, with absolute scores of 1.65 to 3.56 (of 5.0). Error analysis shows baseline failures cluster in routing and multi-table SQL composition, the operations our orchestration explicitly addresses. Code, data, and the EKG artifact are available at https://github.com/YimingXiao98/DisasterLex and on Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20388029.

</details>


### [214] [SubsurfaceGen: Procedural Generation of Field-Scale Earth Models and Seismic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30541)
*Joseph Stitt,Pratik Rathore,Madeleine Udell,Ching-Yao Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SubsurfaceGen is a GPU-accelerated generator for 3D velocity models and seismic data, releasing a large-scale, geologically diverse dataset (4,276 samples across 6 settings) to address limitations in existing FWI training data, with experiments revealing failure modes in field-scale ML-based FWI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FWI training data resources (Marmousi, SEAM, OpenFWI) lack spatial/temporal extent, geological diversity, and physical realism needed for field-scale machine learning approaches.

Method: Built SubsurfaceGen as a GPU-accelerated 3D velocity model and seismic data generator; released a dataset of 4,276 2D velocity slices, 5s wavefields, and 8s shot gathers from 42 realistic 3D models (10 km x 10 km x 6.19 km at 10m resolution) spanning six geological settings; evaluated neural operators for wavefield prediction and encoder-decoders for velocity inversion with OOD testing.

Result: Experiments surface failure modes at field-scale and demonstrate SubsurfaceGen's utility for ML-based FWI, particularly through out-of-distribution testing on held-out geological settings.

Conclusion: SubsurfaceGen and its associated dataset can significantly impact ML-based FWI by providing more realistic, diverse, and field-scale training data, enabling better evaluation of failure modes.

Abstract: Full waveform inversion (FWI) is the gold standard for subsurface imaging, with applications from carbon sequestration to energy and mineral exploration to earthquake hazard assessment. Machine learning approaches to FWI need field-scale, geologically diverse, and physically realistic training data, but existing resources such as Marmousi, SEAM, and OpenFWI fall short on spatial extent, temporal extent, geological diversity, and physical realism. We address these limitations with SubsurfaceGen, a GPU-accelerated generator for 3D velocity models and seismic data. Along with SubsurfaceGen, we release a paired dataset of 4,276 2D velocity slices, 5 s wavefields, and 8 s shot gathers drawn from 42 realistic, field-scale 3D velocity models, each spanning 10 km x 10 km laterally and 6.19 km deep at 10 m resolution. The dataset spans six geological settings -- four built with SubsurfaceGen and two drawn from prior sources -- relevant for carbon sequestration and hydrocarbon exploration. We use this dataset to evaluate neural operators on wavefield prediction and encoder-decoders on end-to-end velocity inversion, holding out one geological setting for out-of-distribution testing. These experiments surface failure modes at field-scale and demonstrate how SubsurfaceGen and the associated dataset can impact ML-based FWI.

</details>


### [215] [Early Prediction of Future Behavioral Strategy from Process Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30550)
*Robert Kasumba,Dennis Barbour,Chien-Ju Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM) that uses partial behavioral traces from source tasks to infer cross-task person-level tendencies and predict strategies in a held-out target task, demonstrated in a gameplay dataset and controlled simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive systems need to make early task-specific predictions about people from limited evidence, but standard approaches (aggregate summaries or single-task process models) fail to capture transferable person-level tendencies that generalize across tasks.

Method: The authors propose a Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM) that encodes task-specific process traces and fuses them into a shared person-level latent representation to predict behavior in a held-out target task.

Result: In PowerWash Simulator gameplay data, PLVM uses partial traces from two cleaning tasks to predict Zone Planner vs. Zone Hopper strategies in a held-out Fire Station level. Controlled simulations confirm cross-task fusion improves prediction when source tasks reveal complementary latent dimensions.

Conclusion: Process-level cross-task modeling enables early prediction of target-task strategy when sufficient target-task behavior is impractical, by leveraging transferable person-level structure across tasks.

Abstract: Adaptive systems often need to make task-specific decisions about people from limited evidence: a tutor may need to anticipate how a learner will approach a new problem, a game may need to adapt when a player enters a new level, and a human-AI system may need to infer whether a partner will persist with a plan or switch goals. These decisions depend on person-level tendencies that shape how people solve related tasks, but such tendencies are difficult to infer from standard behavioral evidence. One approach is to use aggregate outcome summaries, such as scores, completion rates, or productivity; these summaries are compact and available across tasks, but can collapse distinct behavioral processes into similar outcomes. Another approach is to use process-level traces, which record how behavior unfolds; however, process modeling within one task can entangle stable person-level tendencies with task-specific layout and affordances. In this work, we study early cross-task behavioral inference: whether partial source-task process traces can reveal transferable person-level structure that predicts strategy in a held-out target task. We introduce a Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM), which encodes task-specific traces and fuses them into a shared person-level latent representation for cross-task prediction. In PowerWash Simulator, a naturalistic telemetry dataset of human gameplay, PLVM uses partial traces from two cleaning tasks to predict locally persistent Zone Planner behavior versus frequent Zone Hopper behavior in the held-out Fire Station level. Controlled simulations with known latent types show that cross-task fusion helps when source tasks reveal complementary dimensions of a shared latent process. These results suggest that process-level cross-task modeling can support early prediction of target-task strategy when observing sufficient target-task behavior is impractical.

</details>


### [216] [Supervised Training Rapidly Degrades Early Visual Cortex Alignment Across Biologically Plausible Learning Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30556)
*Nils Leutenegger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Untrained neural networks match early visual cortex similarity, but training—especially with global error signals like backpropagation—reduces V1 alignment, while local learning rules like predictive coding and STDP better preserve brain-like representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why untrained (random) neural networks often show equal or better representational similarity to early visual cortex than trained networks, challenging the assumption that learning improves brain alignment.

Method: Track RSA alignment to human fMRI data across training (epochs 0-40) for four learning rules (BP, FA, PC, STDP) using 720 object images from THINGS database and fMRI data from three subjects across six visual ROIs, measuring Spearman correlations between model and brain RDMs.

Result: (1) Single epoch reduces V1 alignment by 25-90%; (2) BP reduces V1 alignment most (delta r = -0.080), PC and STDP preserve more (delta r ~ -0.04); (3) Opposite weak trend in LOC where BP increases alignment slightly.

Conclusion: Untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics through inductive biases; global error signals (BP) disrupt early representations more than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better retain brain-like structure.

Abstract: Random, untrained neural networks consistently match or exceed trained networks in representational similarity to early visual cortex. This puzzling finding challenges the assumption that learning improves brain alignment. We investigate it by tracking representational similarity analysis (RSA) alignment to human fMRI data across training for four learning rules: backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP). Using 720 object images from the THINGS database and fMRI data from three subjects across six visual ROIs, we measure Spearman correlations between model and brain representational dissimilarity matrices at eight training checkpoints (epochs 0-40). We find that (1) a single epoch of training reduces V1 alignment by 25-90%, depending on the learning rule; (2) backpropagation reduces V1 alignment most severely (delta r = -0.080), while predictive coding and STDP preserve substantially more (delta r ~ -0.04); and (3) a weaker, opposite tendency appears in object-selective cortex (LOC), where BP shows the largest increase in alignment during training, although the absolute change is small. These results suggest that untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics through inductive biases alone, and that global error signals (BP) reshape early representations more aggressively than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better preserve brain-like structure.

</details>


### [217] [Zeroth-Order Non-Log-Concave Sampling with Variance Reduction and Applications to Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30573)
*M. Berk Sahin,Behzad Sharif,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin sampling method for high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions in black-box settings. It provides the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling, and introduces ZO-APMC for black-box inverse problems with score-based generative priors, supported by theoretical and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling from high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions with unnormalized densities is challenging, especially in black-box settings where gradient information is unavailable or costly. Existing zeroth-order methods suffer from high variance and lack non-asymptotic convergence guarantees.

Method: The authors propose a variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin sampling method with a gradient estimator that reduces variance and eliminates unfavorable dimensional dependence in batch size. They also propose ZO-APMC for posterior sampling in black-box inverse problems using pre-trained score-based generative priors.

Result: First non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling in ε-relative Fisher information; under Poincaré inequality, squared total variation distance guarantees are also provided. First non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for posterior sampling with score-based priors in black-box inverse problems.

Conclusion: The method enables practical and stable sampling in black-box settings, supported by synthetic experiments and strong empirical performance on linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

Abstract: Sampling from high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions with unnormalized densities remains a fundamental challenge in machine learning, particularly in black-box settings where gradient information is inaccessible or computationally prohibitive.
