The quantile-core certificate delivers non-vacuous bounds where the covering certificate fails, and tracks empirical ESS within 7%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learned-transport MCMC methods lack automatic, rigorous convergence guarantees. Existing spectral-gap bounds are often vacuous in practice, and it is unclear whether poor bounds reflect actual algorithm failure or limitations of the proof technique.

Method: The framework uses a normalizing flow to map a Gaussian reference to an approximation of the target posterior, serving as both the independence Metropolis-Hastings proposal and the basis for a computable spectral-gap bound. Two certificates are developed: (1) a covering certificate using finite-sample covering arguments with O(n^{-1/D}) correction, and (2) a quantile-core certificate focusing on a high-probability residual core with O(n^{-1/2}) slack. A matching Omega(n^{-1/D}) lower bound is proved for the covering certificate.

Result: On synthetic targets (D=2-20), structural-engineering posteriors (D=6,8), real-data logistic regression on Heart Disease data (D=13), and synthetic Bayesian logistic regression (D=20), the quantile-core certificate delivers non-vacuous spectral-gap bounds where the covering certificate is vacuous. Its spectral-gap proxy tracks empirical ESS within 7%. A negative control shows it discriminates flow quality by >10x, while acceptance rates differ by only 1.15x.

Conclusion: The dual-certificate framework is the first to provide automatic, dimension-aware convergence certificates for learned-transport MCMC, successfully distinguishing genuine transport failure from proof-technique limitations.

Abstract: We propose CerT-MCMC, a framework that equips learned-transport Markov chain Monte Carlo with automatic, rigorous convergence certificates. A normalising flow maps a Gaussian reference to an approximation of the target posterior; the same flow then serves as both the independence Metropolis-Hastings proposal and the basis for a computable spectral-gap bound. We develop two complementary certificates. The covering certificate bounds the weight-ratio oscillation over the full proposal support via finite-sample covering arguments, yielding full-support spectral-gap bounds when a conservative gradient bound is available; its correction term scales as O(n^{-1/D}), making it rapidly weak and eventually vacuous as dimension increases. We prove a matching Omega(n^{-1/D}) lower bound, establishing that this barrier is intrinsic to pointwise Lipschitz certification. The quantile-core certificate restricts attention to a high-probability residual core on which the oscillation is controlled by one-dimensional empirical quantiles, with a finite-sample probability slack of O(n^{-1/2}), independent of the ambient dimension. On synthetic targets (D=2-20), structural-engineering posteriors (D=6,8), real-data logistic regression on the Heart Disease data set (D=13), and synthetic Bayesian logistic regression (D=20), the quantile-core certificate delivers non-vacuous spectral-gap bounds where the covering certificate is vacuous, and its spectral-gap proxy tracks empirical effective sample sizes within 7%. A negative control experiment confirms that the certificate discriminates flow quality by a factor exceeding 10x, whereas acceptance rates differ by only 1.15x. To our knowledge, the dual-certificate framework is the first to provide automatic, dimension-aware convergence certificates for learned-transport MCMC, distinguishing genuine transport failure from proof-technique limitations.

</details>


### [246] [Reducing the GPU Memory Bottleneck with Lossless Compression for ML -- Extended](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30728)
*Aditya K Kamath,Arvind Krishnamurthy,Marco Canini,Simon Peter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IBP is a lossless compression algorithm that reduces data transfer bottlenecks in ML pipelines by identifying and eliminating invariant bits across tensor groups, achieving significant speedups in GNN training (74%), DLRM inference (180%), and LLM inference (24%) with easy-to-use APIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML training/inference often processes data exceeding GPU memory capacity, relying on PCIe for tensor transfers which creates critical bottlenecks. Lossy compression introduces workload-dependent accuracy loss that is complex to deploy in existing ML systems.

Method: IBP (Invariant Bit Packing) identifies and eliminates invariant bits across tensor groups, uses GPU-optimized decompression with warp parallelism, low-overhead bit operations, and asynchronous PCIe transfers, and provides easy-to-use APIs.

Result: 74% faster GNN training, 180% faster DLRM embedding lookup, 24% faster LLM inference on average.

Conclusion: Lossless compression (IBP) avoids the deployment complexity of lossy compression while effectively reducing data transfer bottlenecks in ML pipelines, demonstrating significant speedups across diverse ML workloads.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) training and inference often process data sets far exceeding GPU memory capacity, forcing them to rely on PCIe for on-demand tensor transfers, causing critical transfer bottlenecks. Lossy compression has been proposed to relieve bottlenecks but introduces workload-dependent accuracy loss, making it complex or even prohibitive to use in existing ML deployments. We explore lossless compression as an alternative that avoids this deployment complexity. We identify where lossless compression can be integrated into ML pipelines while minimizing interference with GPU execution. Based on our findings, we introduce Invariant Bit Packing (IBP), a novel lossless compression algorithm designed to minimize data transfer time for ML. IBP identifies and eliminates invariant bits across groups of tensors, improving throughput through GPU-optimized decompression that leverages warp parallelism, low-overhead bit operations, and asynchronous PCIe transfers. We provide easy-to-use APIs, showcasing them by adding IBP support to GNN training, as well as DLRM and LLM inference frameworks. IBP achieves, on average, 74% faster GNN training, 180% faster DLRM embedding lookup, and 24% faster LLM inference.

</details>


### [247] [SemStruct: Contextualizing Semantic Embeddings with Structural Information for Schema Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30729)
*Inwon Kang,Kavitha Srinivas,Nandana Mihindukulasooriya,Sola Shirai,Parikshit Ram,Horst Samulowitz,Oshani Seneviratne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SemStruct combines frozen PLMs with GNNs to model table structure as heterogeneous graphs, achieving SOTA schema matching without fine-tuning language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PLM-based schema matching methods serialize tabular data as text sequences, discarding row-level co-occurrences (relational context) that are critical for structural understanding.

Method: Models tables as heterogeneous graphs (columns + values as nodes, rows as edges), uses frozen PLM for semantic encoding + lightweight GNN for structural propagation.

Result: Outperforms fully fine-tuned baselines on Valentine and SOTAB-SM benchmarks; ablation shows row representations serve as topological conduits, not semantic entities.

Conclusion: Explicit structural modeling is necessary for schema matching; frozen PLM + GNN framework is both effective and efficient, eliminating the need for LLM fine-tuning.

Abstract: Schema matching is a fundamental step in integrating heterogeneous data sources. While Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) have revolutionized this task by capturing linguistic semantics, they typically process tabular data as serialized text sequences of standalone column descriptions. This serialization discards critical structural information -- specifically, the row-level co-occurrences, i.e. the relational context -- forcing models to rely solely on column header semantics or standalone distributions. To bridge this gap, we propose SemStruct, a framework that joins the semantic power of frozen PLMs with the structural inductive bias of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). We model the table as a heterogeneous graph where columns and values are nodes connected by rows, allowing the GNN to propagate disambiguating context across the structure. Unlike other state-of-the-art methods that require proprietary LLM access and fine-tuning of language models, SemStruct keeps the language model frozen and trains only a lightweight structural encoder. Extensive experiments on the Valentine and SOTAB-SM benchmarks demonstrate that SemStruct achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming fully fine-tuned baselines on complex, semantically joinable datasets. Furthermore, our ablation studies reveal that row representations serve primarily as topological conduits rather than semantic entities, validating the necessity of explicit structural modeling in schema matching.

</details>


### [248] [Beyond Accuracy: Evaluating Efficiency, Robustness and Explainability in Deep Learning for Malaria Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30734)
*Olivier Kanamugire,Kerol Djoumessi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks deep learning models for malaria screening, finding that lightweight models match heavy ones in performance, but post-hoc explanations (XAI) are unreliable under image corruption even when predictions remain accurate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Malaria is a leading cause of death in sub-Saharan Africa, and scarce diagnostic infrastructure makes accurate diagnosis challenging. Deep learning offers a path forward, but clinical adoption is limited by computational cost and opacity in decision-making.

Method: Four deep learning models of varying architectures and capacities are benchmarked on the NLM-Malaria dataset, jointly evaluating predictive performance, robustness, and post-hoc explainability using CAM-based and fine-grained attribution methods. The Friedman test is used for statistical comparison, and robustness is tested under three types of image corruption.

Result: Lightweight models match heavier ones in predictive performance with no statistically significant differences (Friedman test). CAM-based XAI localizes diagnostically relevant regions better than fine-grained attribution. Model confidence degrades faster than accuracy under corruption. No XAI method is robust to corruption at clinically plausible noise levels.

Conclusion: Lightweight architectures are suitable for malaria diagnosis in resource-constrained settings, but the vulnerability of post-hoc explanations under corruption is a critical concern for responsible clinical deployment.

Abstract: Malaria remains a leading cause of mortality in sub-Saharan Africa, where scarce diagnostic infrastructure makes timely, accurate diagnosis particularly challenging. While deep learning offers a compelling path toward automated malaria screening, clinical adoption is hindered by computational cost and opacity in decision-making. This work benchmarks four deep learning models spanning a wide range of designed design architectures and model capacities on the NLM-Malaria dataset, jointly evaluating predictive performance, robustness, and post-hoc explainability. We find that lightweight, efficient-by-design models match their heavier counterparts in predictive performance, and the Friedman test confirms no statistically significant performance differences. CAM-based XAI methods consistently localize diagnostically relevant regions, while fine-grained attribution methods produce less targeted explanations, particularly with heavier backbones. Robustness evaluation under three types of image corruption further reveals that model confidence degrades faster than accuracy, providing a practical signal for human review. However, no XAI method is robust to corruption, with explanation reliability degrading at noise levels plausible in clinical practice, even when predictions remain accurate. These findings support the deployment of lightweight architectures for malaria diagnosis in resource-constrained settings, while highlighting the vulnerability of post-hoc explanations as an important consideration for responsible clinical deployment.

</details>


### [249] [OrcaRouter: A Production-Oriented LLM Router with Hybrid Offline-Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30736)
*Zhenghua Bao,Fengya Tian,Chris Zhang,Zhenjun Chen,Xile Ma,Yi Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrcaRouter is a production-oriented LLM router that uses a LinUCB contextual bandit with hybrid offline-online learning to select cost-effective models for incoming requests, achieving strong leaderboard performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently route queries to different LLMs based on their capabilities and costs, optimizing the trade-off between response quality and inference expense in production settings.

Method: Combines a LinUCB-based contextual bandit (using lexical and sentence-embedding features) with hybrid offline-online learning: offline fitting of ridge regressors per arm via full-information reward matrix, then online bandit learning with optional continued updates.

Result: Ranked 2nd on RouterArena leaderboard (score 72.08) with 75.54% accuracy at $1.00 per 1,000 queries.

Conclusion: OrcaRouter demonstrates effective cost-quality trade-offs in production LLM routing, with strong performance validated on a public benchmark.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models, each with distinct capabilities and inference costs, raises a practical deployment question: given an incoming request, which model should handle it? We present OrcaRouter, a production-oriented LLM router that combines a LinUCB-based contextual bandit over lexical and sentence-embedding features with a hybrid offline-online learning protocol. Offline, OrcaRouter obtains full-information feedback by evaluating each candidate model on a curated set of routing prompts, yielding a reward matrix used to fit one ridge regressor per arm. At deployment time, it initializes from these parameters and can optionally continue learning from bandit feedback, updating only the selected model's arm after observing its reward. At the time of our RouterArena submission (May 20, 2026), OrcaRouter-Adaptive ranked second on the public RouterArena leaderboard with an arena score of 72.08, achieving 75.54% accuracy at a cost of USD 1.00 per 1,000 queries.

</details>


### [250] [Chain-of-Thought and Compressed Looped Transformers: A Memory-Budget Separation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30757)
*Haozhou Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper compares three memory regimes for test-time computation—compressed latent loops, full sequence-state loops, and chain-of-thought scratchpads—and shows that compressed loops are fundamentally limited by their recurrent state size, unable to solve P-complete problems, while chain-of-thought can. This reveals persistent mutable memory as a key resource for reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the role of persistent mutable memory in test-time reasoning by comparing different memory regimes (compressed loops, full sequence-state loops, and chain-of-thought scratchpads) and identifying fundamental limitations of compressed recurrent state.

Method: The authors theoretically compare compressed latent loops, full sequence-state loops, and chain-of-thought scratchpads under computational complexity theory, and empirically validate with pointer-chasing and associative-recall tasks.

Result: Compressed loops cannot decide P-complete problems under logspace reductions even with many steps, while polynomial-length chain-of-thought can. Full sequence-state loops perform closer to scratchpads. Performance depends on matching the persistent-state budget to task working-memory demands.

Conclusion: Persistent mutable memory capacity is a central resource for test-time reasoning; compressed loops are fundamentally limited as small-space reasoners regardless of computation steps, whereas chain-of-thought's growing scratchpad enables solving harder problems.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought prompting and looped Transformers both give a fixed model more test-time computation, but they differ in what they remember. Chain-of-thought stores intermediate state in generated tokens that remain in the context, whereas a looped Transformer carries state through recurrent hidden activations. We argue that this persistent mutable memory is a central resource for test-time reasoning.
  We compare three memory regimes, the compressed latent loop, the full sequence-state loop, and the chain-of-thought scratchpad. Our main result shows that a compressed loop is limited by the size of its recurrent state. Running the loop longer adds computation but does not by itself create a growing scratchpad, so a loop with a small recurrent state remains a small-space reasoner even when run for many steps. Under a standard complexity assumption, such loops cannot decide problems that are P-complete under logspace reductions, whereas polynomial-length chain-of-thought can.
  The separation is specific to compressed loops, as full sequence-state loops carry state at every input position and live in a memory-rich regime closer to explicit scratchpads. Controlled pointer-chasing and associative-recall sweeps illustrate this memory-budget view, with performance sensitive to whether the persistent-state budget matches the task's working-memory demand.

</details>


### [251] [Efficient and Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Framework for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30776)
*Ha Manh Bui,Metod Jazbec,Eric Nalisnick,Anqi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DUAL is a diffusion-based uncertainty-aware framework for offline-to-online RL that uses Laplace approximation and distance-based shift detection to improve exploration and exploitation, achieving better online returns than existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the distribution shift problem in offline-to-online RL by using uncertainty quantification to guide exploration vs. exploitation, reducing costly online interactions while maintaining performance.

Method: DUAL distills a fast-sampling diffusion actor policy and transition model in the offline phase using prior diffusion model knowledge, then employs Laplace approximation for epistemic uncertainty estimation and distance-based transition-state-shift detection to guide online exploration.

Result: DUAL improves the online expected return over O2O-RL baselines across multiple settings and environments.

Conclusion: The actor loss with Laplace approximation provides a principled estimate of epistemic uncertainty, and the overall framework effectively balances exploration/exploitation in the online phase.

Abstract: Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning (O2O-RL) leverages an offline, pre-trained policy to minimize costly online interactions. Although data-efficient, O2O-RL is susceptible to shifts between offline and online distributions. Existing work aims to mitigate the harm of this shift by finetuning the policy on trajectory data sampled from a diffusion model. Inspired by this line of work, we propose DUAL: an efficient \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{U}ncertainty-\textbf{A}ware framework for offline-to-online reinforcement \textbf{L}earning. DUAL utilizes the prior knowledge of the diffusion model to distill a fast-sampling diffusion actor policy and transition model in the offline phase. DUAL also employs a Laplace approximation and distance transition-state-shift detection, thereby using uncertainty quantification to improve exploration versus exploitation in the online phase. We formally show that our actor loss with the Laplace approximation provides a proxy for a principled estimate of epistemic uncertainty. Empirically, DUAL improves the online expected return over O2O-RL baselines across multiple settings and environments.

</details>


### [252] [AbstainGNN: Teaching Graph Neural Networks to Abstain for Graph Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30786)
*Xixun Lin,Zhiheng Zhou,Zhengyin Zhang,Yancheng Chen,Shuai Zhang,Ge Zhang,Shichao Zhu,Lixin Zou,Chuan Zhou,Peng Zhang,Shirui Pan,Yanan Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AbstainGNN is a theory-driven framework that enables graph neural networks to abstain from making predictions under high uncertainty, using a PAC-Bayesian approach to balance classification errors and rejection costs, with a two-stage training strategy that outperforms existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNNs are forced to make predictions even under high uncertainty or unknown conditions, leading to unreliable decisions that can severely impact downstream tasks, especially in safety-critical scenarios.

Method: AbstainGNN explicitly models both predictive and abstention functions, uses PAC-Bayesian theory to characterize the trade-off between classification errors and rejection costs, and employs a two-stage training strategy (predictive function warm-start + abstention function calibration).

Result: On five benchmark datasets, AbstainGNN outperforms existing abstention methods, achieving superior classification performance under the same rejection rates.

Conclusion: The proposed theory-driven abstention framework effectively enables GNNs to reject uncertain predictions, improving reliability in graph classification tasks.

Abstract: Graph classification is a core task in graph data mining with widespread real-world applications. Recent advances in graph neural networks (GNNs) have led to substantial performance improvements for graph classification. However, existing GNNs are typically forced to make predictions even under high uncertainty or unknown conditions, resulting in unreliable decisions that can severely impact downstream tasks, particularly in safety-critical scenarios. To address this critical limitation, we propose AbstainGNN, a novel and theory-driven framework for graph classification with abstention, which enables GNNs to reject uncertain predictions instead of producing incorrect decisions. Specifically, AbstainGNN explicitly models both the predictive function and the abstention function, allowing for effective utilization of graph structural information. Moreover, unlike existing heuristic abstention methods, we theoretically characterize the trade-off between classification errors and rejection costs from a PAC-Bayesian generalization perspective, and derive a unified learning objective for model optimization. Guided by this theoretical insight, we further develop an efficient two-stage training strategy consisting of predictive function warm-start and abstention function calibration. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets show that AbstainGNN outperforms existing abstention methods, achieving superior classification performance under the same rejection rates.

</details>


### [253] [Smaller Models are Natural Explorers for Policy-Level Diversity in GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30789)
*Yiming Ren,Yiran Xu,Zicheng Lin,Chufan Shi,Yukang Chen,Dingdong Wang,Tianhe Wu,Junjie Wang,Yujiu Yang,Yu Qiao,Ruihang Chu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S2L-PO enhances GRPO for LLMs by using smaller models as structured explorers to generate diverse, temporally coherent rollouts for training larger models, outperforming token-level randomness methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GRPO methods rely on token-level randomness for diversity, which introduces step-wise noise and incoherent trajectories; the authors discover smaller models offer better policy-level diversity (higher pass@k) that is temporally correlated and logically consistent.

Method: Proposes S2L-PO (Small-to-Large Policy Optimization) using fixed small models as natural explorers to generate structured rollouts for training larger models, with a progressive annealing strategy transitioning from offline small-model rollouts to the large learner's own sampling to balance exploration and exploitation.

Result: +8.8% accuracy improvement on AIME 24 using a 1.7B explorer to guide an 8B model, with faster convergence, higher performance ceiling, and reduced rollout compute.

Conclusion: Leveraging small models for structured exploration in GRPO is more effective than token-level randomness, enabling better diversity, coherence, and training efficiency for large LLMs.

Abstract: We identify a new dimension for enhancing rollout diversity in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for LLMs. While GRPO relies on diverse rollouts, prevailing strategies primarily increase diversity by injecting more token-level randomness, which may introduce step-wise noise and lead to incoherent trajectories. We uncover that smaller models within the same model family inherently exhibit higher policy-level diversity, indicated by their superior pass@k relative to larger counterparts as sample counts increase. Unlike token-level noise, this diversity is temporally correlated, preserves logical consistency, and provides structured exploration signals for gradient estimation. We thus propose S2L-PO (Small-to-Large Policy Optimization), a framework that leverages fixed small models as natural explorers to train larger models. To balance exploration and exploitation, we design a progressive annealing strategy that transitions from offline small-model rollouts to the large learner's own sampling. This shift elegantly avoids mid-training performance drops caused by the small model's capacity limits, achieving faster convergence and unlocking a higher performance ceiling. S2L-PO improves accuracy on diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., +8.8% on AIME 24 using a 1.7B explorer to guide the 8B model) while reducing rollout compute.

</details>


### [254] [Conformal Reliability: A New Evaluation Metric for Conditional Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30807)
*Yachen Gao,Xinwei Sun,Yikai Wang,Ye Shi,Jingya Wang,Jianfeng Feng,Yanwei Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new evaluation metric called "reliability score" based on conformal prediction for assessing conditional generative models, and introduces the CReL framework to efficiently compute this metric by constructing prediction sets with guaranteed coverage and optimizing worst-case performance within them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for conditional generative models typically assess a single output and fail to capture the variability or potential risks inherent in generation. There is a lack of a suitable metric that accounts for model uncertainty and worst-case performance.

Method: The authors propose a reliability score based on conformal prediction to measure worst-case performance within a prediction set at a pre-specified confidence level. They introduce CReL (Conformal ReLiability), a framework that (i) constructs prediction sets with desired coverage and (ii) optimizes the reliability score within the constructed set.

Result: Theoretical results on coverage are provided. Empirical experiments on synthetic data, image-to-text, and text-to-image tasks show that the method produces more informative prediction sets than existing approaches, and demonstrates the interpretability, validity, and effectiveness of the proposed metric and framework.

Conclusion: The reliability score and CReL framework offer a principled way to evaluate conditional generative models with uncertainty quantification, outperforming existing methods in producing informative prediction sets.

Abstract: Conditional generative models have recently achieved remarkable success in various applications. However, a suitable metric for evaluating the reliability of these models, which takes into account their inherent uncertainty, is still lacking. Existing metrics, which typically assess a single output, may fail to capture the variability or potential risks in generation. In this paper, we propose a novel evaluation metric called reliability score based on conformal prediction, which measures the worst-case performance within the prediction set at a pre-specified confidence level. However, computing this score is challenging due to the high-dimensional nature of the output space and the nonconvexity of both the metric function and the prediction set. To efficiently compute this score, we introduce Conformal ReLiability (CReL), a framework that can (i) construct the prediction set with desired coverage; and (ii) accurately optimize the reliability score within the constructed prediction set. We provide theoretical results on coverage and demonstrate empirically that our method produces more informative prediction sets than existing approaches. Experiments on synthetic data and the image-to-text and text-to-image tasks further demonstrate the interpretability of our new metric, and the validity and effectiveness of our computational framework. Source code can be found at https://ggc29.github.io/CReL/.

</details>


### [255] [IRIS: time-structured manifold projections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30810)
*Brian Ondov,Chia-Hsuan Chang,Weipeng Zhou,Xingjian Zhang,Xueqing Peng,Yutong Xie,Huan He,Qiaozhu Mei,Hua Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IRIS is a new Manifold Learning algorithm that structures layouts both chronologically and by manifold topology for dynamic biomedical data visualization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Manifold Learning algorithms like t-SNE and UMAP cannot incorporate time-ordering in their layouts, obscuring the dynamics of cell types or other classes in high-dimensional temporal biomedical data.

Method: IRIS is a Manifold Learning algorithm that structures layouts using both chronological ordering and manifold topology.

Result: IRIS can visualize a wide range of dynamic biomedical data, including scRNA-seq, comparative metagenomics, and literature.

Conclusion: IRIS addresses the limitation of existing algorithms by incorporating temporal information into layouts, enabling better visualization of dynamics in high-dimensional biomedical data.

Abstract: High-dimensional biomedical data, such as cell-by-gene matrices, are increasingly generated temporally. However, Manifold Learning algorithms, like t-SNE and UMAP, cannot incorporate time-ordering in their layouts, obfuscating the dynamics of cell types or other classes. As a solution, we present IRIS, a new Manifold Learning algorithm that structures layouts both chronologically and by manifold topology. IRIS can visualize a wide range of dynamic biomedical data, including scRNA-seq, comparative metagenomics, and literature.

</details>


### [256] [Non-destructive Identification of Oyster Species is possible from Hyperspectral Images with Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30811)
*Ethan Kane Waters,Max Wingfield,Aiden Mellor,Paul Stewart,Iman Tahmasbian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that hyperspectral imaging (HSI) combined with PLS-DA can achieve 100% accuracy in distinguishing Black-Lip rock oysters from Sydney rock oysters non-destructively, outperforming CNN models. The method exploits differences in elemental composition and layer structure between the two species.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a rapid, non-destructive alternative to traditional destructive DNA profiling for differentiating oyster species, which is important for aquaculture development and supply chain traceability.

Method: HSI scanning (950-2515nm) of 156 live oyster samples; PLS-DA and CNN models with Monte Carlo Cross Validation; SEM with EDX for elemental/mineralogical analysis; transmittance analysis.

Result: PLS-DA achieved 100% median test accuracy for both left and right valves, outperforming CNN (83% left, 96% right). BL oysters had more layers (4 vs 2) and higher carbon content, while SR oysters had higher oxygen content in the outer right valve layer.

Conclusion: HSI combined with PLS-DA is an effective rapid, non-destructive methodology for oyster species discrimination, with potential for commercial and traceability applications.

Abstract: Differentiating between oyster species is important for developing new commercial oyster species suited to production systems and is critical for traceability in seafood supply chains. Common methods, such as DNA profiling, are destructive and time consuming. The possibility of using hyperspectral imaging (HSI) for discriminating between Black-Lip rock (BL) and Sydney rock (SR) oysters was investigated. Live BL and SR samples (N = 156) were scanned with a HSI camera (950-2515nm). Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis and Convolutional Neural Networks were trained with Monte Carlo Cross Validation to distinguish BL and SR oysters from the spectral reflectance of their left and rights valves. The PLS-DA model successfully distinguished between the species from both the left and right valves with a median test set classification accuracy of 100%, out performing the CNN with 83% and 96% respectively. Elemental and mineralogical composition in the surface and cross-section of oyster valves were measured with electron microscopy. Analysis of the right valve revealed a greater number of layers in BL compared to SR (4 vs 2). The concentrations of carbon and oxygen varied in the outer layer of the right valves, with BL being rich in carbon and SR being rich in oxygen. The variation in carbon and oxygen concentrations observed between BL and SR right valves may reflect differences in the relative abundance or composition of chitin and glycoproteins. This is supported by model-derived wavelength importance corresponding to vibrational modes of functional groups characteristic of these compounds. Transmittance analysis revealed that light was transmitted through the valves, around the valve edges, indicating that the spectral signatures may have been influenced by the other valve or the meat. Ultimately, the findings highlight an effective rapid, non-destructive methodology for oyster species.

</details>


### [257] [Learning Permutation-invariant Macroscopic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30812)
*Zhichao Han,Mengyi Chen,Qianxiao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a permutation-invariant autoencoder framework for learning macroscopic dynamics from unordered microscopic systems (e.g., particle systems), where the decoder reconstructs mass distributions rather than per-sample points, enabling joint learning of latent states and macroscopic dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard autoencoders for learning low-dimensional latent dynamics assume a fixed ordering of microscopic degrees of freedom, but many systems (e.g., particle systems) are inherently unordered, making permutation-invariant representations necessary for accurate macroscopic modeling.

Method: The method uses a permutation-invariant encoder and a decoder that reconstructs mass distributions centered at observed points (instead of per-sample reconstruction), jointly learning macroscopic dynamics of observables alongside latent states.

Result: The method demonstrates effectiveness and robustness across diverse microscopic settings: energy dynamics in interacting particle systems, mixing dynamics in Lennard-Jones fluids, and stretching dynamics from polymer video data in elongational force fields.

Conclusion: Permutation-invariant autoencoding with mass-distribution reconstruction is a robust approach for learning macroscopic dynamics from unordered microscopic data, outperforming fixed-order methods.

Abstract: Accurately modeling the macroscopic dynamics of high-dimensional microscopic systems is of broad interest across the sciences. Many data-driven approaches learn a low-dimensional latent state through an autoencoder trained for pointwise input reconstruction. These methods typically assume a fixed ordering of microscopic degrees of freedom in the input. However, in many settings, such as particle systems, the microscopic state is inherently unordered. This motivates an autoencoder framework that learns permutation-invariant latent representations. To this end, we adopt a permutation-invariant encoder and design the decoder to reconstruct the mass distribution centered at the observed points rather than per-sample reconstruction. We then jointly learn the macroscopic dynamics of the observables together with the latent states. We demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method across a range of microscopic settings, including learning the energy dynamics in interacting particle systems, predicting mixing dynamics in Lennard-Jones fluids, and modeling the stretching dynamics from video data of polymers moving in an elongational force field.

</details>


### [258] [Unlearning in Diffusion Models: A Unified Framework with KL Divergence and Likelihood Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30825)
*Shervin Khalafi,Alejandro Ribeiro,Dongsheng Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a constrained optimization framework for diffusion model unlearning, using KL divergence and likelihood constraints with proven strong duality and superior retention-unlearning tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unlearning methods suffer from the fundamental conflict between removing undesirable content and preserving model utility. A principled framework is needed to balance these competing objectives.

Method: Formulates three constrained optimization problems using reverse KL, forward KL, and likelihood constraints. Establishes strong duality despite nonconvexity, characterizes optimal solutions theoretically, and develops primal-dual algorithms.

Result: KL-constrained approach outperforms weight-based baselines on retention-unlearning tradeoffs for both concept and data unlearning. Likelihood-based method matches unlearning effectiveness while better preserving retained concepts.

Conclusion: Constrained optimization with KL divergences provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective framework for diffusion model unlearning, with closed-form optimal solutions enabled by strong duality.

Abstract: Unlearning in diffusion models aims to remove undesirable data or concepts while preserving the utility of pretrained models -- two fundamentally conflicting objectives. We propose a principled constrained optimization framework that formulates unlearning as minimizing the deviation from a pretrained model, subject to explicit separation constraints from the unlearning distributions. Specifically, we formulate three constrained optimization problems based on reverse and forward KL divergences, and likelihood constraints. The first two generalize existing approaches for concept and data unlearning, while the third offers a novel and natural formulation for unlearning. Despite the nonconvexity of the KL constraints, we establish strong duality for all three problems, enabling us to explicitly characterize their optimal solutions as unlearning targets and develop primal-dual algorithms for each formulation. Experimental results demonstrate that our KL-constrained approach achieves superior retention-unlearning tradeoffs compared to weight-based baselines for concept and data unlearning, and that our likelihood-based approach matches unlearning effectiveness while better preserving retained concepts compared to baselines.

</details>


### [259] [Cross-Layer Subspace Coupling for LLM Compression: A Unifying Framework and Its Empirical Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30836)
*Snigdha Chandan Khilar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified optimization for SVD-based LLM compression methods (SVD LLM, Basis Sharing) improves weight reconstruction error by up to 46% but fails in practice, causing severe degradation in perplexity and accuracy. The failure occurs because the transformer residual stream decouples layers during forward passes despite mathematical coupling, making per-layer optimality more important than cross-layer optimization. Weight-space reconstruction is a flawed objective for cross-layer compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify recent SVD-based compression methods (SVD LLM, Basis Sharing) under a single optimization framework and evaluate whether cross-layer weight reconstruction can improve compression quality for large language models.

Method: Unifies SVD LLM and Basis Sharing into a single optimization problem; tests on Pythia models comparing weight reconstruction error against downstream task performance.

Result: The unified approach reduces weight reconstruction error by up to 46%, but perplexity and accuracy severely degrade compared to standard per-layer SVD LLM.

Conclusion: Weight-space reconstruction is a flawed objective for cross-layer compression; future methods should focus on per-layer activation reconstruction instead.

Abstract: Recent SVD based compression methods for large language models like SVD LLM and Basis Sharing can be unified under one optimization problem. While mathematical proofs and tests on Pythia models show this unified approach improves weight reconstruction error by up to 46% percent it fails in practical tasks. Downstream metrics like perplexity and accuracy severely degrade compared to standard per layer SVD LLM. The authors explain this failure mechanistically. Although the bundle method mathematically couples adjacent layers the transformer residual stream actually decouples them during forward passes. Thus per layer optimality matters more than joint cross layer optimization. The paper concludes that weight space reconstruction is a flawed objective for cross layer compression and future methods must focus on per layer activation reconstruction instead.

</details>


### [260] [CoMem: Context Management with A Decoupled Long-Context Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30842)
*Yuwei Zhang,Chengyu Dong,Shuowei Jin,Changlong Yu,Hejie Cui,Hongye Jin,Xinyang Zhang,Hamed Bonab,Colin Lockard,Jianshu Chen,Zhenyu Shi,Jingbo Shang,Xian Li,Bing Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoMem is a framework that decouples memory management from the agent workflow, enabling parallel summarization via a k-step-off asynchronous pipeline, achieving 1.4x latency improvement on SWE-Bench-Verified while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing context management for long-horizon tasks incurs substantial decoding overhead due to extra summarization tokens, causing high end-to-end latency. The paper aims to reduce this latency by parallelizing memory management with agent inference.

Method: CoMem introduces (1) a k-step-off asynchronous pipeline that overlaps memory model summarization with agent inference, (2) a reward-driven training strategy to align the memory model for robust performance under asynchrony, and (3) theoretical analysis showing superior efficiency-effectiveness trade-off.

Result: On SWE-Bench-Verified, CoMem achieves 1.4x latency improvement over vanilla long-context solutions while preserving most performance. Latency gains scale favorably with increased system throughput.

Conclusion: CoMem offers a modular path forward for independent optimization of agent reasoning and memory compression, with a superior efficiency-effectiveness trade-off compared to coupled architectures.

Abstract: Context management enables agentic models to solve long-horizon tasks through iterative summarization of previous interaction histories. However, this process typically incurs substantial decoding overhead for the extra summarization tokens, which significantly affect the end-to-end response latency at deployment. In this paper, we introduce CoMem, a novel framework that decouples memory management from the primary agent workflow, enabling these processes to execute in parallel. We propose a $k$-step-off asynchronous pipeline that overlaps the memory model's summarization with the agent's inference, effectively masking the latency of context processing. To ensure robustness under this asynchronous setting, we introduce a reward-driven training strategy that aligns the memory model to capture sufficient statistics for the agent's decision-making. Theoretical analysis confirms that CoMem offers a superior efficiency-effectiveness trade-off compared to coupled architectures. Our extensive experimental results on SWE-Bench-Verified show that CoMem provides 1.4x latency improvements upon vanilla long-context solutions while preserving most of the performance. Furthermore, we demonstrate that these latency gains scale favorably with increased system throughput, offering a modular path forward for the independent optimization of agent reasoning and memory compression.

</details>


### [261] [A Lecture Note on Offline RL and IRL, Part II: Foundations of Inverse Reinforcement Learning and Dynamic Discrete Choice Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30843)
*Enoch Hyunwook Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves the equivalence between structural econometric DDC models and entropy-regularized IRL, then systematically compares classical identification results and computational methods (Rust NFXP, Hotz-Miller CCP, TD approaches) with modern ML/IRL methods (adversarial IRL, occupancy matching, IQ-Learn, offline ML-IRL), concluding with the empirical-risk-minimization framework of Kang et al.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify two separate communities (econometrics and machine learning) working on the same inverse reinforcement learning problem under different names, and to provide a comprehensive comparative analysis of classical and modern approaches for identifying rewards from expert demonstration data.

Method: The paper first proves equivalence between DDC models and entropy-regularized IRL, then systematically reviews and compares classical approaches (Magnac-Thesmar identification, Rust NFXP, Hotz-Miller CCP, linear semi-gradient TD, approximate value iteration) with modern ML methods (adversarial IRL, occupancy matching, IQ-Learn, offline ML-IRL), and finally presents the empirical-risk-minimization framework of Kang et al.

Result: The paper establishes formal equivalence between econometric DDC and ML IRL, identifies key limitations of classical methods (dimensionality, transition-kernel estimation, deadly triad, projected fixed-point bias), and characterizes what each modern method does and does not identify.

Conclusion: The unified framework bridges two research communities and highlights the empirical-risk-minimization approach as a promising gradient-based estimator for offline IRL/DDC that addresses limitations of prior methods.

Abstract: In the forward reinforcement-learning problem, the reward is fixed and known; the learner is asked to find a good policy or value function. Here we turn the question around. Given offline data generated by an expert, can we recover the reward the expert was optimizing? This is the inverse reinforcement learning problem, and remarkably, two communities, structural econometricians studying dynamic discrete choice (DDC) and machine learners studying entropy-regularized IRL, have been working on exactly the same probabilistic model under different names. We begin by proving their equivalence. We then develop the classical identification result of Magnac and Thesmar and the classical computational paradigms that grew out of it: Rust's nested fixed-point algorithm, the conditional-choice-probability approach of Hotz and Miller, and the two temporal-difference approaches of Adusumilli and Eckardt: linear semi-gradient TD and approximate value iteration. Each route has its limits: dimensionality, transition-kernel estimation, the deadly triad, or projected fixed-point bias. We then walk through the modern ML/IRL strand: adversarial IRL, occupancy matching, IQ-Learn, and offline ML-IRL, deriving each method's actual objective and stating precisely what it does and does not identify. We close with the empirical-risk-minimization framework of Kang et al., which yields a gradient-based estimator for offline IRL/DDC.

</details>


### [262] [ForecastCompass: Guiding Agentic Forecasting with Adaptive Factor Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30858)
*Yurui Chang,Yongkang Du,Yuanpu Cao,Jinghui Chen,Lu Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FoCo is a memory framework for agentic forecasting that organizes past experiences into factor memory (reusable predictive dimensions) and reasoning memory (calibration principles), using retrospective analysis to iteratively improve forecast accuracy and calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic forecasting in dynamic environments is challenging due to incomplete, time-limited evidence. Existing agent-memory methods store generic interactions/reflections but lack explicit representation of reusable predictive factors and calibration knowledge needed for forecasting tasks.

Method: FoCo (ForecastCompass) organizes forecasting experience with a hierarchical task taxonomy, maintains factor memory (predictive dimensions) and reasoning memory (probability updating/calibration principles), and iteratively revises memory via a verbalized memory-revision procedure using retrospective analyses as learning signals.

Result: Experiments on Prophet Arena and FutureX with GPT-5-mini and Gemini-2.5-Flash demonstrate that FoCo improves both probabilistic accuracy and calibration compared to baselines.

Conclusion: A specialized factor-based memory framework can effectively transfer forecasting knowledge across tasks, improving agentic forecasting performance in terms of both accuracy and calibration.

Abstract: Agentic forecasting is important for decision-making in dynamic environments, but it remains challenging because agents must reason from incomplete, time-limited evidence and produce calibrated probabilities before outcomes are resolved. Memory provides a natural mechanism for transferring experience from resolved forecasts to future prediction tasks. However, existing agent-memory methods are not tailored to forecasting, as they typically store past interactions, reflections, or factual associations without explicitly representing reusable predictive factors or calibration knowledge. We propose ForecastCompass (FoCo), an adaptive factor-based memory framework for agentic forecasting. FoCo organizes forecasting experience with a hierarchical forecasting-task taxonomy, enabling retrieval task-relevant forecasting knowledge. It maintains two complementary memory components: factor memory, which captures reusable predictive dimensions, and reasoning memory, which encodes probability updating, uncertainty handling, and calibration principles. Using retrospective analyses as learning signals, FoCo iteratively revises memory through a verbalized memory-revision procedure, enabling the agent to accumulate transferable forecasting knowledge over time. Experiments on Prophet Arena and FutureX with GPT-5-mini and Gemini-2.5-Flash show that FoCo improves both probabilistic accuracy and calibration.

</details>


### [263] [DARTS: Distribution-Aware Active Rollout Trajectory Shaping for Accelerating LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30859)
*Yujie Wang,Siwei Chen,Longzan Luo,Xinyi Liu,Xupeng Miao,Fangcheng Fu,Bin Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes active distribution shaping to address rollout efficiency bottlenecks in RL caused by long-tail response length distributions, achieving up to 1.77x acceleration by reducing ineffective verbosity through distribution-aware trajectory sampling and adaptive redundancy allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL systems suffer from rollout efficiency bottlenecks due to long-tail response length distributions. Current works only mitigate this via prompt-level tail scheduling, but fail to address the root cause—the distribution itself, particularly intra-prompt long tails consisting of ineffective verbosity.

Method: The paper introduces active distribution shaping with two components: (1) a distribution-aware trajectory sampling mechanism that selects trajectories from redundant exploration space for each prompt, and (2) an adaptive redundancy allocation scheme that maximizes both shaping effectiveness and system efficiency.

Result: Experiments demonstrate up to 1.77x acceleration over state-of-the-art systems without compromising model performance.

Conclusion: By fundamentally resolving tail-induced overheads through active distribution shaping (rather than just managing tail effects), the proposed paradigm achieves significant speedups while maintaining model quality, offering a new direction for RL efficiency optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become pivotal for improving model capabilities yet suffers from rollout efficiency bottlenecks due to the long-tail response length distribution. While existing works mitigate the impact of long tails via prompt-level tail scheduling, we focus on the root source of inefficiency: the distribution itself. Specifically, we characterize the long-tail distribution at a finer granularity, identifying intra-prompt long tails, and revealing that they frequently consist of ineffective verbosity. To address this, we propose a novel paradigm of active distribution shaping to shape the rollout distribution towards conciseness and certainty, thereby fundamentally resolving tail-induced overheads. We achieve this through a distribution-aware trajectory sampling mechanism, which selects trajectories from a redundant exploration space for each prompt, and an adaptive redundancy allocation scheme to maximize both shaping effectiveness and system efficiency. Experiments demonstrate significant acceleration over state-of-the-art systems by up to 1.77x without compromising model performance.

</details>


### [264] [Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior for Personalized User Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30873)
*Jabin Koo,Hoyoung Kim,Minwoo Jang,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedVPA-GP is a federated learning framework that uses variational inference with Gumbel-Softmax prior and orthogonal loss to disentangle conflicting user preferences (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness) in LLM alignment without compromising privacy, outperforming monolithic baselines on HH-RLHF.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL frameworks for LLM alignment use a monolithic reward model that averages conflicting user preferences. Variational Preference Learning (VPL) could enable personalization but suffers from posterior collapse in decentralized settings due to local data scarcity and heterogeneity.

Method: (1) Federated Mixture Prior: clients leverage aggregate population distribution as a dynamic prior to stabilize variational inference; (2) Orthogonal Loss: explicitly enforces separation of preference prototypes in latent space; (3) Gumbel-Softmax prior for discrete preference modeling.

Result: On HH-RLHF dataset, FedVPA-GP significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, successfully disentangles conflicting user intents, and enables dynamic preference switching.

Conclusion: FedVPA-GP effectively addresses the posterior collapse problem in decentralized VPL, enabling preference disentanglement and personalization in federated LLM alignment while preserving privacy.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving pathway for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs); however, existing frameworks typically enforce a monolithic reward model, inevitably averaging out inherently conflicting user preferences (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness). While Variational Preference Learning (VPL) offers a pathway to personalization, adapting it to decentralized settings presents a fundamental challenge: posterior collapse driven by severe local data scarcity and heterogeneity. In this paper, we propose Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior (FedVPA-GP), a framework designed to disentangle diverse preferences without compromising privacy. To stabilize variational inference, we introduce a Federated Mixture Prior that enables clients to leverage the aggregate population distribution as a dynamic prior. Furthermore, we incorporate an Orthogonal Loss that explicitly enforces the separation of preference prototypes in the latent space. Experiments on the HH-RLHF dataset demonstrate that FedVPA-GP significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, successfully disentangling conflicting user intents and enabling dynamic preference switching.

</details>


### [265] [GlucoFM: A Dual-Stream Foundation Model for Continuous Glucose Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30865)
*Zechen Li,Keerthana Natarajan,Weizhi Zhang,Menglian Zhou,Simon A. Lee,Yuwei Zhang,Maxwell A. Xu,Zeinab Esmaeilpour,Flora D. Salim,Mark Malhotra,Lindsey Sunden,Shwetak Patel,Yuzhe Yang,Ahmed A. Metwally*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlucoFM is a lightweight CGM foundation model that decomposes glucose dynamics into slow physiological state and transient event streams, pretrained on 109,066 hours of data, and achieves state-of-the-art results across seven clinical prediction tasks with 4.1 PR-AUC improvement over prior CGM-specific models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generic time-series and CGM-specific foundation models encode glucose traces as entangled single-stream sequences, leaving the distinct temporal structure (slow physiological baselines vs. transient events) of glycemic dynamics only implicitly modeled.

Method: GlucoFM aligns irregular CGM recordings to a 24-hour chronological grid, preserves observation masks, decomposes glucose dynamics into slow physiological state and transient event streams, and is pretrained on 109,066 hours of unlabeled data from 477 subjects using masked contextual latent prediction and temporal dynamics prediction.

Result: Across four cohorts and seven clinical tasks, GlucoFM achieves the best linear-probing performance, improving average PR-AUC by 4.1 points over prior best CGM-specific model. It leads on all diabetes-risk and β-cell dysfunction tasks and 3 of 4 insulin-resistance tasks, with strong cross-dataset transfer and few-shot adaptation.

Conclusion: Physiology-aware decomposition (slow state + transient event streams) serves as an effective inductive bias for transferable CGM representation learning, yielding consistent gains especially in core metabolic outcome prediction and multi-day aggregation settings.

Abstract: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) provides a dense view of daily metabolic physiology, yet existing generic time-series and CGM-specific foundation models often encode glucose traces as entangled single-stream sequences, leaving the distinct temporal structure of glycemic dynamics only implicitly modeled. We present GlucoFM, a lightweight CGM foundation model that aligns irregular recordings to a 24-hour chronological grid, preserves observation masks, and decomposes glucose dynamics into slow physiological state and transient event streams, capturing low-frequency glycemic baselines and short-term deviations that may reflect acute physiological responses or sensor artifacts. GlucoFM is pretrained on 109,066 hours of unlabeled CGM recordings from 477 subjects with two complementary objectives: masked contextual latent prediction over fused daily representations and temporal dynamics prediction over state and event streams. Across four diverse cohorts and seven clinical prediction tasks, GlucoFM achieves the strongest subject-disjoint linear-probing performance among evaluated baselines, improving average PR-AUC by 4.1 points over the best CGM-specific foundation model. Its gains are most pronounced on core metabolic outcomes, leading PR-AUC on all diabetes-risk and $β$-cell dysfunction tasks and on 3 of 4 insulin-resistance tasks. GlucoFM also achieves the best overall cross-dataset transfer performance and strong few-shot adaptation among evaluated methods, and consistent gains when aggregating multiple days for subject-level prediction, highlighting physiology-aware decomposition as an effective inductive bias for transferable CGM representation learning.

</details>


### [266] [Inverse Reinforcement Learning without an Optimal Demonstrator: A Feasible Reward Set Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30903)
*Kihyun Kim,Shripad Deshmukh,Nikos Vlassis,Jiawei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a feasible-reward-set framework for inverse reinforcement learning from multiple imperfect demonstrators with heterogeneous suboptimality levels, providing theoretical guarantees on reward recovery and demonstrating effectiveness in grid-world and LLM fine-tuning settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard IRL assumes single optimal demonstrator, but real-world data often comes from multiple imperfect demonstrators with varying suboptimality levels. Existing methods struggle to handle this heterogeneity and reward ambiguity.

Method: For each demonstrator, encode suboptimality level as a linear constraint; intersect feasible reward sets across demonstrators. Provide theoretical analysis on monotonic shrinkage, characterization of when new demonstrators tighten the set, and two recovery guarantees. Introduce practical strategies for addressing reward ambiguity and an offline algorithm with function approximation.

Result: Joint feasible set shrinks monotonically with added data. Two recovery guarantees established: one based on closeness to optimal occupancy, another requiring sufficient coverage with no near-optimal demonstrator. Experiments in tabular grid-world and LLM fine-tuning confirm theoretical predictions and show superiority over baselines.

Conclusion: The feasible-reward-set framework effectively handles multiple imperfect demonstrators with heterogeneous suboptimality, providing both theoretical guarantees and practical algorithms for reward learning in this realistic setting.

Abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) typically assumes demonstrations from a single optimal demonstrator, but in many applications data come from multiple imperfect demonstrators with heterogeneous suboptimality levels. We study reward learning in this setting through a feasible-reward-set framework: for each demonstrator, we encode its declared suboptimality level as a linear constraint and intersect the resulting feasible sets across demonstrators. Our theoretical analysis shows that the joint feasible set shrinks monotonically as data are added, and we give an exact characterization of when a new demonstrator strictly tightens it. We further establish two recovery guarantees for the feasible reward set of the ground-truth optimal demonstrator: one bound depends on closeness to the optimal occupancy, while the other requires only sufficient coverage and no near-optimal demonstrator. On the practical side, we introduce strategies to address the inherent reward ambiguity in the obtained reward set and provide an offline algorithm with function approximation for high-dimensional environments. Experiments in tabular grid-world and large language model (LLM) fine-tuning settings are consistent with the theoretical predictions and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework over baselines.

</details>


### [267] [Bandwidth Allocation with Device Partitioning for Federated Learning over Industrial IoT networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30892)
*Kangmin Kim,Jaeyoung Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel bandwidth allocation policy for federated learning in IIoT that partitions devices into ordered subsets with exclusive full-bandwidth access, proving it reduces training time and energy consumption compared to any non-partitioning scheme.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication time is a primary bottleneck in FL systems for IIoT, and conventional bandwidth allocation approaches (designed for individual QoS) are suboptimal for FL's collective goal of efficient global model convergence.

Method: The policy partitions selected devices into ordered subsets, granting each subset exclusive full-bandwidth access sequentially rather than distributing bandwidth among all devices simultaneously. Formal proof shows this achieves strictly lower training time than any non-partitioning scheme.

Result: Experiments on GC10-Det (industrial surface defect benchmark) and CIFAR-10 demonstrate consistent reductions in training time and energy consumption compared to existing schemes, approaching the theoretical lower bound on round time.

Conclusion: The partitioning-based bandwidth allocation policy effectively minimizes total training time and energy consumption in FL for IIoT, exploiting device heterogeneity and outperforming all non-partitioning approaches.

Abstract: We consider a federated learning (FL) system in which Industrial Internet-of-Things (IIoT) devices collaboratively train a global model over wireless channels without sharing local data. In such systems, communication time is a primary bottleneck that constrains overall training efficiency. Unlike conventional networks that prioritize individual quality-of-service requirements, FL systems collectively aim to converge to an optimal global model as efficiently as possible, which calls for a fundamentally different approach to bandwidth allocation. In this paper, we propose a novel bandwidth allocation policy that exploits the heterogeneity of device computing capabilities to minimize total training time. Rather than distributing bandwidth among all selected devices simultaneously, the proposed policy partitions the participating devices into ordered subsets and sequentially grants each subset exclusive access to the full bandwidth. We formally prove that this partitioning-based policy achieves a strictly lower training time than any bandwidth allocation scheme without partitioning, irrespective of the underlying scheduling algorithm. Furthermore, by reducing per-device transmission duration, the proposed policy also minimizes uplink energy consumption, which is particularly beneficial for battery-constrained IIoT devices. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets - including GC10-Det, an industrial surface defect benchmark, and CIFAR-10, a standard image classification benchmark - demonstrate that the proposed policy consistently reduces training time and energy consumption compared to existing bandwidth allocation schemes, approaching the theoretical lower bound on round time.

</details>


### [268] [De-attribute to Forget for LLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30919)
*Xinyang Lu,Jiabao Pan,Rachael Hwee Ling Sim,See-Kiong Ng,Anthony Kum Hoe Tung,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DareU is a novel LLM unlearning framework that uses reinforcement learning with data attribution rewards to zero out attribution to forget data, outperforming existing loss-based methods in balancing forget quality and model utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM unlearning methods rely on optimizing prediction losses (e.g., maximizing loss on forget set), which cause over-forgetting and poor model utility. The authors aim to address these issues by framing unlearning as zeroing out data attribution instead.

Method: Proposes DareU, the first LLM unlearning framework based on data attribution rewards. It uses reinforcement learning to update the LLM by reducing attribution scores of generated responses to forget data owners. An LLM classifier approximates attribution efficiently.

Result: DareU outperforms existing baselines, achieving effective unlearning while balancing forget quality and model utility well.

Conclusion: Framing unlearning as zeroing out data attribution via reinforcement learning is a promising alternative to loss-based optimization, effectively mitigating over-forgetting and utility degradation.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns on the use of inappropriate data for training, which has led to a growing interest in LLM unlearning. Many existing LLM unlearning approaches rely on optimizing prediction loss(es), such as maximizing the loss on the forget set, but often face critical issues like over-forgetting and poor model utility. To address them, this paper novelly frames the optimization objective for LLM unlearning as one of zeroing out data attribution instead. In particular, we propose the first LLM unlearning framework based on data attribution rewards called DareU that performs reinforcement learning to update the LLM by reducing the attribution score of its generated responses (i.e., de-attributing) to the forget data owners. Empirical evaluation using an LLM classifier as an efficient approximation of attribution shows that DareU outperforms existing baselines by achieving effective unlearning while balancing forget quality and model utility well.

</details>


### [269] [Zero Collapse: A Failure Mode of Policy Gradient Methods in Discontinuous Reward Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30896)
*Nishant Kumar,Enrique Areyan Viqueira,Amy Greenwald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies "zero collapse" in RL for repeated auctions, where agents get trapped in zero-reward regions due to discontinuous reward landscapes, and proposes mitigation strategies for actor-critic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bidding in repeated auctions is challenging for RL due to discontinuous, cliff-like reward landscapes (e.g., first-price auctions), where standard policy gradient and value-based methods struggle.

Method: The authors analyze the "zero collapse" failure mode where stochastic exploration and gradient updates cause policies to overshoot optimal regions into zero-reward zones, trapping the agent. They examine actor-critic susceptibility due to biased value estimates.

Result: They provide: (1) mechanistic explanation of discontinuous rewards leading to vanishing signals; (2) analysis of policy stochasticity and step size interaction; (3) empirical demonstrations across REINFORCE and actor-critic variants.

Conclusion: Proposes practical mitigation strategies (initialization and architectural choices) and introduces a formal RL framework highlighting unique structural properties of auction environments.

Abstract: Bidding in repeated auctions is a central challenge for reinforcement learning (RL), combining continuous control with the strategic complexities of digital advertising. While policy gradient and value-based methods seem well-suited for these settings, they often struggle with the discontinuous, "cliff-like" nature of auction reward landscapes. In a first-price auction, for example, a bidder receives zero reward until they cross a specific threshold, after which the reward decreases as the bid increases. This creates a landscape of flat, zero-reward regions separated by sharp boundaries.
  We identify a fundamental failure mode in this setting termed "zero collapse." We show that stochastic exploration and gradient-based updates can cause policies to overshoot optimal high-reward regions and enter flat, zero-reward regimes. Once there, the lack of an informative gradient signal makes recovery extremely sample-inefficient, effectively trapping the agent. We find that actor-critic methods are particularly susceptible, as biased value estimates can accelerate this movement toward unstable regions.
  Our contributions include: (1) a mechanistic explanation of how discontinuous rewards lead to vanishing signals and zero collapse; (2) an analysis of the interaction between policy stochasticity and step size; and (3) an empirical demonstration of this phenomenon across REINFORCE and actor-critic variants. We propose practical mitigation strategies involving initialization and architectural choices to improve stability. Finally, we introduce a formal RL framework for auction environments highlighting their unique structural properties.

</details>


### [270] [Density-Guided Robust Counterfactual Explanations on Tabular Data under Model Multiplicity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30901)
*Jun Tan,Qing Guo,Zicheng Xu,Jinglin Li,Qi Fang,Ning Gui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DensityFlow is a generative framework using Neural ODEs and density scores to produce robust counterfactual explanations that stay within high-confidence data regions, with a local proxy distillation for black-box settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactual explanations often fail in low-density regions due to high classifier variance; existing ensemble methods are computationally expensive.

Method: Models counterfactual generation as continuous-time Neural ODE dynamics guided by a differentiable density score (learned via Noise Contrastive Estimation with a K+1-way discriminator). For black-box models, uses local proxy distillation along the CE trajectory.

Result: Superior validity under model multiplicity with significantly reduced query costs compared to ensemble-based baselines.

Conclusion: DensityFlow provides a query-efficient, reliable approach for generating robust counterfactual explanations by actively avoiding uncertain low-density regions.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CEs) are essential for actionable recourse, yet their reliability is often compromised in low-density regions, where classifiers exhibit high variance. Unlike existing methods that rely on expensive ensemble intersections to define stability, we propose \textit{DensityFlow}, a generative framework that constructs robust CEs by adhering to the high-confidence data manifold. Specifically, we model the counterfactual generation as continuous-time dynamics parameterized by Neural ODE, guided by a differentiable density score to actively avoid uncertain, low-density areas. This density score is learned via Noise Contrastive Estimation, effectively leveraging a $(K{+}1)$-way discriminator to estimate density ratios. For black-box settings, we introduce a local proxy distillation mechanism that aligns a lightweight surrogate with the target model strictly within the trajectory of CE generation, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization with minimal queries. Experiments demonstrate that \textit{DensityFlow} achieves superior validity under model multiplicity while significantly reducing query costs compared to ensemble-based baselines. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/G-AILab/DensityFlow.

</details>


### [271] [DEM: A Distilled Explanation Model for Interpretable Anomaly Detection in Physiological Sensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31007)
*Jyotirmoy Singh,Anushka Roy,Shreea Bose,Chittaranjan Hota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DEM is a three-stage glass-box framework that distills gradient boosting knowledge into interpretable decision trees for anomaly detection in WBANs, achieving high accuracy (0.9964 AUC) and fast inference (0.17ms/1000 samples) with built-in explanation fidelity metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomaly detection in WBAN physiological data requires both high accuracy and interpretability, but existing models sacrifice one for the other—black-box models lack transparency and post-hoc methods like SHAP are computationally expensive approximations.

Method: DEM is a three-stage framework: (1) a linear baseline model, (2) a gradient boosting (XGBoost) expert capturing non-linear residuals, and (3) a shallow decision tree distilled from the expert's residuals, producing inherently interpretable if-then rules with a novel fidelity metric.

Result: 0.9964 AUC on clinical anomaly detection, 0.9047 AUC on stress detection, 0.17ms inference per 1000 samples (1235x faster than SHAP), with controllable depth and explicit accuracy-interpretability trade-off.

Conclusion: DEM provides a principled, real-time capable, and intrinsically interpretable alternative to black-box models and post-hoc explanations, with a unique fidelity metric for trustworthiness assessment.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in physiological sensor data from Wireless Body Area Networks (WBANs) can be caused by sensor faults, network disruptions, or missing data, leading to false alarms. Hence, it demands both high predictive accuracy and clinically interpretable explanations. Existing approaches rely either on black-box models that achieve strong performance but offer no transparency, or on post-prediction explanation methods such as SHAP and LIME. In this paper, we propose the Distilled Explanation Model (DEM), a three-stage glass-box framework that distills the non-linear knowledge of a gradient boosting expert into an interpretable decision tree operating on residuals relative to a linear baseline, so that the explanation is not an approximation but the prediction itself. DEM introduces a novel distillation fidelity metric that quantifies how faithfully the explanation tree captures the expert model's non-linear contribution, providing a principled measure of explanation trustworthiness absent from prior interpretable models. Evaluated across four physiological datasets, including MIMIC-IV, WESAD, eICU, and an in-house SmartNet WBAN corpus, DEM achieves an AUC of 0.9964 on clinical contextual anomaly detection and 0.9047 on wearable stress detection while producing human-readable if-then rules at a controllable depth. Inference requires 0.17ms per 1000 samples, rendering DEM 1235x faster than SHAP-based post-hoc explanation and suitable for real-time physiological monitoring. Ablation studies confirm that the XGBoost distillation step provides measurable gains over naive residual fitting, and depth-sensitivity analysis demonstrates an explicit, user-controlled accuracy-interpretability trade-off unique to DEM among existing intrinsically interpretable models.

</details>


### [272] [PINNs Failure Modes are Overfitting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30910)
*Nigel T. Andersen,Takashi Matsubara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs fail on certain PDEs due to overfitting to collocation points, and regularization with extended double backpropagation resolves this, achieving SOTA results with fewer points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs are known to fail on simple equations despite low residual loss, and these failure modes have been extensively studied but not well understood.

Method: Visualizing the residual directly to identify overfitting, applying regularization, and extending double backpropagation over the full set of residuals.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on four standard failure mode equations with up to 23× fewer collocation points using a vanilla architecture.

Conclusion: Failure modes in PINNs are due to overfitting on collocation points, and regularization/improved loss design can eliminate them.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a common class of machine learning-based partial differential equation (PDE) solvers which train a network to represent a solution by minimizing a residual loss that encodes the PDE. Despite their successes, they are known to fail on certain simple equations, converging to an incorrect solution despite low loss.
