These failure modes have garnered significant attention in the literature over the past several years, motivating both architectural and optimization based solutions. By directly visualizing the residual, we show that failure modes are the result of overfitting: the loss is minimized on the collocation points, but not elsewhere. Applying regularization causes the failure modes to vanish. Finally, we extend double backpropagation over the full set of residuals, and use it to achieve state-of-the-art performance on four standard failure mode equations with up to $23\times$ fewer collocation points and a vanilla architecture.

</details>


### [273] [Annealed Softmax Greedy in Many-Armed Bayesian Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31034)
*William Overman,Mohsen Bayati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper theoretically analyzes why reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and GRPO can work without explicit uncertainty tracking, by showing that an annealed softmax policy in a many-armed bandit achieves near-optimal Bayes regret under a "β-regularity" condition (abundance of near-optimal arms), providing an analogy to RLVR where a base policy with non-negligible correct completion probability plays a similar role.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why RLVR and GRPO methods are effective despite not having explicit mechanisms to track epistemic uncertainty, providing a theoretical explanation for their success.

Method: The paper studies an annealed softmax (Boltzmann) policy in a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit setting. It analyzes Bayes regret under a linear upper-tail condition on the prior (β=1 case of β-regularity), proving regret bounds and comparing with empirical-mean greedy policy.

Result: Under β-regularity, the annealed softmax greedy achieves Bayes regret Õ(m + T/m) and Õ(√T) when m = Θ(√T), which is near-optimal. Under many arms, softmax tends to sample near-optimal arms rather than clearly inferior ones. However, with a small number of arms, the same policy can suffer linear regret.

Conclusion: The abundance of near-optimal arms (β-regularity) is key to the effectiveness of uncertainty-agnostic softmax updates. This provides a structural analogy to RLVR: a base policy with non-negligible probability of correct completions plays the role of β-regularity.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and group-based policy optimization methods such as GRPO update a stochastic policy by sampling multiple completions per prompt and increasing the policy's probability on those with higher reward, regularized by a KL penalty toward a reference policy. These updates do not include explicit mechanisms that track epistemic uncertainty. This paper studies a stylized explanation for why such uncertainty-agnostic updates can nevertheless be effective. We analyze an annealed softmax (Boltzmann) policy that selects actions according to a softmax of empirical mean rewards in a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit. Under a linear upper-tail condition on the prior (the $β=1$ case of $β$-regularity), which implies an abundance of near-optimal arms, we prove that annealed softmax greedy achieves Bayes regret $\tilde{O}(m + T/m)$, and in particular $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ when the number of arms scales as $m = Θ(\sqrt{T})$. This is the near-optimal Bayes regret rate in this regime, attained also by empirical-mean greedy. Under $β$-regularity, many arms maintain empirical means close to the optimum throughout learning, so when softmax samples an arm other than the empirically best, that arm tends to be another near-optimal one rather than a clearly inferior one. By contrast, with a small number of arms, the same kind of softmax policy can suffer linear regret. The result also provides a structural analogy to RLVR, where a base policy with a non-negligible probability of producing a correct completion plays the role of $β$-regularity.

</details>


### [274] [Automating Formal Verification with Reinforcement Learning and Recursive Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30914)
*Max Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This thesis explores using verifier environments to improve LLM-generated verified programs and proofs through RLVR and verifier-guided search. In Dafny, RLVR improved verified pass rates but revealed specification hacking; filtering and multi-turn training raised pass rate from 9.7% to 31.1%. In Lean, verifier-guided scaffolding improved pass rates from 46.2% to 69.2% and solved 7 previously unsolved VERINA tasks. A new Lean benchmark (Dalek-Bench) was introduced but results remain weak.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated formal verification is difficult for LLMs due to scarce training data for proof assistants and the requirement for machine-checkable correctness rather than plausible code. This motivates developing methods that leverage verifier feedback to improve LLM generation of verified programs and proofs.

Method: (1) RLVR using GRPO and variants: train open-source Dafny models by assembling generated candidates and scoring with compiler/verifier outcomes, with multi-turn RLVR on a refined benchmark. (2) Verifier-guided inference scaffold in Lean: structured search over decomposed subgoals with verifier feedback, diagnostics, and repair, using proof revisers. Also introduced Dalek-Bench as a repository-scale Lean benchmark.

Result: Dafny: RLVR increased verified reward from 2.2% to 58.1% initially; after filtering, multi-turn RLVR improved pass rate from 9.7% to 31.1%. Lean: scaffold improved pass rate from 46.2% to 69.2% (pilot), solved 7 of 42 previously unsolved VERINA tasks. Dalek-Bench results remained weak.

Conclusion: Verifier feedback is effective for improving LLM-generated verified programs and proofs, but challenges like specification hacking remain. Stronger progress evaluation and task-specific tool-use policies are needed, especially for repository-scale verification tasks like Dalek-Bench.

Abstract: Automated formal verification remains challenging for large language models because data for proof assistants and verification-aware languages is scarce, and correctness depends on satisfying precise machine-checkable specifications rather than producing plausible code. This thesis studies how verifier environments can improve LLM generation of verified programs and proofs through reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) and verifier-guided inference-time search. First, we train open-source models in Dafny with RLVR using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and related variants, assembling generated candidates into complete programs and scoring them with compiler and verifier outcomes. Initial experiments on an APPS-derived Dafny dataset increased verified reward from 2.2% to 58.1%, but revealed specification hacking, where models exploit weak formal specifications instead of implementing the intended solutions. After filtering underspecified and vulnerable tasks, multi-turn RLVR on the refined benchmark improves the verified pass rate from 9.7% to 31.1%. Second, we develop a verifier-guided inference scaffold in Lean that treats proof generation as structured search over decomposed subgoals, verifier feedback, diagnostics, and repair. With a fixed base model, the full scaffold with proof reviser improves pass rate on an initial VeriCoding pilot set from 46.2% under direct repair to 69.2%. On the larger VERINA dataset, whole-task decomposition plus proof reviser solves 7 of 42 previously unsolved tasks. We also introduce Dalek-Bench, a repository-scale Lean benchmark derived from the Rust $\texttt{curve25519-dalek}$ verification project; preliminary results remain weak, indicating that stronger progress evaluation and task-specific tool-use policies are still needed.

</details>


### [275] [Welfare, Improvability, and Variance: A Principal-Agent Approach to Optimal Benchmark Item Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30916)
*Andreas Haupt,Justin Hartenstein,Anka Reuel,Mykel Kochenderfer,Sanmi Koyejo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper models AI benchmarking as a principal-agent game, showing that uniform averaging of item scores leads to welfare loss. It proposes auditing benchmarks along three dimensions—welfare alignment, marginal improvability, and performance variance—and demonstrates the framework on OLMES items.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark aggregation has received far less attention than other limitations like contamination and saturation. The standard practice of uniformly averaging item scores implicitly treats all items as equally valuable, which may misrepresent model capabilities and lead to suboptimal benchmarking.

Method: The authors model benchmarking as a multitask principal-agent game, identifying three item-level primitives that jointly determine welfare loss: alignment with normative welfare priorities, marginal improvability, and performance variance. They translate this into an audit framework that ranks items along these axes, applying it to OLMES using WORKBank, EvoLM 4B, and PolyPythias 410M.

Result: The framework successfully surfaces Pareto-inferior items within OLMES under a pro-worker welfare operationalization, demonstrating that certain benchmark items are suboptimal and could be identified systematically.

Conclusion: Benchmark aggregation matters significantly; moving beyond uniform averaging by considering welfare alignment, improvability, and variance can improve benchmarking practices. The authors provide an audit framework and open-source code for others to apply.

Abstract: AI benchmarks have well-documented limitations, with prior work examining contamination, saturation, and construct underspecification. Aggregation has received far less attention: benchmarks are typically summarized by uniformly averaging item-level scores, implicitly treating every test item as equally valuable. We model benchmarking as a multitask principal-agent game and show that the welfare loss from a benchmark is determined jointly by three item-level primitives: alignment with normative welfare priorities, marginal improvability, and performance variance. We translate the theory into an audit framework that ranks items along each of these three axes, and apply it to OLMES items using WORKBank for welfare, the EvoLM 4B suite for improvability, and the PolyPythias 410M panel for variance. The framework surfaces items that are Pareto-inferior within OLMES subject to a pro-worker welfare operationalization. All code is available at https://github.com/stair-lab/principal-agent-benchmarks.

</details>


### [276] [Learning to Solve and Optimize by Evolving Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31049)
*Veronika Semmelrock,Benedetta Strizzolo,Francesco Zuccato,Gerhard Friedrich,Patrick Rodler,Konstantin Schekotihin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHECKMATE uses code evolution guided by formal specifications and natural language to automatically generate algorithms that outperform state-of-the-art solvers on industrial combinatorial optimization problems, eliminating the need for expert-crafted solution strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combinatorial/optimization problems require careful formalization, specialized solvers, and expert-designed heuristics, forcing experts to specify both what solutions are and how they are derived, which is labor-intensive and expertise-dependent.

Method: CHECKMATE introduces algorithm generation via code evolution: a formal specification ensures solution correctness and enables performance evaluation (the what), while a natural language description guides the evolutionary process, removing the need to specify the how.

Result: On selected industrial configuration and scheduling problems, evolved algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art solvers across all cases.

Conclusion: Formal methods can effectively guide code evolution for automatically solving complex real-world problems, representing a paradigm shift in how industrial optimization challenges are addressed.

Abstract: Combinatorial and optimization problems are fundamental to many industrial AI applications. Solving large-scale real-world instances of such problems typically requires careful problem formalization, specialized solvers, and expert-designed heuristics. Thus, experts need to specify not only what solutions are, but also how they are derived. By introducing the tool CHECKMATE, we show that algorithm generation via code evolution represents a paradigm shift by eliminating the need to formulate the how. CHECKMATE solely relies on the what. Specifically, a formal specification ensures solutions' correctness and enables systematic performance evaluation of the generated programs, while a natural language description guides the evolutionary process. The effectiveness of our method is demonstrated on selected problems from two industrial domains: configuration and scheduling. In all cases, the evolved algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art solvers. This underscores the potential of formal methods in guiding code evolution for automatically solving complex real-world problems.

</details>


### [277] [STEP: Learning STructured Embeddings for Progressive Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31061)
*Lucas Thil,Jesse Read,Rim Kaddah,Guillaume Doquet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-supervised contrastive method learns interpretable latent representations of progressive time series, using polar coordinates (θ, r) to encode state progression and operational mode, achieving competitive performance on end-state prediction, forecasting, and phase separation across diverse domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Progressive time series (e.g., degradation, task completion) are common but often modeled with black-box approaches that lack interpretability. Existing methods struggle to disentangle state progression from operating conditions without proxy labels.

Method: Self-supervised contrastive learning to embed observations into a low-dimensional latent space anchored between two orthogonal prototype vectors. The latent space geometry yields polar coordinates: θ tracks state progression (e.g., healthy→failed), r identifies active mode (e.g., operating condition). No proxy labels required.

Result: Evaluated on industrial degradation, robotic tasks, and neural activity. Matches or improves over black-box counterparts on end-state prediction, multi-step forecasting, and interpretable phase separation. A linear regressor on latent compass coordinates competes with deep architectures.

Conclusion: The latent compass provides geometrically accessible, interpretable state encoding without sacrificing predictive performance. The method offers transparency into underlying mechanisms while being competitive with black-box models.

Abstract: We present a novel method for learning interpretable representations of progressive time series, that is, data capturing irreversible state transitions such as degradation or task completion. Our approach uses a self-supervised contrastive objective to learn a low-dimensional latent space whose geometry is itself the interpretation: each observation becomes a point on a manifold anchored between two fixed orthogonal prototype vectors, and a trajectory becomes a path across that manifold. From this structure we read a latent compass, the polar coordinates (θ, r) of the latent vector, in which θ tracks the progression of the underlying state (e.g., from healthy to failed) and r identifies the active mode (e.g., the operating condition), without any proxy labels. We evaluate the approach against the state of the art on diverse domains, including industrial degradation, robotic tasks, and neural activity, validating three key capabilities: (1) end-state prediction, (2) multi-step forecasting, and (3) interpretable phase separation. Our method matches or improves over black-box counterparts on all of these while providing transparency about the underlying mechanisms. A simple linear regressor on top of the latent compass coordinates is competitive with deep architectures, direct quantitative evidence that the underlying state is encoded in a geometrically accessible form.

</details>


### [278] [Unsupervised Diffusion Solver for Combinatorial Optimization via Combinatorial Adjoint Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30920)
*Shengyu Feng,Tarun Suresh,Yiming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAM is an unsupervised training framework for diffusion-based combinatorial optimization solvers that uses discrete adjoint dynamics to propagate optimization signals through CTMC-based diffusion trajectories, outperforming existing unsupervised methods and matching supervised approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization require supervised training with large collections of near-optimal solutions, which is expensive and limits applicability.

Method: Formulates diffusion-based CO as a stochastic control problem over Continuous-Time Markov Chains (CTMCs), introduces discrete adjoint dynamics for propagating optimization signals, and proposes Combinatorial Adjoint Matching (CAM) for unsupervised training with structured, low-variance trajectory-level signals.

Result: CAM consistently outperforms existing unsupervised diffusion baselines and achieves performance competitive with supervised diffusion solvers and traditional solvers across diverse CO problems.

Conclusion: Adjoint-based trajectory optimization can be effectively extended to discrete combinatorial domains, enabling unsupervised training of diffusion solvers that rival supervised approaches.

Abstract: Diffusion-based neural solvers have shown strong promise for combinatorial optimization (CO), but existing methods typically rely on supervised training with large collections of near-optimal solutions. In this work, we extend adjoint-based trajectory optimization methods to discrete combinatorial domains. We formulate diffusion-based CO as a stochastic control problem over Continuous-Time Markov Chains and introduce discrete adjoint dynamics for propagating optimization signals through discrete generative trajectories. Building on this formulation, we propose Combinatorial Adjoint Matching (CAM), an unsupervised training framework for discrete diffusion solvers with structured and low-variance trajectory-level optimization signals. Empirically, CAM consistently outperforms existing unsupervised diffusion baselines and achieves performance competitive with strong supervised diffusion solvers and even traditional solvers across diverse combinatorial optimization problems. Our code is available at https://github.com/Shengyu-Feng/CAM.

</details>


### [279] [Local linear convergence of gradient methods for overparameterized Gaussian mixtures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30936)
*Jingxing Wang,Vasileios Charisopoulos,Maryam Fazel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that slow convergence in overparameterized Gaussian mixture models can be overcome by alternating short gradient descent steps and long Polyak steps, achieving linear convergence rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overparameterization in Gaussian mixture models is needed to avoid spurious local optima and enable global recovery, but it dramatically slows down local convergence rates. The authors aim to show this slow convergence is not intrinsic and can be overcome.

Method: The method alternates between several "short" gradient descent steps that approach a manifold of slow growth and "long" Polyak steps that contract distance to minimizers, exploiting the favorable loss landscape structure.

Result: Under certain mixture weight assumptions, the Polyak stepsize reduces loss geometrically on a slow-growth manifold, and the proposed method achieves locally linear convergence. For arbitrary weights, it converges to nearly optimal solutions up to a natural misspecification threshold.

Conclusion: Slow convergence in overparameterized GMMs is not an intrinsic challenge but can be addressed by appropriately exploiting the loss landscape structure via alternating gradient and Polyak steps.

Abstract: We study the problem of learning Gaussian mixture models under overparameterization. Prior work has shown that while overparameterization is essential for avoiding spurious local optima and enables global recovery of the ground-truth model using the gradient-EM (expectation-maximization) algorithm, it can dramatically slow down the local rate of convergence. Under certain assumptions on the mixture weights, we show that a standard divergence measure minimized by statistical learning procedures possesses a manifold of slow growth on which the well-known Polyak stepsize reduces the loss geometrically, and design a gradient-based method that converges to minimizers at a locally linear rate. Additionally, we show that our method converges to nearly optimal solutions -- up to a natural misspecification threshold -- for mixtures with arbitrary weights. At a high level, the method alternates between several "short" gradient descent steps that approach the manifold and "long" Polyak steps that contract the distance to minimizers. Our results suggest that slow convergence is not an intrinsic challenge of overparameterization, but can be overcome by exploiting the favorable structure of the loss landscape.

</details>


### [280] [Spectral Anatomy of Quantum Gaussian Process Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30952)
*Jian Xu,Chao Li,Guang Lin,Yuning Qiu,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that the normalized spectral entropy $S(K)/\log n$ of kernel Gram matrices governs both the dequantization of HHL-based QGP regression and posterior pathologies in quantum kernel Bayesian optimization. The authors prove theoretical bounds linking entropy to Nyström approximation error, variance contraction, and target-dependent optimality, and empirically validate across diverse kernel families and IBM Heron hardware with low transfer error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify two seemingly unrelated recent results—the ruling out of exponential speedups in HHL-based QGP regression and posterior pathologies in quantum kernel Bayesian optimization—by identifying a common governing quantity.

Method: The authors prove a Cauchy–Schwarz tail bound on Nyström approximation error, a finite-sample variance-contraction identity via Bach's degrees of freedom, and a characterization of target-dependent optimal entropy. They empirically test across hardware-efficient, matchgate, IQP, RBF/Matérn/RFF, and deep-kernel families, and transfer to IBM Heron hardware.

Result: All kernel families collapse onto identical $S/\log n$ curves on dequantization, ECE, and variance-contraction panels. The diagnostic transfers to IBM Heron hardware with median absolute error 3.2% and mean 5.2% at $n_q=4$, and scales to $n_q=6$ with mean error 1.7%, all without error mitigation.

Conclusion: Normalized spectral entropy is a kernel-agnostic, hardware-transferable diagnostic that unifies two major quantum Gaussian process phenomena, offering a practical tool for predicting QGP behavior across settings.

Abstract: Two recent results have reshaped quantum Gaussian processes (QGPs). On the one hand, \citet{lowe2025assessing} rule out the exponential speedups claimed by HHL-based QGP regression in the typical, well-conditioned regime; on the other, an independent line of work shows that highly expressive quantum kernels suffer posterior pathologies that break Bayesian optimization. We show that these seemingly unrelated phenomena are governed by the same quantity: the normalized spectral entropy $S(K)/\log n$ of the kernel Gram matrix. We prove a Cauchy--Schwarz tail bound on Nyström approximation error, a finite-sample variance-contraction identity in terms of Bach's degrees of freedom $d_σ(K)$, and a characterization of the \emph{target-dependent} optimal entropy via the intrinsic dimension of the target in the kernel eigenbasis. Empirically, the diagnostic is kernel-agnostic: hardware-efficient, matchgate, IQP \emph{and} RBF/Matérn/RFF/deep-kernel families all collapse onto identical $S/\log n$ curves on dequantization, ECE, and variance-contraction panels. The NLL sweet spot lives at high entropy for smooth targets and at low entropy for band-limited quantum-data targets. The diagnostic transfers from simulator to IBM Heron hardware with median absolute error $3.2\%$ and mean $5.2\%$ in $S/\log n$ across $24$ configurations at $n_q = 4$, with matchgate and IQP within $5\%$ mean and a single HE configuration returning a $30\%$ outlier that drops to $0.5\%$ on rerun (attributed to calibration drift); the same diagnostic transfers to a second Heron backend (mean error $2.7\%$) and to a $n_q = 6$ scale-up on the original backend (mean error $1.7\%$). No error mitigation is applied throughout.

</details>


### [281] [Revisiting Zeroth-Order Hessian Approximation: A Single-Step Policy Optimization Lens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30960)
*Junbin Qiu,Zhaowei Hong,Renzhe Xu,Yao Shu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZoVH unifies zeroth-order Hessian estimation via policy optimization, introducing variance-reduced estimators for the Hessian, its inverse, and inverse Hessian-gradient product with provable optimal baseline and query reuse strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate ZO Hessian estimation is critical for bilevel optimization, Bayesian inference, and uncertainty quantification, but existing methods lack low-variance estimators for the full Hessian and its inverse in high-dimensional settings.

Method: ZoVH reinterprets ZO Hessian approximation through single-step Policy Optimization, establishing equivalence between ZO estimators and smoothed PO objective Hessians. It introduces an optimal baseline for variance minimization and a query reuse strategy for sample efficiency.

Result: Theoretical analysis proves unbiasedness, variance optimality, error bounds, and convergence guarantees. Empirical results show superior estimation accuracy and convergence on real-world tasks.

Conclusion: ZoVH provides a theoretically grounded, variance-reduced suite for ZO Hessian estimation with strong empirical performance, with code publicly available.

Abstract: Accurate Zeroth-Order (ZO) Hessian estimation is a cornerstone of derivative-free methods, essential for tasks such as bilevel optimization, Bayesian inference, and uncertainty quantification. However, obtaining a complete suite of low-variance estimators for the Hessian and its inverse in high-dimensional settings remains a significant challenge. To address this, we propose a unified framework that reinterprets ZO Hessian approximation through the lens of single-step Policy Optimization (PO). This perspective establishes a theoretical equivalence between general ZO Hessian estimators and the Hessian of a smoothed PO objective, unifying distinct classical randomized estimators as specific instances of baseline selection. Building on this foundation, we introduce ZoVH, a comprehensive suite of variance-reduced estimators for the full Hessian matrix, its regularized inverse, and the bias-corrected inverse Hessian-gradient product. ZoVH leverages two key techniques: (1) a unique optimal baseline derived to provably minimize variance, and (2) a query reuse strategy that incorporates historical function queries to enhance sample efficiency without inflating costs. Our rigorous theoretical analysis confirms the unbiasedness of the Hessian estimator, validates the variance optimality of our baseline, provides error bounds for the entire ZoVH suite, and establishes convergence guarantees for the resulting curvature-aware ZO algorithm. Extensive empirical results validate our theoretical findings, demonstrating that ZoVH achieves superior estimation accuracy and convergence performance in real-world applications. Code is available at https://github.com/Qjbtiger/ZoVH

</details>


### [282] [Parallel Tempering Initial Sampling in Inference-Time Reward Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30991)
*Myeongjun Oh,Gwangho Kim,Sungyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PATHS improves inference-time reward alignment for diffusion/flow models by using parallel tempering to initialize particles from flattened reward landscapes, avoiding mode trapping in complex, multi-modal reward spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SMC-based methods initialize particles from a standard prior, where high-reward regions in complex reward landscapes are extremely rare, and even recent reward-aware approaches get trapped in local modes due to multi-modality.

Method: PATHS couples multiple sampling chains through parallel tempering, maintaining a ladder of reward-tempered chains with periodic Metropolis swaps for efficient exploration across flattened reward landscapes.

Result: Experiments on layout-to-image and quantity-aware generation show consistent gains in alignment quality, especially on complex prompts.

Conclusion: PATHS effectively mitigates mode-trapping and enhances finite-budget exploration of rare, high-reward regions, outperforming standard SMC-based reward alignment methods.

Abstract: Inference-time reward alignment steers pretrained diffusion and flow-based generative models to satisfy user-specified rewards without retraining. Recently, Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) has emerged as a powerful framework for this task by iteratively filtering and propagating multiple particles. However, we show that standard SMC-based methods often suffer from poor performance because they initialize particles from a standard prior, whereas high-reward regions in complex reward landscapes are extremely rare. Further, we show that even recent reward-aware initial sampling approaches remain vulnerable to getting trapped in local modes, as complex reward landscapes are often multi-modal. To overcome these limitations, we propose PATHS (PArallel Tempering for High-complexity reward Sampling), a novel initialization method that couples multiple sampling chains through parallel tempering. PATHS maintains a ladder of reward-tempered chains and periodically performs Metropolis swaps, enabling efficient exploration across flattened reward landscapes, thereby mitigating the mode-trapping issues. Our analysis reveals that this mechanism substantially enhances the finite-budget exploration of rare, high-reward regions that are typically challenging to sample. Experiments on layout-to-image and quantity-aware generation show that PATHS achieves consistent gains in alignment quality, particularly on complex prompts.

</details>


### [283] [Eigenvectors of Experts are Training-free Non-collapsing Routers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30992)
*Giang Do,Hung Le,Truyen Tran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes SSMoE, a training-free framework using Singular Value Decomposition of expert weight matrices to solve the expert collapse problem in Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) models, improving performance without additional training or fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expert collapse degrades SMoE model performance. Existing router-based solutions require costly training from scratch or fine-tuning, and the issue persists even in well-pretrained models.

Method: SSMoE leverages Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) on expert weight matrices, using their eigenvectors (which encode rich semantic information) as an alternative routing strategy—requiring no training.

Result: SSMoE demonstrates strong generalization and robustness across diverse language and vision tasks under both clean and corrupt data settings.

Conclusion: Understanding model internals (spectral properties of expert weights) can guide development of more effective, training-free SMoE architectures.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) architectures improve the training efficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs) by routing input tokens to a selected subset of specialized experts. Despite their remarkable success, both training and inference in SMoE models suffer from the expert collapse issue (Chi et al., 2022), which degrades model performance. Prior studies primarily focus on improving the router; however, such methods rely on training from scratch or fine-tuning, which requires high computational and data-processing costs. Furthermore, we demonstrate that, despite these efforts, the issue persists when advancing well-pretrained SMoE models, as evidenced by both theoretical and empirical results. To fill that gap, we analyze the advanced SMoE models and observe that the eigenvectors of expert weight matrices encode rich semantic information, pointing to an effective alternative to conventional routing strategies. Building on this insight, we propose Singular Value Decomposition SMoE (SSMoE), a novel and training-free framework that leverages spectral properties of the expert weights to address the collapse issue and enhance model performance. Extensive experiments across diverse language and vision tasks, under both clean and corrupt data settings, demonstrate the strong generalization and robustness of SSMoE. Our findings highlight how a deeper understanding of model internals can guide the development of more effective SMoE architectures. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/giangdip2410/SSMoE.

</details>


### [284] [Learning Multi-Agent Coordination via Sheaf-ADMM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31005)
*Jeffrey Seely,Bartłomiej Cupiał,Llion Jones*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A differentiable optimization framework using ADMM and cellular sheaves enables multi-agent coordination with heterogeneous consensus, achieving strong results on maze pathfinding, MNIST, and Sudoku while offering interpretable coordination dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable multi-agent systems to coordinate effectively when individual agents have insufficient local views, and to provide interpretable coordination dynamics that standard message-passing architectures lack.

Method: Decomposes input into overlapping local views processed by neural-encoder-parameterized convex subproblems; agents coordinate via ADMM with inter-agent constraints specified by a cellular sheaf, enabling heterogeneous consensus; training via backpropagation through unrolled optimization.

Result: Outperforms standard CNN on MNIST under distribution shifts; achieves higher solve rates than MPNN baselines on Sudoku; ADMM structure exposes interpretable primal, consensus, and dual state variables.

Conclusion: The proposed differentiable optimization framework effectively trains multi-agent systems to coordinate from local views, yields robustness and superior performance, and offers interpretability advantages over black-box message-passing approaches.

Abstract: We present a differentiable optimization framework for multi-agent coordination. An input is decomposed into overlapping local views, each processed by an agent that solves a convex subproblem parameterized by a neural encoder. Agents coordinate through the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) with inter-agent constraints specified by a cellular sheaf. The sheaf specifies which aspects of neighboring solutions must agree, allowing for heterogeneous notions of global consensus. Backpropagating through the unrolled optimization jointly trains all components of the multi-agent system. We evaluate on maze pathfinding, image classification, and Sudoku, where agents with individually insufficient local views learn to coordinate to produce correct global outputs. On MNIST, the local-view decomposition yields improved robustness to distribution shifts relative to a standard CNN. On Sudoku, the optimization-derived structure yields markedly higher solve rates than parameter-matched MPNN baselines. Finally, the ADMM structure exposes distinct primal, consensus, and dual state variables, opening the coordination dynamics to direct analysis and intervention -- a property unavailable in standard message-passing architectures.

</details>


### [285] [Trust-Region Behavior Blending for On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31159)
*Daniil Plyusov,Alexey Gorbatovski,Alexey Malakhov,Nikita Balagansky,Boris Shaposhnikov,Daria Korotyshova,Daniil Gavrilov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRB is a warmup method that blends teacher behavior into student rollouts via a KL trust region to improve on-policy distillation for math reasoning, then anneals the budget back to pure student rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation suffers from poor early student rollouts, causing teacher supervision to be applied to weak or low-quality prefixes.

Method: TRB replaces the early rollout policy with the closest-to-teacher behavior policy inside a student-centered KL trust region, keeping the per-prefix reverse-KL OPD loss unchanged, and anneals the KL budget to zero after warmup.

Result: Across two math-reasoning distillation settings, TRB attains the strongest average among compared methods.

Conclusion: TRB effectively addresses the early poor-rollout problem in on-policy distillation by blending teacher behavior in a trust region, then smoothly transitioning back to student rollouts.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on prefixes sampled from its own policy while matching a stronger teacher. This addresses the prefix mismatch of offline distillation, but early student rollouts can still be poor, placing teacher supervision on weak or low-quality prefixes. We propose Trust-Region behavior Blending (TRB), a warmup method that replaces the early rollout policy with the closest-to-teacher behavior policy inside a student-centered KL trust region, while keeping the per-prefix reverse-KL OPD loss unchanged. The KL budget is annealed to zero, so training returns to pure student rollouts after warmup. Across two math-reasoning distillation settings, TRB attains the strongest average among the compared methods.

</details>


### [286] [Physics-Informed Coarsening for Multigrid Graph Neural Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31013)
*Amir Bazzi,David Cardinaux,Ramy Nemer,Jose Alaves,Arjun Kalkur Matpadi Raghavendra,Elie Hachem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multigrid graph neural network for solid mechanics that uses physics-informed coarsening (retaining nodes based on strain/stress concentration) to achieve accurate and stable surrogates for deformable solids.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust surrogates for deformable solids remain underexplored despite challenges from nonlinear elasticity, plasticity, and transient behavior, while existing methods focus on fluids and structured geometries.

Method: Proposes a multigrid graph neural network with encoder-processor-decoder architecture and residual-based node scoring for physics-informed coarsening, retaining high-strain/stress regions to preserve long-range interactions via hierarchical message passing.

Result: Outperforms standard sampling baselines in accuracy and rollout stability across linear, nonlinear, and transient solid mechanics datasets.

Conclusion: Physics-informed coarsening is crucial for scalable and robust surrogate modeling in solid mechanics.

Abstract: Learning-based surrogates for partial differential equations have recently matched the accuracy of classical solvers while achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups, predominantly in fluid settings and structured geometries. In contrast, robust surrogates for deformable solids remain underexplored, despite the presence of nonlinear elasticity, plasticity, and transient behavior that challenge standard architectures. We introduce a multigrid graph neural network for solid mechanics that couples an encoder-processor-decoder backbone with a physics-informed coarsening strategy. Instead of downsampling via geometric heuristics, our method scores nodes using a residual-based measure of local physical activity and preferentially retains regions of high strain or stress concentration, allocating multiscale capacity where it is most needed. This preserves long-range interactions through hierarchical message passing while improving stability over long rollouts. We evaluate on multiple datasets covering linear, nonlinear, and transient regimes, and observe consistent gains in accuracy and rollout stability compared to standard sampling baselines. Our results highlight the importance of physics-informed coarsening for scalable surrogate modeling in solid mechanics.

</details>


### [287] [SDM-Q: Cost-Aware Staged Decision-Making for Multi-Omics Classification with Deep Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31014)
*Nan Mu,Xiaoyang Fan,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDM-Q is a reinforcement learning framework that reformulates multi-omics classification as a sequential decision problem, adaptively acquiring omics modalities to balance diagnostic accuracy with cost, achieving over 99% accuracy with a single modality on BRCA dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning methods assume full modality availability during inference, leading to substantial redundancy and limited practicality in clinical settings, where acquiring complete multi-omics profiles is expensive and time-consuming.

Method: Reformulates multi-omics diagnosis as a finite-horizon sequential decision problem using reinforcement learning, with an action-value function deciding whether to acquire additional modalities or terminate and predict, plus a backward stage-wise optimization strategy for training stability.

Result: On BRCA and KIPAN datasets, over 99% and 95% of subjects achieved accurate classification using only a single omics modality; average acquired modalities remained below two for ROSMAP and LGG while maintaining competitive performance.

Conclusion: Cost-aware sequential decision-making provides an effective paradigm for improving efficiency in precision medicine workflows by reducing redundant modality acquisition without sacrificing classification accuracy.

Abstract: Multi-omics data provide complementary molecular characterizations of disease phenotypes and play an important role in disease diagnosis and subtype classification in precision medicine. However, acquiring complete multi-omics profiles is expensive and time-consuming, while most existing deep learning methods assume full modality availability during inference, resulting in substantial redundancy and limited practicality in clinical settings. To address this issue, we propose SDM-Q, a reinforcement learning framework for adaptive and cost-aware multi-omics classification. Specifically, multi-omics diagnosis is reformulated as a finite-horizon sequential decision problem, where the currently acquired omics modalities define the diagnostic state at each stage. An action--value function determines whether to acquire an additional modality or terminate the decision process and output the final prediction. To balance diagnostic utility and acquisition cost, the reward is defined only at the terminal stage and jointly determined by classification correctness and cumulative modality acquisition cost. A backward stage-wise optimization strategy is introduced to improve policy consistency and training stability. Experiments on four public multi-omics datasets, including ROSMAP, LGG, BRCA, and KIPAN, demonstrate that SDM-Q effectively reduces redundant modality acquisition while maintaining competitive classification performance compared with methods using complete multi-omics inputs. In the BRCA and KIPAN datasets, more than 99\% and 95\% of subjects, respectively, achieve accurate classification using only a single omics modality, while the average number of acquired modalities remains below two for ROSMAP and LGG. These results suggest that cost-aware sequential decision-making provides an effective paradigm for improving the efficiency of precision medicine workflows.

</details>


### [288] [An Efficient and Scalable Graph Condensation with Structure-Preserving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31016)
*Yulin Hu,Fuyan Ou,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SP-ESGC is a decoupled graph condensation method that separates node condensation from graph structure generation, using heat kernel propagation, hybrid clustering, and a pre-trained edge predictor for efficient and architecture-generalizable graph compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GC methods suffer from computational inefficiency due to coupled optimization and poor generalization across different GNN architectures.

Method: 1) Heat kernel feature propagation for node representation via spectral graph theory; 2) Hybrid clustering strategy to extract discriminative intra-class centroids; 3) Pre-trained edge predictor to infer transferable structural patterns from the original graph.

Result: SP-ESGC achieves precise graph condensation with significantly high computational efficiency and generalizes well across diverse GNN architectures on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The decoupled design of SP-ESGC effectively addresses the efficiency and generalization limitations of existing GC methods.

Abstract: Graph condensation (GC) is pivotal for enabling Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) deployment in resource-constrained scenarios by compressing large-scale graphs into compact synthetic counterparts. Existing GC methods commonly suffer from computational inefficiency due to coupled optimization as well as encountering poor generalization across GNN architectures. To address these challenges, this study proposes an Efficient and Scalable Graph Condensation with Structure-Preserving (SP-ESGC), which possesses a decoupled design that separates node condensation from graph structure generation. Specifically, it first employs heat kernel feature propagation to generate node representation via spectral graph theory-inspired diffusion. Further, a novel hybrid clustering strategy is designed to extracts discriminative intra-class centroids from the node representation. Finally, a pre-trained edge predictor infers transferable structural patterns from the original graph, ensuring accurate synthetic graph generation. Extensive experiments on real-world graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed SP-ESGC implementes a precise GC with significantly high computational efficiency. Moreover, SP-ESGC also generalizes well across diverse GNN architectures.

</details>


### [289] [What changes after deployment? A survey on On-device Learning in TinyML](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31226)
*Massimo Pavan,Luca Pezzarossa,Fabrizio Pittorino,Manuel Roveri,Xenofon Fafoutis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Survey of ~70 TinyML on-device learning works, analyzed through the lens of distribution change types, revealing a gap between benchmarks and real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-deployment distribution change undermines static TinyML models; existing literature lacks characterization of how change types occur and require different solutions.

Method: Survey analysis of ~70 ODL works under one principle: the distribution change regime, examining how change types affect applications, hardware, and solution structures.

Result: Identified a persistent gap between methodological benchmarks and real-world deployment scenarios in TinyML on-device learning.

Conclusion: Different distribution change types demand tailored ODL solutions; current benchmarks do not adequately reflect real-world deployment conditions.

Abstract: Machine learning models on microcontroller-class devices (TinyML) face a fundamental challenge: post-deployment distribution change undermines static models. On-device learning (ODL) addresses this by running the learning process directly on the device. The existing literature has not characterized how distribution change occurs or how different change types require different solutions. Approximately 70 ODL works are surveyed under one principle: the distribution change regime. The survey analyzes how different types of distribution change influence the applications addressable on-device, the hardware employed, and the structure of the solutions. A persistent gap between methodological benchmarks and real-world deployment scenarios is also identified.

</details>


### [290] [Augmented Lagrangian Predictive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31022)
*Jeffrey Seely,Julian Gould*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PC-ALM modifies predictive coding to achieve exact BP gradients via layer-local Lagrange multipliers, matching backpropagation performance even in deep narrow networks while maintaining local learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive coding (PC) is a local-learning alternative to backpropagation (BP) but underperforms BP, especially in deep narrow networks. The authors aim to bridge this performance gap while preserving PC's local computation and inference budget.

Method: The authors introduce Augmented Lagrangian Predictive Coding (PC-ALM), which augments PC with layer-local Lagrange multipliers that accumulate per-layer constraint errors. This transforms the scalar energy minimization into dual ascent on an augmented Lagrangian, producing dynamics that propagate credit signals "ballistically" rather than diffusively.

Result: In linear PC networks, PC-ALM provably converges to exact BP gradients. In nonlinear networks up to depth 128, PC-ALM matches BP performance across all width-depth regimes, including deep narrow networks where standard PC fails. Credit signals are evenly distributed across layers.

Conclusion: PC-ALM offers a generalization of PC that achieves BP-level performance through purely local dynamics, and the augmented Lagrangian framework may provide insights into how distributed neural systems could compute and propagate error signals locally.

Abstract: Predictive coding (PC) is a local-learning alternative to backpropagation (BP), training deep networks via local energy-minimization dynamics rather than a global backward pass. We introduce Augmented Lagrangian Predictive Coding (PC-ALM), which maintains PC's inference budget but aligns each weight update toward BP by accumulating per-layer constraint errors into a layer-local Lagrange multiplier. In linear PC networks, PC-ALM converges to an equilibrium with exact BP gradients distributed across the network via only layer-local updates. We analyze PC-ALM in nonlinear PC networks up to depth 128 and show that it matches BP performance across all width-depth regimes, notably in deep narrow networks where PC underperforms. PC-ALM introduces recurrent dynamics in each layer's activations. Compared to PC's heat flow on a scalar energy, PC-ALM dynamics are driven by dual ascent on the augmented Lagrangian. We observe "ballistic" credit propagation across very deep networks, with credit signals evenly distributed across layers, compared to PC's slow, diffusive credit propagation. Beyond the algorithm itself, the augmented Lagrangian framework offers a generalization of PC, and may yield insights into how distributed systems could compute and propagate BP-like credit signals through purely local dynamics.

</details>


### [291] [EchoRL: Reinforcement Learning via Rollout Echoing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31228)
*Jinhe Bi,Aniri,Minglai Yang,Xingcheng Zhou,Wenke Huang,Sikuan Yan,Yujun Wang,Zixuan Cao,Michael Färber,Xun Xiao,Volker Tresp,Yunpu Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes EchoRL, a lightweight module that addresses the advantage-degeneration problem in RLVR post-training by extracting valuable learning signals (EchoClip) from verified-success rollouts using step-level entropy analysis, consistently improving performance across diverse benchmarks and methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR post-training suffers from advantage-degeneration, where many rollouts become verified-successful with zero reward variance, causing vanishing policy gradients and capping training performance. The authors argue these rollouts still contain valuable signals that existing methods overlook.

Method: EchoRL is a lightweight module that: 1) identifies an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts based on step-level entropy values (inspired by analyzing entropy patterns of expert-generated golden trajectories), and 2) feeds this clip as an auxiliary supervision signal into the RL objective.

Result: Extensive experiments across 10 benchmarks, 5 LLM backbones, and 4 popular RLVR post-training methods show EchoRL consistently improves RLVR post-training with minimal overhead.

Conclusion: EchoRL effectively mitigates the advantage-degeneration problem in RLVR by recovering previously wasted learning signals, leading to consistent performance gains across diverse settings at low computational cost.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards is an effective route for post-training to strengthen the reasoning capability of large language models. However, as training proceeds, the learning signal can collapse thus makes the training gain become marginal and ineffective. Specifically, a growing fraction of prompts' rollouts become advantage-degenerated: all the self-generated rollouts show verified-success, making the standard deviation over their rewards be zero; accordingly each rollout's advantage becomes degenerated (zero) as well. Given such rollouts' advantages, the policy-gradient for model optimization eventually vanishes, capping the training performance. We argue that some of these rollouts still contain valuable learning signals but unfortunately omitted with the existing RLVR methods. In this paper, inspired through analyzing the entropy pattern behind golden trajectories produced by external expert models, we propose EchoRL for better exploiting the advantage-degenerated rollouts to further improve the training performance. EchoRL is a lightweight module that first identifies an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts based on their step-level entropy values, and then feeds this clip back as an auxiliary supervision signal in the RL objective. Extensive experiments across 10 benchmarks, 5 LLM backbones, and 4 popular RLVR post-training methods demonstrate that EchoRL consistently improves RLVR post-training with minimal overhead.

</details>


### [292] [Multi-Scale Separable Fourier Neural Networks for Solving High-Frequency PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31027)
*Qihong Yang,Qiaolin He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MS-SFNN is a novel neural architecture for solving high-frequency PDEs using fixed random weights, separable coordinate subnetworks with cosine activations, and memory-efficient batched QR decomposition, outperforming PINNs and SV-SNN significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural PDE solvers struggle with high-frequency problems due to spectral bias, dense collocation memory issues, and the computational cost of automatic differentiation in high dimensions.

Method: Uses d independent coordinate-wise subnetworks with fixed random weights, cosine activations for Fourier features, element-wise multiplication to form separable basis functions, least-squares coefficient solving, and memory-efficient batched QR decomposition instead of automatic differentiation.

Result: Achieves unprecedented accuracy across challenging linear/nonlinear high-frequency PDEs, significantly outperforming PINN and SV-SNN in numerical experiments.

Conclusion: Fixed random weights with separable Fourier feature construction and analytical derivatives offer a highly accurate and memory-efficient paradigm for high-frequency PDE solving.

Abstract: We propose a novel neural network architecture, termed Multi-Scale Separable Fourier Neural Networks (MS-SFNN), for the accurate and efficient solution of linear and nonlinear high-frequency partial differential equations (PDEs). MS-SFNN exploits a separable representation: given a $d$-dimensional input, it employs $d$ independent subnetworks -- each acting on a single coordinate -- and constructs basis functions via element-wise multiplication of their outputs. The PDE solution is approximated as a linear combination of these basis functions, with coefficients determined by least squares. Critically, all network weights and biases are randomly initialized once, from a uniform distribution with unit variance, and remain fixed thereafter. To enhance expressivity, a tunable scaling factor is introduced in each subnetwork to modulate the frequency content of the resulting basis functions. Fourier features are explicitly embedded through cosine activations, endowing the method with strong spectral approximation capabilities. To mitigate the memory bottleneck associated with dense collocation in high-frequency or three-dimensional problems, we replace automatic differentiation with analytically derived basis function derivatives and develop a memory-efficient batched QR decomposition algorithm for solving large-scale least-squares systems. Numerical experiments demonstrate that MS-SFNN achieves unprecedented accuracy across a range of challenging PDEs, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods such as Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN) and Separated-Variable Spectral Neural Networks (SV-SNN).

</details>


### [293] [Learning Cardiac Latent Representations in Vectorcardiogram Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31249)
*Bosong Huang,Panzhen Zhao,Zengxiang Li,Patricia Lee,Wei Jin,Alan Wee-Chung Liew,Ming Jin,Shirui Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LVCG is a self-supervised representation learning framework that learns cardiac representations in the VCG space (instead of ECG space) to reduce redundancy, improve robustness, and enhance generalization across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard 12-lead ECG representations contain substantial redundancy due to multiple spatial projections of the same cardiac activity, leading to spurious correlations and overfitting. The authors propose learning representations in the physically grounded VCG space instead.

Method: The paper introduces LVCG, the first general self-supervised representation learning framework that operates in the VCG (vectorcardiogram) space rather than the observable ECG signal space, learning view-invariant latent representations of cardiac electrical activity.

Result: LVCG outperforms ECG-space baselines across various tasks, demonstrating enhanced robustness and generalization, particularly under domain shift conditions.

Conclusion: Learning representations in the physically grounded VCG space reduces redundancy and improves generalization compared to traditional ECG-space approaches, offering a more robust foundation for cardiac representation learning.

Abstract: Electrocardiography (ECG) is a cornerstone of cardiac assessment, making the learning of informative ECG representations fundamental to tasks ranging from disease diagnosis to clinical report generation. However, existing methods operate almost exclusively in the observable ECG signal space. In practice, the standard twelve-lead ECG represents multiple projections of the same underlying cardiac electrical activity from different spatial orientations. Therefore, representation learning in the ECG space inevitably introduces substantial redundancy, which may lead to spurious correlations and increased risk of overfitting. To address this and motivated by the Frank vectorcardiogram (VCG) model, we propose learning a unified latent representation of cardiac electrical activity directly in the VCG space. We introduce LVCG, the first general self-supervised representation learning framework designed to operate in this physically grounded latent space. By learning view-invariant latent VCG representations rather than lead-specific artifacts, VCG minimizes redundancy and improves generalization. LVCG generally outperforms ECG-space baselines across tasks, demonstrating enhanced robustness and generalization, especially in domain shift settings.

</details>


### [294] [UniRTL: Unifying Code and Graph for Robust RTL Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31040)
*Yi Liu,Hongji Zhang,Lei Chen,Mingxuan Yuan,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniRTL is a multimodal pretraining framework that integrates RTL code and Control Data Flow Graphs (CDFG) to learn unified representations, outperforming prior single-modality methods on performance prediction and code retrieval tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RTL representation approaches rely on a single data modality (either code or graph), limiting expressiveness and generalization. The authors argue that integrating complementary code and CDFG modalities is essential for thorough RTL understanding.

Method: UniRTL jointly leverages code and CDFG via fine-grained alignment through mutual masked modeling, with a hierarchical training strategy including a pretrained graph-aware tokenizer and staged alignment of functional summary text and code before graph integration.

Result: UniRTL consistently outperforms prior methods on two downstream tasks (performance prediction and code retrieval) under multiple settings, establishing a more robust foundation for hardware design automation.

Conclusion: Multimodal pretraining that integrates code and CDFG representations yields superior RTL understanding, advancing hardware design automation.

Abstract: Developing effective representations for register transfer level (RTL) designs is crucial for accelerating the hardware design workflow. Existing approaches, however, typically rely on a single data modality, either the RTL code or its associated graph-based representation, limiting the expressiveness and generalization ability of the learned representations. For RTL, the control data flow graph (CDFG) offers a comprehensive structural representation that preserves complete information, while the code modality explicitly encodes semantic and functional information. We argue that integrating these complementary modalities is essential for a thorough understanding of RTL designs. To this end, we propose UniRTL, a multimodal pretraining framework that learns unified RTL representations by jointly leveraging code and CDFG. UniRTL achieves fine-grained alignment between code and graph through mutual masked modeling and employs a hierarchical training strategy that incorporates a pretrained graph-aware tokenizer and staged alignment of text (i.e., functional summary) and code prior to graph integration. We evaluate UniRTL on two downstream tasks, performance prediction and code retrieval, under multiple settings. Experimental results show that UniRTL consistently outperforms prior methods, establishing it as a more robust and powerful foundation for advancing hardware design automation.

</details>


### [295] [The Challenges of Using Reinforcement Learning for Controlling Industrial Energy Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31044)
*Tobias Lademann,Théo Vincent,Jan Peters,Matthias Weigold*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes challenges of deploying reinforcement learning in real-world industrial energy systems (thermal heating networks), showing operational stability but a significant performance gap compared to simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most RL studies for industrial energy systems are limited to simulations; the authors aim to identify and analyze the real-world deployment challenges.

Method: The task is formulated as a Markov Decision Process, with systematic analysis of challenges including partial observability, action space design, reward design, and the sim-to-real gap, grounded in an actual real-world deployment.

Result: RL achieves operational stability in the real-world industrial heating network but exhibits a significant performance gap compared to simulation results.

Conclusion: Real-world deployment of RL in industrial energy systems faces substantial challenges that cause performance degradation relative to simulation, highlighting the need for bridging the sim-to-real gap.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has shown promising results for optimizing the control of industrial energy systems, yet most existing studies remain limited to the application in simulation environments. We investigate the challenges of deploying reinforcement learning in a real-world industrial energy system, considering a thermal heating network as a use case. We formulate the task as a Markov Decision Process and systematically analyze the associated challenges along the structure of the formal description, including partial observability, action space design, reward design, and the simulation-to-reality gap. The challenges are grounded in an existing real-world deployment, where reinforcement learning achieves operational stability but shows a significant performance gap compared to simulation.

</details>


### [296] [Why Linear Recurrent Memory Works in Partially Observable Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31261)
*Yike Zhao,Onno Eberhard,Malek Khammassi,Ali H. Sayed,Michael Muehlebach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper theoretically justifies why linear RNNs work well as memory in RL by constructing linear filters that can recover belief states in HMMs (exactly for deterministic transitions, approximately for near-deterministic ones), and extends this to action-controlled settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a theoretical justification for the empirical effectiveness of linear recurrent neural networks as recurrent memory units in partially observable reinforcement learning.

Method: The authors construct and study two linear filters: (1) one that exactly reproduces belief vector logits in an HMM under deterministic transitions, (2) another that achieves vanishing state-decoding error under nearly deterministic transitions. Results are extended to action-controlled HMMs with time-varying, action-dependent filters.

Result: Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical findings and show the constructed linear filter serves as a strong feature extractor in a small RL game.

Conclusion: Linear RNNs can provably recover sufficient statistics (belief states) for optimal policy learning in partially observable settings, offering theoretical backing for their empirical success.

Abstract: The family of linear recurrent neural networks has shown strong performance as recurrent memory units in partially observable reinforcement learning. We provide a theoretical justification for their empirical effectiveness by constructing and studying two linear filters: (i) the first exactly reproduces the pre-softmax logits of the belief vector in a hidden Markov model (HMM) under a deterministic transition matrix, thereby serving as a sufficient statistic for optimal policy learning, (ii) the second achieves vanishing state-decoding error under a nearly deterministic transition matrix, thus reducing state ambiguity to near zero. The results extend to action-controlled HMMs, where the corresponding linear filters become time-varying with action-dependent dynamics. We illustrate our main results through numerical experiments and further show that the constructed linear filter serves as a strong feature extractor in a small reinforcement learning game.

</details>


### [297] [Best-Arm Identification-Based Trust Region Selection for Bayesian Optimization on Multimodal Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31050)
*Nobuo Namura,Sho Takemori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A trajectory-aware framework combining best-arm identification with trust region-based Bayesian optimization to efficiently solve multimodal optimization problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaussian process-based BO degrades on complex multimodal or high-dimensional problems. Trust region-based BO helps but selecting effective regions is challenging.

Method: Integrates best-arm identification (BAI) with trust region-based BO; extrapolates optimization trajectories of multiple local optimizers to predict final performance and progressively eliminates suboptimal candidates via BAI.

Result: The BAI-guided BO converges faster to the global optimum than conventional BO under mild assumptions; demonstrated effectiveness on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed trajectory-aware BAI-guided BO efficiently solves multimodal optimization problems with faster convergence.

Abstract: Gaussian process-based Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular approach for expensive black-box optimization, but its performance often degrades on complex multimodal or high-dimensional problems. Trust region-based BO mitigates this issue by focusing on local regions, and recent studies suggest that selecting an effective region can be formulated as a multi-armed bandit problem. We propose a trajectory-aware framework that integrates best-arm identification (BAI) with trust region-based BO to efficiently solve multimodal optimization problems. Our method extrapolates the optimization trajectories of multiple locally initialized optimizers to predict their final performance and progressively eliminates suboptimal candidates via BAI. We theoretically show that the proposed BAI-guided BO converges faster to the global optimum than conventional BO under mild assumptions, and demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.

</details>


### [298] [Learning to Bid in FCR Markets: A Best-of-Both-Worlds Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31070)
*Marius Potfer,Cheng Wan,Pierre Gruet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reformulates the European FCR market bidding problem as an online learning problem using a Best-of-Both-Worlds combinatorial semi-bandit algorithm, achieving logarithmic pseudo-regret in stochastic settings and O(√T) regret in adversarial ones, with practical validation on historical data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flexibility providers face challenges in the FCR market due to hidden competing offers and limited feedback (only clearing price and awarded quantity), motivating the need for a learning-based bidding approach that works under uncertainty.

Method: The multi-country FCR clearing problem is recast as a repeated multi-unit uniform-price auction against endogenous opposing bids, then solved using a Best-of-Both-Worlds combinatorial semi-bandit algorithm that operates on standard market feedback.

Result: The algorithm achieves logarithmic pseudo-regret in stochastic environments and O(√T) regret in adversarial ones; synthetic experiments confirm expected scaling, and backtests on historical European FCR data show competitive performance, especially on stable products.

Conclusion: Learning-based bidding in FCR markets is theoretically grounded and practically useful, but the learning rule must match the product-level market stability—EXP3-type baselines may be safer under stronger non-stationarity.

Abstract: Bidding in the European Frequency Containment Reserve (FCR) market is challenging for flexibility providers because competing offers are hidden and bidders observe only partial feedback form the market, such as, clearing price and awarded quantity. For a participant active in a single country, we show that the multi-country FCR clearing problem can be recast as a repeated multi-unit uniform-price auction against an endogenous vector of opposing bids. This reformulation yields an online learning problem and allows us to adapt a Best-of-Both-Worlds combinatorial semi-bandit algorithm implementable from this standard market feedback. The resulting bidder achieves logarithmic pseudo-regret in stochastic environments and $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret in adversarial ones. Synthetic experiments confirm the expected scaling, and backtests on historical European FCR data show competitive performance in practice: the method performs especially well on stable products, while EXP3-type baselines can be safer under stronger non-stationarity. Overall, the results show that learning-based bidding in FCR markets is theoretically grounded and practically useful when the learning rule matches product-level market stability.

</details>


### [299] [Riemannian Diffusion Models on General Manifolds via Physics-Informed Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31106)
*Gyeonghoon Ko,Juho Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) to approximate the heat kernel on manifolds for Riemannian diffusion models, enabling generative modeling on diverse manifolds beyond simple symmetric ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Riemannian diffusion models require the manifold heat kernel for training, which is only available in closed form for a few highly symmetric manifolds, limiting their applicability.

Method: Given an explicit manifold specification, choose a coordinate system, derive the heat (Fokker-Planck) equation with a short-time asymptotic approximation, and train a PINN to learn the log heat kernel as a surrogate.

Result: The learned surrogate enables forward noising (heat-kernel sampling) and conditional-score evaluation for denoising score matching. Demonstrated on $S^2$, $SO(3)$, $\mathrm{SPD}(n)$, and permutation-quotiented point clouds.

Conclusion: The PINN-based heat kernel approximation provides a general, practical approach to Riemannian diffusion models on arbitrary manifolds without requiring closed-form heat kernels.

Abstract: Riemannian diffusion models generalize score-based generative modeling to manifold-supported data via stochastic diffusion equations on the manifold. However, training requires sampling from and differentiating the manifold heat kernel, which is rarely available in closed form beyond a few highly symmetric manifolds. We propose a general approach that approximates the heat kernel by directly solving the manifold heat equation with a physics-informed neural network (PINN). Given an explicit manifold specification, we choose a coordinate system, derive the corresponding heat (Fokker--Planck) equation and a short-time asymptotic approximation, and then train a PINN to learn the log heat kernel. The resulting surrogate enables both forward noising (heat-kernel sampling) and conditional-score evaluation for denoising score matching. We demonstrate the method on diverse manifolds including $S^2$, $SO(3)$, $\mathrm{SPD}(n)$, and permutation-quotiented point clouds.

</details>


### [300] [The Terminal Representation in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31289)
*Amir Esterhuysen,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Terminal Representation (TR), a novel reward-weighted state representation for RL that captures similar knowledge to the Default Representation but at lower dimensionality, without requiring eigendecomposition, enabling more efficient learning, storage, and downstream use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing representations like SR and DR require eigendecomposition for downstream tasks (option discovery, reward shaping, etc.), which imposes symmetric transition dynamics assumptions and computational overhead. The authors aim to design a representation that bypasses these limitations while preserving the same knowledge.

Method: The TR is formulated as a reward-weighted trajectory encoding similar to DR but learnable as a lower-dimensional object. The paper provides theoretical foundations including derivation, convergence proofs for two learning algorithms, zero-shot compositionality properties, and equivalences between reward formulations.

Result: The TR is shown to be embedded within the top DR eigenvector, capturing equivalent knowledge without eigendecomposition. Empirical evidence demonstrates the TR is a viable alternative to SR and DR in subsidiary applications with lower computational overhead for learning, storage, and usage.

Conclusion: The Terminal Representation offers a more computationally efficient alternative to existing successor and default representations for RL, bypassing the need for eigendecomposition and symmetric dynamics assumptions, while maintaining comparable utility for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Representation learning is a powerful tool for spatio-temporal abstraction within reinforcement learning (RL). Two well established approaches are through the successor representation (SR) and the default representation (DR). The SR encodes states by the future trajectories they induce, capturing information flow decoupled from reward. The DR builds on this by weighting trajectories with reward, integrating credit-assignment structure into the representation. Eigenvectors of both representations have been used to support a range of downstream tasks -- including option discovery, reward shaping, transfer learning, and exploration. We introduce a structurally distinct formulation: the terminal representation (TR). The TR encodes reward-weighted trajectories similarly to the DR, but can be learned as a lower-dimensionality object, and can be used directly for the mentioned applications without eigenvector computations. Eigendecomposition also imposes the assumption of symmetric transition dynamics, which the TR can bypass. In this work we develop the theoretical foundations of the TR: its derivation, convergence of two learning algorithms, its use for zero-shot compositionality, and equivalences between alternative reward formulations. We further show the TR is embedded in the top DR eigenvector, allowing it to capture the same underlying knowledge without eigendecomposition. Additionally, we provide empirical evidence of the TR as a viable alternative to existing representations in subsidiary applications, while requiring less computational overhead to learn, store, and use.

</details>


### [301] [Subspace-Decomposed JEPAs: Disentangling Progression and Content in Latent World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31111)
*Lucas Thil,Jesse Read,Rim Kaddah,Guillaume Doquet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SD-JEPA carves the JEPA latent into orthogonal progression (low-dim) and content (high-dim) subspaces, improving control benchmarks and providing a scene-aware angular compass that outperforms prediction-error scalars at localizing surprise events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) learn compact latent world models by predicting future embeddings, but no single coordinate of the latent is designated to encode task progression.

Method: Carve the JEPA latent into two orthogonal subspaces: a low-dimensional progression subspace shaped by cosine-margin triplet loss, and a high-dimensional content subspace regularized by SIGReg. Proof that anti-collapse forces act on disjoint coordinates, composing additively.
