Result: SD-JEPA improves over LeWM baseline on control benchmarks; outperforms non-LeWM JEPA on Push-T; 1-D angular progression coordinate functions as scene-aware compass; |Δθ_t| outperforms latent-prediction-error surprise at localizing semantic events (+0.18 pooled AUROC, 97.5% per-episode win rate); 8-D progression subspace (4.2% latent) explains 72-95% task-progress variance.

Conclusion: The subspace split is load-bearing; the progression coordinate separates moment of surprise from its meaning, which prediction-error scalars cannot do.

Abstract: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) learn compact latent world models by predicting future embeddings, but no single coordinate of the latent is designated to encode task progression. We carve the JEPA latent into two orthogonal subspaces with disjoint roles: a low-dimensional progression subspace shaped by a cosine-margin triplet loss, and a high-dimensional content subspace regularised by the existing SIGReg objective of LeWM. We prove that the two anti-collapse forces act on disjoint coordinates, so they compose additively rather than competing on the same dimensions. Our method, SD-JEPA improves over the LeWM baseline on the majority of its control benchmarks at matched compute, and outperforms the strongest non-LeWM JEPA baseline on Push-T; a subspace-ablation falsifier confirms the split is the load-bearing ingredient. Beyond planning, the resulting 1-D angular progression coordinate functions as a scene-aware compass on the latent. It advances with task progress, regresses when the agent backtracks, and under controlled perturbations both spikes and relocalises to a semantically appropriate new task-phase sector, separating the moment of surprise from its meaning in a way that prediction-error scalars cannot. Three quantitative tests back this up: $|Δθ_t|$ outperforms the standard latent-prediction-error surprise at localising semantic events on 40 held-out cube episodes by up to +0.18 pooled AUROC (97.5% per-episode win rate at $\pm 1$-step tolerance); a within-episode linear probe across all four environments (40 episodes per env) shows the 8-dimensional progression subspace (4.2% of the latent) explains 72-95% of task-progress variance..

</details>


### [302] [Inconsistency-Aware Minimization: Improving Generalization with Unlabeled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31324)
*Hee-Sung Kim,Hyeonseong Kim,Sungyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a label-free generalization measure called local inconsistency, derived from information geometry, and introduces Inconsistency-Aware Minimization (IAM) that leverages it to improve generalization in supervised, semi-supervised, and self-supervised learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a generalization measure that can be computed without labeled data and use it to improve optimization methods for deep learning models.

Method: The paper introduces local inconsistency from an information-geometric perspective, connects it to the Fisher information matrix and loss Hessian theoretically, and proposes IAM that incorporates local inconsistency into the training objective.

Result: Local inconsistency correlates with the generalization gap; IAM enhances generalization in supervised learning (comparable to Sharpness-Aware Minimization) and shows efficacy in semi- and self-supervised learning scenarios.

Conclusion: Local inconsistency is an effective label-free generalization measure, and IAM successfully leverages it to improve generalization across various learning paradigms.

Abstract: Estimating the generalization gap and developing optimization methods that improve generalization are crucial for deep learning models, for both theoretical understanding and practical applications. Leveraging unlabeled data for these purposes offers significant advantages in real-world scenarios. This paper introduces a novel generalization measure, local inconsistency, derived from an information-geometric perspective on the parameter space of neural networks. A key feature of local inconsistency is that it can be computed without explicit labels. We establish theoretical underpinnings by connecting local inconsistency to the Fisher information matrix and the loss Hessian. Empirically, we demonstrate that local inconsistency correlates with the generalization gap. Based on these findings, we propose Inconsistency-Aware Minimization (IAM), which incorporates local inconsistency into the training objective. We demonstrate that in standard supervised learning settings, IAM enhances generalization, achieving performance comparable to that of existing methods such as Sharpness-Aware Minimization. Furthermore, IAM exhibits efficacy in semi- and self-supervised learning scenarios, where the local inconsistency is computed from unlabeled data.

</details>


### [303] [Scalable Bayesian Inference for Nonlinear Conservation Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31127)
*Tim Weiland,Philipp Hennig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a Bayesian numerical method for nonlinear conservation laws that provides uncertainty quantification, inherits accuracy of classical solvers, and scales to large-scale problems using sparse approximation, outperforming neural baselines in inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear conservation laws are central to many scientific/engineering systems, but practical applications involve uncertainty from sparse/noisy measurements, making inference ill-posed. Both classical numerical methods and deep learning struggle to treat uncertainties appropriately.

Method: Frames classical numerical methods as Bayesian inference under Gaussian process priors for physics-aware uncertainty treatment. Develops a numerically conservative method for uncertainty-aware simulations and uses sparse approximation techniques to scale up to large-scale forward and inverse problems.

Result: Forward simulation inherits accuracy of classical solvers while providing structured uncertainty quantification. On inverse problems, recovers posteriors over nonparametric source fields in seconds, outperforming neural baselines that take minutes for less accurate point estimates.

Conclusion: The proposed Bayesian numerical method effectively handles uncertainty in nonlinear conservation laws, achieving both accuracy and computational efficiency superior to neural network-based approaches.

Abstract: Nonlinear conservation laws are at the heart of many of the most important dynamical systems in science and engineering. In practical applications, such systems are often subject to various sources of uncertainty, e.g. due to sparse or noisy measurements. Inferring physical quantities and fields of interest then becomes an ill-posed problem which both classical numerical methods and modern deep learning-based methods struggle to treat appropriately. Recent work has framed classical numerical methods as Bayesian inference under Gaussian process priors, resulting in a physics-aware treatment of uncertainties. Following this line of work, we develop a novel numerically conservative method for uncertainty-aware simulations of nonlinear conservation laws. We use recent sparse approximation techniques to scale up to large-scale forward and inverse problems. For forward simulation, we inherit the accuracy of classical solvers while providing structured uncertainty quantification. On inverse problems, we recover posteriors over nonparametric source fields in seconds -- outperforming neural baselines that take minutes to produce a less accurate point estimate.

</details>


### [304] [dashi: A Python library for Dataset Shift Characterization to Support Trustworthy AI Development and Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31360)
*David Fernández-Narro,Pablo Ferri,Ángel Sánchez-García,Juan M. García-Gómez,Carlos Sáez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: dashi is an open-source Python library for exploring, quantifying, and characterizing dataset shifts (temporal and multi-source) in AI lifecycles, offering both unsupervised (information geometry) and supervised approaches, demonstrated on three health AI case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset shifts (temporal or multi-source) can severely degrade AI model performance and compromise data quality, especially in health AI where patient safety is at risk. While theoretical foundations exist, there is a lack of accessible, comprehensive software tools for practical shift analysis.

Method: dashi provides a dual approach: (1) unsupervised - leveraging information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds for data variability characterization (e.g., Information Geometric Temporal plots, Global Probabilistic Deviation, Source Probabilistic Outlyingness); (2) supervised - quantifying and characterizing model performance degradation across user-defined temporal and domain/source batches.

Result: Demonstrated utility on three health AI case studies: gestational diabetes mellitus, COVID-19, and emergency medical dispatch, showing interactive visual analytics and variability metrics that support robust ML pipelines.

Conclusion: dashi supports trustworthiness of AI lifecycle stages by enabling assessment of data coherence and AI performance, contributing to robust and safe machine learning pipelines through accessible dataset shift analysis tools.

Abstract: The Artificial Intelligence (AI) life cycle requires a thorough understanding of the underlying data dynamics for robust, safe and cost-effective AI development and use. Dataset shifts are defined as changes between train and test data distributions. Whether occurring over time (temporal) or across different sites (multi-source), they can severely degrade model performance and compromise data quality. This is particularly important in health AI, where the safety and fundamental rights of patients can be severely affected by uncontrolled shifts both at training and operational stages. While the theoretical foundations of covariate, prior, and concept shifts are well established, there is a lack of accessible and comprehensive software tools to perform their analysis. We introduce dashi, an open-source Python library designed for the exploration, quantification, and characterization of dataset shifts. dashi provides a dual approach: an unsupervised approach that leverages information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds to data variability characterization and analysis (e.g., Information Geometric Temporal plots and Multi-Source Variability metrics like Global Probabilistic Deviation and Source Probabilistic Outlyingness), and a supervised approach that quantifies and characterizes model performance degradation. Both unsupervised and supervised approaches work across user-defined temporal and domain/source batches. We demonstrate the utility of dashi on three simulated and real-world health AI case studies on gestational diabetes mellitus, COVID-19 and emergency medical dispatch. By providing interactive visual analytics and variability metrics, dashi supports trustworthiness of AI life cycle stages enabling robust and safe machine learning pipelines through the assessment of data coherence and AI performance.

</details>


### [305] [Generalizing Multi-Scale Time-Series Modeling with a Single Operator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31129)
*Cheonwoo Lee,Dooho Lee,Doyun Choi,Jaemin Yoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SiGMA is a new multi-scale time-series forecasting model that replaces fixed, discrete scaling with a learnable discrete Gaussian kernel, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy while being up to 5.3x faster and 3.8x more memory-efficient than competitors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-scale models rely on fixed and discrete scaling, lacking a principled foundation. The paper aims to unify scaling methods and overcome this fundamental limitation.

Method: The authors formalize existing scaling methods into a scaling operator family, then propose SiGMA (Single Generalized Multi-scale Architecture) with a learnable discrete Gaussian (LDG) kernel grounded in scale-space theory for distance-aware scaling.

Result: SiGMA outperforms all competitors on long- and short-term forecasting, achieving best performance in 13 out of 16 long-term settings. It also improves training speed by up to 5.3x and reduces memory consumption by up to 3.8x over the strongest competitors.

Conclusion: The LDG kernel effectively addresses the fixed/discrete scaling limitation, making SiGMA both more accurate and more efficient for time-series forecasting.

Abstract: Multi-scale modeling has emerged as an effective design principle for time-series forecasting by capturing temporal dynamics at multiple resolutions. As no principled foundation has been established in the literature, we unify existing scaling methods into a scaling operator family, revealing a fundamental limitation of existing approaches: reliance on fixed and discrete scaling. To address this limitation, we propose SiGMA (Single Generalized Multi-scale Architecture), which enables distance-aware scaling via the learnable discrete Gaussian (LDG) kernel grounded in scale-space theory. We evaluate SiGMA comprehensively on long- and short-term forecasting benchmarks against state-of-the-art multi-scale baselines. SiGMA outperforms all competitors on both tasks, especially achieving the best performance in 13 out of 16 long-term evaluation settings. Beyond accuracy, SiGMA significantly improves training speed by up to 5.3 times and reduces memory consumption by up to 3.8 times over the strongest competitors. Code is available at https://github.com/cheonwoolee/SiGMA.

</details>


### [306] [Scaling Higher-Order Graph Learning with Maximal Clique Complexes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31373)
*Antoine Vialle,Aref Einizade,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros,Jhony H. Giraldo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes simplified and factored cellular Weisfeiler-Lehman tests (sCWL and fCWL) and a CliqueWalk method to make higher-order graph neural networks based on cell complexes more scalable while preserving expressivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs are limited to pairwise interactions, and higher-order cell complex models, while more expressive, suffer from poor scalability due to computational overhead.

Method: Introduces sCWL and fCWL tests to preserve expressivity of CWL while improving efficiency; proposes maximal clique complex and CliqueWalk (biased random walk) to avoid explicit clique enumeration and achieve linear scalability.

Result: Reduced time and memory complexity while retaining strong empirical performance; CliqueWalk scales linearly with graph size.

Conclusion: The proposed methods yield a scalable topological learning framework for higher-order graph representation that balances expressivity and efficiency.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are limited to modeling pairwise interactions, while higher-order models based on cell complexes achieve greater expressivity but often suffer from poor scalability. We introduce simplified and factored cellular Weisfeiler Leman tests (sCWL and fCWL), which preserve the expressivity of the CWL test while improving computational efficiency. We further introduce the maximal clique complex, enabling scalable CWNs with reduced time and memory complexity while retaining strong empirical performance. To avoid explicit clique enumeration, we propose CliqueWalk, a biased random walk that samples maximal cliques and scales linearly with graph size. These contributions yield a scalable topological learning framework for higher-order graph representation.

</details>


### [307] [Learning Hyperspherical Time-Frequency Representations for Time-Series Out-of-Distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31155)
*Willian T. Lunardi,Samridha Shrestha,Martin Andreoni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hyperspherical embedding framework (HyperTF) for OOD detection in time-series data, combining time- and frequency-domain features with vMF-based representation learning, achieving superior performance on UCR/UEA archives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series OOD detection is underexplored compared to vision and language, and there is limited principled understanding of how supervised time-series representations can be leveraged for reliable OOD detection under distributional shifts.

Method: Formulates OOD detection as representation learning with hyperspherical embeddings using vMF likelihood-based objective on the unit sphere, combining time- and frequency-domain encoders into a joint embedding space. Detection uses k-NN and Mahalanobis distance scores.

Result: Consistent improvements over contrastive learning and post-hoc baselines on the full UCR and UEA time-series archives under a cross-dataset protocol.

Conclusion: Hyperspherical embeddings with multi-domain fusion (time + frequency) provide a principled and effective approach for time-series OOD detection, outperforming existing methods at scale.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for time-series data remains comparatively underexplored compared to vision and language, with a limited principled understanding of how supervised time-series representations can be leveraged for reliable detection under distributional shifts. This work formulates time-series OOD detection as representation learning with hyperspherical embeddings, where class-conditional structure is induced by a von Mises-Fisher (vMF) likelihood-based objective on the unit sphere. The learned representation combines time- and frequency-domain views of the input signal via domain-specific encoders, integrating them into a joint embedding space for OOD detection. Detection uses distance-based scores over the learned embeddings, including k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) and Mahalanobis scores. We evaluate the approach at scale on the complete UCR and UEA time-series archives under a cross-dataset protocol. Empirical results show consistent improvements under both k-NN and Mahalanobis scoring over strong contrastive learning and post-hoc baselines in the same setting. Code is available at https://github.com/tiiuae/hypertf-time-series-ood.

</details>


### [308] [PithTrain: A Compact and Agent-Native MoE Training System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31463)
*Ruihang Lai,Hao Kang,Haozhan Tang,Akaash R. Parthasarathy,Zichun Yu,Junru Shao,Todd C. Mowry,Chenyan Xiong,Tianqi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "agent-task efficiency" (ATE) as a new metric for evaluating coding agents on training frameworks, builds PithTrain (an agent-native MoE training framework) and ATE-Bench (a benchmark for training-framework tasks), showing that PithTrain matches production throughput while achieving up to 62% fewer Agent Turns and 64% less Active GPU Time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current production MoE training frameworks are expensive to evolve for new architectures and optimizations. While AI coding agents could help automate this development, existing throughput-only evaluations hide costs associated with agents understanding, operating, and extending these frameworks.

Method: The authors propose four agent-native design principles and build PithTrain, a compact MoE training framework. They also introduce ATE-Bench, a benchmark covering real-world training-framework tasks to evaluate agent-task efficiency.

Result: PithTrain matches the throughput of production frameworks while achieving up to 62% fewer Agent Turns and 64% less Active GPU Time on ATE-Bench tasks.

Conclusion: Agent-task efficiency (ATE) is a critical but missing dimension in evaluating coding agents for training frameworks, and agent-native design principles can significantly reduce the cost of using AI agents to develop and extend such frameworks.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the dominant architecture for frontier language models. To meet this demand, production frameworks have built optimized MoE training stacks over years of engineering effort. Yet evolving these stacks for new architectures and system optimizations remains expensive. With the rise of AI coding agents, they could automate parts of training-framework development and accelerate this evolution. But applying them to these existing frameworks carries hidden costs, invisible to today's throughput-only evaluations. We name this missing dimension agent-task efficiency (ATE): the cost of using coding agents to understand, operate, and extend a framework. Grounded in four agent-native design principles, we build PithTrain, a compact, agent-native MoE training framework. We further introduce ATE-Bench, covering real-world training-framework tasks. Our evaluation shows PithTrain matches the throughput of production frameworks, and on ATE-Bench, PithTrain enables higher agent-task efficiency, with up to 62% fewer Agent Turns and 64% less Active GPU Time.

</details>


### [309] [TabCausal: Pretraining Across Causal Environments for Tabular Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31156)
*Zi-Rong Li,Si-Yang Liu,Tian-Zuo Wang,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabCausal is a causal discovery foundation model that uses broad causal pretraining with dynamic task construction to achieve robust structure recovery from observational and interventional data, outperforming both classical methods and prior CDFMs on synthetic and semantic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing causal discovery foundation models (CDFMs) fail to consistently match strong classical methods because a key bottleneck is how causal pretraining tasks are constructed.

Method: TabCausal is trained with broad causal pretraining over diverse graph priors, structural mechanisms, noise models, dimensions, sample sizes, and intervention regimes, using a dynamic task construction strategy to compose these environments into varied discovery tasks.

Result: On large-scale synthetic benchmarks, TabCausal achieves better macro-averaged performance than diverse causal discovery baselines. It demonstrates robust structure recovery in both synthetic and semantic environments, especially under interventional evidence.

Conclusion: Broad causal pretraining is a key ingredient for transferable amortized causal discovery. The paper also introduces a protocol-guided and LLM-audited semantic causal environment benchmark for out-of-distribution analysis.

Abstract: Causal discovery aims to recover directed causal relations from observational and interventional data, providing a basis for mechanistic understanding and reliable decision-making. Causal discovery foundation models (CDFMs) seek to amortize this problem by mapping a dataset directly to a causal graph in a single forward pass, avoiding per-dataset testing, search, or optimization. However, existing CDFMs remain limited, often failing to consistently match strong classical methods, and we find that a key bottleneck is how causal pretraining tasks are constructed. Based on this observation, we propose TabCausal, a data-driven CDFM trained with broad causal pretraining over diverse graph priors, structural mechanisms, noise models, dimensions, sample sizes, and intervention regimes. A dynamic task construction strategy composes these causal environments into varied discovery tasks, enabling more transferable structural learning from observational and mixed-interventional data. On large-scale synthetic benchmarks, TabCausal achieves better macro-averaged performance than a diverse set of causal discovery baselines. To further bridge abstract synthetic generators and realistic causal reasoning scenarios, we introduce a protocol-guided and LLM-audited semantic causal environment benchmark, where domain-grounded SCMs generate interpretable observational and interventional datasets for out-of-distribution analysis. Across both synthetic and semantic environments, TabCausal demonstrates robust structure recovery, especially under interventional evidence, highlighting broad causal pretraining as a key ingredient for transferable amortized causal discovery.

</details>


### [310] [GPU Forecasters: Language Models as Selective Surrogates for Kernel Runtime Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31464)
*Zaid Khan,Justin Chih-Yao Chen,Jaemin Cho,Elias Stengel-Eskin,Mohit Bansal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using LLMs as selective surrogate models for GPU kernel performance evaluation, reducing costly on-device measurements during kernel optimization searches. The surrogate forecasts kernel performance, knows when to defer to actual GPU measurements, and is improved via reinforcement learning, enabling faster kernel discovery under limited evaluation budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GPU kernel evaluation requires costly compilation and repeated execution on hardware, becoming a bottleneck as LLM-driven kernel searches scale to large budgets. The goal is to reduce this on-device evaluation overhead.

Method: LLMs serve as selective surrogates that forecast kernel performance and defer to GPU when uncertain. Reinforcement learning improves forecast accuracy and confidence calibration. The surrogate is evaluated on accuracy, calibration, and practical utility under limited GPU budgets.

Result: LLMs can accurately forecast relative kernel performance, and reinforcement learning improves their utility. In kernel search, the surrogate enables considering several times more candidates under the same GPU budget, leading to finding faster kernels than an equal-budget baseline.

Conclusion: LLMs can play a broader role in kernel optimization as virtual GPU models (performance surrogates) rather than solely as kernel generators, effectively reducing the bottleneck of on-device evaluation.

Abstract: GPU kernels are the workhorse of modern deep learning, and optimizing them (via evolutionary search or coding agents) usually requires repeated measurement on target hardware. While these measurements provide the ground-truth signal necessary for kernel search, they are costly, because each evaluation of a kernel requires compilation and repeated execution on a GPU. As improvements in LLM inference reduce the cost of writing novel kernels and LLM-driven searches scale to large search budgets, on-device evaluation becomes a bottleneck. To address this, we study how LLMs can serve as selective GPU surrogates for kernel evaluation, by forecasting the performance of proposed kernels. A useful surrogate should be accurate, and it should be selective, by knowing when it could be wrong, and deferring to the GPU. To evaluate surrogates, we measure whether their forecasts are accurate, calibrated, and practically useful for recovering fast kernels under limited GPU-measurement budgets. Next, we study whether reinforcement learning can improve forecast accuracy and confidence calibration. Our experiments demonstrate that LLMs can accurately forecast relative kernel performance, that their utility can be improved through reinforcement learning. Used inside a kernel search, the surrogate lets the search consider several times as many candidates under the same GPU evaluation budget, and that leads to finding faster kernels than an equal-budget baseline. These results suggest that LLMs can play a broader role in kernel optimization, by acting as virtual models of a GPU rather than solely as kernel generators for search.

</details>


### [311] [On Efficient Scaling of GNNs via IO-Aware Layers Implementations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31500)
*Daria Fomina,Daniil Krasylnikov,Alexey Boykov,Andrey Dolgovyazov,Vyacheslav Zhdanovskiy,Fedor Velikonivtsev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents GPU kernels for three GNN kernel families (SpMM-based convolutions, reduction-based aggregations, and attention-based layers) that reduce memory traffic and improve data locality, achieving significant speedups (up to 10x) and memory reductions (up to 76x) over existing frameworks like DGL and PyTorch Geometric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs suffer from sparse, irregular memory access patterns. Existing frameworks materialize edge-wise intermediates, increasing memory traffic and limiting scalability on large graphs.

Method: The authors identify three GNN kernel families (SpMM-based, reduction-based, attention-based) and develop custom GPU kernels for each that reduce data movement and improve locality. They also study graph reordering's impact on different kernel mappings.

Result: Up to 3.9x speedup for Graph Transformer (median 1.6x), 7.3x with Tensor Core variants, 8.5x for GATv2 (median 2.0x), 76x memory reduction (median 6x), 10x for reduction kernels (median 2.6x), and up to 8x for SpMM-based layers over DGL.

Conclusion: Hardware-aware kernel design with proper caching and fusion significantly outperforms general frameworks; implementations are released as drop-in replacements for reproducible acceleration.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are bottlenecked by sparse, irregular memory access. Popular frameworks such as DGL and PyTorch Geometric support general message passing, but complex layers often materialize edge-wise intermediates, increasing memory traffic and limiting scalability on large graphs. We take an I/O- and arithmetic-intensity--centric view and show that widely used layers fall into three kernel families: SpMM-based convolutions, reduction-based aggregations, and attention-based layers (GATv2/Graph Transformer). For each family, we develop GPU kernels that reduce data movement, improve locality, and remain robust across realistic graphs. We also study graph reordering and find that its impact depends on the kernel mapping: it benefits neighbor-parallel (gather-dominated) kernels more consistently than feature-parallel designs. Empirically, our fused attention kernels reach up to $\textbf{3.9}\times$ speedup for Graph Transformer (median $\textbf{1.6}\times$), with Tensor Core (block-sparse) variants up to $\textbf{7.3}\times$ on locally dense graphs; for GATv2 we reach up to $\textbf{8.5}\times$ speedup (median $\textbf{2.0}\times$) while reducing peak memory by up to $\textbf{76}\times$ (median $\textbf{6}\times$). Our degree-aware reduction kernels achieve up to $\textbf{10}\times$ speedup (median $\textbf{2.6}\times$). For SpMM-based layers, properly cached cuSPARSE achieves up to $\textbf{8}\times$ speedup over DGL and outperforms evaluated custom baselines in the majority of evaluations. We release our implementations as drop-in replacements to support reproducible, hardware-aware GNN acceleration.

</details>


### [312] [Convergence of Two-Timescale Markovian Stochastic Approximations with Applications in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31172)
*Vagul Mahadevan,Claire Chen,Shuze Daniel Liu,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves stability and convergence of two-timescale stochastic approximation under Markovian noise (without projection or compact noise assumptions) and establishes the first almost sure convergence of TDC with eligibility traces under off-policy learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior convergence results for two-timescale SA methods (e.g., TDC, actor-critic) only held under i.i.d. noise, which is unrealistic for RL where data is typically Markovian. The goal is to extend these guarantees to Markovian noise settings.

Method: The key technical innovation is controlling the fast timescale parameter using the running max of the slow timescale parameter (rather than the current slow parameter as in prior work). This avoids the need for projection operators or compact noise assumptions.

Result: Established stability and convergence of two-timescale SA under Markovian noise without projection or compact noise requirements. As a key application, proved the first almost sure convergence of TDC with eligibility traces under off-policy learning with linear function approximation.

Conclusion: The proposed technique enables stronger convergence guarantees for two-timescale RL algorithms under realistic (Markovian) noise conditions, advancing theoretical foundations for methods like TDC and actor-critic.

Abstract: This work studies the convergence of two-timescale stochastic approximations (SA), a class of iterative algorithms that update two sets of parameters in fast and slow timescales respectively. Notable examples of two-timescale SA in reinforcement learning (RL) include temporal difference learning with gradient correction (TDC) and actor-critic methods. Previously, the stability (i.e., boundedness) and convergence of two-timescale SA were only established under i.i.d. noise. This work instead establishes the stability and convergence of two-timescale SA under Markovian noise, a setup that is more realistic in RL. Notably, we do not need to use any projection operator and the noise does not need to live in a compact space. Our key technical novelty is to control the fast timescale parameter with the running max of the slow timescale parameter, instead of with the current slow timescale parameter, as most prior works do. As a key application, we establish the first almost sure convergence of TDC with eligibility traces under off-policy learning with linear function approximation.

</details>


### [313] [Skill Reuse as Compression in Agentic RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31509)
*Zhikun Xu,Yu Feng,Jacob Dineen,Taiwei Shi,Jieyu Zhao,Ben Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReuseRL improves RL agent generalization by using the Minimum Description Length principle to compress successful trajectories into reusable skill patterns, outperforming baselines across multiple environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents trained with RL learn brittle, task-specific shortcuts that fail to generalize; the authors hypothesize that structurally compressible trajectories (decomposable into reusable patterns) lead to better generalization.

Method: Grounds agentic RL in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle; extracts a shared skill dictionary from successful trajectories; augments RL objective with a segmentation cost that penalizes idiosyncratic, poorly-encodable behaviors; proven with a PAC-Bayes generalization bound.

Result: ReuseRL improves in-distribution and out-of-distribution success rates over vanilla GRPO and strong round-length baselines across ALFWorld, TextWorld-Cooking, and Countdown-Stepwise environments.

Conclusion: Explicitly penalizing incompressible behaviors during RL training yields more generalizable agent policies, validated theoretically via PAC-Bayes bounds and empirically across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning (RL) often learn brittle, task-specific shortcuts. We hypothesize that agents generalize better when their successful trajectories are structurally compressible, decomposed into a small set of reusable abstract patterns. To formalize this, we introduce ReuseRL, which grounds agentic RL in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. ReuseRL extracts a shared skill dictionary from successful trajectories and augments the RL objective with a segmentation cost, explicitly penalizing idiosyncratic behaviors that encode poorly. We prove a PAC-Bayes generalization bound for this compression penalty. Across ALFWorld, TextWorld-Cooking, and Countdown-Stepwise, ReuseRL improves in- and out-of-distribution success over vanilla GRPO and strong round-length baselines.

</details>


### [314] [Retriever Portfolios: A Principled Approach to Adaptive RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31176)
*Miltiadis Stouras,Vincent Cohen-Addad,Silvio Lattanzi,Ola Svensson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes automatically selecting a small, diverse portfolio of retrievers for RAG systems to handle heterogeneous queries, achieving better accuracy and efficiency than single-retriever or naive multi-retriever approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems face highly heterogeneous queries (from simple factoid to complex multi-hop reasoning), but typically rely on a single retriever and fixed hyperparameters, which cannot cover diverse query types effectively.

Method: Formalizes the problem as an expected best-of-k objective over the query distribution, proposes an efficient portfolio construction algorithm with near-optimal guarantees, and builds a router pipeline to select the best retriever for each query.

Result: Across multiple QA benchmarks, the learned portfolios and router pipeline outperform single-retriever and naive multi-retriever baselines on both retrieval metrics and answer quality. Fixed portfolios achieve comparable or better accuracy with lower latency and token cost compared to inference-time hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: A small, diverse portfolio of retrievers with a learned router effectively handles heterogeneous queries in RAG systems, offering both accuracy improvements and efficiency gains through parallel execution.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems typically rely on a single retriever and a single set of hyperparameters, despite facing highly heterogeneous queries that range from simple factoid questions to complex multi-hop reasoning. We propose a method that automatically selects a small, diverse subset of retrievers (a portfolio) from a large pool of candidates, to cover different regions of the target query distribution. We formalize this setting via an expected best-of-$k$ objective over the query distribution and show that it admits an efficient portfolio construction algorithm with near-optimal guarantees. Across multiple QA benchmarks, our learned portfolios and router pipeline consistently outperform single-retriever and naive multi-retriever baselines on both retrieval metrics and answer quality. In addition, compared to inference-time hyperparameter tuning approaches, fixed portfolios enable parallel retrieval and LLM calls, achieving comparable (and sometimes better) accuracy with substantially lower latency and token cost.

</details>


### [315] [How well does Classification Accuracy capture Concept Drift Detection Quality? An overview of Concept Drift Detection evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31186)
*Joanna Komorniczak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the relationship between drift detection quality metrics and classifier performance in synthetic nonstationary data streams, studying eight drift detection quality metrics across seven synthetic data stream generation tools to identify the most informative set of metrics for evaluating concept drift detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scientific community lacks a unified framework for evaluating concept drift detection tasks, and existing research often relies on classification quality metrics that can be affected by multiple factors and may not reliably reflect drift detection quality.

Method: The research studies eight drift detection quality metrics in relation to classifier performance across seven synthetic data stream generation tools, additionally considering drift dynamics as a factor.

Result: The studies identify the most informative set of drift detection quality metrics and provide a deep understanding of the method's evaluation.

Conclusion: The paper provides insights into which drift detection quality metrics are most informative for evaluating concept drift detection, contributing toward establishing a unified evaluation framework.

Abstract: Data streams are nowadays among the most frequently analyzed data structures, with the concept drift posing a major challenge encountered by processing systems. Despite the proposition of numerous solutions to counteract the accuracy degeneration due to concept drift, the scientific community has not yet established a unified framework for evaluating the concept drift detection task. Existing research often relies on classification quality metrics, but these can be affected by multiple factors and may not reliably reflect drift detection quality. In this work, we present an in-depth overview of the relationship between metrics for quantifying drift detection quality and classification performance in synthetic nonstationary data streams. The proposed research studies eight drift detection quality metrics in relation to the classifier's performance across seven synthetic data stream generation tools, additionally considering drift dynamics as a factor. The studies aim to identify the most informative set of drift detection quality metrics and provide a deep understanding of the method's evaluation.

</details>


### [316] [FlagGAM: Rule-Based Generalized Additive Modeling for Explainable Tabular Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31189)
*Zijie Zhao,Roy E. Welsch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlagGAM is a rule-defined basis framework for tabular prediction that converts features into sparse, human-readable univariate bases (threshold flags, category flags, tail deviations, step functions) and combines them via a GAM-style predictor, achieving accuracy close to EBM and robustness to noise while maintaining interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes tabular domains need models that are simultaneously accurate, transparent, and robust to imperfect inputs (missing/noisy data).

Method: FlagGAM uses a Flag Core Module to produce sparse, interpretable univariate bases (threshold flags, category flags, tail deviations, categorical step functions), then combines them with a default additive GAM-style head or optional flexible heads for mixed-type prediction.

Result: Default FlagGAM is competitive with EBM in transparent additive mode, outperforms ridge regression on mixed-type tasks, and shows smaller AUROC degradation under missing/noisy perturbations compared to common baselines. Flexible heads approach tree-based accuracy.

Conclusion: FlagGAM offers a practical middle ground for tabular prediction balancing competitive accuracy, communicable rules, and robustness to input imperfections.

Abstract: Tabular prediction in high-stakes domains requires models that are accurate, transparent, and robust to imperfect inputs. We propose FlagGAM, a rule-defined basis framework that separates feature-level rule construction from prediction. A Flag Core Module converts numerical and categorical variables into sparse, human-readable univariate bases, including threshold flags, category-level flags, tail-deviation bases, and categorical step functions; a default additive head then combines these bases as a restricted GAM-style predictor. Rather than reducing triggered rules to compact count summaries, FlagGAM retains a sparse rule-basis matrix that supports mixed-type classification and regression, feature-specific weighting, and optional flexible prediction heads. Across tabular benchmarks, default FlagGAM remains close to EBM in transparent additive mode, improves substantially over ridge regression on mixed-type regression, and shows smaller AUROC degradation than common baselines under missing and noisy perturbations. Flexible heads further improve accuracy and approach strong tree-based baselines, with the caveat that the resulting model should be interpreted as a rule-basis representation followed by a nonlinear predictor rather than as a fully additive GAM. Overall, FlagGAM provides a practical middle ground for tabular settings that require competitive accuracy, communicable rules, and robustness to imperfect inputs.

</details>


### [317] [Student Capacity Moderates Knowledge Distillation Effectiveness: A Systematic Study Across ResNet Teacher-Student Pairs on CIFAR-10](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31191)
*Umut Onur Yasar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines how teacher-student capacity gaps affect knowledge distillation in ResNet-based CIFAR-10 classification, finding that student capacity matters more than teacher-student accuracy gap, that fixing a gradient clipping bug in Feature-KD changes comparative results, and that correct input-resolution-aware architecture has a much larger impact than any KD gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how teacher-student capacity relationships modulate knowledge distillation effectiveness, and to ensure reproducible and fair comparisons between Logit-KD and Feature-KD methods.

Method: Controlled experiments on CIFAR-10 with three ResNet teacher-student pairs (R50->R18, R34->R18, R50->R34), comparing Logit-KD and Feature-KD under 3 seeds with mean+/-std reporting, including bug-fix analysis for gradient clipping in Feature-KD.

Result: (1) R34 students benefit more from KD than R18 students despite comparable accuracy gaps; (2) Feature-KD after bug fix matches or outperforms Logit-KD in 2/3 pairs; (3) Correcting ResNet stem for 32x32 inputs improves teacher accuracy by >5pp, far exceeding any KD gain.

Conclusion: Student capacity is a key factor in KD gain; implementation correctness (gradient clipping bug) critically affects Feature-KD comparisons; input-resolution-aware architecture is a prerequisite that dwarfs KD effects.

Abstract: We investigate how teacher-student capacity relationships modulate knowledge distillation (KD) effectiveness in ResNet-based image classification on CIFAR-10. Across three teacher-student pairs -- R50->R18, R34->R18, and R50->R34 -- we compare Logit-KD and Feature-KD under controlled, reproducible conditions (3 seeds, mean+/-std reported throughout). We report three main findings. First, student capacity is a key moderating factor in distillation gain: R34 students benefit substantially more from KD than R18 students even when teacher-student accuracy gaps are comparable, with the strongest gain of +0.30pp observed for R50->R34 Feature-KD versus +0.18pp for R34->R18 Feature-KD and +0.00pp for R34->R18 Logit-KD. Second, implementation correctness critically affects Feature-KD: a gradient clipping bug that excluded projection layers suppressed Feature-KD performance and produced misleading comparisons with Logit-KD. After correction, Feature-KD matches or outperforms Logit-KD in two of three pairs, reaching 95.55% on R50->R34 against a baseline of 95.25%. Third, input-resolution-aware architecture is a prerequisite for effective distillation: correcting the ResNet stem for 32x32 inputs raises teacher accuracy by over 5pp -- an order of magnitude larger than any KD gain. All code and results are available at github.com/umutonuryasar/kd-capacity-gap.

</details>


### [318] [Positional versus Symbolic Attention Heads: Learning Dynamics, RoPE Geometry, and Length Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31558)
*Felipe Urrutia,Juan José Alegría,Cinthia Sanchez Macias,Jorge Salas,Cristian B. Calderon,Cristobal Rojas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies attention head dynamics in GPT-J trained on two structurally equivalent multi-hop reasoning tasks (number/positional and letter/symbolic). It shows successful learning produces pure heads (positional or symbolic), identifies distinct mechanistic demands between tasks, characterizes head functions via RoPE-based attention, and demonstrates that symbolic mechanisms generalize better to longer sequences while positional mechanisms face limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the internal mechanisms by which Transformer-based language models solve structured tasks, enabling safer deployment by predicting model behavior in novel scenarios.

Method: Train decoder-only Transformer (GPT-J) on two structurally equivalent multi-hop reasoning tasks (number/positional reasoning and letter/symbolic reasoning). Use a metric to classify attention-head behavior as positional or symbolic. Provide theoretical constructions showing how single-layer RoPE-based attention implements these functions. Formalize robustness through a notion of discrepancy.

Result: Successful learning leads to pure heads (positional or symbolic). Number task requires both positional and symbolic heads, while letter task requires only symbolic heads. Symbolic mechanisms extrapolate more reliably to longer sequences than positional mechanisms.

Conclusion: Despite structural equivalence between tasks, they impose different mechanistic demands. The discrepancy between positional and symbolic mechanisms explains differences in robustness to longer sequences, with symbolic mechanisms generalizing better.

Abstract: Transformer-based language models are widespread in today's society. As such, understanding the mechanisms by which they solve structured tasks and predicting how they may behave in novel scenarios is of great importance for safe deployment. We study the learning dynamics of attention heads in a controlled setting by training a decoder-only Transformer (GPT-J) on two structurally equivalent multi-hop reasoning tasks: a number task requiring positional reasoning and a letter task requiring symbolic reasoning. Using a recently introduced metric that classifies attention-head behavior as positional or symbolic for a given prompt, we show that successful learning is associated with the emergence of pure heads, i.e., heads that express themselves as either positional or symbolic. Despite the tasks' structural equivalence, they impose different mechanistic demands: the number task requires both positional and symbolic heads, whereas the letter task requires only symbolic heads. We then identify the computational roles of these heads, characterize the basic functions they implement, and give theoretical constructions showing how single-layer RoPE-based attention can realize these functions through geometrically interpretable query, key, and value operations. This analysis yields a quantitative separation between positional and symbolic mechanisms in their robustness to longer sequences, formalized through a novel notion of discrepancy. We empirically validate the resulting predictions in both controlled and real-world models, showing that symbolic mechanisms extrapolate more reliably to longer sequences while positional mechanisms face sharper limitations.

</details>


### [319] [Geometry-based Schrödinger Bridges for Trustworthy Multimodal Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31193)
*Jiayu Xiong,Jing Wang,Qi Zhang,Wanlong Wang,Jun Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GMF uses geometry-based transport correction (Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge) to evaluate multimodal data reliability, breaking the circular dependency of confidence-based methods by providing an independent reliability signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trustworthy fusion methods rely on model prediction confidence to judge data quality, creating a circular dependency: when a confident model is wrong, errors go undetected.

Method: Proposes Geometry-based Multimodal Fusion (GMF) using Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow. Squared initial velocity of transport correction serves as an independent reliability score for input data quality.

Result: GMF significantly improves robustness against severe sensor noise and semantic conflicts compared to confidence-based baselines.

Conclusion: Geometry-based reliability signals act as an independent judge, effectively flagging unreliable inputs even when the classifier is fooled, breaking the circular dependency loop.

Abstract: Real-world multimodal systems must be robust against low-quality data, such as sensor noise, incomplete multimodal data and conflicting inputs. However, existing trustworthy fusion methods rely on the model's own prediction confidence to judge data quality. This creates a circular dependency: when a model is confident but wrong, these methods fail to detect the error. To break this loop, we propose Geometry-based Multimodal Fusion (GMF). Instead of relying on predictions, we evaluate reliability by measuring how much transport correction the input needs in latent space. We implement Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow, where the squared initial velocity gives an efficient learned correction score. Valid data has low squared velocity magnitude, while noisy, incomplete data or conflicting data requires stronger transport correction. This geometry-based reliability signal acts as an independent judge, effectively flagging unreliable inputs even when the classifier is fooled. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GMF significantly improves robustness against severe sensor noise and semantic conflicts compared to confidence-based baselines.

</details>


### [320] [Beyond Additive Decompositions: Interpretability Through Separability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31200)
*Jinyang Liu,Munir Eberhardt Hiabu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSL is a new regression model that learns rank-1 product functions instead of additive components, avoiding signal cancellation from interactions, with theoretical guarantees and competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interpretable models (GAMs, SHAP, functional ANOVA) rely on additive representations that suffer from signal cancellation and off-support extrapolation when strong interactions exist, making their explanations unfaithful.

Method: TSL learns a sum of rank-1 products of univariate per-feature functions via a stagewise greedy procedure with orthogonal refitting, enforcing separability to avoid additive projection losses.

Result: TSL provides approximation-rate guarantees for functions with bounded mixed p-th order partial derivatives and competes with black-box models on regression benchmarks.

Conclusion: TSL offers a faithful and interpretable alternative to additive models by preserving interaction structure, with strong theoretical grounding and practical competitiveness.

Abstract: Interpretable machine learning requires models that are accurate and structurally faithful to the data.Existing explainability methods rely heavily on additive representations (e.g., Generalized Additive Models (GAMs), SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), functional ANOVA), which can suffer from signal cancellation and off-support extrapolation in the presence of strong interactions. We propose Tensor Separation Learning (TSL), a regression model that learns a sum of rank-1 products of univariate per-feature functions via a stagewise greedy procedure with orthogonal refitting. By enforcing separability, TSL avoids the information loss inherent in additive projections caused by marginalizing higher-order interactions. The learned TSL model can be fully reconstructed from first-order partial dependence functions, up to constant factors. This stage-wise correspondence ensures that the resulting visualizations are faithful to the fitted components. We establish approximation-rate guarantees for functions with bounded mixed $p$-th order partial derivatives and demonstrate that TSL competes with black-box models on regression benchmarks.

</details>


### [321] [Fixed-Point Masked Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31215)
*Andrea Miele,Yiming Qin,Alba Carballo-Castro,Justin Deschenaux,Pascal Frossard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FP-MGMs (CoFRe) replace part of the denoiser in Masked Generative Models with a fixed-point solver over shared attention layers, enabling adaptive depth with fewer parameters. It introduces cross-step consistency loss and three-state reuse (3SR) to improve masked generation quality, achieving better quality-cost trade-offs across modalities with reduced parameters, training time, and VRAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MGMs require full-sequence bidirectional transformers at every step, making training costly and degrading quality under low sampling budgets. Existing efficiency improvements still allocate fixed computation per refinement step.

Method: Fixed-Point Masked Generative Models (FP-MGMs) with: (1) fixed-point solver over shared attention layers for adaptive depth, (2) cross-step consistency loss to align representations across neighboring denoising steps, (3) three-state reuse (3SR) for warm-starting the solver by treating unchanged, still-masked, and newly revealed tokens differently. Pre-trained MGMs can be converted via fine-tuning.

Result: OpenWebText: 38.8% fewer parameters, 11.5% less training time, 16.9% less VRAM; perplexity improved from 830.8 to 101.8 (96 block passes). ImageNette: 48.6% less training time, 50.7% less VRAM; improved FID across all budgets tested.

Conclusion: CoFRe provides a practical framework for cheaper training and stronger low-budget masked generation across modalities.

Abstract: Masked Generative Models (MGMs) enable parallel decoding and achieve strong performance across modalities, but require full-sequence bidirectional transformers at every step, making training costly and degrading quality under low sampling budgets. Existing work improves efficiency via better samplers or cheaper fixed-depth denoisers, but they still allocate a fixed amount of denoiser computation to each refinement step. We introduce Fixed-Point Masked Generative Models (FP-MGMs), which replace part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver over shared attention layers to enable adaptive depth with fewer parameters. To make it more effective for masked generation, we first introduce a cross-step consistency loss, which aligns hidden representations at neighboring denoising steps and, second, three-state reuse (3SR) which warm-starts the solver using the previous solution by treating differently unchanged, still-masked, and newly revealed tokens respectively. Together, these components define our complete training-to-inference framework for fixed-point masked generation, \emph{CoFRe}. We also show that pre-trained MGMs can be converted into FP-MGMs with short fine-tuning, avoiding full retraining. Across modalities, CoFRe improves the quality and cost trade-off. On OpenWebText, CoFRe reduces parameters by 38.8\%, training time by 11.5\%, and VRAM by 16.9\%, while improving generative perplexity from 830.8 to 101.8 at a budget of $96$ transformer-block forward passes, compared to MDLM. In ImageNette, CoFRe reduces training time by 48.6\% and VRAM by 50.7\%, while improving FID in all sample budgets tested. Overall, CoFRe offers a practical framework for cheaper training and stronger low-budget masked generation.

</details>


### [322] [Multivariate Distributional Reinforcement Learning Using Sliced Divergences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31222)
*Baptiste Debes,Tinne Tuytelaars*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDRL extends distributional RL to multivariate settings by projecting return distributions onto one-dimensional slices, proving contraction properties and evaluating on multiple environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending distributional RL to multivariate settings is challenging because metrics don't generalize well and matrix discounting lacks contraction results.

Method: Lifts 1D divergences (Wasserstein, Cramér, MMD) to multivariate distributions via sliced projections; proves Bellman contraction for uniform and maximum slicing under different discounting schemes.

Result: Evaluated on toy chain, gridworld, and Atari games; SDRL supports single-sample Bellman updates with appropriate divergence choices.

Conclusion: Sliced DRL provides a tractable and theoretically grounded approach to multivariate distributional RL with proven contraction properties.

Abstract: Distributional reinforcement learning (DRL) models the full return distribution rather than expectations, but extending it to multivariate settings remains challenging. Many common metrics do not naturally generalize beyond one dimension or lose computational tractability, and the multivariate case introduces additional difficulties such as general matrix discounting, for which no contraction results are available. We introduce Sliced Distributional Reinforcement Learning (SDRL), which lifts tractable one-dimensional divergences to multivariate return distributions via projections. We prove Bellman contraction for uniform slicing under shared scalar discounting, and introduce a maximum-slicing variant with contraction under general dense discount matrices. SDRL supports a broad class of base divergences; we analyze Wasserstein, Cramér, and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), and characterize which SDRL variants suit the standard single-sample Bellman update used in distributional RL. We evaluate SDRL on a toy chain problem and a gridworld image-based environment as well as a subset of Atari games.

</details>


### [323] [Bifurcated Remaining Useful Life Prediction: A Hybrid Approach for Realistic Uncertainty Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31241)
*Xabier Belaunzaran,Antonio Nappa,Arkaitz Artetxe,Basilio Sierra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid prognostic framework using LSTM-autoencoder state classification, Conditional Weibull survival analysis for healthy regime, and Probabilistic NN with Monte Carlo Dropout for degraded regime, with continuous state probability weighting via sigmoid function, enabling uncertainty-aware RUL estimation on NASA C-MAPSS data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for uncertainty-aware RUL estimation that produces physically consistent uncertainty bands—high confidence near end-of-life and appropriately wider variance during early operation—for risk-informed maintenance decisions.

Method: 1) State-aware strategy bifurcating lifespan into healthy/degraded regimes. 2) LSTM-autoencoder (trained on RUL>150) monitors reconstruction error as state classifier. 3) Conditional Weibull Survival Analysis for healthy regime MRL. 4) Probabilistic NN with Monte Carlo Dropout for degraded regime (capturing aleatoric + epistemic uncertainties). 5) Calibrated sigmoid converts autoencoder output into continuous state probabilities for dynamic ensemble weighting.

Result: Generates physically consistent uncertainty bands with high-confidence predictions near end-of-life, accurately reflecting inherent variance of early operation.

Conclusion: The framework provides a robust tool for risk-informed maintenance by producing uncertainty-aware RUL estimates that align with physical degradation behavior.

Abstract: This study presents a novel hybrid prognostic framework for uncertainty-aware Remaining Useful Life (RUL) estimation in turbofan engines using the NASA C-MAPSS dataset. The framework employs a state-aware strategy that bifurcates the engines operational lifespan into "healthy" and "degraded" regimes. An LSTM-based autoencoder, trained strictly on nominal data (RUL > 150 cycles), monitors reconstruction error to act as a robust state classifier. For the healthy regime, a Conditional Weibull Survival Analysis is used for Mean Residual Life estimation. For the degraded regime, a Probabilistic Neural Network with Monte Carlo Dropout captures both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. Rather than using rigid binary labels, a calibrated sigmoid function converts the autoencoders output into continuous state probabilities, dynamically weighting the final ensemble prediction. The primary strength of this framework is its generation of physically consistent uncertainty bands, yielding high-confidence predictions near end-of-life while accurately reflecting the inherent variance of early operation, providing a robust tool for risk-informed maintenance.

</details>


### [324] [Spectral Reach: Understanding Neural Scaling as Progress into the Spectral Tail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31244)
*Konstantin Nikolaou,Jonas Scheunemann,Sven Krippendorf,Samuel Tovey,Christian Holm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "spectral position" — a measure of which eNTK eigenmodes drive loss reduction — and shows that learning shifts from dominant to tail eigenvalues. Larger models have greater "spectral reach" into the tail, explaining their lower loss, and feature learning is identified as a key mechanism enabling this reach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural scaling laws are empirically well-established but lack mechanistic understanding. The authors aim to close this gap by developing a scalable analysis tool to probe how model size affects learning dynamics.

Method: The authors define "spectral position" to track which eigenvalues of the empirical NTK are actively driving loss reduction during training. They apply this metric across scaling experiments with varying model sizes.

Result: Spectral position decreases over training (shift from dominant to tail eigenmodes). Larger models have greater "spectral reach" — they can exploit weaker spectral signals. Feature learning adaptively amplifies gradients, enabling this reach unlike frozen representations.

Conclusion: Larger models outperform because they sustain learning on weak spectral signals. Feature learning is critical for spectral reach, suggesting architecture and optimizer interventions to improve scaling.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws describe predictable power-law relationships between model size, dataset size, compute, and performance. While these laws guide the development of modern foundation models, the mechanisms underpinning them remain poorly understood, in part due to the absence of scalable analysis tools. To close this gap, we introduce "spectral position": a scalable measure of which eigenvalues of the empirical neural tangent kernel (eNTK) currently drive loss reduction. Applying this measure to scaling experiments, we find that spectral position decreases throughout training: learning shifts from dominant eigenmodes into the spectral tail. Larger models reach further into the tail than smaller models, revealing a size-dependent capacity we call "spectral reach". This suggests why larger models achieve lower losses: they sustain learning on weak spectral signals inaccessible to smaller models. We further identify feature learning as a key enabler of spectral reach. It adaptively amplifies gradient magnitudes as learning advances, sustaining progress where frozen representations stall. This points to concrete interventions through architecture and optimizer design.

</details>


### [325] [Toward Identifiable Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31245)
*Walter Nelson,Theofanis Karaletsos,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAEs are unstable across training runs; iSAE improves stability via architecture changes and achieves near-identifiable sparse codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAEs lack stability—different training runs produce different concept dictionaries and sparse codes—which undermines their interpretability and reliability.

Method: Identifies model properties causing instability, then introduces minimal architectural and training modifications to produce iSAE (identifiable SAE), a variant of TopK SAE.

Result: iSAE achieves lower reconstruction error and improved stability; learned dictionaries satisfy an approximate restricted isometry condition, making sparse codes near-identifiable.

Conclusion: Connecting SAEs with traditional dictionary learning explains the improvement; iSAE yields more stable and interpretable representations.

Abstract: Recently, sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as an attractive tool for interpreting and interacting with representations in practical neural networks. While it is common empirical folklore, we also show theoretically that SAEs are highly unstable: different training runs are likely to produce different concept dictionaries and sparse codes. We characterize the model properties that hinder the stability of real-world SAEs, and address each of these problems through minimal changes to the architecture and training procedure. Together, these changes yield two versions of an \textbf{i}dentifiable SAE (iSAE), a variant of the standard TopK SAE with lower reconstruction error and improved stability. We explain this improvement theoretically by connecting SAEs with traditional dictionary learning approaches, and show that the dictionaries learned in practice satisfy an approximate restricted isometry condition, rendering the corresponding sparse codes in those models near-identifiable.

</details>


### [326] [Fraud Type Decomposition and the Observation-Mechanism Taxonomy:Class-Specific Detection Limits in Payment Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31257)
*Gaurav Dhama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that treating fraud as a homogeneous binary variable is structurally incorrect and inefficient. It introduces a five-class observation-mechanism taxonomy, proves that estimating fraud by class strictly dominates pooled estimation, and identifies binding theoretical constraints for each class.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fraud detection methods incorrectly treat fraud as a homogeneous binary variable, ignoring that labels come from heterogeneous and imperfect observation processes. The authors aim to show this assumption is structurally flawed and leads to provable inefficiency.

Method: The authors introduce a five-class taxonomy based on censorship and labeling pipelines. They prove via Jensen penalty analysis that class-wise estimation strictly dominates pooled estimation. For each class, theoretical detection constraints are derived (e.g., label corruption, non-observability, feature non-informativeness).

Result: Fraud detection is fundamentally a collection of distinct estimation problems, each with its own observation structure and detection limit. Pooled estimation is provably less efficient than class-separated estimation.

Conclusion: Treating fraud as homogeneous is structurally incorrect; detection systems must account for heterogeneous observation mechanisms to achieve optimal efficiency.

Abstract: Fraud detection in payment networks relies on labels generated through heterogeneous and imperfect observation processes, yet existing approaches treat fraud as a homogeneous binary variable. We show that this assumption is structurally incorrect and leads to provable inefficiency. We introduce an observation-mechanism taxonomy that partitions fraud into five classes, each defined by a distinct censorship and labeling pipeline. We prove that estimating fraud rates separately by class and aggregating strictly dominates pooled estimation, with the efficiency gap characterized as a Jensen penalty arising from heterogeneous observation rates. For each class, we derive the binding theoretical constraint on detection, including endogenous label corruption, structural non-observability, and feature non-informativeness. These results establish that fraud detection is fundamentally a collection of distinct estimation problems, each governed by its own observation structure and detection limit.

</details>


### [327] [Lightweight CNN-Based Anomaly Detection for High Voltage Converter Modulators in the Spallation Neutron Source](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31259)
*Alberto D. Cencillo,Leonardo Concepción,Julián Luengo,Isaac Triguero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how architectural inductive bias—specifically the order of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing—affects fault detection in high-power pulsed converters using multi-channel sensor data, achieving state-of-the-art results on the SNS HVCM dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unscheduled trips of high-power pulsed converters cause significant downtime at large accelerator facilities (e.g., SNS). Existing deep-learning approaches use standard convolutional pipelines that entangle temporal and cross-channel operations from the first layer, providing no explicit mechanism to represent channel independence or structured inter-channel interaction, motivating a need for better architectural design.

Method: The authors systematically vary the order of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing in neural network architectures, and test whether per-pulse adaptive channel reweighting further improves sensitivity. They evaluate on the public HVCM dataset across four SNS subsystems (RFQ, DTL, CCL, SCL).

Result: The best variant achieves pooled AUC-PR of 0.816 and AUC-ROC of 0.934, outperforming state-of-the-art on most subsystems and five of six fault families. Ablations identify three dominant input channels and link per-fault-family performance to whether precursors manifest as amplitude shifts or subtler joint channel patterns.

Conclusion: Architectural inductive bias—specifically the ordering of temporal and cross-channel operations—significantly impacts fault detection performance. Adaptive channel reweighting further improves sensitivity, and different fault types benefit from different architectural designs depending on precursor manifestation.

Abstract: Unscheduled trips of high-power pulsed converters are a leading source of downtime at large accelerator facilities. At the Spallation Neutron Source (SNS), the High Voltage Converter Modulators (HVCMs) are consistently the second-largest contributor to lost beam time. Each HVCM pulse is recorded across sensor channels spanning currents, voltages, and magnetic fluxes, whose mutual interactions encode the operating state of the system. Fault precursors do not manifest uniformly across these channels: depending on fault type, they may alter the temporal structure of individual signals, change the statistical dependencies among channels, or both. Existing deep-learning approaches typically process multi-channel signals with standard convolutional pipelines that entangle temporal and cross-channel operations from the first layer, giving the model no explicit mechanism to represent channel independence or structured inter-channel interaction. We hypothesise that architectural inductive bias, specifically the ordering of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing, plays a central role in detection performance on this class of data. To test this, we vary the order in which these two operations are applied, and examine whether per-pulse adaptive channel reweighting further improves sensitivity. Evaluated on the public HVCM dataset across all four SNS subsystems (RFQ, DTL, CCL, SCL), our best variant achieves a pooled AUC-PR of 0.816 and AUC-ROC of 0.934, outperforming the state of the art on most subsystems and five of the six fault families. Ablations identify three dominant input channels and link per-fault-family performance to whether precursors manifest as amplitude shifts in individual channels or as subtler patterns requiring joint channel representations to surface.

</details>


### [328] [Algorithmic Recourse of In-Context Learning for Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31272)
*Wenshuo Dong,Jiaming Zhang,Shaopneg Fu,Hongbin Lin,Di Wang,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents the first study of algorithmic recourse for tabular data under in-context learning (ICL), proposing a zeroth-order framework called ASR-ICL that efficiently generates actionable and sparse recourse for black-box ICL models, with theoretical analysis showing bounded recourse and convergence toward classical solutions as context size increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As predictive models (e.g., credit approval) are deployed in high-stakes settings, there is a growing need for post-hoc recourse methods. However, algorithmic recourse for tabular decision-making under ICL remains unexplored, motivating this work.

Method: The authors propose ASR-ICL (Adaptive Subspace Recourse for In-Context Learning), a novel zeroth-order recourse framework that efficiently generates actionable and sparse recourse for black-box ICL models, naturally extending to multi-class tabular tasks.

Result: Experiments across multiple real-world datasets and models show ASR-ICL achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods with fewer queries. The empirical results confirm the predicted convergence behavior, supporting the theoretical analysis.

Conclusion: Recourse remains well-defined and bounded under ICL, converges toward classical solutions as context size increases, and ASR-ICL provides efficient recourse generation for black-box ICL models.

Abstract: As predictive models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings such as credit approval, there is a growing need for post-hoc methods that provide recourse to affected individuals. Many such models operate on tabular data, where features correspond to real-world attributes. Recently, in-context learning (ICL) has enabled large language models to perform tabular prediction by conditioning on labeled examples at inference time, without explicit training. However, algorithmic recourse for tabular decision-making under ICL remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first study of algorithmic recourse for tabular data under ICL. We carry out a theoretical analysis, showing that recourse remains well-defined and bounded, and we characterize how recourse converges toward classical solutions as the context size increases. In practice, we propose a novel zeroth-order recourse framework, Adaptive Subspace Recourse for In-Context Learning (ASR-ICL), that efficiently generates actionable and sparse recourse for black-box ICL models. The proposed framework naturally extends to multi-class tabular tasks. Experiments across multiple real-world datasets and models demonstrate that ASR-ICL achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods with fewer queries and empirically confirm the predicted convergence behavior, supporting our theoretical analysis.

</details>


### [329] [Interpretability Without Tradeoffs: Disentangling Polysemanticity At Equal Predictive Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31304)
*Doğukan Bağcı,Bernt Schiele,Simone Schaub-Meyer,Jonas Fischer,Robin Hesse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ELUDe is a method that improves DNN interpretability by disentangling mixed neuron signals into clear, monosemantic features without altering model outputs or degrading performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Individual neurons in DNNs encode multiple unrelated concepts, making interpretation difficult. Existing solutions like sparse autoencoders can separate mixed signals but degrade downstream performance.

Method: ELUDe reorganizes information flow between layers by rerouting concept-specific contributions while preserving the original computation by construction. It requires no training, no labels, and can be applied to pretrained models.

Result: Across vision models (DINOv2, supervised ViT-B/16), ELUDe improves interpretability, keeps downstream accuracy unchanged, runs efficiently, and supports practical uses like steering model representations.

Conclusion: ELUDe offers interpretability almost without a tradeoff — providing clearer, scalable, and actionable model insights with no loss in performance.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are widely used, but interpreting what they actually learn remains difficult. A major obstacle is that individual neurons often encode multiple unrelated concepts, obscuring the decision process of the network.
