While prior work, such as sparse autoencoders, can separate these mixed signals into more meaningful, "monosemantic" features, this typically requires altering the model in ways that can degrade downstream performance. To overcome this, we introduce ELUDe (explicit, lossless, unsupervised disentanglement), a method for improving the interpretability of DNNs while preserving their functional equivalence. ELUDe breaks latent representations into clear, inspectable sub-units that behave like interpretable features, while guaranteeing that the model's outputs remain exactly the same. It requires no explicit training, no labels, and can be applied to pretrained models. ELUDe works by reorganizing how information flows between layers, re-routing concept-specific contributions while preserving the original computation by construction. Across several vision models, including DINOv2 and supervised ViT-B/16, ELUDe improves interpretability, keeps downstream accuracy unchanged, runs efficiently, and supports practical uses such as steering model representations. In short, ELUDe offers interpretability (almost) without a tradeoff: clearer, scalable, and actionable model insights with no loss in performance.

</details>


### [330] [Survival Reinforcement Learning: Toward Scalable Self-Supervised RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31273)
*Franki Nguimatsia-Tiofack,Fabian Schramm,Théotime Le Hellard,Justin Carpentier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SRL is an online classification-based RL method that replaces contrastive losses to enable stable long-horizon goal-conditioned planning, outperforming CRL by 2-8x on locomotion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaled CRL struggles with long-horizon goal-conditioned planning due to the uniformity-tolerance dilemma in contrastive losses.

Method: SRL reformulates RL as survival value learning by maximizing dwell time at target goals, using online classification to bypass CRL's structural constraints and mitigate "bang-bang" control solutions.

Result: SRL matches CRL on manipulation and outperforms it by 2x to 8x on stable long-horizon locomotion tasks.

Conclusion: Classification-based methods like SRL may serve as a key primitive for scaling reinforcement learning.

Abstract: While self-supervised Contrastive Reinforcement Learning (CRL) has shown remarkable depth-scaling capabilities, successfully using networks over 64 layers, scaled CRL still struggles with long-horizon goal-conditioned planning due to the uniformity-tolerance dilemma inherent in contrastive losses. We introduce Survival Reinforcement Learning (SRL), an online classification-based alternative that extends the survival value learning framework by maximizing the agent's dwell time at target goals. SRL bypasses the structural constraints of CRL and mitigates the "bang-bang" control solutions inherent to survival frameworks, which often induce undesirable behavior in complex dynamical systems. Evaluated across diverse robotic benchmarks, scaled SRL matches state-of-the-art CRL on manipulation tasks and outperforms it by 2x to 8x on stable, long-horizon locomotion tasks. Our results provide strong additional evidence that classification-based methods may serve as a key primitive in the broader effort to scale reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [331] [A Unifying View of Variational Generative Wasserstein Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31369)
*Paul Caucheteux,Clément Bonet,Anna Korba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified theoretical framework called Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF) that shows many existing generative models can be derived as parametric JKO schemes for f-divergence objectives, extends this to IPMs and MMD, and empirically studies JKO regularization impact and parametric Wasserstein flows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified theoretical framework bridging various generative models (GANs, diffusion models, etc.) through the lens of Wasserstein gradient flows and the JKO scheme, clarifying their connections and enabling new algorithmic derivations.

Method: The authors formulate Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF) framework, showing existing methods are instances of parametric JKO schemes for f-divergence objectives. They extend the framework beyond f-divergence to IPMs and squared MMD, derive new JKO-based algorithms, and analyze parametric Wasserstein flows where dynamics are restricted to parametrized map-induced distributions.

Result: Established equivalences between several recently proposed algorithms; derived new JKO-based generative algorithms for IPMs and MMD; empirically demonstrated the impact of JKO regularization across various objectives.

Conclusion: The GWF framework unifies a broad class of generative models under Wasserstein gradient flow theory, reveals connections between existing methods, and provides a principled foundation for deriving new generative algorithms.

Abstract: Many modern generative models can be viewed as minimizing divergences between probability distributions, yet they rely on different algorithmic and geometric principles. Wasserstein gradient flows provide a continuous-time formulation for optimizing over distributions, and can be approximated through their implicit discretization via the Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (JKO) scheme. In this work, we present a unified theoretical framework for generative modeling based on Wasserstein gradient flows, which we refer to as Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF). We show that a broad class of existing methods can be derived as instances of parametric JKO schemes for $f$-divergence objectives, and we establish equivalences between several recently proposed algorithms. We extend this framework beyond f-divergence to Integral Probability Metrics and squared Maximum Mean Discrepancy, deriving new JKO-based generative algorithms, and clarifying their connections with GANs. We study empirically the impact of the JKO regularization for a wide set of objectives. Finally, we analyze parametric Wasserstein flows, where the dynamics are restricted to distributions induced by parametrized maps.

</details>


### [332] [Learning Parametric Nitrogen Fertilizer Response Curves Using Neuro Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31276)
*Giorgio Morales,John Sheppard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a neuro symbolic regression approach to learn interpretable parametric N-response curves for precision agriculture without predefined functional forms, using a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction strategy that recovers shared functional structures across management zones, achieving lower fitting errors than traditional models on real-world winter wheat data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately modeling crop response to Nitrogen fertilization is challenging in precision agriculture due to the limitations of existing approaches: predefined parametric forms are too rigid, while opaque machine learning models lack interpretability for discovering site-specific functional relationships.

Method: A neuro symbolic regression (SR) approach integrating a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction strategy. It constructs diverse input subsets, enforces consistency across them to recover robust symbolic skeletons, and fits them to observed data using a genetic algorithm.

Result: The SR approach recovers correct expressions even in data-scarce regimes on synthetic problems. On real-world winter wheat data, it learns distinct parametric N-response curves for different management zones, achieving lower fitting errors than traditional models (quadratic-plateau, exponential) while capturing diverse functional behaviors across spatial regions.

Conclusion: Neuro symbolic regression shows strong potential for discovering site-specific agronomic relationships, enabling informed decision-making in precision agriculture by combining interpretability with data-driven discovery.

Abstract: Accurately modeling crop response to Nitrogen (N) fertilization is a fundamental challenge in precision agriculture, as it impacts both economic returns and environmental sustainability. Existing approaches either rely on predefined parametric forms or opaque machine learning models, limiting their ability to interpret or discover site-specific functional relationships from data. In this work, we propose a neuro symbolic regression (SR) approach to learn parametric N-response curves without assuming a predefined functional form. Our approach integrates a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction strategy, enabling the discovery of shared functional structures across multiple subdomains or management zones (MZs). By constructing diverse input subsets and enforcing consistency across them, the method recovers robust symbolic skeletons that are subsequently fitted to observed data using a genetic algorithm. This framework was first evaluated on synthetic one-dimensional problems to assess its robustness under varying levels of epistemic uncertainty. The results demonstrate the ability of the proposed SR approach to recover correct expressions even in data-scarce regimes. In this work, we present the results of applying our method to real-world winter wheat data, learning distinct parametric N-response curves for different MZs within a field. The results show that the discovered expressions not only achieve lower fitting errors than traditional models such as quadratic-plateau and exponential functions, but also capture diverse functional behaviors across spatial regions. This demonstrates the potential that neuro SR has to enable the discovery of site-specific agronomic relationships and support informed decision-making in precision agriculture.

</details>


### [333] [Non-Asymptotic Convergence of Stochastic Iterative Algorithms: A Lyapunov Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31309)
*Zaiwei Chen,Siva Theja Maguluri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper surveys Lyapunov-based techniques for finite-time analysis of stochastic approximation algorithms, using generalized Moreau envelopes as universal Lyapunov functions. It covers contractive operators with i.i.d. noise, mean-square convergence guarantees, applications to SGD, linear SA, and RL algorithms (Q-learning, TD learning), and extensions to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive operators, dissipative operators, and high-probability bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified and self-contained framework for finite-time analysis of stochastic iterative algorithms (stochastic approximation) using Lyapunov-based methods, particularly addressing the need for a systematic approach that works across different norms and algorithm types, with special emphasis on reinforcement learning applications.

Method: The authors introduce generalized Moreau envelopes as universal Lyapunov functions for analyzing stochastic approximation algorithms. They first consider the standard setting where the operator is contractive with respect to some norm with i.i.d. noise, then extend to more complex scenarios including Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive operators, dissipative operators, and high-probability bounds.

Result: Demonstrates how the Lyapunov-based framework yields mean-square convergence guarantees and applies to stochastic gradient descent, linear SA, and value-based reinforcement learning algorithms like Q-learning and temporal-difference learning.

Conclusion: Presents a unified roadmap for finite-time analysis of SA and its applications (especially in RL), concluding with open problems for future research.

Abstract: We survey Lyapunov-based techniques for the finite-time analysis of stochastic iterative algorithms, also known as stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms, for solving fixed-point equations $\bar{F}(x)=x$, where the operator $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ can only be accessed through a noisy oracle. We first focus on the standard setting in which $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ is contractive with respect to some norm and the noise is i.i.d., and explain how generalized Moreau envelopes serve as universal Lyapunov functions, regardless of the underlying norm. We then show how this framework yields mean-square convergence guarantees and applies to stochastic gradient descent, linear SA, and value-based reinforcement learning algorithms such as Q-learning and temporal-difference learning. Finally, we discuss extensions to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive operators, dissipative operators, and high-probability bounds, and conclude with open problems. The goal is to present a unified and self-contained roadmap for the finite-time analysis of SA and its applications, especially in reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [334] [Graph Neural Networks Are Not Continuous Across Graph Resolutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31315)
*Christian Koke,Yuesong Shen,Abhishek Saroha,Marvin Eisenberger,Bastian Rieck,Michael Bronstein,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNNs are not continuous across graph convergence modes, causing different embeddings for similar graphs (e.g., same object at different resolutions). This is due to information-propagation schemes, and the authors propose a principled modification to restore continuity, validated experimentally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the conventional wisdom that GNNs are continuous with respect to natural graph convergence, and to address the practical problem that GNNs assign vastly different latent embeddings to graphs representing the same underlying object at different resolution scales.

Method: The authors identify a structural obstruction in commonly used information-propagation schemes that causes discontinuity. They then derive a principled modification to standard GNN architectures to equip models with continuity across scales.

Result: GNNs are not continuous under all natural modes of graph convergence. The proposed modification enables consistent integration of distinct resolutions and reliable generalization between them, validated through numerical experiments.

Conclusion: Contrary to conventional belief, GNNs exhibit discontinuities across graph scales. The proposed architectural fix restores continuity, enabling better generalization across resolutions.

Abstract: We show that contrary to conventional wisdom in the community, graph neural networks (GNNs) are not continuous with respect to all natural modes of graph convergence. As a result, GNNs may generate substantially different latent representations for graphs that are very similar. In particular they assign vastly different latent embeddings to graphs that represent the same underlying object at different resolution scales. We trace this failure of continuity back to a structural obstruction arising from commonly used information-propagation schemes. Building on this insight we then derive a principled modification to standard GNN architectures which equips models with continuity across scales. The proposed modification enables consistent integration of distinct resolutions and reliable generalization between them. We systematically validate our theoretical findings in a wide range of numerical experiments.

</details>


### [335] [Forgetting Has Neighbors: Localized Collateral Forgetting in Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31317)
*Polina Dolgova,Sebastian U. Stich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies "localized collateral forgetting" where unlearning methods cause prediction discrepancies concentrated near forget-set examples, and proposes Local Teacher Distillation using soft labels from a neighbor-trained teacher to mitigate this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard aggregate metrics for evaluating machine unlearning can hide localized failures. The authors aim to study this failure mode at the example level and understand why these discrepancies occur.

Method: The authors analyze pointwise discrepancy between unlearned and retrained models, identifying that surrogate targets used during unlearning (e.g., gradient ascent, random labeling) can be inconsistent with local prediction structure, causing propagation through shared representations. They propose Local Teacher Distillation: replacing random targets with soft labels from a small teacher trained only on retained neighbors of the forget set.

Result: On CIFAR-100 partial-class deletion, the local teacher method brings the unlearned model substantially closer to retraining, especially near the forget set, while maintaining competitive aggregate unlearning metrics.

Conclusion: Localized collateral forgetting is a real phenomenon in unlearning, and the proposed Local Teacher Distillation effectively mitigates it without sacrificing aggregate performance.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of selected training examples without full retraining. Standard evaluations often summarize unlearning quality with aggregate metrics, such as accuracy- and forgetting-based scores, which can hide localized failures. We study this failure mode at the example level by comparing the predictions of an unlearned model to those of the model retrained after deletion. We show that this pointwise discrepancy can be highly non-uniform: for gradient-ascent and random-labeling methods, with and without retain-set fine-tuning, it grows with geometric proximity to the forget set. We call this phenomenon localized collateral forgetting. Our analysis identifies a mechanism behind the effect: surrogate targets used during unlearning can be inconsistent with the local prediction structure induced by retraining, and this inconsistency propagates through shared representations to nearby examples. Motivated by this mechanism, we propose Local Teacher Distillation, a simple mitigation strategy that replaces random targets with soft labels from a small teacher trained only on retained neighbors of the forget set. On CIFAR-100 partial-class deletion, this local teacher brings the unlearned model substantially closer to retraining, especially near the forget set, while maintaining competitive aggregate unlearning metrics.

</details>


### [336] [Generalized Intention Modeling in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31318)
*Mateusz Odrowaz-Sypniewski,Jasmine Bayrooti,Ajay Shankar,Amanda Prorok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A task-adaptive opponent modeling framework that learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in multi-agent reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing opponent modeling methods assume fixed intent representations (e.g., next action or future state) are universally effective, but intentions are actually task- and environment-dependent, limiting performance.

Method: Proposes a task-adaptive framework that learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations, plus a new intention representation maximizing mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns.

Result: Consistently matches or exceeds state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks; provides insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

Conclusion: Task-adaptive opponent modeling is necessary because no single intent representation works universally; performance-driven mixture and mutual information maximization improve decision-making in competitive multi-agent settings.

Abstract: Modeling an opponent's intent is critical for effective decision-making in non-cooperative, competitive, and general-sum multi-agent reinforcement learning. Existing opponent modeling methods encode intent using an embedding derived from episode information chosen a priori, such as the opponent's next action or a future environment state, and use this to guide the ego-agent's behavior. These approaches assume that the chosen information is universally representative of intent; however, we show empirically that this is not the case as intentions are often task- and environment-dependent. To address this, we introduce a task-adaptive opponent modeling framework that learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations. We further introduce a new intention representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns, thereby capturing opponent information that is most directly relevant to performance. Our approach consistently matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and yields insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

</details>


### [337] [Trading Complexity for Expressivity Through Structured Generalized Linear Token Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31367)
*Erwan Fagnou,Paul Caillon,Blaise Delattre,Alexandre Allauzen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified framework for token mixing layers in causal language models, separating direct input-output influence from recurrent information propagation, and introduces new recurrence patterns that trade runtime for expressivity in a principled way, validated on synthetic tasks and language modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore new trade-offs between decoding speed, memory requirements, and cache size in token mixing layers for causal generation, by unifying attention and state-space models under a general recurrence framework.

Method: The authors propose a unified framework separating two features: (i) direct influence of inputs on outputs in one generation step, and (ii) recurrent propagation through past outputs. They generalize recurrence equations to allow each state to depend on multiple past states, design new recurrence patterns with provable complexity, and provide theoretical expressivity analysis.

Result: New recurrence patterns that achieve desired complexity bounds with principled trade-offs between runtime and expressivity, validated on synthetic tasks and language modeling.

Conclusion: The framework provides a unified toolkit for understanding and designing efficient and expressive token mixers across different model families.

Abstract: Token mixing layers play a key role in how language models can learn and generate long-range dependencies. Their efficiency relies on the necessary trade-off between decoding speed and the memory requirements, along with the cache size. Considering causal generation, this paper explores new trade-offs thanks to a unified framework which separates two crucial features: (i) the direct influence of inputs on outputs in one generation step; (ii) the recurrent propagation of information through past outputs. This framework encompasses major architectures such as attention and state-space models, but also generalizes the recurrence equations by allowing each state to depend on multiple past states rather than only the immediate predecessor. By introducing structure, we design new recurrence patterns that provably achieve the desired complexity, while providing theoretical insights on their expressivity -- trading runtime for expressivity in a principled way. Empirical validation is performed on synthetic tasks, along with language modeling. Together, these results provide a unified toolkit for the understanding and design of efficient and expressive token mixers across model families.

</details>


### [338] [Softsign: Smooth Sign in Your Optimizer For Better Parameter Heterogeneity Handling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31371)
*Dmitrii Feoktistov,Timofey Belinsky,Andrey Veprikov,Amir Zainullin,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SoftSignum, a smooth relaxation of sign-based optimization that replaces hard sign maps with a temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, enabling flexible transitions between sign-like and SGD-like updates, with theoretical convergence guarantees and strong empirical results including LLM pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sign-based optimizers suffer from fixed-magnitude updates that decouple from gradient magnitudes and ignore parameter heterogeneity, causing oscillation and poor terminal convergence.

Method: Proposes SoftSignum (soft-sign relaxation with temperature control) and SoftMuon (matrix-valued extension), with an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule, grounded in a generalized geometry-relaxation framework via strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates.

Result: Consistent improvements over hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW across diverse deep learning tasks, including LLM pretraining.

Conclusion: SoftSignum and SoftMuon effectively address the oscillation problem of sign-based optimizers while maintaining their benefits, with theoretical convergence guarantees in stochastic non-convex settings.

Abstract: Sign-based and LMO-inspired optimizers have recently attracted substantial attention in deep learning due to their strong performance and low memory footprint. However, their fixed-magnitude updates can hurt terminal convergence: they decouple update mechanisms from gradient magnitudes and fail to account for parameter heterogeneity, often leading to oscillation rather than convergence. We propose SoftSignum, a smooth relaxation of sign-based optimization that replaces the hard sign map with a temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, enabling a parameter-wise transition from sign-like updates to magnitude-sensitive SGD-like steps. We complement it with an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule and extend the same principle to matrix-valued optimizers, obtaining SoftMuon. We also develop a generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates, proving convergence in stochastic non-convex setting. Experiments on diverse deep learning tasks, including LLM pretraining, show that SoftSignum and SoftMuon consistently improve over their hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW.

</details>


### [339] [Constrained Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning with Max-Min Criterion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31388)
*Giseung Park,Hyunyoung Nam,Woohyeon Byeon,Amir Leshem,Youngchul Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning framework that integrates max-min fairness with explicit constraint satisfaction, supported by theoretical foundations, convergence analysis, and empirical validation across several domains including building control, locomotion, and traffic management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing max-min MORL approaches promote fairness among multiple objectives but are limited in applicability, especially when constraints must be incorporated alongside fairness considerations.

Method: The authors develop a MORL framework that combines the max-min criterion with explicit constraint satisfaction, establishing theoretical foundations and convergence guarantees, then validate it in tabular settings and three simulation domains.

Result: The proposed method achieves effective balance between fairness and constraint satisfaction, validated through convergence analysis and experiments in tabular settings as well as practical domains (building thermal control, locomotion control, traffic management).

Conclusion: The framework successfully extends max-min MORL to handle constraints, demonstrating both theoretical soundness and practical applicability across diverse multi-objective decision-making problems.

Abstract: Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) extends standard RL by optimizing policies with respect to multiple, often conflicting, objectives. While max-min MORL has emerged as an effective approach for promoting fairness, its applicability remains limited, particularly when constraints must be incorporated. In this paper, we propose a MORL framework that integrates the max-min criterion with explicit constraint satisfaction. We establish a theoretical foundation for the proposed framework and validate the resulting algorithm through convergence analysis and experiments in tabular settings. We further demonstrate the practical relevance of our approach in simulated building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion control, and greenhouse-gas-emission-aware traffic management. Across these domains, our method effectively balances fairness and constraint satisfaction in multi-objective decision-making.

</details>


### [340] [Fixed Universal Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31423)
*Jingwen Liu,Alexandr Andoni,Daniel Hsu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Universal transformers can simulate any transformer in a given class via input embedding, with fixed internal parameters—analogous to a universal Turing machine. Randomly initialized transformers are almost surely universal, suggesting expressive power lies in input representation rather than learned weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether a single fixed transformer can simulate any other transformer in a given class, analogous to universal Turing machines, and to understand where transformers' expressive power truly resides.

Method: The authors propose explicit sparse constructions for universality with sufficiently large embedding dimensions, and prove that universality is generic (randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely). They validate empirically on parenthesis balancing and multi-hop reasoning tasks.

Result: Universal transformers exist under suitable conditions; random initialization yields universality with high probability; empirical validation on algorithmic tasks supports the theory.

Conclusion: Much of a transformer's expressive power may reside in its input representation rather than its learned weights, challenging assumptions about where model capabilities come from.

Abstract: We introduce \emph{universal transformers}: fixed transformers that can simulate any transformer in a given class via a suitable input embedding. Analogous to a universal Turing machine, the input embedding encodes a description of the target model while all internal parameters remain fixed. We provide explicit sparse constructions achieving universality when the embedding dimension is sufficiently large, and further show that universality is generic: randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely, which aligns with recent empirical results of Zhong and Andreas (2024). We empirically validate our theory on the algorithmic tasks of parenthesis balancing and multi-hop reasoning. Our results suggest that much of a transformer's expressive power may reside in its input representation rather than its learned weights.

</details>


### [341] [DG-CoLearn: An Efficient Collaborative Learning Framework for Dynamic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31427)
*Ashley Hoi-Ting Au,Zikun Zhang,Ligang He,Qiang Ni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DG-CoLearn is a privacy-preserving collaborative dynamic graph learning framework that uses incremental snapshot processing to achieve up to 33.8× training speedup and 27.4× communication reduction while improving prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DGL methods suffer from computational overhead due to full-snapshot retraining and lack support for collaborative settings with partitioned data where cross-partition edges exist but direct graph structure sharing violates privacy.

Method: DG-CoLearn employs incremental graph snapshot processing that focuses computation only on temporally updated graph regions, preserves historical information via temporal modelling, and uses a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism for multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information.

Result: 33.8× faster training, 27.4× communication reduction, up to 13.36% F1 improvement on node classification, and up to 8.27% MAP improvement on link prediction.

Conclusion: DG-CoLearn effectively bridges efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

Abstract: Dynamic graph learning (DGL) is essential for modelling evolving graph data, but existing methods suffer from significant computational overhead due to repeated full-snapshot retraining and are not well-suited for collaborative settings with partitioned data. In realistic graph systems, cross-partition edges are unavoidable, but direct sharing of graph structure between clients may violate privacy constraints. We propose DG-CoLearn, a client-oblivious collaborative dynamic graph learning framework built on incremental graph snapshot processing, which focuses computation on graph regions affected by temporal updates while preserving historical information through temporal modelling. This incremental design is consistently applied across the entire graph processing pipeline, including a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism to enable accurate multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DG-CoLearn achieves up to 33.8$\times$ speedup in training time and 27.4$\times$ reduction in communication overhead, while consistently improving predictive performance on both node classification (up to 13.36% F1 improvement) and link prediction (up to 8.27% MAP improvement) tasks. These results highlight the effectiveness of DG-CoLearn in bridging efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

</details>


### [342] [Flow map learning in nonlinear vector autoregressive models: influence of the feature-library structure on the training error](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31438)
*Markus Gross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates identifiability in NVAR/NG-RC models, showing that training error scales with time resolution based on whether the feature library exactly or approximately represents flow map Lie-series coefficients. For polynomial systems, error depends on temporal resolution, nonlinear degree, and delay terms—but delays improve long-horizon forecasts only when sufficient nonlinearity exists, revealing a pitfall where small training error coexists with poor generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the identifiability conditions and scaling laws of training error in nonlinear vector autoregressive models (NVAR/NG-RC) for forecasting dynamical systems, and to explain when small training error fails to guarantee good generalization.

Method: The authors derive asymptotic scaling laws for training error as a function of time resolution, analyzing whether the feature library can exactly or only approximately represent Lie-series coefficients of the flow map. They study monomial and Fourier libraries for polynomial vector fields, and examine the roles of temporal resolution, nonlinear degree, and delay terms.

Result: Training error follows characteristic pre-asymptotic scaling laws dependent on feature library expressiveness. Delay terms reduce one-step error but only improve long-horizon forecasts when the library has sufficient nonlinearity. Model mismatch can yield small training error yet poor generalization.

Conclusion: Careful feature library design is critical for NVAR/NG-RC models; small training error does not guarantee good forecasting, especially when the model class mismatches the true dynamics.

Abstract: Time series forecasting often requires learning nonlinear and time-delayed dependencies. A paradigmatic class of forecasting models are nonlinear vector autoregressive processes (NVAR), also known as next-generation reservoir computers (NG-RCs). These models approximate the Koopman operator on the space spanned by their explicit feature library. We consider the identifiability problem for learning Markovian nonlinear dynamical systems and show that the training error as a function of time resolution follows characteristic (pre-)asymptotic scaling laws. These laws depend on whether the feature library can represent the early Lie-series coefficients of the flow map (propagator) exactly or merely approximately. For dynamical systems governed by polynomial vector fields, we demonstrate the mechanism for NVAR/NG-RC models with monomial and Fourier feature libraries. We determine the dependence of the training error on the temporal resolution, the involved nonlinear degree, and the number of delay terms. While delay terms reduce the optimal one-step training error, they improve long-horizon forecasts only when the library provides sufficient nonlinearity. Thus, small training error coexists with weak generalization as the model class is mismatched to the true data-generating process. Numerical experiments on various chaotic dynamical systems confirm the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [343] [DRIFT: Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Efficient Multi-Turn Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31455)
*Jian Mu,Tianyi Lin,Chengwei Qin,Zhongxiang Dai,Yao Shu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRIFT proposes a framework that decouples rollout from optimization by using offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy with importance weighting, achieving multi-turn RL performance with SFT-level efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online RL effectively handles multi-turn dynamics but is expensive due to full correction trajectory generation, while offline SFT is efficient but suffers from distribution shift and behavioral collapse. There's a need for a method that bridges this gap.

Method: DRIFT operationalizes the insight that KL-regularized RL equals importance-weighted supervised learning. It samples offline trajectories from a fixed reference policy, computes return-based importance weights, and optimizes via weighted SFT on the collected dataset.

Result: DRIFT matches or exceeds multi-turn RL baselines while maintaining the training efficiency and simplicity of standard supervised fine-tuning.

Conclusion: DRIFT effectively bridges the gap between online RL and offline SFT, achieving strong multi-turn interactive performance without the prohibitive cost of online trajectory generation.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed in multi-turn interactive settings where users or environments can iteratively provide lightweight feedback. Unfortunately, optimizing such behavior presents a sharp dilemma in practice: online reinforcement learning is able to effectively address multi-turn dynamics but is prohibitively expensive due to the cost of generating full correction trajectories at every update, whereas offline supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is efficient but suffers from distribution shift and behavioral collapse. To this end, we novelly propose DRIFT (Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning), a framework that operationalizes the theoretical insight that the KL-regularized RL objective is equivalent to importance-weighted supervised learning. DRIFT decouples rollout from optimization by sampling offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, deriving return-based importance weights, and optimizing the policy via weighted SFT on the resulting dataset. Empirically, we demonstrate that DRIFT matches or exceeds the performance of multi-turn reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining the training efficiency and simplicity of standard supervised fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/2020-qqtcg/DRIFT.

</details>


### [344] [Balanced LoRA: Removing Parameter Invariance to Accelerate Convergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31484)
*Valérie Castin,Kimia Nadjahi,Pierre Ablin,Gabriel Peyré*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA's overparameterization leads to different condition numbers across equivalent low-rank factorizations. BaLoRA improves convergence by projecting onto a balanced manifold, achieving faster training and better performance with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA is overparameterized—multiple low-rank factor pairs yield the same weight matrix but exhibit different condition numbers, which impacts convergence rates.

Method: BaLoRA (Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation) projects LoRA iterates onto a balanced manifold during training to improve conditioning of the loss landscape while preserving the adapted matrix, with a computationally lightweight projection step.

Result: BaLoRA converges faster than standard LoRA and achieves superior performance across multiple fine-tuning tasks.

Conclusion: The conditioning of low-rank factor pairs matters for convergence; balancing them via projection yields practical improvements in fine-tuning efficiency and performance.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the most widely adopted method for fine-tuning large language models. Notably, LoRA is inherently overparameterized: multiple pairs of low-rank factors can yield the same adapted weight matrix. We show--both theoretically and empirically--that these pairs exhibit significantly different condition numbers. As a result, converging to different loss minimizers directly impacts the convergence rate of LoRA. Building on this observation, we introduce Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation (BaLoRA), a variant of LoRA that projects iterates onto a balanced manifold. This manifold improves the conditioning of the loss landscape while preserving the adapted matrix. The projection step is computationally lightweight and integrates seamlessly into existing fine-tuning pipelines. Empirically, BaLoRA converges faster than standard LoRA and achieves superior performance across a range of fine-tuning tasks.

</details>


### [345] [Graphical einops: bridging tensor networks and computation graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31485)
*Vincent Wang-Maścianica,Nikhil Khatri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a formal graphical calculus for tensor programming (based on einops) that represents tensor axes as nested graded tubes, enabling diagrammatic proofs of tensor identities and equivariance, with applications to optimizing attention masks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Architecture diagrams in deep learning are merely representational; tensor identities are still proved using prose and manual axis manipulation. There is a need for a formal, proof-enabling graphical language that captures the structural fragment of tensor programming.

Method: The authors develop a graphical calculus where tensor axes are represented as nested graded tubes around a base type. Tube boundaries recover undirected tensor-network views while interiors retain operational computation graph semantics. The key rewrite rule is "grade-naturality" (sliding spectacles over tubes).

Result: Equivariance proofs become short diagrammatic derivations. The rewrite system can convert attention masks into pre-processing operations, recovering efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.

Conclusion: The proposed graphical calculus bridges the gap between intuitive diagrams and formal proofs in deep learning, providing a rigorous foundation for tensor program transformations with practical benefits for attention mechanism optimization.

Abstract: Architecture diagrams are ubiquitous in deep learning, but they are usually only representational: the tensor-program identities they suggest are still proved by prose and tensor-axis manipulation. We introduce a formal graphical calculus for the structural fragment of tensor programming underlying einops, making such diagrams proof-enabling. Our calculus represents tensor axes as nested graded tubes around a base type. The tube boundary recovers the undirected tensor-network view of axes, while the directed interior retains the operational reading of computation graphs. The key rewrite is grade-naturality: sliding spectacles over tubes. Standard equivariance proofs become short diagrammatic derivations. We additionally demonstrate how our rewrite system may be applied to convert attention masks into pre-processing operations, recovering efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.

</details>


### [346] [Assign and Add: A Mechanistic Study of Compositional Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31497)
*Brady Exoo,Alberto Bietti,John Sous*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies how transformers compositionally generalize by analyzing a controlled setting of variable assignment and modular addition. The authors find that transformers reuse a modular addition module regardless of input source, observe three learning phases (modular addition → variable assignment structure → refinement), and provide a theoretical framework explaining compositional generalization as a natural outcome of internal mechanism compositionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how transformers compose skills for complex tasks unseen during training, specifically the mechanism behind compositional generalization which remains elusive.

Method: The authors use a controlled setting with variable assignment and modular addition, partition training data into disjoint sets, conduct mechanistic analysis of transformer internals, and empirically study training dynamics across three learning phases. They also develop a theoretical framework.

Result: The same modular addition MLP module is used regardless of whether inputs are direct or indirect (via variable assignment). Learning proceeds in three phases: modular addition first, then variable assignment structure, then refinement for hard sequences. Compositional generalization emerges naturally.

Conclusion: Compositional generalization can be a natural consequence of the compositionality of internal mechanisms in transformers, rather than requiring special architectural design.

Abstract: Large language models are able to compose skills in order to perform complex tasks, many of which might not have been seen during training. The details of how exactly this composition occurs remain elusive. In this paper, we study a mechanism for compositional generalization in transformers by considering a simple controlled setting involving variable assignment and modular addition. By partitioning our training data into disjoint sets, we observe that small transformers are able to generalize to previously unseen combinations of variables and numbers. Our mechanistic analysis shows that the same ``modular addition'' MLP module is used whether the inputs are given directly or indirectly through a separate variable assignment mechanism. We also analyze the training dynamics from an empirical lens, which reveals three phases of learning: first, modular addition is learned, then the structure required for variable assignment, and finally a refinement phase where the model generalizes to some hard sequences not seen in training. Finally, we provide a theoretical framework to explain how compositionality emerges from training dynamics. These results suggest that compositional generalization can be a natural consequence of the compositionality of internal mechanisms in~transformers.

</details>


### [347] [Scalable Inference-Time Annealing with Surrogate Likelihood Estimators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31498)
*Daniel Peñaherrera,Rishal Aggarwal,David Ryan Koes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SITA is a scalable inference-time annealing method that retrains flow-based models to generate molecular samples at lower temperatures using energy-based surrogate likelihoods, avoiding expensive divergence computations and achieving state-of-the-art performance on peptide systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently sampling Boltzmann distributions of molecules is a long-standing challenge. Existing diffusion model-based annealing methods require computing divergence over the score field for importance weights, which is intractable for larger molecular systems.

Method: SITA retrains flow-based models along a temperature ladder using an energy-based model to provide fast surrogate likelihoods, avoiding costly divergence terms during inference-time annealing.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide, while avoiding costly divergence computations.

Conclusion: Scalable inference-time annealing (SITA) offers a tractable and efficient alternative for sampling Boltzmann distributions, eliminating the intractable divergence term that limited prior methods.

Abstract: A long standing challenge in computational chemistry and biophysics is efficiently sampling the Boltzmann distribution of molecules. Advances in generative modeling have been proposed to address the limitations of conventional sampling techniques by eliminating the computational cost of simulation. A promising direction is iteratively finetuning diffusion models along a temperature ladder whereby training data is generated via importance sampling during inference-time annealing. Unfortunately, these methods require computing a divergence over the score field to estimate importance weights, rendering them intractable for larger systems. Here we present scalable inference-time annealing (SITA), which retrains flow-based models to generate samples at progressively lower temperatures using an energy-based model to facilitate fast surrogate likelihoods. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms. Our code is available at: https://github.com/countrsignal/sita.git

</details>


### [348] [When Are Multimodal Predictions Biologically Supported? A Diagnostic Evaluation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31504)
*Dylan Steiner,Gustavo Arango-Argoty,Gerald Sun,Etai Jacob*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DECAT is a model-agnostic post-hoc framework that classifies multimodal representations into four diagnostic scenarios to distinguish shared biology, modality-specific biology, and confounding, validated on synthetic and real TCGA data across multiple model classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal oncology models can be accurate but fail to reveal whether their predictions stem from shared biology, modality-specific signals, or spurious correlations due to confounders.

Method: DECAT uses five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure to classify representations into four diagnostic scenarios, operating post-hoc on learned representations without requiring confounder labels.

Result: Validated on over 2,500 synthetic representations and 8,979 TCGA patients; entangled models like CLIP detect shared biology well but falsely claim it when absent, especially with stronger confounding. DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC.

Conclusion: DECAT provides a principled, confounder-label-free method to diagnose what multimodal models learn, revealing that high accuracy can mask spurious correlations and that stronger representations may yield more confidently incorrect diagnoses.

Abstract: Multimodal models in oncology can produce accurate predictions, but accurate prediction does not reveal whether the model has learned biology that is shared across modalities, biology confined to one modality, or spurious correlations that reflect confounders rather than genuine biology. We introduce DECAT, a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation framework that classifies multimodal representations into four diagnostic scenarios for a given task and modality, using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure. The framework operates on learned representations, requires no knowledge of which specific confounder is present, and returns indeterminate when the evidence is insufficient. We validate DECAT on synthetic data across four multimodal model classes (over 2,500 trained representations) and on real data from 8,979 TCGA patients, evaluating both multimodal embeddings and five pretrained pathology foundation models. Entangled models (e.g., CLIP) achieve near-perfect shared biology detection but falsely claim shared biology in the majority of cases where it is absent on real foundation model embeddings. This false claim rate increases with confound strength so that larger cohorts and stronger representations produce more confident but still incorrect diagnoses. Applied to both multimodal TCGA embeddings and five pathology foundation models without paired RNA, DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC without requiring the confounder labels, as confirmed by post-hoc stratification.

</details>


### [349] [On the Relationship Between Activation Outliers and Feature Death in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31518)
*Elana Simon,Etowah Adams,James Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAEs suffer from "feature death" where many features never activate, caused by dimension-level activation outliers misaligning features with the activation mean. This death rate is predicted by a simple metric (γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖), and mean-centering preprocessing eliminates the problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature death in SAEs wastes dictionary capacity and can reintroduce superposition, yet its causes were poorly understood. The authors aim to explain why death rates vary dramatically across models (0% on GPT-2 vs 70%+ on AlphaFold3) and propose a principled solution.

Method: The authors identify dimension-level activation outliers as the root cause, formalized as γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖. They analyze 454 model-layer combinations across diverse domains and measure initial death rates for both TopK and ReLU activation functions. They test mean-centering as a preprocessing step to eliminate outlier-induced death.

Result: γ predicts initial death rates with Spearman ρ = 0.89 (TopK) and 0.82 (ReLU). Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, which is prohibitively slow at high γ. Mean-centering eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models.

Conclusion: Feature death in SAEs is caused by activation outliers shifting pre-activations at initialization. Mean-centering provides a principled, effective preprocessing fix that eliminates the issue across diverse model types.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose neural network activations into interpretable features, but many learned features never activate, a problem called feature death that wastes dictionary capacity and can reintroduce superposition. Death rates vary dramatically between models: near-zero on GPT-2, over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations. We find that dimension-level activation outliers (dimensions whose mean magnitude is large relative to per-token variation) cause this by shifting pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean. Features anti-aligned with the mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire. We formalize outlier severity as $γ= \|μ\|/\|σ\|$; it predicts initial death rates (Spearman $ρ= 0.89$ for dead-by-TopK, $0.82$ for dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer combinations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models. Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, a process that is prohibitively slow at high $γ$. Mean-centering (subtracting the activation mean) sidesteps this and eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models, confirming the mechanism and providing a principled basis for when and why this preprocessing step is necessary.

</details>


### [350] [Chem-PerturBridge: a harmonized compendium of small molecule perturbation transcriptomic effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31522)
*Artur Szałata,Olga Novitskaia,Maiia Shulman,Matthew Mella,Altynbek Zhubanchaliyev,Fabian J. Theis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chem-PerturBridge is a harmonized resource of over 37k compounds, 136 cell contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types, enabling cross-dataset signature evaluation and improved compound representation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling are fragmented across technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines, hindering large perturbation model development.

Method: The authors harmonized multi-dataset transcriptomic data into Chem-PerturBridge with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects, then evaluated cross-dataset agreement and pretrained compound representations.

Result: Matched same-compound conditions show weak fine-grained logFC agreement across datasets but stable direction agreement. Pretrained embeddings on Chem-PerturBridge outperform L1000-only, Morgan fingerprints, and baselines under held-out evaluations.

Conclusion: Chem-PerturBridge supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data.

Abstract: Large perturbation models require training data encompassing chemical, cellular, and assay diversity. Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling, however, are fragmented across technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines. We introduce Chem-PerturBridge, a harmonized multi-dataset resource comprising over 37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types, with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. We use the resource to evaluate matched-condition agreement across datasets and replicate agreement within datasets. Matched same-compound conditions generally show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings and magnitudes across most dataset pairs, often falling below same-context different-compound baselines. In contrast, logFC direction agreement is substantially more stable and usually exceeds these baselines. We further evaluate Chem-PerturBridge as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning. Under a compound-held-out OP3 evaluation split, embeddings pretrained on Chem-PerturBridge improve over L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and the descriptor-free OP3 baseline across metrics. An extensive molecule-holdout evaluation across 11 datasets further shows that models trained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match those that are not. Chem-PerturBridge therefore supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data.

</details>


### [351] [Value Functions as Supermartingale Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31524)
*Alessandro Abate,Daniel Contro,Mirco Giacobbe,Agustín Martínez-Suñé,Diptarko Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper bridges certification methods for stochastic systems (using supermartingale certificates) with reinforcement learning for ω-regular tasks by showing that value functions of policies that almost-surely satisfy ω-regular properties encode Streett supermartingale certificates, enabling certificate synthesis via RL across finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Certification methods provide formal proofs for ω-regular properties using supermartingale certificates, but RL methods for ω-regular tasks typically lack formal guarantees except for finite state/action spaces. The authors aim to bridge these two research lines.

Method: The paper establishes a theoretical connection showing that under an appropriate reward design, the value function of a policy that almost-surely satisfies an ω-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate. This is validated experimentally on finite MDPs.

Result: The results hold for finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces, suggesting a principled route to certificate synthesis via RL.

Conclusion: The work bridges certification and RL for ω-regular tasks, enabling formal guarantees for learned policies through certificate synthesis derived from value functions.

Abstract: Certification methods for stochastic systems provide sufficient proof rules, based on real-valued supermartingale certificates, to determine the almost-sure satisfaction of $ω$-regular properties (and therefore of linear temporal logic) over general state spaces, encompassing both countably infinite and continuous state spaces. Conversely, reinforcement learning (RL) methods for $ω$-regular tasks have received considerable attention, but they typically lack formal guarantees that the learned policy satisfies the specification, except possibly for finite state and action spaces. We bridge these two lines of research by establishing a novel theoretical connection: under an appropriate reward, the value function associated to a policy that almost surely satisfies an $ω$-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for that specification. Our results, validated experimentally on finite Markov decision processes, hold for finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces, suggesting a principled route to certificate synthesis via RL.

</details>


### [352] [The Dynamic-Probabilistic Consistency Gap in Chaotic Surrogate Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31547)
*Andre Herz,Matthijs Pals,Daniel Durstewitz,Georgia Koppe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies a dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap in dynamical systems reconstruction, where finite-horizon probabilistic objectives degrade dynamics or decouple uncertainty from tangent dynamics. Three mechanisms (core collapse, noise masking, blind uncertainty) are identified. The authors propose KAFFEE, a Kalman-filter-based training framework that mitigates this gap and improves reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To expose and address the inconsistency between probabilistic objectives and dynamical fidelity in DSR, where standard training methods produce uncertainty estimates that fail to reflect the true local dynamics.

Method: KAFFEE uses a differentiable extended Kalman filter framework that evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) while propagating covariance through learned local Jacobians, ensuring uncertainty is dynamically consistent.

Result: On stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty; improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants; maintains competitive predictive scores; and enables in-context Bayesian filtering across 13 chaotic systems while preserving zero-shot dynamics.

Conclusion: The DPC gap is a fundamental issue in DSR that can be effectively mitigated by incorporating Kalman filtering principles into training, producing models with dynamically meaningful uncertainty estimates.

Abstract: Dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR) aims to learn surrogate models that capture the dynamics underlying time-series data. Reliably deploying these surrogates requires uncertainty estimates consistent with the learned dynamics. We expose a dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap: the pursuit of finite-horizon probabilistic objectives can degrade dynamics or decouple predictive uncertainty from the local tangent dynamics it ought to reflect. We isolate three mechanisms behind this gap: core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty. Specifically, we show that open-loop Gaussian rollout objectives can penalize Jacobian-generated covariance growth in chaotic systems, encouraging optimization shortcuts that weaken physical expansion or decouple uncertainty from it. To mitigate this gap, we propose KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation), a differentiable extended Kalman filter-based training framework that evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) while transporting covariance through learned local Jacobians. On stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces the identified failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants relative to open-loop objectives, and maintains competitive predictive scores. We further show that the DPC gap appears when probabilistically adapting a DSR foundation model across 13 chaotic systems, where KAFFEE enables in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.

</details>


### [353] [Functional Attention: From Pairwise Affinities to Functional Correspondences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31559)
*Jiefang Xiao,Maolin Gao,Simon Weber,Guandao Yang,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Functional Attention, a method that reinterprets attention mechanisms as functional correspondences between adaptive bases, replacing softmax affinities with structured linear operators for operator learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transformer-based operators treat continuous fields as discrete tokens with token-wise attention, ignoring the global functional structure of infinite-dimensional function spaces.

Method: Functional Attention reinterprets attention as functional correspondence between adaptive bases, inspired by geometric functional maps, replacing softmax affinities with structured linear operators for compact, resolution-invariant representations.

Result: Matches state-of-the-art performance in operator learning tasks including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, while remaining robust to varying discretizations.

Conclusion: Functional Attention provides a more structured, generalizable, and resolution-invariant approach to operator learning compared to standard token-wise attention mechanisms.

Abstract: Learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces, or operator learning, is essential for many machine learning applications. Although transformer-based operators are popular, they often rely on token-wise attention. These methods treat continuous fields as discrete tokens and usually ignore the global functional structure. We introduce \emph{Functional Attention}, which reinterprets attention as a functional correspondence between adaptive bases. Inspired by geometric functional maps, our method replaces softmax affinities with structured linear operators. This yields a compact, generalizable, resolution-invariant representation that explicitly captures global dependencies. Experiments demonstrate that \emph{Functional Attention} can match state-of-the-art performance in many operator learning tasks, including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, while remaining robust to varying discretizations. Project page is available at https://github.com/xjffff/FUNCATTN.

</details>


### [354] [Effective Biological Representation Learning by Masking Gene Expression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31562)
*Kian Kenyon-Dean,Alina Selega,Ihab Bendidi,Jordan M. Sorokin,Luca Bertinetto,David Errington,Hayley Donnella,Oren Kraus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TxFM is a self-supervised masked autoencoding model for RNA-seq data that, through careful architectural design and training on a curated 1.4M-sample corpus (DiverseRNA-1.4M), outperforms foundation models trained on corpora over 100x larger, demonstrating that inductive representation learning with proper data curation is a viable approach for transcriptomics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transcriptomic foundation models underperform relative to linear baselines, raising the question of whether deep representation learning provides advantages over raw transcript counts. The authors aim to explore this by developing a model with a focus on inductive representation learning.

Method: TxFM employs a masked autoencoding approach tailored to diverse RNA-seq count data. The authors conduct ablation studies to identify crucial architecture configurations and curate a public training corpus called DiverseRNA-1.4M.

Result: TxFM trained on DiverseRNA-1.4M yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform FMs trained on atlas-scale corpora over 100x larger.

Conclusion: Inductive self-supervised learning is a viable modeling approach for transcriptomics representation, provided careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation.

Abstract: RNA sequencing produces rich and diverse datasets of gene expression, offering compelling insights into cellular state and function that have many applications in drug discovery. Modeling such data is challenging due to inherent technical noise and experimental batch effects, as evidenced by many existing transcriptomic foundation models (FMs) underperforming relative to linear baselines. Such results raise the question of whether deep representation learning provides a distinct advantage over the direct use of raw transcript counts. Our work explores this by developing a new self-supervised model, TxFM, with a focus on inductive representation learning evaluations. TxFM employs a masked autoencoding approach tailored to diverse RNA-seq count data, and our ablation study empirically identifies crucial architecture configurations required for strong transfer performance. Additionally, we curate a public training corpus, DiverseRNA-1.4M, and find that TxFM trained on this curated dataset yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform FMs trained on atlas-scale corpora over 100x larger. Overall, our results indicate that inductive self-supervised learning is a viable modeling approach for transcriptomics representation, provided a careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation.

</details>


### [355] [Giving Sensors a Voice: Multimodal JEPA for Semantic Time-Series Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31580)
*Utsav Dutta,Gerardo Pastrana,Sina Khoshfetrat Pakazad,Henrik Ohlsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHARM is a Transformer-based model for heterogeneous multivariate time series that uses channel-level text descriptions and a JEPA training objective to learn robust, interpretable representations, achieving strong performance across anomaly detection, classification, and forecasting tasks with only linear probes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Transformer-based architectures have advanced sequence modeling in language and vision, general-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series remains underexplored. The authors aim to fill this gap.

Method: CHARM uses a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order, incorporates channel-level textual descriptions, is trained with a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), and uses a novel loss promoting informative and temporally stable embeddings. Description-aware gating provides interpretability through learned inter-channel relationships.

Result: The learned embeddings achieve strong performance across anomaly detection, classification, and short- and long-term forecasting using only a linear probe. Performance is driven primarily by the JEPA objective and conditioning architecture.

Conclusion: Text descriptions serve as channel identifiers enabling cross-dataset generalization. The JEPA objective and conditioning architecture are the main drivers of performance.

Abstract: Transformer-based architectures have advanced sequence modeling in language and vision, yet general-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series remains underexplored. We introduce CHARM (Channel-Aware Representation Model), which incorporates channel-level textual descriptions into a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order. CHARM is trained with a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and a novel loss promoting informative, temporally stable embeddings; latent-space prediction encourages robustness to sensor noise while description-aware gating provides interpretability through learned inter-channel relationships. Across anomaly detection, classification, and short- and long-term forecasting, the learned embeddings achieve strong performance using only a linear probe. Performance is driven primarily by the JEPA objective and conditioning architecture, with text descriptions serving as channel identifiers for cross-dataset generalization.

</details>


### [356] [A Tight Theory of Error Feedback Algorithms in Distributed Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31594)
*Daniel Berg Thomsen,Adrien Taylor,Aymeric Dieuleveut*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides tight convergence analyses for two error-feedback algorithms (EF and EF21) in distributed learning, identifying optimal step-size choices and Lyapunov functions to understand their performance under gradient compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication costs are a major bottleneck in distributed learning, and while gradient compression helps, it degrades convergence. Error feedback mechanisms can mitigate this, but their relative performance is poorly understood.

Method: Tight convergence analyses for two error-feedback algorithms (EF and EF21) by identifying optimal step-size choices and constructing optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each method.

Result: The analyses hold independently of the number of agents and recover the best known guarantees possible in the single-agent regime.

Conclusion: Optimal step-size and Lyapunov function choices yield tight convergence bounds for EF and EF21, clarifying their performance relative to each other in distributed optimization.

Abstract: Communication costs are a major bottleneck in distributed learning and first-order optimization. A common approach to alleviate this issue is to compress the gradient information exchanged between agents. However, such compression typically degrades the convergence guarantees of gradient-based methods. Error feedback mechanisms provide a simple and computationally cheap remedy for this issue, but numerous variants have been proposed, and their relative performance remains poorly understood. This paper provides tight convergence analyses for two of the main error-feedback algorithms from the literature, the classic Error Feedback method (EF) and Error Feedback 21 (EF21), by identifying optimal step-size choices and constructing optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each method. The results hold independently of the number of agents and recover the known best guarantees possible in the single-agent regime.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [357] [PhyDrawGen: Physically Grounded Diagram Generation from Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30512)
*Nafiul Haque,Syed Nazmus Sakib,Shifat E Arman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PhyDrawGen is a neuro-symbolic pipeline for generating physically accurate physics diagrams from text, using LLMs for scene graph extraction, deterministic solvers for constraint encoding, and a vision model with propose-verify loop for correction, outperforming GPT-5-image and Gemini models on a 1,449-problem benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative models produce visually plausible physics diagrams but systematically hallucinate force vectors, ignore conservation laws, and violate geometric constraints, necessitating a more physically rigorous approach.

Method: A three-stage neuro-symbolic pipeline: (1) LLM extracts a typed scene graph from text; (2) a deterministic solver converts the graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG) encoding exact physical primitives; (3) a fine-tuned Qwen-VL model uses a visually grounded propose-verify loop to iteratively correct constraint violations.

Result: On a benchmark of 1,449 problems across mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism, PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro, showing robust accuracy even on unusual-object problems.

Conclusion: Decoupling semantic understanding from physical constraint satisfaction via a neuro-symbolic approach yields substantially more physically accurate physics diagrams than purely generative models.

Abstract: Generating physics diagrams from text requires strict adherence to physical laws. While current generative models produce visually plausible outputs, they systematically hallucinate force vectors, ignore conservation laws, and violate geometric constraints. We present PhyDrawGen, a neuro-symbolic pipeline that decouples semantic scene understanding from physical constraint satisfaction. First, a large language model extracts a typed scene graph from the problem text. A deterministic solver then converts this graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG), encoding force balance, optical paths, and field topologies as exact geometric primitives. Finally, a fine-tuned Qwen-VL model implements a visually grounded propose-verify loop to iteratively correct any constraint violations. Evaluated on a benchmark of 1,449 problems spanning mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism, PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro, demonstrating robust physical accuracy even on unusual-object problems.

</details>


### [358] [Physically Viable World Models: A Case for Query-Conditioned Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30542)
*Adam J. Thorpe,Stepan Tretiakov,Cheng-Hsi Hsiao,Su Ann Low,Xingjian Li,Hassan Iqbal,Neel P. Bhatt,Ufuk Topcu,Krishna Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that world models for embodied AI must be physically viable—answering intervention queries about action outcomes rather than just predicting observations. It exposes failures of observation-predictive models, proposes decomposable world models with the simplest sufficient physical abstraction, and demonstrates this approach on queries where existing systems fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing observation-predictive world models produce visually plausible but physically wrong rollouts; distinct physical systems can look identical yet diverge under intervention. This leads to recommending infeasible actions, mispredicting interaction outcomes, or certifying unsafe behavior.

Method: The paper proposes world models with modular components (environment representation, latent state/parameter estimation, action specification, interventional dynamics, query-level response) and an autonomous orchestrator that identifies the simplest sufficient physical abstraction, composing compatible learned and structured components per query. The transition model can be analytic, simulated, learned, or hybrid but must preserve structure determining interventional outcomes.

Result: Demonstrates the approach on queries that existing observation-predictive systems fail to answer correctly, showing that the right abstraction is the simplest model preserving distinctions relevant to the query, not the most detailed model.

Conclusion: Physically viable world models require decomposable, interpretable, verifiable, and auditable structures. The approach provides a design principle for new models and a feasibility test for existing ones, with an orchestrator dynamically assembling and adapting models for planning, control, and verification.
